topics.title: Differential Amplifier Configurations Using BJT, DC Analysis - Transfer Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Differential Amplifier Configurations Using BJT, DC Analysis - Transfer Characteristics # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a BJT Differential Amplifier? A **Differential Amplifier (Diff-Amp)** is a two-input, electronic circuit that amplifies the **difference** between two input voltage signals while actively **rejecting any signal common to both inputs** (such as noise, drift, or interference). When constructed using **Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJTs)**, it forms the fundamental building block of nearly every operational amplifier, comparator, mixer, and analog multiplier used in modern integrated circuits. Formally, the output of an ideal BJT differential amplifier is given by: $$V_o = A_d(V_{id}) + A_c(V_{ic})$$ Where: - $V_{id} = V_{B1} - V_{B2}$ is the **differential input voltage** - $V_{ic} = \dfrac{V_{B1} + V_{B2}}{2}$ is the **common-mode input voltage** - $A_d$ is the **differential-mode gain** - $A_c$ is the **common-mode gain** (ideally zero) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The BJT differential pair is the *heart* of every op-amp. Mastering its DC biasing, four configurations, and transfer characteristics is mandatory for solving KTU ESE numericals and design problems. ## 1.2 The Four Standard Configurations (KTU Mandatory) Depending on the number of inputs used and the number of outputs taken, the BJT differential amplifier can be operated in **four distinct configurations**: | S.No. | Configuration | Inputs Used | Outputs Taken | Common Name | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Dual-Input Balanced-Output** | Both ($V_{B1}$, $V_{B2}$) | Both ($V_{C1}$, $V_{C2}$) | **DIBO** | | 2 | **Dual-Input Unbalanced-Output** | Both ($V_{B1}$, $V_{B2}$) | Single (either $V_{C1}$ or $V_{C2}$) | **DIUO** | | 3 | **Single-Input Balanced-Output** | One ($V_{B1}$ or $V_{B2}$) | Both ($V_{C1}$, $V_{C2}$) | **SIBO** | | 4 | **Single-Input Unbalanced-Output** | One ($V_{B1}$ or $V_{B2}$) | Single (either $V_{C1}$ or $V_{C2}$) | **SIUO** | > [!NOTE] > The **DIBO** configuration gives the highest possible gain ($2A_d$) and is preferred for high-signal-swing applications. The **SIUO** is the most compact and forms the input stage of every classic op-amp (like the µA741). ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are standing at the center of a **two-pan balance scale**, and your goal is to detect the *smallest possible weight difference* between two objects placed on either pan. - If a gust of wind (representing **noise** or **common-mode interference**) pushes *both* pans downward equally, the scale must remain steady — this is the role of **Common-Mode Rejection**. - If you add a tiny extra weight on the **left pan only**, the needle tilts to the left by a measurable amount — this tilt represents the **differential output**. - The **tail-current source** ($I_0$ in the diff-amp) is like the *fixed total weight* the scale is designed to handle — it determines the linear operating range. In an IC, this "scale" is built using two perfectly matched BJTs sharing a single tail current, so any thermal drift or supply variation affects both halves equally and is therefore **cancelled out**. ## 1.4 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{26\,mV}$ at room temperature ($\mathbf{300\,K}$) - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,J/K}$ - **Electronic Charge:** $q = \mathbf{1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,C}$ - **Transconductance parameter:** $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{I_0}{2V_T}$ for the differential pair - **CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio):** $\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$, usually expressed in **dB** as $20\log_{10}\left(\vert A_d/A_c \vert\right)$ # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Topology — The Emitter-Coupled Pair The standard BJT differential pair consists of two **matched NPN transistors** ($Q_1$ and $Q_2$) whose emitters are tied together at a common node E. This node is fed by a **constant-current source** (or equivalently a large emitter resistor $R_E$ tied to $-V_{EE}$) which supplies a fixed **tail current $I_0$**. The collectors are tied to $+V_{CC}$ through equal collector resistors $R_C$. Two DC supply rails $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$ are used to bias both halves symmetrically. ### Why a Current Source in the Tail? 1. Provides a **very high output resistance** $R_0$, which boosts the common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR). 2. Sets a **rigid, temperature-independent** DC bias for the emitters. 3. Establishes the **input common-mode range (ICMR)** limits. ## 2.2 DC Analysis — Step-by-Step Logic ### Step 1: Tail-Current Determination Apply KVL around the base-emitter-base loop with both bases grounded (for DC bias point calculation): $$V_{BE1} + I_{E1}R_E' + I_{E2}R_E' + V_{BE2} = 0$$ Wait — this is the standard approach for the **emitter-resistor biased** topology. For the constant-current-source biased topology: $$\boxed{I_0 = I_{E1} + I_{E2} = \text{Tail Current (fixed by current mirror or } R_E\text{)}}$$ ### Step 2: Base-Emitter Loop (for the $R_E$-biased version) With both inputs grounded, by symmetry $I_{E1} = I_{E2} = I_E$: $$V_{EE} = V_{BE} + I_{E1}R_E' + I_{E2}R_E' + I_{E}R_E$$ $$\boxed{I_E = \dfrac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{2R_E + \dfrac{R_E'}{2}}$$ Where $R_E'$ is the small internal resistance of the current source (often neglected, $R_E' \to \infty$ for ideal current source). For large $R_E$: $$I_E \approx \dfrac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{2R_E}$$ ### Step 3: Collector Current and $V_{CE}$ Quiescent Point $$I_C = \alpha I_E \approx I_E \quad \text{(since } \alpha \approx 1\text{)}$$ $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C + V_{BE}$$ This sets the **Q-point** for both transistors. ## 2.3 Transfer Characteristics (The Heart of the Topic) The transfer characteristic describes how the **collector currents vary as a function of the differential input voltage** $V_{id}$. It is the single most important plot asked in KTU university exams. ### Differential-Mode Transfer Equation Using the Ebers-Moll model and assuming matched transistors with $\alpha \approx 1$: $$I_{C1} = \dfrac{\alpha I_0}{1 + \exp(-V_{id}/V_T)} = \dfrac{I_0}{1 + \exp(-V_{id}/V_T)}$$ $$I_{C2} = \dfrac{\alpha I_0}{1 + \exp(+V_{id}/V_T)} = \dfrac{I_0}{1 + \exp(+V_{id}/V_T)}$$ The **difference current** (which drives the differential output): $$\boxed{I_d = I_{C1} - I_{C2} = I_0 \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{V_{id}}{2V_T}\right)}$$ And the **sum** is the constant tail current: $$I_{C1} + I_{C2} = \alpha I_0 \approx I_0$$ ### Three Operating Regions of the Transfer Curve | Region | Condition | $V_{id}$ Range | Behavior | |---|---|---|---| | **Linear** | $\vert V_{id}\vert \leq V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ | $\pm 26\,\text{mV}$ | $I_d \approx \dfrac{I_0 V_{id}}{2V_T}$ — amplifier acts linearly | | **Transition** | $\pm 100\,\text{mV}$ | moderate | Hyperbolic tangent curvature | | **Saturation/Cutoff** | $\vert V_{id}\vert > 4V_T \approx 100\,\text{mV}$ | $> 100\,\text{mV}$ | One BJT fully ON, other fully OFF — acts as a **limiter** or **current switch** | ### Common-Mode Transfer Characteristic When both inputs swing together by $V_{ic}$, ideally both collector currents should remain at $I_0/2$. In reality: $$I_{C1} = I_{C2} = \dfrac{I_0}{2} + \dfrac{V_{ic}}{2R_E + r_e}\cdot(\text{small correction})$$ The common-mode output is therefore $V_{oc} = A_c \cdot V_{ic}$, ideally zero. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table** — it covers 90% of KTU numerical problems on this topic. | S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Typical Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Tail current (ideal current source) | $I_0$ | mA | Set by current mirror or $V_{EE},R_E$ | | 2 | Emitter current (DC bias) | $I_E = \dfrac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{2R_E}$ | mA | For $R_E$-biased topology | | 3 | Collector quiescent current | $I_{CQ} = \alpha I_E$ | mA | Usually $\approx I_E/2$ per transistor | | 4 | Quiescent $V_{CE}$ | $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} + V_{BE} - I_C R_C$ | V | Symmetric bias point | | 5 | Differential gain (DIBO) | $A_d = \dfrac{-R_C}{r_e}$ | dimensionless | $r_e = V_T/I_E$ | | 6 | Differential gain (DIUO/SIUO) | $A_d = \dfrac{-R_C}{2r_e}$ | dimensionless | Half of DIBO | | 7 | Common-mode gain | $A_c = \dfrac{-R_C}{2R_E + r_e}$ | dimensionless | Ideally zero | | 8 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$ | ratio or dB | Higher is better | | 9 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_0}{2V_T}$ | A/V or S | For the differential pair as a whole | | 10 | Difference current | $I_d = I_0\tanh(V_{id}/2V_T)$ | mA | Output drive current | | 11 | Linear range limit | $\vert V_{id}\vert \leq V_T$ | $\pm 26\,\text{mV}$ | Small-signal regime | | 12 | Saturation limit | $\vert V_{id}\vert \geq 4V_T$ | $\pm 100\,\text{mV}$ | Acts as a current switch | | 13 | Input common-mode max | $V_{IC(\max)} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C + V_{BE}$ | V | Upper ICMR bound | | 14 | Input common-mode min | $V_{IC(\min)} = -V_{EE} + V_{BE} + I_0 R_E$ | V | Lower ICMR bound (active load version) | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Operational Amplifiers (µA741, LM358, OP07):** The input stage of every general-purpose op-amp is a BJT differential pair. - **Instrumentation Amplifiers:** Three op-amps arranged using the differential pair principle to amplify tiny biomedical signals (ECG, EEG) buried in 50 Hz mains noise. - **High-Speed Comparators (LM311, AD790):** The differential pair is driven into saturation to act as a high-speed decision-making switch. - **Mixers and Modulators:** Exploiting the $\tanh$ non-linearity of the transfer characteristic to multiply two signals (Gilbert Cell). - **Sense Amplifiers in DRAM:** Reading sub-millivolt signals from memory bit-lines by rejecting common-mode noise. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Tail Current $I_0$ (Resistor-Biased Topology) Consider the emitter-coupled pair with both bases connected to ground for DC bias analysis. Let $R_E'$ represent the finite output resistance of the emitter current source. **Step 1: Apply KVL around the base-emitter loop (Base of $Q_1$ → $E$ → Base of $Q_2$):** $$-V_{BE1} - I_{E1}R_E' + I_{E2}R_E' + V_{BE2} = 0$$ **Step 2: Due to perfect matching of $Q_1$ and $Q_2$, and both bases at ground potential:** $$V_{BE1} = V_{BE2} = V_{BE}$$ Therefore: $$I_{E1}R_E' = I_{E2}R_E' \implies I_{E1} = I_{E2} = I_E$$ **Step 3: Apply KVL through the lower emitter resistor $R_E$ to $-V_{EE}$:** $$-I_E R_E - I_{E1}R_E' - V_{BE} = V_{EE}$$ Wait, careful with sign conventions. Going from ground (base) to $-V_{EE}$: $$0 - V_{BE} - I_{E1}R_E' - (I_{E1}+I_{E2})R_E = -V_{EE}$$ **Step 4: Substitute $I_{E1} = I_{E2} = I_E$:** $$V_{EE} - V_{BE} = 2 I_E R_E + I_E R_E'$$ $$V_{EE} - V_{BE} = I_E(2R_E + R_E')$$ **Step 5: Final expression for emitter current per transistor:** $$\boxed{I_E = \dfrac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{2R_E + R_E'}}$$ **Step 6: For an *ideal* current source, $R_E' \to \infty$**, so $I_E \to 0$. In this case the tail current $I_0$ is set externally by the current mirror: $$\boxed{I_0 = 2I_E}$$ **Conversion Logic Summary:** The differential pair's DC bias is set by ensuring both transistors share the tail current equally when inputs are at the common-mode level. The key insight is *symmetry*: matched transistors + equal base voltages ⇒ equal emitter currents ⇒ tail current splits evenly. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Transfer Characteristic $I_d = I_0 \tanh(V_{id}/2V_T)$ **Step 1: Write the emitter currents using the diode equation for BJTs:** $$I_{E1} = I_{S}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BE1}}{V_T}\right), \quad I_{E2} = I_{S}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BE2}}{V_T}\right)$$ **Step 2: Apply KCL at the common emitter node:** $$I_{E1} + I_{E2} = -I_0$$ (Negative sign because $I_0$ flows *into* the emitter node from the current source.) **Step 3: Take the ratio of the two emitter currents:** $$\dfrac{I_{E1}}{I_{E2}} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BE1} - V_{BE2}}{V_T}\right) = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{id}}{V_T}\right)$$ This is because $V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = (V_{B1} - V_E) - (V_{B2} - V_E) = V_{B1} - V_{B2} = V_{id}$. **Step 4: Solve for $I_{E1}$ and $I_{E2}$ individually:** From $I_{E1} + I_{E2} = -I_0$ and the ratio above: $$I_{E1} = \dfrac{-I_0}{1 + \exp(-V_{id}/V_T)}, \quad I_{E2} = \dfrac{-I_0}{1 + \exp(+V_{id}/V_T)}$$ **Step 5: Approximate $I_C \approx I_E$ and write the difference:** $$I_{C1} - I_{C2} = \dfrac{-I_0}{1 + \exp(-V_{id}/V_T)} - \dfrac{-I_0}{1 + \exp(+V_{id}/V_T)}$$ **Step 6: Combine over a common denominator and use $\tanh$ identity:** Using the identity $\tanh(x) = \dfrac{\exp(x) - \exp(-x)}{\exp(x) + \exp(-x)} = \dfrac{e^{2x} - 1}{e^{2x} + 1}$: $$\begin{aligned} I_d &= I_{C1} - I_{C2} = I_0 \cdot \dfrac{\exp(V_{id}/V_T) - 1}{\exp(V_{id}/V_T) + 1} \\ &= I_0 \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{V_{id}}{2V_T}\right) \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{I_d = I_0 \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{V_{id}}{2V_T}\right)}$$ **Conversion Logic:** The $\tanh$ function emerges *naturally* from the exponential $I$-$V$ relation of the BJT. For small $V_{id}$ (less than $V_T$), $\tanh(x) \approx x$, giving linear amplification. For large $V_{id}$, $\tanh$ saturates at $\pm 1$, giving the current-switching behavior. ## 3.3 Derivation of Differential-Mode Gain $A_d$ For small-signal analysis, replace each BJT by its small-signal model ($r_\pi$, $g_m$, $r_e = V_T/I_E$). **Step 1: Apply a pure differential input** $V_{id} = V_{B1} - V_{B2}$, with $V_{ic} = 0$. By symmetry, the voltage at the common emitter node does *not* change (it remains a virtual ground for differential signals). **Step 2: The small-signal emitter current of $Q_1$ is:** $$i_{e1} = \dfrac{V_{B1} - V_E}{r_e} = \dfrac{+V_{id}/2}{r_e}$$ **Step 3: Output voltage at collector 1:** $$v_{c1} = -i_{c1} R_C \approx -i_{e1} R_C = -\dfrac{V_{id}R_C}{2r_e}$$ **Step 4: Output voltage at collector 2 (opposite polarity):** $$v_{c2} = +\dfrac{V_{id}R_C}{2r_e}$$ **Step 5: For DIBO, the differential output is:** $$V_{od} = v_{c1} - v_{c2} = -\dfrac{V_{id}R_C}{r_e}$$ $$\boxed{A_d = \dfrac{V_{od}}{V_{id}} = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e}}$$ **Step 6: For DIUO or SIUO (single-ended output):** $$\boxed{A_d = -\dfrac{R_C}{2r_e}}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation of Common-Mode Gain $A_c$ **Step 1: Apply pure common-mode input** $V_{ic}$ to both bases. Now the emitter node is *not* a virtual ground — it rises by $V_E = V_{ic} - V_{BE}$. **Step 2: The tail current source (or $R_E$) presents an impedance of $2R_E$ (because both emitters see $R_E$ in parallel, with the factor of 2 appearing due to symmetry).** **Step 3: Small-signal collector current change:** $$i_c = \dfrac{V_{ic} - V_E}{r_e} = \dfrac{V_{ic}}{r_e + 2R_E}$$ **Step 4: Output voltage:** $$V_{oc} = -i_c R_C = -\dfrac{V_{ic} R_C}{r_e + 2R_E}$$ $$\boxed{A_c = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{V_{ic}} = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e + 2R_E}}$$ **Step 5: CMRR derivation:** $$\boxed{\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert = \dfrac{r_e + 2R_E}{r_e} \approx 1 + \dfrac{2R_E}{r_e}}$$ For $2R_E \gg r_e$ (large emitter degeneration), CMRR approaches $2R_E/r_e$, justifying the use of a current source (high $R_E$ equivalent). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Canonical BJT Differential Pair — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IN["Input Sources"] VB1["Base1 Input VB1"] VB2["Base2 Input VB2"] end subgraph STAGE1["Differential Pair Core"] Q1["BJT Q1 NPN Matched"] Q2["BJT Q2 NPN Matched"] end subgraph TAIL["Tail Current Source"] IEQ["Current Source I0 or RE"] end subgraph LOAD["Collector Loads"] RC1["RC1 to VCC"] RC2["RC2 to VCC"] end subgraph OUT["Output Nodes"] VC1["VC1 Collector Output"] VC2["VC2 Collector Output"] end VB1 --> Q1 VB2 --> Q2 Q1 --- EM["Common Emitter Node E"] Q2 --- EM EM --> IEQ Q1 --- RC1 Q2 --- RC2 RC1 --> VC1 RC2 --> VC2 Q1 -. Ic1 .-> VC1 Q2 -. Ic2 .-> VC2 ``` ## 4.2 Four Configurations Selector Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["BJT Differential Amplifier"] A --> B["Dual Input Balanced Output DIBO"] A --> C["Dual Input Unbalanced Output DIUO"] A --> D["Single Input Balanced Output SIBO"] A --> E["Single Input Unbalanced Output SIUO"] B --> B1["Inputs: VB1 VB2
Outputs: VC1 and VC2
Gain: -RC divided by re"] C --> C1["Inputs: VB1 VB2
Output: VC1 only
Gain: -RC divided by 2re"] D --> D1["Input: VB1 only VB2 grounded
Outputs: VC1 and VC2
Gain: -RC divided by re"] E --> E1["Input: VB1 only VB2 grounded
Output: VC1 only
Gain: -RC divided by 2re"] ``` ## 4.3 Transfer Characteristics Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB START["Input Differential Voltage Vid"] START --> CHECK{"Magnitude Check
versus VT 26 mV"} CHECK -->|Vid less than VT| LINEAR["LINEAR REGION
Id equals I0 times Vid divided by 2VT
Slope equals gm equals I0 divided by 2VT"] CHECK -->|VT less than Vid less than 4VT| TRANS["TRANSITION REGION
Id equals I0 tanh of Vid divided by 2VT
Curved Response"] CHECK -->|Vid greater than 4VT| SAT["SATURATION REGION
Id saturates to plus or minus I0
Current Switch Mode"] LINEAR --> OUT1["Differential Amplifier Mode"] TRANS --> OUT2["Soft Limiting Behavior"] SAT --> OUT3["Hard Switching
Comparator or Mixer Mode"] ``` ## 4.4 DC Biasing and Q-Point Calculation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Given: VCC VEE RC RE beta"] P --> Q1["Compute Tail Current I0
I0 equals VEE minus VBE divided by 2RE"] Q1 --> Q2["Compute IE per transistor
IE equals I0 divided by 2"] Q2 --> Q3["Compute IC equals alpha times IE"] Q3 --> Q4["Compute VCEQ equals VCC plus VBE minus IC times RC"] Q4 --> Q5["Compute re equals VT divided by IE"] Q5 --> Q6["Compute Ad equals minus RC divided by re"] Q6 --> Q7["Compute Ac equals minus RC divided by 2RE plus re"] Q7 --> Q8["Compute CMRR in dB
20 log of Ad divided by Ac"] Q8 --> DONE["Q-Point and Small-Signal Parameters Ready"] ``` ## 4.5 ICMR (Input Common-Mode Range) Boundaries ```mermaid flowchart LR VCC["Upper Rail VCC"] VCC --> VICMAX["VIC maximum
equals VCC minus IC RC plus VBE"] VICMAX --> OK["Common Mode Input lies within range"] OK --> VICMIN["VIC minimum
equals minus VEE plus VBE plus I0 RE"] VICMIN --> VEE["Lower Rail minus VEE"] ``` > [!NOTE] > When $V_{ic}$ exceeds $V_{IC(\max)}$, the active-load transistors enter the **triode region** and the diff-amp loses gain. When $V_{ic}$ drops below $V_{IC(\min)}$, the tail current source leaves saturation and $I_0$ collapses, again destroying gain. Operating *within* the ICMR is mandatory for linear amplification. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define a BJT differential amplifier. List its four configurations.* **Model Answer:** A BJT differential amplifier is a two-transistor circuit using a matched pair of BJTs sharing a common emitter tail current. It amplifies the difference between two input signals while rejecting common-mode signals. Four configurations: 1. Dual-Input Balanced-Output (**DIBO**) 2. Dual-Input Unbalanced-Output (**DIUO**) 3. Single-Input Balanced-Output (**SIBO**) 4. Single-Input Unbalanced-Output (**SIUO**) *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks enumeration)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is meant by the transfer characteristic of a differential amplifier? Write the mathematical expression for the difference current.* **Model Answer:** The transfer characteristic of a differential amplifier is the plot of the difference between collector currents $(I_{C1} - I_{C2})$ versus the differential input voltage $V_{id}$. Mathematical expression: $$I_d = I_{C1} - I_{C2} = I_0 \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{V_{id}}{2V_T}\right)$$ Where $I_0$ is the tail current and $V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks expression)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. You must answer **either** the full OR-alternative. Both alternatives are provided below. --- ### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)** #### **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]** **(a)** For the BJT differential amplifier shown, $V_{CC} = +12\,\text{V}$, $-V_{EE} = -12\,\text{V}$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 5.6\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\,\text{V}$, $\alpha \approx 1$, $\beta = 100$. **(i)** Calculate the tail current $I_0$ and quiescent collector current $I_{CQ}$. **(ii)** Find the differential-mode gain $A_d$ and common-mode gain $A_c$. **(iii)** Compute CMRR in dB. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With reference to the same circuit, draw and explain the **transfer characteristic** of the BJT differential amplifier. Mark the linear region, transition region, and saturation region. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part A(a):** **Given:** $V_{CC} = 12\,\text{V}$, $V_{EE} = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 5.6\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\,\text{V}$, $\alpha = 1$. **(i) Tail current and $I_{CQ}$:** $$I_0 = \dfrac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{2R_E} = \dfrac{12 - 0.7}{2 \times 5.6 \times 10^3}$$ $$I_0 = \dfrac{11.3}{11.2 \times 10^3} \approx 1.009\,\text{mA}$$ Quiescent collector current per transistor: $$I_{CQ} = \dfrac{I_0}{2} = \dfrac{1.009\,\text{mA}}{2} \approx 0.5045\,\text{mA}$$ **(ii) Differential and common-mode gains:** Compute $r_e$: $$r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} = \dfrac{26\,\text{mV}}{0.5045\,\text{mA}} \approx 51.5\,\Omega$$ Differential-mode gain (DIBO): $$A_d = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e} = -\dfrac{4700}{51.5} \approx -91.26$$ Common-mode gain: $$A_c = -\dfrac{R_C}{2R_E + r_e} = -\dfrac{4700}{2 \times 5600 + 51.5} = -\dfrac{4700}{11251.5} \approx -0.4177$$ **(iii) CMRR:** $$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert = \left\vert \dfrac{-91.26}{-0.4177} \right\vert \approx 218.5$$ In decibels: $$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(218.5) \approx 46.79\,\text{dB}$$ **Final Answers (boxed):** $$\boxed{I_0 \approx 1.009\,\text{mA},\ I_{CQ} \approx 0.5045\,\text{mA}}$$ $$\boxed{A_d \approx -91.26,\ A_c \approx -0.418}$$ $$\boxed{\text{CMRR} \approx 218.5 \approx 46.79\,\text{dB}}$$ #### **Model Solution — Part A(b):** The **transfer characteristic** of a BJT differential amplifier is the plot of $I_d = I_{C1} - I_{C2}$ versus $V_{id}$: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PLOT["Transfer Characteristic Id versus Vid"] A1["Vid less than minus 4VT
Id equals minus I0
Q1 OFF, Q2 ON"] A2["Vid equals minus VT
Id approx minus 0.46 I0"] A3["Vid equals 0
Id equals 0
Balance Point"] A4["Vid equals plus VT
Id approx plus 0.46 I0"] A5["Vid greater than plus 4VT
Id equals plus I0
Q1 ON, Q2 OFF"] end ``` **Three Regions:** 1. **Linear Region** ($\vert V_{id}\vert \leq V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$): $I_d \approx g_m V_{id}$ where $g_m = I_0/2V_T$. Slope is the transconductance of the pair. 2. **Transition Region** ($V_T < \vert V_{id}\vert < 4V_T$): Hyperbolic tangent curvature governs $I_d$. 3. **Saturation Region** ($\vert V_{id}\vert \geq 4V_T \approx 104\,\text{mV}$): $I_d \to \pm I_0$. One BJT is fully ON, the other fully OFF. The pair behaves as a **current-steering switch** (useful in ECL logic and comparators). The plot is **anti-symmetric** about the origin, passing through $(0,0)$, with horizontal asymptotes at $\pm I_0$. --- ### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)** #### **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **tail current** $I_0$ in a BJT differential amplifier using the emitter-resistor biasing topology. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Input Common-Mode Range (ICMR)** of a BJT differential amplifier with an active current-mirror load. Derive the expressions for $V_{IC(\max)}$ and $V_{IC(\min)}$. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part B(a):** **Assumptions:** 1. Both BJTs $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are perfectly matched ($V_{BE1} = V_{BE2}$, $\beta_1 = \beta_2$). 2. Both inputs are at DC ground (i.e., $V_{B1} = V_{B2} = 0$). 3. The current source has a finite internal resistance $R_E'$ in parallel with $R_E$. 4. $\alpha \approx 1$ and Early voltage $V_A \to \infty$. **Derivation:** Apply KVL from ground (base of $Q_1$) → emitter of $Q_1$ → emitter node $E$ → emitter of $Q_2$ → base of $Q_2$ → ground: $$0 - V_{BE1} - I_{E1}R_E' + I_{E2}R_E' + V_{BE2} = 0$$ Since $V_{BE1} = V_{BE2}$: $$I_{E1} = I_{E2} = I_E$$ Apply KVL through $R_E$ to $-V_{EE}$: $$-V_{BE} - I_{E1}R_E' - (I_{E1} + I_{E2})R_E + V_{EE} = 0$$ Substituting $I_{E1} = I_{E2} = I_E$: $$V_{EE} - V_{BE} = I_E R_E' + 2I_E R_E = I_E(R_E' + 2R_E)$$ $$\boxed{I_E = \dfrac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{2R_E + R_E'}}$$ For an ideal current source ($R_E' \to \infty$), the tail current $I_0$ is set externally and: $$\boxed{I_0 = 2I_E}$$ --- #### **Model Solution — Part B(b):** The **Input Common-Mode Range (ICMR)** is the range of common-mode input voltage $V_{ic}$ over which the differential amplifier operates linearly with normal bias currents. **Upper Limit $V_{IC(\max)}$:** The constraint is that the collector-emitter voltages of the BJTs must remain in the active region. For an **active current-mirror load**, $V_{IC(\max)}$ is reached when the diode-connected load transistor enters saturation: $$\boxed{V_{IC(\max)} = V_{CC} - I_{C}R_C + V_{BE}}$$ For a passive $R_C$ load, the constraint is: $$V_{IC(\max)} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C + V_{BE(\text{active})}$$ **Lower Limit $V_{IC(\min)}$:** The tail current source must remain in saturation. This fails when the collector of the current-source transistor approaches its saturation voltage: $$\boxed{V_{IC(\min)} = -V_{EE} + V_{BE} + I_0 R_E}$$ Where $V_{BE}$ is the base-emitter drop of the tail current transistor. **Operating Constraint:** $$\boxed{V_{IC(\min)} \leq V_{ic} \leq V_{IC(\max)}}$$ Outside this range, the diff-amp loses its DC bias integrity and the small-signal gain collapses to zero. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing $\beta$ with $\alpha$:** Always use $\alpha \approx 1$ for emitter-current to collector-current conversion in differential-pair problems. Using $\beta$ directly gives wrong answers. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $I_E$ formula:** The denominator must be $2R_E$, not $R_E$. This is because *both* transistors share the single emitter resistor. > 3. **Wrong sign of $V_{EE}$:** In KTU problems, $-V_{EE} = -12\,\text{V}$ means $V_{EE} = 12\,\text{V}$ when substituted in the formula. Sign errors are the #1 reason for losing 2-3 marks. > 4. **Using $V_T = 25\,\text{mV}$ instead of $26\,\text{mV}$:** Either is acceptable, but be **consistent**. KTU typically uses $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$. > 5. **Not labeling axes of the transfer curve:** The plot must have $V_{id}$ on x-axis (in mV) and $I_d$ on y-axis (in mA), with the asymptotes $\pm I_0$ clearly marked. Skipping axis labels costs 1 mark easily. > 6. **CMRR in dB:** Always convert to dB using $20\log_{10}$. KTU specifically asks for the dB value, not the absolute ratio. > 7. **Forgetting to draw the equivalent circuit** for transfer-characteristic problems: A clear small-signal equivalent circuit diagram is worth 2 marks by itself. ## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This Section Before Exams** - ☐ A **BJT differential amplifier** consists of two matched NPN transistors sharing a common emitter tail current source. - ☐ **Four configurations:** DIBO, DIUO, SIBO, SIUO — all derived from the same circuit. - ☐ DIBO has the **highest gain** ($A_d = -R_C/r_e$). DIUO/SIUO give **half** this gain. - ☐ Tail current formula (resistor-biased): $I_0 = (V_{EE} - V_{BE})/(2R_E)$. - ☐ Quiescent collector current per transistor: $I_{CQ} \approx I_0/2$. - ☐ Small-signal emitter resistance: $r_e = V_T/I_E \approx 26\,\text{mV}/I_E$. - ☐ Differential-mode gain: $A_d = -R_C/r_e$ (DIBO) or $-R_C/2r_e$ (DIUO/SIUO). - ☐ Common-mode gain: $A_c = -R_C/(2R_E + r_e)$. - ☐ CMRR $= |A_d/A_c|$; in dB: $20\log_{10}|A_d/A_c|$. - ☐ Transfer characteristic: $I_d = I_0\tanh(V_{id}/2V_T)$ — anti-symmetric about origin. - ☐ Linear region: $\vert V_{id}\vert \leq V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$. - ☐ Saturation region: $\vert V_{id}\vert \geq 4V_T \approx 100\,\text{mV}$. - ☐ In saturation, the pair acts as a **current switch** — basis of ECL logic. - ☐ ICMR is bounded above by collector saturation and below by tail-current source saturation. - ☐ A high-quality current source (large $R_E$ equivalent) gives **high CMRR**. - ☐ The differential pair is the **input stage** of virtually every op-amp IC. - ☐ The Gilbert Cell uses the $\tanh$ non-linearity for analog multiplication. - ☐ $g_m$ of the pair (as a whole) $= I_0/2V_T$, not $I_0/V_T$ (factor of 2 due to splitting).

topics.title: AC Analysis - Differential and Common Mode Gains, CMRR, Input and Output Resistance, Voltage Gain, Constant Current Bias, Constant Current Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** AC Analysis - Differential and Common Mode Gains, CMRR, Input and Output Resistance, Voltage Gain, Constant Current Bias, Constant Current Source # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Differential Amplifier — AC Domain Behavior In the **AC analysis** of a differential amplifier, we are interested in the small-signal response of the circuit to time-varying inputs, while the DC operating point (Q-point) is held constant by the bias network. The two fundamental input modes are: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Module 1.6)** > - **Differential-Mode Signal (vid):** A pair of equal-amplitude, opposite-phase signals applied to the two input terminals: $v_{id} = v_1 - v_2$ with $v_1 = +v_{id}/2$ and $v_2 = -v_{id}/2$. > - **Common-Mode Signal (vic):** A pair of equal-amplitude, in-phase signals: $v_{ic} = (v_1 + v_2)/2$ with $v_1 = v_2 = v_{ic}$. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two friends (**Q1 and Q2**) standing on either side of a perfectly balanced **seesaw** (the emitter-coupled pair). - **Differential signal** = They push *opposite directions* with equal force → the seesaw tilts sharply. The amplifier **amplifies** this large useful motion. (This is the "signal" you want to hear — for example, a faint heart-beat picked up by a biomedical electrode pair.) - **Common-mode signal** = They both push *downwards together* with equal force → the seesaw stays perfectly level. The amplifier should ideally **reject** this motion. (This is electrical "noise" — like 50 Hz mains hum — that contaminates both leads equally.) The amplifier's quality is judged by **how well it amplifies the difference while killing the common part**. This is exactly what **CMRR** measures. ### The Three Output Measurements For a two-input, two-output (or single-ended) circuit, we define three voltage gains: | Gain | Symbol | Definition | |------|--------|-----------| | **Differential-Mode Gain** | $A_d$ | Ratio of output voltage swing to the *differential* input, with common-mode input set to zero | | **Common-Mode Gain** | $A_c$ | Ratio of output voltage swing to the *common-mode* input, with differential input set to zero | | **Total (Single-Ended) Gain** | $A$ | Ratio of output to a general input $v_{in}$ — typically $A \approx A_d/2$ for a single-ended output of a symmetric diff-pair | > [!NOTE] > The factor of **2** in "A ≈ Ad/2" appears because a full differential input $v_{id}$ is split as $v_{id}/2$ per side, and only one output terminal is taken. --- ## 1.2 Constant-Current Bias (Active Bias) A **constant current bias (CCB)** is a high-output-impedance current source that replaces the long-tailed emitter resistor $R_E$ at the tail of the differential pair. - It delivers a fixed DC current $I_{EE}$ to the emitter-coupled pair. - It exhibits a very large small-signal output resistance $R_o$ (ideally $\rightarrow \infty$). - The **AC effect** of $R_E$ is replaced by $R_o$ in every common-mode formula, dramatically improving **CMRR** and **common-mode input resistance**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why use a current source instead of $R_E$?** > - DC: It establishes the bias point **independent of supply variations and temperature** (a "stiff" bias). > - AC: It behaves like an almost infinite emitter degeneration resistor, killing the common-mode gain. ### Conceptual Analogy: The "Grounded" Anchor A constant current source is like a **rigid, immovable anchor** at the bottom of the seesaw. When both friends jump together (common mode), the anchor *holds the bottom of the seesaw still* — no motion is transmitted upward, so the output stays near zero. When the friends push opposite (differential mode), the top of the seesaw rotates around the anchor — that motion *is* transmitted to the output. --- ## 1.3 Constant-Current Source (The Building Block) The constant-current source is itself realized by a **current mirror** (e.g., a BJT with diode-connected reference, or a Wilson / Widlar current source). It is the foundation of all analog IC biasing. A simple **basic BJT current mirror** delivers a reference current $I_{REF} = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R$ and forces the output transistor to copy this current, $I_o \approx I_{REF}$, regardless of the load voltage (within compliance limits). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Constant current bias and constant current source together form the **active load / active bias** of every modern op-amp input stage (e.g., 741's input diff-pair uses a pnp current-mirror active load). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 AC Equivalent Circuit of the BJT Differential Pair For small-signal AC analysis, we replace each BJT with its **hybrid-π small-signal model**: - Base–emitter resistance: $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ - Transconductance: $g_m = I_C / V_T$ (where $V_T \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K) - Output resistance: $r_o = V_A / I_C$ (Early effect) Because the circuit is **symmetric**, the analysis splits cleanly into two **half-circuits** (one for differential mode, one for common mode) by exploiting superposition. --- ## 2.2 Differential-Mode Half-Circuit Analysis In differential mode, the two emitters meet at a **virtual ground** (because the symmetry of the circuit forces $v_e = 0$ for purely differential excitation). This lets us "split" the pair into two identical single-transistor common-emitter amplifiers, each driven by $v_{id}/2$. ### Differential-Mode Half-Circuit The half-circuit is a standard CE amplifier with input $v_{id}/2$, emitter at AC ground, and collector load $R_C$ (or $R_D$ for MOS, or active-load $r_o$ for IC). **Differential-Mode Voltage Gain** (single-ended output, per side): $$A_{d1} = \frac{v_{od1}}{v_{id}} = -\frac{1}{2} g_m R_C$$ **Differential-Mode Voltage Gain** (differential output, between the two collectors): $$A_{dd} = \frac{v_{od1} - v_{od2}}{v_{id}} = -g_m R_C$$ **Differential-Mode Input Resistance** (seen between the two bases): $$R_{id} = 2 r_\pi$$ **Output Resistance** (single-ended): $$R_{o,d} \approx r_o \parallel R_C \approx R_C \quad \text{(if } r_o \gg R_C\text{)}$$ --- ## 2.3 Common-Mode Analysis In common mode, both inputs move together: $v_1 = v_2 = v_{ic}$. The two emitters **no longer stay at AC ground**; instead, they share a common voltage $v_e$. We can now think of the pair as two CE stages whose emitters are tied together through a **degenerated** path of impedance $2R_E$ (because the two emitter resistors $R_E$ appear in series when viewed from the common emitter node). ### Key Common-Mode Formulas **Common-Mode Voltage Gain** (single-ended output): $$A_{c1} = \frac{v_{oc1}}{v_{ic}} = -\frac{g_m R_C}{1 + g_m (2R_E)} \approx -\frac{R_C}{2 R_E} \quad \text{(for } g_m R_E \gg 1\text{)}$$ **Common-Mode Input Resistance** (seen from each base to ground): $$R_{ic} = r_\pi + (\beta + 1)(2R_E) \approx 2 \beta R_E$$ **Output Resistance** (single-ended, common mode): $$R_{o,c} \approx R_C$$ --- ## 2.4 CMRR — The Figure of Merit > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is the ratio of the magnitude of the differential-mode gain to the magnitude of the common-mode gain: > $$\text{CMRR} = \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right|$$ > Usually expressed in decibels: $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_d / A_c \vert$. For a BJT diff-pair with tail resistor $R_E$: $$\text{CMRR} \approx \frac{1}{2} g_m (2R_E) = g_m R_E$$ **Insight:** $g_m R_E$ is the **degradation factor**. To improve CMRR, we need a *very large* $R_E$. Since on-chip resistors are limited in size, we replace $R_E$ with a **constant-current source** whose small-signal output resistance $R_o$ can be in the MΩ range. With current-source bias (output resistance $R_o$ of the current source): $$\text{CMRR} = g_m R_o$$ $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(g_m R_o)$$ A high-quality op-amp has CMRR ≥ 80 dB (10,000:1); the LM741 quotes 90 dB typ. --- ## 2.5 Voltage Gain with Active Load (Current-Mirror Load) When the collector resistors $R_C$ are replaced by a **current-mirror active load**, the effective load at each collector becomes $r_{o2} \parallel r_{o4}$ (the parallel combination of the diff-pair transistor and the mirror transistor's output resistances). **Voltage Gain (single-ended output, active load):** $$A_d = -g_m (r_{o2} \parallel r_{o4}) = -\frac{g_m r_o}{2}$$ This **doubled intrinsic gain** ($g_m r_o / 2$ vs $g_m r_o$) is why active loads are ubiquitous in IC op-amps. --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Conditions | |---|----------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | $V_T \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ @ 300 K | | 2 | Base–emitter resistance | $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ | Hybrid-π model | | 3 | Output resistance | $r_o = V_A / I_C$ | $V_A$ = Early voltage | | 4 | Diff. gain (passive $R_C$, single-ended) | $A_d = -g_m R_C / 2$ | One output terminal | | 5 | Diff. gain (passive $R_C$, differential out) | $A_{dd} = -g_m R_C$ | Between two collectors | | 6 | Diff. gain (active load, single-ended) | $A_d = -g_m (r_{o2} \parallel r_{o4})$ | Typically $-g_m r_o / 2$ | | 7 | Common-mode gain | $A_c = -g_m R_C / (1 + 2g_m R_E)$ | Approximates $-R_C / (2 R_E)$ | | 8 | Common-mode gain (current source) | $A_c \approx -R_C / (2 R_o)$ | $R_o$ = current-source output resistance | | 9 | CMRR (with $R_E$) | $\text{CMRR} = g_m R_E$ | Dimensionless; dB: $20\log_{10}$ | | 10 | CMRR (with current source) | $\text{CMRR} = g_m R_o$ | Very high — typically 80–100 dB | | 11 | Diff. input resistance | $R_{id} = 2 r_\pi$ | Between the two bases | | 12 | Common-mode input resistance | $R_{ic} = r_\pi + 2(\beta+1) R_E$ | Each base to ground | | 13 | Output resistance (single-ended) | $R_o \approx r_o \parallel R_C$ | | | 14 | Output resistance (differential) | $R_{od} = 2(r_o \parallel R_C)$ | | | 15 | Current-mirror output current | $I_O = I_{REF} = (V_{CC} - V_{BE}) / R$ | Basic 2-transistor mirror | | 16 | Widlar mirror output | $I_O = V_T \ln(I_{REF}/I_O) / R_E$ | For low currents | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Biomedical amplifiers (ECG/EEG):** CMRR > 80 dB is mandatory to reject 50/60 Hz mains pickup. - **Audio line drivers:** Diff-pair with active load forms the input stage of nearly every op-amp. - **Sensor signal conditioning:** Constant-current bias provides stable operating point across temperature, vital in industrial sensors. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Differential-Mode Gain (Half-Circuit) **Step 1 — Decompose the input into differential and common-mode parts:** Given any two inputs $v_1$ and $v_2$: $$v_{id} = v_1 - v_2, \qquad v_{ic} = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$ Inverse transformation: $$v_1 = v_{ic} + \frac{v_{id}}{2}, \qquad v_2 = v_{ic} - \frac{v_{id}}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Set $v_{ic} = 0$ to obtain pure differential excitation:** $$v_1 = +\frac{v_{id}}{2}, \qquad v_2 = -\frac{v_{id}}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Exploit symmetry:** By symmetry, the voltage at the common emitter node stays at AC ground: $v_e = 0$. This is the **virtual-ground** property of the differential pair. **Step 4 — Analyze the half-circuit (left transistor Q1):** Replace Q1 with its hybrid-π model. The emitter is at AC ground, the base sees $v_{id}/2$, and the collector is loaded by $R_C$ (plus $r_o$ in parallel, which we neglect for simplicity). The small-signal collector current is: $$i_c = g_m \cdot v_{be} = g_m \cdot \frac{v_{id}}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the output voltage at the collector of Q1:** $$v_{od1} = -i_c \cdot R_C = -g_m R_C \cdot \frac{v_{id}}{2}$$ **Step 6 — Form the differential-mode gain (single-ended output):** $$A_{d1} = \frac{v_{od1}}{v_{id}} = -\frac{g_m R_C}{2}$$ **Step 7 — Differential output (between two collectors):** By symmetry, $v_{od2} = -v_{od1}$: $$v_{od1} - v_{od2} = 2 v_{od1} = -g_m R_C \, v_{id}$$ $$\boxed{A_{dd} = \frac{v_{od1} - v_{od2}}{v_{id}} = -g_m R_C}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Common-Mode Gain **Step 1 — Set $v_{id} = 0$ for pure common-mode excitation:** $$v_1 = v_2 = v_{ic}$$ **Step 2 — Emitter node is no longer at ground:** Let $v_e$ be the AC voltage at the common emitter. Apply KCL at the emitter node (the two emitter currents sum into the tail): $$\frac{v_{ic} - v_e}{r_\pi} + i_b = i_e$$ A more systematic approach uses the standard result for emitter degeneration. For each transistor, the effective input voltage driving the collector current is $v_{be} = v_{ic} - v_e$. **Step 3 — KCL at the common emitter node (current out through $2R_E$):** $$2 \cdot g_m v_{be} + \frac{v_e}{R_E} = 0 \quad \text{(approximately, neglecting base currents)}$$ Wait — let us be precise. The total emitter current is $2 i_e \approx 2 g_m v_{be}$ (sum of both transistors' emitter currents). This must equal the current flowing down through $R_E$: $$\frac{v_e}{R_E} = 2 g_m v_{be}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $v_{be}$:** $$v_e = 2 g_m R_E \, v_{be}$$ The KVL from base to emitter gives: $$v_{be} = v_{ic} - v_e = v_{ic} - 2 g_m R_E \, v_{be}$$ $$v_{be} (1 + 2 g_m R_E) = v_{ic}$$ $$v_{be} = \frac{v_{ic}}{1 + 2 g_m R_E}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the collector current and output voltage:** $$i_c = g_m v_{be} = \frac{g_m v_{ic}}{1 + 2 g_m R_E}$$ $$v_{oc1} = -i_c R_C = -\frac{g_m R_C}{1 + 2 g_m R_E} \cdot v_{ic}$$ **Step 6 — Form the common-mode gain:** $$\boxed{A_c = \frac{v_{oc1}}{v_{ic}} = -\frac{g_m R_C}{1 + 2 g_m R_E} \approx -\frac{R_C}{2 R_E}}$$ The last approximation holds when $g_m R_E \gg 1$ (typical for BJT). --- ## 3.3 Derivation: CMRR Starting from the exact expressions: $$\text{CMRR} = \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right| = \left| \frac{-(g_m R_C / 2)}{-g_m R_C / (1 + 2 g_m R_E)} \right|$$ $$\text{CMRR} = \frac{g_m R_C / 2}{g_m R_C / (1 + 2 g_m R_E)} = \frac{1 + 2 g_m R_E}{2}$$ $$\boxed{\text{CMRR} \approx g_m R_E \quad \text{for } g_m R_E \gg 1}$$ In dB: $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(g_m R_E)$. **With a current source** of output resistance $R_o$, substitute $R_E \to R_o$: $$\text{CMRR} = g_m R_o \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(g_m R_o)$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Common-Mode Input Resistance **Step 1 — Find the current drawn from one base** when $v_{ic}$ is applied: $$i_b = \frac{v_{ic} - v_e}{r_\pi}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $v_e$ from above:** $$v_e = 2 g_m R_E \, v_{be} = 2 g_m R_E \cdot (v_{ic} - v_e)$$ $$v_e (1 + 2 g_m R_E) = 2 g_m R_E \, v_{ic} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_e = \frac{2 g_m R_E}{1 + 2 g_m R_E} v_{ic}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $i_b$:** $$i_b = \frac{v_{ic} - v_e}{r_\pi} = \frac{v_{ic}}{r_\pi} \left( 1 - \frac{2 g_m R_E}{1 + 2 g_m R_E} \right) = \frac{v_{ic}}{r_\pi (1 + 2 g_m R_E)}$$ **Step 4 — Form the common-mode input resistance:** $$R_{ic} = \frac{v_{ic}}{i_b} = r_\pi (1 + 2 g_m R_E) = r_\pi + 2(\beta + 1) R_E$$ $$\boxed{R_{ic} \approx r_\pi + 2 \beta R_E \approx 2 \beta R_E}$$ This is why the common-mode input resistance is **2β times larger** than the differential-mode input resistance $R_{id} = 2 r_\pi$. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A BJT differential amplifier has $I_{EE} = 1 \text{ mA}$, $R_C = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 200$, $V_A = 100 \text{ V}$. Find (a) $A_d$, (b) $A_c$, (c) CMRR in dB, (d) $R_{id}$, (e) $R_{ic}$. **Solution:** **(a) Differential gain:** Each transistor carries $I_C = I_{EE}/2 = 0.5 \text{ mA}$. $$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{0.5 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 19.23 \text{ mA/V}$$ $$A_d = -\frac{g_m R_C}{2} = -\frac{(19.23)(10)}{2} = -96.15 \text{ V/V}$$ **(b) Common-mode gain:** $$2 g_m R_E = 2 \times 19.23 \times 5 = 192.3 \gg 1$$ $$A_c \approx -\frac{R_C}{2 R_E} = -\frac{10}{2 \times 5} = -1.0 \text{ V/V}$$ **(c) CMRR:** $$\text{CMRR} = \frac{|A_d|}{|A_c|} = \frac{96.15}{1.0} = 96.15$$ $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(96.15) = 39.66 \text{ dB}$$ **(d) Differential input resistance:** $$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{200}{19.23 \text{ mA/V}} = 10.4 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$R_{id} = 2 r_\pi = 20.8 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **(e) Common-mode input resistance:** $$R_{ic} = r_\pi + 2(\beta + 1) R_E = 10.4 + 2(201)(5) = 10.4 + 2010 = 2020.4 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 2.02 \text{ M}\Omega$$ **Result summary:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | $A_d$ | $-96.15$ V/V | | $A_c$ | $-1.0$ V/V | | CMRR | 39.66 dB | | $R_{id}$ | $20.8 \text{ k}\Omega$ | | $R_{ic}$ | $\approx 2.02 \text{ M}\Omega$ | > [!NOTE] > The huge ratio $R_{ic} / R_{id} \approx 97$ confirms that the diff-pair is far more sensitive to differential signals than to common-mode signals — exactly the design intent. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 BJT Differential Pair with Constant-Current Bias (Full Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph INPUTS["Differential Input Sources"] V1["v1 = vic + vid/2"] V2["v2 = vic - vid/2"] end subgraph DIFFPAIR["Emitter-Coupled Differential Pair"] Q1["Q1
BJT NPN
rc1 at collector"] Q2["Q2
BJT NPN
rc2 at collector"] EMNODE["Common Emitter Node
AC virtual ground for diff mode"] end subgraph CCS["Constant Current Source (Active Tail)"] Q3["Q3 (Mirror Transistor)
IREF copy"] REQ["Output Resistance Ro
very high, MΩ range"] end subgraph OUTPUTS["Outputs"] VO1["vod1 at collector 1
single-ended output"] VOD["vod = vod1 - vod2
differential output"] end V1 --> Q1 V2 --> Q2 Q1 --- EMNODE Q2 --- EMNODE EMNODE --> REQ REQ --> Q3 Q1 --> VO1 Q1 --> VOD Q2 --> VOD ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The "tail" of the diff-pair is a current source Q3 with output resistance $R_o$. In differential mode, the emitter node is a virtual ground. In common mode, the high $R_o$ pins this node, killing the common-mode gain and boosting CMRR. --- ## 4.2 Signal-Flow Block Diagram — Mode Decomposition ```mermaid graph LR A["Input v1, v2"] --> B["Decompose"] B --> C1["vid = v1 - v2
Differential signal"] B --> C2["vic = v1+v2 / 2
Common mode signal"] C1 --> D1["Half-circuit
CE amp with emitter grounded"] D1 --> E1["Gain Ad = -gmRc/2
single-ended"] D1 --> E2["Gain Add = -gmRc
differential output"] C2 --> D2["Full circuit
CE with 2RE degeneration"] D2 --> F1["Gain Ac = -Rc / 2Ro
approx zero"] E1 --> G["Output vod1"] E2 --> H["Output vod"] F1 --> I["Output voc1 small"] G --> J["CMRR computation
20 log Ad/Ac"] H --> J I --> J ``` --- ## 4.3 Current-Mirror Constant-Current Source (Schematic Block) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph MIRROR["Basic BJT Current Mirror"] VCC["VCC"] RREF["RREF
sets IREF"] QREF["Q1
Diode-connected
VBE drop"] QOUT["Q2
Mirror output transistor"] IREF["IREF flows down through RREF and Q1"] IOUT["IOUT = IREF
delivered to load"] LOAD["Tail of diff-pair
or any load"] end VCC --> RREF RREF --> QREF QREF --- QOUT QOUT --> LOAD QREF -.->|matched VBE| QOUT IREF --> QREF IOUT --> QOUT ``` > **Operating principle:** Q1 and Q2 share the same $V_{BE}$ (their bases and emitters are tied together). If they are matched and have the same $V_{CE}$ (by design), then $I_C$ of Q2 equals $I_C$ of Q1, which equals $I_{REF}$. --- ## 4.4 Complete System-Level Architecture of an Op-Amp Input Stage ```mermaid graph TB subgraph STAGE1["Input Differential Stage with Active Load"] DIFF["BJT Diff-Pair Q1 Q2
tail biased by current mirror Q3"] ACTLOAD["PMOS or PNP Current Mirror
acts as active load"] DIFF --> ACTLOAD end subgraph STAGE2["Second Gain Stage"] GAIN2["CE amplifier
high voltage gain"] end subgraph STAGE3["Output Buffer"] BUF["Class AB push-pull
low output resistance"] end ACTLOAD --> GAIN2 --> BUF subgraph FEEDBACK["Negative Feedback Network"] FB["Resistive divider
sets closed-loop gain"] end BUF --> FB FB --> DIFF ``` > **Why this matters:** The diff-pair + active-load stage is *exactly* the architecture analyzed in this module. The active load (current mirror) replaces the passive $R_C$, boosting the gain by orders of magnitude and providing the high CMRR characteristic of op-amps. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define CMRR. Why is a high CMRR desirable in a differential amplifier?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** is defined as the ratio of the magnitude of the differential-mode gain $A_d$ to the magnitude of the common-mode gain $A_c$: > $$\text{CMRR} = \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right|$$ > In decibels: $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_d / A_c \vert$. > > A high CMRR is desirable because: > - Real-world signals are always contaminated by **common-mode noise** (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum, thermal drift, ground-loop voltages). > - A high CMRR means the amplifier **rejects** this noise while **amplifying** the useful differential signal — yielding a clean, accurate output. > - Applications: biomedical (ECG/EEG), instrumentation, audio line receivers. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **What is a constant current source? State its use in differential amplifiers.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > A **constant current source (CCS)** is a two-terminal circuit that delivers a fixed DC current to a load, independent of the voltage across it (within compliance limits). It exhibits a very high small-signal output resistance. > > In a differential amplifier, the CCS is used as a **tail bias** to: > 1. Provide a stable, temperature-independent bias current $I_{EE}$ to the pair. > 2. Replace the tail resistor $R_E$, whose large AC value is impractical in IC form. > 3. Greatly **increase CMRR** (since $\text{CMRR} = g_m R_o$, and $R_o$ is very large for a CCS). > 4. Stabilize the Q-point against supply variations. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]` **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the differential-mode gain $A_d$ of a BJT differential amplifier using the half-circuit concept. State the assumptions made. **(b)** [7 Marks] For the same amplifier, derive the expression for CMRR and explain how it can be improved by replacing the tail resistor $R_E$ with a constant current source. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions:** 1. BJT is in active region. 2. Small-signal operation: $v_{id} \ll V_T$. 3. Perfectly matched transistors. 4. $r_o \to \infty$ (initially; we add $r_o$ later). 5. Emitter resistors $R_E$ are large enough that the bias current is determined mainly by $I_{EE}$. **Step 1:** Decompose inputs: $$v_1 = +v_{id}/2, \quad v_2 = -v_{id}/2 \quad (\text{with } v_{ic} = 0)$$ **Step 2:** By symmetry, the common-emitter node is at AC ground ($v_e = 0$). **Step 3:** The half-circuit for Q1 is a CE stage with input $v_{id}/2$, emitter grounded, collector load $R_C$. **Step 4:** Collector current of Q1: $$i_{c1} = g_m \cdot \frac{v_{id}}{2}$$ **Step 5:** Output voltage at Q1 collector: $$v_{od1} = -i_{c1} R_C = -\frac{g_m R_C}{2} v_{id}$$ **Step 6:** Differential-mode gain (single-ended): $$\boxed{A_{d1} = \frac{v_{od1}}{v_{id}} = -\frac{g_m R_C}{2}}$$ **Step 7:** Differential output: $$v_{od1} - v_{od2} = 2 v_{od1} = -g_m R_C \, v_{id} \;\Rightarrow\; A_{dd} = -g_m R_C$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1:** From earlier derivations, $A_c = -\dfrac{g_m R_C}{1 + 2 g_m R_E}$. **Step 2:** Form CMRR: $$\text{CMRR} = \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right| = \left| \frac{-(g_m R_C / 2)}{-g_m R_C / (1 + 2 g_m R_E)} \right| = \frac{1 + 2 g_m R_E}{2}$$ **Step 3:** For $g_m R_E \gg 1$: $$\boxed{\text{CMRR} \approx g_m R_E \quad \text{(in dB: } 20 \log_{10}(g_m R_E)\text{)}}$$ **Step 4 — Improvement via current source:** Replacing $R_E$ with a current source of output resistance $R_o$ modifies the formula to: $$\text{CMRR} = g_m R_o$$ **Step 5 — Numerical comparison:** - With $R_E = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $g_m = 20 \text{ mA/V}$: $\text{CMRR} = 100$ → **40 dB**. - With $R_o = 10 \text{ M}\Omega$ (current source): $\text{CMRR} = 200{,}000$ → **106 dB**. The CMRR improves by ~66 dB — a factor of 2000 — just by replacing $R_E$ with an active current source. This is why **every practical op-amp uses active bias**. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]` **(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a basic BJT current mirror used as a constant current source. Derive the expression for the output current. **(b)** [7 Marks] For a BJT differential amplifier with $I_{EE} = 2 \text{ mA}$, $R_C = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_A = 100 \text{ V}$, and tail resistance replaced by a current source of output resistance $R_o = 5 \text{ M}\Omega$, calculate: (i) Differential-mode gain $A_d$ (ii) Common-mode gain $A_c$ (iii) CMRR in dB (iv) Differential input resistance $R_{id}$ (v) Output resistance $R_o$ (single-ended) #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Circuit description:** The basic BJT current mirror consists of two matched npn transistors Q1 (diode-connected) and Q2, sharing a common base connection and a common emitter (grounded). The reference current $I_{REF}$ flows through R and Q1. Q2 mirrors this current to the load. **Operation:** Since $V_{BE1} = V_{BE2}$ (bases and emitters tied together), and Q1, Q2 are matched, $I_{C1} = I_{C2}$. The reference current: $$I_{REF} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R}$$ **Derivation of output current:** Apply KVL around the reference branch: $$V_{CC} = I_{REF} R + V_{BE1} \;\Rightarrow\; I_{REF} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R}$$ The base currents are $I_{B1} = I_{B2} = I_B$, so: $$I_{REF} = I_{C1} + 2 I_B = I_{C1} + \frac{2 I_{C1}}{\beta} = I_{C1} \left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right)$$ The output current is $I_O = I_{C2} = I_{C1}$ (matched devices): $$I_O = I_{C2} = \frac{I_{REF}}{1 + 2/\beta} \approx I_{REF} \quad (\text{for } \beta \gg 1)$$ $$\boxed{I_O = I_{REF} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R}}$$ **Key features:** Very high small-signal output resistance $r_{o2}$ (typically tens of kΩ to MΩ), low sensitivity to supply variations, easy IC integration. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $I_{EE} = 2 \text{ mA}$, $R_C = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_A = 100 \text{ V}$, $R_o = 5 \text{ M}\Omega$, $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$. **Preliminary calculations:** - $I_C = I_{EE}/2 = 1 \text{ mA}$ per transistor - $g_m = I_C / V_T = 1 \text{ mA} / 26 \text{ mV} = 38.46 \text{ mA/V}$ - $r_\pi = \beta / g_m = 100 / 38.46 \text{ mA/V} = 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$ - $r_o = V_A / I_C = 100 / 1 \text{ mA} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$ **(i) Differential-mode gain (passive $R_C$, single-ended):** $$A_d = -\frac{g_m R_C}{2} = -\frac{38.46 \times 5}{2} = -96.15 \text{ V/V}$$ **(ii) Common-mode gain (with current source):** $$A_c = -\frac{R_C}{2 R_o} = -\frac{5 \text{ k}\Omega}{2 \times 5 \text{ M}\Omega} = -\frac{1}{2000} = -5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/V}$$ **(iii) CMRR in dB:** $$\text{CMRR} = \frac{|A_d|}{|A_c|} = \frac{96.15}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 192{,}300$$ $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(192{,}300) = 20 \times 5.284 = 105.68 \text{ dB}$$ **(iv) Differential input resistance:** $$R_{id} = 2 r_\pi = 2 \times 2.6 = 5.2 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **(v) Output resistance (single-ended):** $$R_{out} = r_o \parallel R_C = 100 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 5 \text{ k}\Omega = \frac{100 \times 5}{105} = 4.76 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Final Result Table:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | $A_d$ | $-96.15$ V/V | | $A_c$ | $-5 \times 10^{-4}$ V/V | | CMRR | **105.68 dB** | | $R_{id}$ | $5.2 \text{ k}\Omega$ | | $R_{out}$ | $4.76 \text{ k}\Omega$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the single-ended gain $A_d = g_m R_C / 2$. Many students incorrectly write $A_d = g_m R_C$. > 2. **Using $R_E$ instead of $R_o$** in the CMRR formula when a current source is present — this is the single most common error and costs 3–4 marks. > 3. **Sign convention:** Output is inverted (180° phase shift), so $A_d$ is **negative**. Always include the sign. > 4. **Unit confusion:** CMRR in "ratio" form is dimensionless; in dB, take $20 \log_{10}$, **not** $10 \log_{10}$ (voltage ratio, not power). > 5. **Neglecting $r_o$** in approximate calculations. If the question specifies $V_A$, you **must** compute $r_o$ and include it in $R_{out}$. > 6. **Confusing $R_{id}$ with $R_{ic}$:** $R_{id}$ is *between* the two bases; $R_{ic}$ is *from each base to ground* — different quantities! --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Top 12 Take-Aways for KTU Module 1 — AC Analysis of Differential Amplifier** > > 1. **Two input modes** — Differential ($v_{id} = v_1 - v_2$) and Common-mode ($v_{ic} = (v_1+v_2)/2$). The amplifier amplifies the first, rejects the second. > > 2. **Half-circuit trick** — For differential mode, the common-emitter node is a *virtual ground*; analyze only one transistor as a CE amp. > > 3. **Differential gain (single-ended)** $A_d = -g_m R_C / 2$; **differential gain (differential output)** $A_{dd} = -g_m R_C$. > > 4. **Common-mode gain** $A_c = -R_C / (2 R_E)$ for large $g_m R_E$. With current source, $A_c = -R_C / (2 R_o)$. > > 5. **CMRR** = $|A_d / A_c|$ ≈ $g_m R_E$ (with $R_E$) or $g_m R_o$ (with CCS). Expressed in dB as $20 \log_{10}(\text{CMRR})$. > > 6. **Differential input resistance** $R_{id} = 2 r_\pi$ — *finite and modest* (kΩ range). > > 7. **Common-mode input resistance** $R_{ic} \approx 2 \beta R_E$ — *huge* (MΩ range with $R_E$ or with $R_o$). > > 8. **Output resistance (single-ended)** $R_o \approx r_o \parallel R_C$. With active load, replace $R_C$ by $r_{o,\text{load}}$. > > 9. **Constant current bias** replaces the tail resistor with an active current source, giving a *much* larger $R_o$ → vastly improved CMRR. > > 10. **Active load (current mirror)** at the collectors gives $A_d = -g_m (r_{o2} \parallel r_{o4}) = -g_m r_o / 2$ — far higher than passive $R_C$ gain. > > 11. **BJT current mirror** output $I_O = I_{REF} = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R$, accurate for $\beta \gg 1$. > > 12. **Active vs passive biasing:** Always prefer active bias in IC design — better PSRR, better CMRR, smaller chip area, temperature-stable Q-point. ---

topics.title: Concept of Current Mirror: Two-Transistor Current Mirror, Wilson and Widlar Current Mirrors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Current Mirror: Two-Transistor Current Mirror, Wilson and Widlar Current Mirrors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Current Mirror (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *current mirror* is a two-port analog circuit block that actively copies (mirrors) a reference current flowing in one branch into one or more output branches, maintaining the mirrored current largely independent of the load conditions, supply voltage variations, and transistor parameters (provided the output transistor remains in the active/saturation region).

Mathematically, for an ideal current mirror:

$$I_{OUT} = K \cdot I_{REF}$$

where **K** is the current transfer ratio (scaling factor), and for a basic mirror, **K = 1**.

The current mirror is constructed using **matched transistors** (BJTs or MOSFETs) fabricated on the same silicon die, ensuring that process, temperature, and bias variations are experienced identically by both devices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plumbing" Model

Imagine a building's water-distribution manifold:

| Component | Plumbing Equivalent | Circuit Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Main inlet pipe | Incoming water flow | Reference current source $I_{REF}$ |
| Calibrated valve | Restrictor setting base flow | Diode-connected transistor |
| Parallel branch lines | Identical faucets | Output transistors (mirrored load) |
| Pressure regulator | Mains pressure | Supply voltage $V_{CC}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as the calibrated valve sets a base flow, and every parallel faucet delivers the **same** flow (because they share identical pipes and pressure), a current mirror uses a *reference-setting* device to define a base current, and the *output* devices replicate this current because they share identical $V_{BE}$ (BJT) or $V_{GS}$ (MOS) voltages.

## 1.3 Why Current Mirrors? — The KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In analog IC design, generating stable, temperature-independent bias currents is a fundamental requirement. Current mirrors are used as:
> - **Active loads** in differential amplifiers (replacing bulky resistive loads to achieve high gain)
> - **Biasing networks** for op-amp input stages
> - **Current sources/sinks** for analog signal processing
> - **Building blocks** in DACs, reference circuits, and transconductance stages

**Key physical constants/metrics** used in this module:
- Thermal Voltage: $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{26\,mV}$ at **300 K**
- Silicon bandgap voltage reference: $\approx \mathbf{1.205\,V}$
- BJT current gain range in ICs: $\beta \approx \mathbf{100 \text{ to } 500}$
- MOSFET process transconductance parameter: $k'_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \approx \mathbf{50 \text{ to } 200\,\mu A/V^2}$

---

> 📘 **Syllabus Highlight (PCECT403 — Module 1):**
> *"Current Mirrors — Basic Two-Transistor Mirror, Wilson Current Mirror, Widlar Current Mirror — Analysis, matching errors, output resistance, and transfer ratio."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Basic Principle of Operation (BJT Case)

The fundamental physics of a BJT current mirror relies on the **exponential relationship** between collector current and base–emitter voltage:

$$I_C = I_S \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right)$$

Because of this exponential law:
1. A small change in $V_{BE}$ produces a **large** fractional change in $I_C$.
2. Two matched BJTs sharing the same $V_{BE}$ (by tying their bases together and emitters to a common rail) **must** carry identical collector currents (assuming equal emitter areas).

## 2.2 Operating Logic Steps

For the basic two-transistor mirror (transistors $Q_1$ and $Q_2$):

- **Step 1 — Reference Branch Formation:** $Q_1$ is *diode-connected* (base tied to collector). This forces it into saturation/active boundary and defines a precise $V_{BE1}$ for the given $I_{REF}$.
- **Step 2 — Voltage Cloning:** Because the bases of $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are tied together, $V_{BE2} = V_{BE1}$ — i.e., the controlling voltage is replicated exactly.
- **Step 3 — Current Replication:** With identical $V_{BE}$ and matched $I_S$ values, $I_{C2}$ tracks $I_{C1}$.
- **Step 4 — Base-Current Compensation (Ideal vs. Real):** The reference current supplies **two** base currents; this causes an error term that the **Wilson mirror** corrects.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board exam ready**. The vertical bar is written as `\vert` to safely render inside markdown tables.

| # | Topology | Key Equation | Description / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic BJT Mirror (matched) | $I_O = I_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{\beta + 2}$ | Output slightly less than $I_{REF}$ due to base currents |
| 2 | Ideal Mirror Limit | $I_O = I_{REF}$ as $\beta \to \infty$ | Theoretical assumption for hand analysis |
| 3 | Scaled BJT Mirror | $\dfrac{I_O}{I_{REF}} = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | $A_i$ = emitter area ratio |
| 4 | Basic MOS Mirror | $I_O = I_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}$ | Direct geometric scaling |
| 5 | Widlar Mirror Output | $V_{T}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{REF}}{I_O}\right) = I_O R_E$ | Implicit equation — solved iteratively |
| 6 | Widlar Output Resistance | $r_{o2}\!\left[1 + g_{m2} R_E\right]$ | Output resistance boosted by factor $(1+g_m R_E)$ |
| 7 | Wilson (BJT) Transfer Ratio | $I_O = I_{REF}\!\left[\dfrac{\beta^2 + 2\beta}{\beta^2 + 2\beta + 2}\right]$ | Approaches $I_{REF}$ as $\beta \to \infty$ |
| 8 | Wilson Output Resistance | $\approx \dfrac{\beta r_o}{2}$ | Approximately doubled over basic mirror |
| 9 | Matching Error (BJT) | $\dfrac{\Delta I_O}{I_O} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ | Percentage error reduces with high $\beta$ |
| 10 | MOSFET Threshold Mismatch | $\Delta V_T$ tolerance | Typical $\pm 2$ to $\pm 10$ mV in modern processes |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Current Mirror |
|---|---|
| **Operational Amplifiers (e.g., µA741)** | Active load in the gain stage and tail-current source in differential input |
| **Bandgap Voltage References** | PTAT (Proportional To Absolute Temperature) current generation |
| **Video Amplifiers** | Class-AB push-pull output stages |
| **Data Converters (DAC/ADC)** | R-2R and current-steering architectures |
| **Telecom / RF ICs** | Biasing of low-noise amplifiers (LNAs) and mixers |
| **Battery Management ICs** | Precision current sources for cell balancing |

---

> 📐 **Examiners' Tip:** Always state the **assumptions** explicitly at the start of any derivation — (i) matched devices, (ii) identical temperature, (iii) active-region operation, (iv) Early-effect neglected unless asked.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Basic Two-Transistor BJT Current Mirror

### Circuit Setup
Consider matched transistors $Q_1$ (diode-connected, sets reference) and $Q_2$ (output, collector delivers $I_O$). Both bases tied together, both emitters grounded. Reference current $I_{REF}$ is forced into the collector of $Q_1$.

### KCL at the Collector Node of $Q_1$

Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law at the node where $I_{REF}$ enters, $I_{C1}$ leaves through $Q_1$, and two base currents leave toward $Q_1$ and $Q_2$:

$$I_{REF} = I_{C1} + I_{B1} + I_{B2}$$

Using $I_B = I_C / \beta$ for each transistor:

$$I_{REF} = I_{C1} + \dfrac{I_{C1}}{\beta} + \dfrac{I_{C2}}{\beta}$$

Because $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are matched and share $V_{BE}$, $I_{C1} = I_{C2} = I_O$:

$$I_{REF} = I_O + \dfrac{I_O}{\beta} + \dfrac{I_O}{\beta}$$

$$I_{REF} = I_O\!\left[1 + \dfrac{2}{\beta}\right]$$

Solving for $I_O$:

$$I_O = I_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{\beta + 2}$$

This is the **KTU standard result** for the basic two-transistor mirror.

### Limiting Cases

- If $\beta \to \infty$ (ideal), then $I_O \to I_{REF}$.
- If $\beta = 100$ (typical), then $I_O = I_{REF} \cdot (100 / 102) \approx 0.9804 \, I_{REF}$, giving a **~2%** error.
- Percentage error formula:

$$\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{I_{REF} - I_O}{I_{REF}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2}{\beta + 2} \times 100$$

> 📐 **For $\beta = 50$:** Error $= \dfrac{2}{52} \times 100 \approx 3.85\%$ — *this is why high-$\beta$ processes are essential for precision mirrors.*

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Widlar Current Mirror

### Circuit Setup
The **Widlar mirror** adds an emitter degeneration resistor $R_E$ in the output branch ($Q_2$ emitter). The reference branch $Q_1$ has its emitter directly grounded. This allows $I_O$ to be **much smaller** than $I_{REF}$, enabling low-current generation with reasonable resistor values.

### Core Derivation

Because bases are still tied together, $V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = I_{O} R_E$ (the voltage drop across $R_E$).

Using the exponential BJT equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} &= V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{C1}}{I_{S1}}\right) - V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{C2}}{I_{S2}}\right) \\
&= V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{C1}/I_{S1}}{I_{C2}/I_{S2}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

For matched transistors, $I_{S1} = I_{S2}$, and assuming $I_{C1} \approx I_{REF}$:

$$I_O R_E = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{REF}}{I_O}\right)$$

This is the **famous Widlar implicit equation**. It must be solved iteratively or graphically for $I_O$.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Given:** $I_{REF} = 1\,\text{mA}$, $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$, find $I_O$ if $R_E = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$.

We need to solve:

$$I_O \cdot 5000 = 0.026 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.001}{I_O}\right)$$

Try $I_O = 10\,\mu\text{A}$:
- LHS $= 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 5000 = 0.05$ V
- RHS $= 0.026 \times \ln(100) = 0.026 \times 4.605 = 0.1197$ V

LHS < RHS, so $I_O$ must be **higher**. Try $I_O = 20\,\mu\text{A}$:
- LHS $= 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 5000 = 0.10$ V
- RHS $= 0.026 \times \ln(50) = 0.026 \times 3.912 = 0.1017$ V

LHS $\approx$ RHS. **Answer:** $I_O \approx 20\,\mu\text{A}$.

The Widlar mirror has thus reduced 1 mA to **20 µA** — a 50× reduction using only a 5 kΩ resistor.

### Output Resistance of Widlar Mirror

The emitter degeneration resistor dramatically boosts the output resistance seen at the collector of $Q_2$:

$$R_{out} \approx r_{o2}\!\left[1 + g_{m2} R_E\right]$$

For our numerical values, with $\beta = 100$, $V_A = 100$ V:

$$g_{m2} = \dfrac{I_O}{V_T} = \dfrac{20\,\mu\text{A}}{26\,\text{mV}} \approx 0.769\,\text{mS}$$

$$r_{o2} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_O} = \dfrac{100}{20\,\mu\text{A}} = 5\,\text{M}\Omega$$

$$R_{out} \approx 5\,\text{M}\Omega \times [1 + 0.769 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{3}]$$

$$R_{out} \approx 5\,\text{M}\Omega \times [1 + 3.846] \approx 23.2\,\text{M}\Omega$$

> 📐 **Compared to basic mirror** ($r_{o2} \approx 5\,\text{M}\Omega$), the Widlar improves output resistance by **~4.6×** — at the cost of a small $V_{BE}$ mismatch.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wilson Current Mirror

### Circuit Setup
The **Wilson mirror** uses three transistors $Q_1$, $Q_2$, $Q_3$ in a clever feedback arrangement:
- $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are matched output transistors (collectors deliver current)
- $Q_3$ is diode-connected and acts as the **base-current compensator** for $Q_1$
- The base of $Q_1$ is fed from the collector of $Q_3$, not directly from $Q_2$

### Derivation of Transfer Ratio

Let $I_O$ be the output current (collector of $Q_2$). Let $I_{C1}$ and $I_{C2}$ be the collector currents of $Q_1$ and $Q_2$. By symmetry:

$$I_{C1} = I_{C2} = I_O$$

The base current of $Q_2$ is $I_{B2} = I_O / \beta$. This base current must come from $Q_3$ (which mirrors $Q_1$):

$$I_{C3} = I_{C1} = I_O$$

The base current supplied by $Q_3$ is $I_{B3} = I_{C3} / \beta = I_O / \beta$.

KCL at the collector node of $Q_3$ (where $I_{REF}$ enters):

$$I_{REF} = I_{C3} + I_{B1} + I_{B2}$$

where $I_{B1} = I_{B2} = I_O / \beta$:

$$I_{REF} = I_O + \dfrac{I_O}{\beta} + \dfrac{I_O}{\beta} = I_O\!\left[1 + \dfrac{2}{\beta}\right]$$

Wait — this looks identical to the basic mirror! The **improvement** in Wilson comes from a subtle re-wiring. Let's re-derive carefully with proper node identification.

**Correct Wilson Topology:**

- $Q_1$ collector is at the output (delivers $I_O$).
- $Q_2$ collector is at $V_{CC}$ and its base is tied to $Q_3$ collector.
- $Q_3$ is diode-connected (base = collector) and its emitter is grounded.
- The bases of $Q_1$ and $Q_3$ are tied together; emitters of all three are grounded.

Let $I_E$ be the emitter current of each transistor (by symmetry, all three are matched and share $V_{BE}$ approximately, but Wilson has a deliberate slight imbalance).

Let the collector current of $Q_1$ be $I_{C1}$ and of $Q_3$ be $I_{C3}$. Then:

$$\dfrac{I_{C1}}{I_{C3}} = \dfrac{I_{S1}}{I_{S3}} \cdot \dfrac{\exp(V_{BE1}/V_T)}{\exp(V_{BE3}/V_T)}$$

The Wilson topology forces $I_{C1} = I_{C3}$ approximately.

KCL at the base-collector node of $Q_3$:

$$I_{REF} = I_{C3} + I_{B1} + I_{B2}$$

Since the bases of $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are tied together:

$$I_{B1} = I_{B2} = \dfrac{I_O}{\beta}$$

Assuming $I_{C3} \approx I_{C1} = I_O$:

$$I_{REF} = I_O + \dfrac{2 I_O}{\beta}$$

This gives the **same first-order** answer as basic mirror. The crucial improvement is in the **negative feedback**: any tendency for $I_O$ to change is corrected by the action of $Q_3$, which adjusts the base drive.

The refined transfer ratio (after careful analysis accounting for $\beta^2$ terms) is:

$$\dfrac{I_O}{I_{REF}} = \dfrac{\beta^2 + 2\beta}{\beta^2 + 2\beta + 2}$$

### Verification by Limiting Cases

- **As $\beta \to \infty$:** ratio $\to 1$ (perfect mirror). ✔
- **For $\beta = 100$:** ratio $= (10000 + 200)/(10000 + 200 + 2) = 10200 / 10202 \approx 0.9998$, i.e., **0.02% error** — vs. **2% error** in basic mirror.
- **For $\beta = 10$:** ratio $= (100 + 20)/(100 + 20 + 2) = 120 / 122 \approx 0.9836$, i.e., **1.64% error** — still better than basic mirror's ~18% error.

### Output Resistance of Wilson Mirror

The Wilson mirror's negative feedback roughly **doubles** the output resistance:

$$R_{out,Wilson} \approx \dfrac{\beta \, r_o}{2}$$

compared to basic mirror's $R_{out} \approx r_o$. This means the Wilson mirror is much more tolerant of output voltage swings — a major advantage in cascode stages.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Basic Mirror | Widlar Mirror | Wilson Mirror |
|---|---|---|---|
| Component count | 2 transistors + $I_{REF}$ | 2 transistors + 1 resistor | 3 transistors |
| Transfer ratio (ideal) | 1 | < 1 (set by $R_E$) | 1 |
| Typical current ratio error | $\dfrac{2}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{V_T \ln(I_{REF}/I_O)}{I_O R_E}$ — design-controlled | $\dfrac{2}{\beta^2}$ |
| Output resistance | $r_o$ | $r_o(1+g_m R_E)$ | $\sim \beta r_o / 2$ |
| Best use case | General biasing | Low-current generation | High-precision biasing, active loads |
| Sensitivity to $\beta$ | High | Moderate | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> ⚙️ **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `q1`, `rE`). No reserved keywords used as node names. Labels use clean uppercase text only (no markdown formatting inside quotes).

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Two-Transistor Current Mirror

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVCC["VCC SUPPLY"] --> nodeRefIn["REFERENCE CURRENT IREF"]
    nodeRefIn --> q1["Q1 DIODE CONNECTED BJT"]
    q1 --> gnd1["GROUND"]
    nodeVCC2["VCC SUPPLY"] --> nodeOut["OUTPUT NODE IO"]
    nodeOut --> q2["Q2 OUTPUT BJT"]
    q2 --> gnd2["GROUND"]
    nodeRefIn -.BASE TIE.-> nodeOut
    q1 -.MATCHED VBE.-> q2
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Widlar Current Mirror

```mermaid
graph TD
    vcc["VCC"] --> refNode["IREF INPUT"]
    refNode --> q1W["Q1 DIODE CONNECTED"]
    q1W --> gndW["GROUND"]
    vcc2["VCC"] --> outNodeW["OUTPUT IO"]
    outNodeW --> q2W["Q2 OUTPUT BJT"]
    q2W --> rE["RE EMITTER DEGENERATION RESISTOR"]
    rE --> gndW
    refNode -.VBE1.-> outNodeW
    q1W -.VBE MATCH.-> q2W
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Wilson Current Mirror

```mermaid
graph TD
    vccW["VCC"] --> q3C["Q3 COLLECTOR"]
    vccW2["VCC"] --> q2C["Q2 COLLECTOR OUTPUT BRANCH"]
    q2C --> q2E["Q2 EMITTER"]
    q3C --> q3Base["Q3 BASE TIED TO COLLECTOR"]
    q3Base --> q3E["Q3 EMITTER GROUND"]
    q2E --> gndW["GROUND"]
    refInput["IREF"] --> q3C
    q3Base -.FEEDBACK BASE.-> q1Base["Q1 BASE"]
    vccW3["VCC"] --> q1C["Q1 COLLECTOR OUTPUT IO"]
    q1C --> q1E["Q1 EMITTER"]
    q1E --> gndW
    q1Base -.MIRROR.-> q2Base["Q2 BASE"]
    q2Base --> q2C
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Current Mirror as a Building Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    refBlock["REFERENCE BIAS STAGE IREF"] --> mirrorCore["MIRROR CORE TRANSISTORS Q1 Q2"]
    mirrorCore --> outBranch["OUTPUT BRANCH DELIVERS IO"]
    outBranch --> loadA["LOAD A DIFFERENTIAL PAIR TAIL"]
    outBranch --> loadB["LOAD B ACTIVE LOAD OF GAIN STAGE"]
    outBranch --> loadC["LOAD C BIAS FOR OUTPUT STAGE"]
    ctrlBlock["CONTROL SIGNAL VCE"] -.MODULATES.-> mirrorCore
```

## 4.5 Signal-Flow Comparison of All Three Topologies

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["REFERENCE CURRENT IREF"] --> basic["BASIC TWO TRANSISTOR MIRROR"]
    start --> widlar["WIDLAR MIRROR WITH RE"]
    start --> wilson["WILSON MIRROR THREE TRANSISTOR"]
    basic --> out1["IO EQUALS IREF X BETA BY BETA PLUS 2"]
    widlar --> out2["IO MUCH LESS THAN IREF SET BY RE"]
    wilson --> out3["IO APPROACHES IREF WITH BETA SQUARED ACCURACY"]
    out1 --> use1["USE FOR GENERAL BIASING"]
    out2 --> use2["USE FOR LOW CURRENT REFERENCES"]
    out3 --> use3["USE FOR HIGH PRECISION ACTIVE LOADS"]
```

> 📘 **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block diagram traces the current path from $V_{CC}$ through the mirror transistors to ground, highlighting where the base connections, emitter degeneration, and feedback paths exist. Students should be able to **redraw** these from memory during the exam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a current mirror and list its two main applications. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A current mirror is a circuit that copies a reference current from one branch into one or more output branches, maintaining the output current nearly constant independent of load, by exploiting the matched-device exponential (BJT) or square-law (MOSFET) characteristics.

> **Applications (1.5 marks):**
> 1. As an **active load** in differential amplifiers to achieve high voltage gain without using large resistors.
> 2. As a **biasing current source/sink** for op-amp input stages and output stages.

---

### Question 2: Why is the Wilson current mirror preferred over the basic two-transistor mirror in high-precision applications? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Reason 1 (1.5 marks):** The Wilson mirror has a transfer-ratio error of order $1/\beta^2$, compared to $2/\beta$ for the basic mirror. For example, at $\beta = 100$, the basic mirror gives ~2% error while the Wilson gives only ~0.02%.
>
> **Reason 2 (1.5 marks):** The Wilson mirror provides **negative feedback** through $Q_3$, which doubles the output resistance (~$ \beta r_o / 2$) and greatly reduces sensitivity to $\beta$ variations and Early effect, making it ideal for precision active loads and reference circuits.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to include **both base currents** ($I_{B1}$ and $I_{B2}$) in KCL at the reference node — this costs **1–2 marks** every time.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In Widlar problems, students treat the equation as **explicit** and solve algebraically. It is **implicit**; you must state the iteration method or use a numerical guess-and-check.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Students often confuse the Wilson mirror's transfer ratio with the basic mirror's. Always derive the Wilson formula step-by-step and state assumptions.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to mention that **matched transistors** is a prerequisite for mirror operation. KTU examiners allot marks for stating assumptions.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
