**Q.** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the output current of a **basic BJT current mirror**. Hence explain the **concept of base-current error** and indicate how it can be reduced.

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Output Current (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Circuit Description (1 mark):**
The basic mirror consists of two matched npn transistors $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ on a common IC die. $Q_1$ is diode-connected (base shorted to collector). The bases of $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are tied together, and both emitters are grounded. A reference current $I_{REF}$ is forced into the collector of $Q_1$, while the collector of $Q_2$ delivers the output current $I_O$ to the load.

**(ii) Voltage Equality (1 mark):**
Because the bases are tied and emitters are common:

$$V_{BE1} = V_{BE2}$$

Since the transistors are matched ($I_{S1} = I_{S2}$), from the exponential BJT equation:

$$I_{C1} = I_{C2} = I_O$$

**(iii) KCL at Reference Node (2 marks):**
At the collector of $Q_1$, three currents meet:

$$I_{REF} = I_{C1} + I_{B1} + I_{B2}$$

Substituting $I_B = I_C / \beta$:

$$I_{REF} = I_O + \dfrac{I_O}{\beta} + \dfrac{I_O}{\beta} = I_O \left(1 + \dfrac{2}{\beta}\right)$$

**(iv) Final Expression (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{I_O = I_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{\beta + 2}}$$

**(v) Limiting Case (1 mark):**
For $\beta \to \infty$, $I_O \to I_{REF}$ — ideal mirror behavior. For typical $\beta = 100$, $I_O \approx 0.98 I_{REF}$.

**(vi) Percentage Error (1 mark):**

$$\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{I_{REF} - I_O}{I_{REF}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2}{\beta + 2} \times 100$$

For $\beta = 100$: error $\approx 1.96\%$.

#### Part (b) — Base-Current Error and Reduction (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition of Base-Current Error (2 marks):**
The base-current error is the difference between the desired output current ($I_{REF}$) and the actual output current $I_O$, caused by the fact that $I_{REF}$ must supply not only the collector current of $Q_1$ but **two base currents** (one for $Q_1$ and one for $Q_2$):

$$I_{REF} - I_O = I_{B1} + I_{B2} = \dfrac{2 I_O}{\beta}$$

**(ii) Quantitative Impact (1 mark):**
For low-$\beta$ transistors, this error becomes significant. For example, with $\beta = 20$, the error is $2/22 \approx 9.1\%$ — unacceptable for precision applications.

**(iii) Reduction Method 1 — Use Wilson Mirror (2 marks):**
The Wilson current mirror uses a third transistor $Q_3$ in a feedback configuration that ensures $I_{REF}$ supplies only one base current directly, while $Q_3$ supplies the second base current. The resulting error is of order $1/\beta^2$:

$$\dfrac{I_O}{I_{REF}} = \dfrac{\beta^2 + 2\beta}{\beta^2 + 2\beta + 2}$$

**(iv) Reduction Method 2 — Use Improved Wilson / Buffered Mirror (2 marks):**
A buffered current mirror adds an emitter follower between the diode-connected $Q_1$ and the output $Q_2$. This eliminates the base-current loading altogether, achieving near-ideal $I_O = I_{REF}$ even for moderate $\beta$ values.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.** Explain the operation of a **Widlar current mirror** with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the output current. Compare its performance with the basic two-transistor mirror in terms of (i) current-transfer ratio, (ii) output resistance, and (iii) area/component count.

#### Part (a) — Widlar Operation and Derivation (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Circuit (1 mark):**
The Widlar mirror consists of matched transistors $Q_1$ (diode-connected) and $Q_2$ (output). The output branch includes a **small-valued emitter-degeneration resistor** $R_E$ in series with the emitter of $Q_2$. The reference branch has no such resistor.

**(ii) Operating Principle (1 mark):**
Because of $R_E$, the emitter of $Q_2$ sits at a higher potential than the emitter of $Q_1$. Therefore $V_{BE2} < V_{BE1}$, forcing $I_{C2} \ll I_{C1}$. This allows the Widlar mirror to generate **very small currents** with reasonably sized resistors — useful where extremely low bias currents are needed.

**(iii) Derivation (3 marks):**
Writing KVL through the base–emitter loop:

$$V_{BE1} = V_{BE2} + I_O R_E$$

Using $V_{BE} = V_T \ln(I_C / I_S)$:

$$V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{C1}}{I_{S1}}\right) = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_O}{I_{S2}}\right) + I_O R_E$$

For matched transistors ($I_{S1} = I_{S2}$) and $I_{C1} \approx I_{REF}$:

$$\boxed{I_O R_E = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{REF}}{I_O}\right)}$$

This implicit equation gives $I_O$ when solved iteratively.

**(iv) Numerical Demonstration (2 marks):**
For $I_{REF} = 1$ mA, $R_E = 5$ k$\Omega$, $V_T = 26$ mV:
- Guess $I_O = 20\,\mu\text{A}$: LHS = $20\mu \times 5k = 0.10$ V; RHS = $0.026 \times \ln(50) = 0.1017$ V ≈ LHS ✓
- Hence $I_O \approx 20\,\mu\text{A}$ (a **50×** reduction).

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Basic Mirror | Widlar Mirror | Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Current transfer ratio | $\dfrac{\beta}{\beta+2}$ (close to 1) | $\ll 1$ (set by $R_E$) | Widlar — *enables low currents* |
| 2 | Output resistance | $r_{o2}$ | $r_{o2}(1 + g_{m2} R_E)$ | Widlar — *much higher by factor $(1+g_m R_E)$* |
| 3 | Component count | 2 transistors | 2 transistors + 1 resistor | Basic — *simpler layout* |
| 4 | Sensitivity to $\beta$ | High ($\propto 1/\beta$) | Low (resistor-dominated) | Widlar — *more robust* |
| 5 | Power consumption | Depends on $I_{REF}$ | Lower (smaller $I_O$ possible) | Widlar — *better for low-power ICs* |
| 6 | Matching requirement | Critical | Slightly relaxed | Widlar — *more tolerant* |
| 7 | Use case | General biasing, active loads | Low-current references, bandgap cores | Application-specific |

**(i) Current-Transfer Ratio (2 marks):**
Basic mirror ratio approaches unity only as $\beta \to \infty$. Widlar's ratio is deliberately much less than unity and is **resistor-programmed**, giving precise control.

**(ii) Output Resistance (2 marks):**
With $g_{m2} R_E \gg 1$ (typical in Widlar), output resistance is enhanced by a large factor. For instance, with $g_m = 1$ mS and $R_E = 5$ k$\Omega$, the factor is $1 + 5 = 6$, giving a **6× improvement** in output resistance — superior load isolation.

**(iii) Area / Component Count (3 marks):**
Basic mirror uses only two transistors (very compact, ideal for ICs). Widlar adds one resistor — typically a few k$\Omega$, which in IC form occupies significant **chip area** (often implemented as a diffused or polysilicon resistor). Hence Widlar has higher silicon area cost. However, this trade-off is acceptable because:
- The Widlar generates small currents that the basic mirror cannot achieve with reasonable resistor values.
- The Widlar's high output resistance is essential for stable current sources across load voltage variations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 1 — Current Mirrors)**

- ⭐ **Current Mirror Definition:** Active circuit that copies a reference current into one or more output branches using matched transistors.
- ⭐ **Basic Mirror Output Current:** $I_O = I_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{\beta + 2}$
- ⭐ **Basic Mirror Error:** $\dfrac{2}{\beta + 2} \times 100\%$ — high-$\beta$ transistors essential.
- ⭐ **Widlar Output:** Implicit equation $I_O R_E = V_T \ln(I_{REF}/I_O)$ — solved iteratively; ideal for **low-current generation**.
- ⭐ **Widlar Output Resistance:** $r_{o2}(1 + g_{m2} R_E)$ — boosted by emitter degeneration.
- ⭐ **Wilson Transfer Ratio:** $\dfrac{\beta^2 + 2\beta}{\beta^2 + 2\beta + 2}$ — error of order $1/\beta^2$.
- ⭐ **Wilson Output Resistance:** $\approx \dfrac{\beta r_o}{2}$ — roughly doubled over basic mirror.
- ⭐ **Wilson Advantage:** Negative feedback via $Q_3$ reduces $\beta$-sensitivity and Early-effect error.
- ⭐ **MOS Mirrors:** $I_O / I_{REF} = (W/L)_2 / (W/L)_1$ — geometric scaling (no $\beta$ issue).
- ⭐ **Key Assumptions to State in Exam:** Matched devices, identical temperature, active-region operation, base-current neglected for ideal case.
- ⭐ **Thermal Voltage at 300 K:** $V_T \approx 26$ mV.
- ⭐ **Most Common Mistake:** Forgetting the two base currents in KCL → loses 1–2 marks.
- ⭐ **Widlar Implicit Equation:** Must use iteration / numerical method — never claim algebraic closed form.
- ⭐ **Wilson Use Case:** Precision active loads, low-drift references, high-output-resistance stages.
- ⭐ **Widlar Use Case:** Bandgap references, low-current biasing (µA range) where small $R_E$ yields manageable chip area.

> 🎯 **KTU 2024 Frequently-Tested Concepts:** (1) Derivation of $I_O$ for basic mirror, (2) Implicit Widlar equation and numerical solution, (3) Wilson transfer ratio with $\beta$-error comparison, (4) Output resistance boost in Widlar.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operational Amplifiers (Op Amps): The 741 Op Amp, Block Diagram, Ideal Op Amp Parameters, Typical Parameter Values for 741, Equivalent Circuit, Open Loop Configurations, Voltage Transfer Curve, Frequency Response Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operational Amplifiers (Op Amps): The 741 Op Amp, Block Diagram, Ideal Op Amp Parameters, Typical Parameter Values for 741, Equivalent Circuit, Open Loop Configurations, Voltage Transfer Curve, Frequency Response Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)?

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and, typically, a single-ended output. It is the single most important building block in analog linear electronics and is fabricated as a monolithic integrated circuit (IC). The name "operational" dates back to its original use in analog computers, where it performed mathematical "operations" such as addition, subtraction, integration, and differentiation on analog signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):** An operational amplifier is a DC-coupled, high-gain differential amplifier that uses external feedback components to control its response. The industry-standard general-purpose op-amp is the **µA741**, introduced by Dave Fullagar at Fairchild Semiconductor in 1968.

## 1.2 The 741 Op-Amp – Brief History & Significance

The **µA741** (Universal Active Linear 741) is the canonical **second-generation op-amp** that revolutionized analog IC design because it incorporated **on-chip compensation** (a 30 pF MOS capacitor) which made it unconditionally stable without external components. Before the 741, designers had to add external frequency compensation networks, which was cumbersome.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the 741 important for KTU exams?**
> The 741 is the "default textbook op-amp." Every parameter (input bias current, offset voltage, slew rate, GBP, etc.) is benchmarked to the 741. If you master the 741, you master the architecture of nearly all later op-amps (like LM358, TL081, OP07).

## 1.3 The Eight Standard Pins of the 741

The 741 is packaged in an 8-pin DIP (Dual-Inline Package). The pin functions are universal across all op-amp ICs:

| Pin # | Name | Function |
|-------|------|----------|
| 1 | Offset Null | Used with a 10 kΩ potentiometer to null the output offset voltage |
| 2 | Inverting Input (V⁻) | Output is inverted (180° phase shift) relative to this input |
| 3 | Non-Inverting Input (V⁺) | Output is in-phase with this input |
| 4 | V⁻⁻ (VEE / VSS) | Negative supply (typically –15 V) |
| 5 | Offset Null | Pair with pin 1 |
| 6 | Output | The amplified signal appears here |
| 7 | V⁺⁺ (VCC / VDD) | Positive supply (typically +15 V) |
| 8 | NC | No Connection |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – The "Steering Wheel" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of an op-amp as a car's power steering system.
> - The **two input pins** are like the steering wheel (small input effort) where the driver nudges it slightly left or right.
> - The **output pin** is the car's wheels (massive power) turning left or right.
> - The **power supply pins** (VCC, VEE) are like the engine (battery) that provides the energy.
> - The op-amp doesn't *create* signal energy; it merely **controls** a much larger source of energy from the supplies using a tiny input control signal — exactly like power steering amplifies a small hand motion into a powerful wheel turn.
> - **Differential action**: The output responds to the *difference* between two inputs (steering angle is the difference between left and right hand positions), not the absolute value at either input.

## 1.5 Block Diagram – The Functional Sections of the 741

The internal architecture of the 741 is built from **four cascaded stages**, plus a bias network and a current source reference. This is a high-yield topic for KTU 14-mark questions.

**The four functional blocks are:**

1. **Input Stage** – A differential amplifier using BJTs (typically with active load) that provides high input impedance, low offset, and differential-to-single-ended conversion.
2. **Gain Stage** – A high-gain CE (common-emitter) or Darlington stage that provides most of the voltage amplification (≈ 100×).
3. **Output Stage** – A Class-AB push-pull complementary emitter follower that provides low output impedance and current-drive capability (short-circuit protection built-in).
4. **Biasing & Reference Current Circuit** – Sets the quiescent (Q-point) currents for all stages and includes the Widlar current source.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Block Diagram of the 741

The 741 is structured as a **three-stage cascaded amplifier** with auxiliary biasing:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** "Draw and explain the block diagram of the 741 op-amp." (14 marks)

### Stage 1: Differential Input Stage
- Uses a pair of NPN emitter-coupled transistors (Q1, Q2) with PNP active load (Q3, Q4) configured as a modified **Gilbert cell** or a **pnp current-mirror load**.
- Provides: differential gain, common-mode rejection, and **converts differential to single-ended** output.
- Gain contribution: ≈ 10× to 50×.

### Stage 2: High-Gain Intermediate Stage (Darlington)
- A Darlington pair (Q15, Q16) with Q17 as active load (current source).
- This stage provides the bulk of the voltage gain (≈ 1000× or 60 dB).
- A **30 pF on-chip MOS capacitor** is connected between the high-impedance node of this stage and ground to provide **Miller compensation** for frequency stability (sets the dominant pole at ≈ 5 Hz).

### Stage 3: Output Stage (Class-AB Push-Pull)
- A complementary pair of NPN (Q14) and PNP (Q20) transistors in emitter-follower configuration.
- Provides a low output impedance (≈ 75 Ω) and a high current drive capability (≈ ±20 mA).
- Includes built-in **short-circuit protection** using Q15, Q21, Q22, and current-sensing resistors.

### Bias Network
- Uses a **Widlar current source** (Q11, Q12) and a **current mirror** (Q8, Q9, Q10) to generate stable bias currents.
- A master reference current is set by an external resistor (typically 39 kΩ between pin 8 and VEE in some schematics, though in modern 741 the bias is internal).

## 2.2 Ideal Op-Amp Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rules of an Ideal Op-Amp (Two Impossibles and Two Zeros):**
> 1. **Infinite Open-Loop Voltage Gain (A = ∞)** — Any infinitesimal difference between inputs is amplified infinitely.
> 2. **Infinite Input Impedance (Rin = ∞)** — The op-amp draws *zero* current from the source.
> 3. **Zero Output Impedance (Rout = 0)** — The op-amp can drive *any* load without voltage drop.
> 4. **Infinite Bandwidth (BW = ∞)** — Gain is constant from DC to infinity.
> 5. **Zero Output when inputs are equal (Vo = 0 when V⁺ = V⁻)** — Perfect balance.
> 6. **Infinite Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR = ∞)** — Amplifies difference only, ignores common-mode signal.
> 7. **Infinite Slew Rate (SR = ∞)** — Instantaneous output change.

## 2.3 Typical Parameter Values for the 741

The 741 is a *real* op-amp, so it deviates from the ideal. The following table lists the parameters KTU examiners love to ask:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (741) | Unit | Ideal Value |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|------|-------------|
| Input Offset Voltage | Vio | 2 | mV | 0 V |
| Input Bias Current | IB | 80 | nA | 0 A |
| Input Offset Current | Iio | 20 | nA | 0 A |
| Differential Input Impedance | Rid | 2 | MΩ | ∞ |
| Common-Mode Input Impedance | Ric | 200 | MΩ | ∞ |
| Output Impedance | Ro | 75 | Ω | 0 Ω |
| Open-Loop DC Voltage Gain | AOL | 2 × 10⁵ (≈ 106 dB) | V/V | ∞ |
| Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | CMRR | 90 | dB | ∞ |
| Supply Voltage Rejection Ratio | PSRR | 30 | µV/V | 0 |
| Slew Rate | SR | 0.5 | V/µs | ∞ |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product | GBP | 1 | MHz | ∞ |
| Output Short-Circuit Current | ISC | 25 | mA | ∞ |
| Supply Voltage | VCC / VEE | ±15 (max ±18) | V | — |
| Power Consumption | PD | 50 | mW | — |
| Common-Mode Input Range | VICR | ±13 | V | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Mode:** $v_d = v_1 - v_2$ (the signal we want to amplify)
> **Common Mode:** $v_{cm} = (v_1 + v_2)/2$ (the noise that must be rejected)

## 2.4 Equivalent Circuit of an Op-Amp

The op-amp can be modeled as a **two-port network** with dependent sources:

**Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source (VCVS) Model** – the most commonly used:

```
   Rin           Rout
  ─┤├──────┬─────────┬──► vo
  v1 (+)   │    A·vd │  ▼
  ─┤├──────┤    ┌────┤
  v2 (−)        │    ▲
                └────┘
```

- **Input side:** Differential input resistance **Rin** between the two inputs.
- **Output side:** Dependent voltage source **A·vd** in series with output resistance **Rout**.
- $v_d = v_1 - v_2$ is the differential input voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The equivalent circuit is essential for deriving the input-output equations of op-amp circuits (inverting, non-inverting, integrator, etc.) using nodal analysis. Every closed-loop analysis begins with this model.

## 2.5 Open-Loop Configurations

There are three open-loop configurations based on which input receives the signal:

### 1. Differential Configuration
- Signal applied *between* both inputs.
- $v_o = A(v_1 - v_2) = A \cdot v_d$

### 2. Inverting Configuration
- $v_1$ (non-inverting) is grounded, $v_2$ (inverting) gets the signal.
- $v_o = A(0 - v_{in}) = -A \cdot v_{in}$ (180° phase shift)

### 3. Non-Inverting Configuration
- $v_1$ (non-inverting) gets the signal, $v_2$ (inverting) is grounded.
- $v_o = A(v_{in} - 0) = +A \cdot v_{in}$ (in-phase)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the *inverting* configuration, the signal is applied to the **inverting (–) terminal**, NOT the non-inverting terminal. Students often confuse this. Also, the phase inversion means the output is **180° out of phase**, NOT 360°.

## 2.6 Voltage Transfer Curve (VTC)

The Voltage Transfer Curve plots **$v_o$ vs $v_{id}$** where $v_{id} = v_1 - v_2$.

**Key features of the VTC:**

- The **linear region** has slope = **A (open-loop gain)**.
- The linear region extends only for $|v_{id}| < v_{id(sat)} = V_{sat}/A$.
- For $v_{id} > +V_{sat}/A$, output saturates at **+V_sat** (typically +13 to +14 V).
- For $v_{id} < -V_{sat}/A$, output saturates at **–V_sat** (typically –13 to –14 V).
- The transition through zero is very steep because A is huge.

**Typical values:**
- $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13 \text{ V}$ (for ±15 V supplies, 2 V headroom)
- $v_{id(sat)} = V_{sat}/A = 13/(2 \times 10^5) = 65 \text{ µV}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication:** Because A is so large, the linear region is *microvolts* wide. Any practical input larger than ~60 µV drives the op-amp into saturation. This is why **negative feedback** is mandatory — it reduces the gain and widens the linear region.

## 2.7 Frequency Response Curve

The 741 is a **low-pass amplifier** with a dominant pole.

**Key equations and features:**

- Open-loop gain magnitude: $A(f) = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{1+(f/f_1)^2}}$
- At low frequencies, $A \approx A_0$ (DC gain).
- The **dominant pole** $f_1$ is at ≈ **5 Hz** (set by the 30 pF Miller capacitor).
- Gain rolls off at **–20 dB/decade** (–6 dB/octave) above $f_1$.
- The **unity-gain bandwidth (or Gain-Bandwidth Product, GBP)** is at ≈ **1 MHz**:
  $\text{GBP} = A_0 \times f_1 = (2 \times 10^5)(5 \text{ Hz}) = 1 \text{ MHz}$
- Beyond GBP, gain continues to fall at –20 dB/decade, and there is a higher-order pole near 2–3 MHz.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The 1 MHz GBP means the 741 is unsuitable for RF or video applications but perfectly adequate for audio, instrumentation, and DC signal conditioning — the bread and butter of industrial electronics.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Output Voltage from the Equivalent Circuit

**Problem Setup:** An op-amp with open-loop gain $A$, input resistance $R_{in}$, and output resistance $R_o$ is driven by differential input $v_d = v_1 - v_2$ and loaded by $R_L$.

**Goal:** Derive the expression for $v_o$.

### Step 1: Define the input loop

Since the op-amp input draws no current (infinite $R_{in}$), the full differential input $v_d$ appears between the two input terminals:

$$
v_d = v_1 - v_2
$$

### Step 2: Write the output loop

The dependent source produces an EMF of $A \cdot v_d$. This is connected in series with $R_o$ to the load $R_L$. Apply the voltage divider rule:

$$
v_o = (A \cdot v_d) \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_o + R_L}
$$

### Step 3: Assume ideal load ($R_L \gg R_o$)

If $R_L \gg R_o$ (which is typical because the op-amp drives a high-impedance next stage), the term $\frac{R_L}{R_o + R_L} \to 1$, giving:

$$
v_o \approx A \cdot v_d = A(v_1 - v_2)
$$

**Engineering insight:** This simple equation $v_o = A(v_1 - v_2)$ is the **master equation** of op-amp analysis. Every closed-loop configuration (inverting, non-inverting, summing, difference amplifier) is derived by combining this with Kirchhoff's laws at the input nodes.

## 3.2 Derivation of Differential and Common-Mode Signals

Given two arbitrary input voltages $v_1$ and $v_2$:

### Step 1: Add and subtract

By definition, *any* two signals can be decomposed into a differential part and a common-mode part. Define:

$$
v_d = v_1 - v_2 \quad \text{(differential mode)}
$$

$$
v_{cm} = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} \quad \text{(common mode)}
$$

### Step 2: Solve for $v_1$ and $v_2$ in terms of $v_d$ and $v_{cm}$

From the first equation: $v_1 = v_2 + v_d$.

Substituting into the second:

$$
v_{cm} = \frac{(v_2 + v_d) + v_2}{2} = v_2 + \frac{v_d}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
v_2 = v_{cm} - \frac{v_d}{2}
$$

$$
v_1 = v_{cm} + \frac{v_d}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Any op-amp's output is:
> $$v_o = A_d \cdot v_d + A_{cm} \cdot v_{cm}$$
> The whole point of the differential input stage is to maximize $A_d$ and minimize $A_{cm}$. The **CMRR** is the metric that captures this trade-off.

## 3.3 Derivation of CMRR

### Step 1: Define differential and common-mode gains

$$
A_d = \frac{v_o}{v_d} \bigg|_{v_{cm}=0} \quad \text{(differential gain)}
$$

$$
A_{cm} = \frac{v_o}{v_{cm}} \bigg|_{v_d=0} \quad \text{(common-mode gain)}
$$

### Step 2: Write total output using superposition

$$
v_o = A_d \cdot v_d + A_{cm} \cdot v_{cm}
$$

### Step 3: Define CMRR

The Common-Mode Rejection Ratio is the ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain:

$$
\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}
$$

### Step 4: Express in decibels

$$
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) \text{ dB}
$$

**Numerical Example (741):** If $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$ and CMRR = 90 dB, then:

$$
A_{cm} = \frac{A_d}{10^{90/20}} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{31622.8} \approx 6.32
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** CMRR of 90 dB means the op-amp amplifies the difference signal 31,623 times more than the common-mode signal. This is why instrumentation amplifiers built from op-amps can extract tiny ECG/EEG signals buried in 50 Hz mains hum — the hum appears as common-mode to both inputs and gets rejected.

## 3.4 Derivation of Slew Rate Limitation

### Step 1: Maximum rate of change of a sinusoid

A sinusoidal output $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t)$ has maximum rate of change:

$$
\left.\frac{dv_o}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_m
$$

### Step 2: Apply the slew rate constraint

Slew rate SR is the maximum possible $\frac{dv_o}{dt}$:

$$
2\pi f V_m \leq SR
$$

### Step 3: Solve for the maximum undistorted frequency (full-power bandwidth)

$$
f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}
$$

**Numerical Example (741, $V_m = 10$ V, $SR = 0.5$ V/µs):**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ V/s}}{2\pi \times 10 \text{ V}} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{62.83} \approx 7.96 \text{ kHz}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Slew rate limits *large-signal* performance. A 10 kHz, 10 V sine wave will be triangularized (distorted) by a 741. This is the reason high-speed op-amps (like LM318, SR = 70 V/µs) are needed for high-frequency large-amplitude signals.

## 3.5 Derivation of Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)

### Step 1: Gain at any frequency (single-pole model)

$$
A(f) = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1+(f/f_1)^2}}
$$

### Step 2: For $f \gg f_1$ (in the roll-off region)

$$
A(f) \approx \frac{A_0}{f/f_1} = \frac{A_0 \cdot f_1}{f}
$$

### Step 3: Define GBP as the gain-bandwidth product

At any frequency in the roll-off region, gain × frequency = constant:

$$
A(f) \cdot f \approx A_0 \cdot f_1 = \text{GBP}
$$

### Step 4: Unity-gain bandwidth

The frequency at which $A(f) = 1$ is the unity-gain bandwidth $f_t$:

$$
f_t = \text{GBP} = A_0 \cdot f_1
$$

**Numerical Example (741):**

$$
f_t = (2 \times 10^5)(5 \text{ Hz}) = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use:** If you use a 741 in a non-inverting amplifier with closed-loop gain of 10, the small-signal bandwidth will be $1\text{ MHz}/10 = 100 \text{ kHz}$. This is a **fundamental trade-off**: gain × bandwidth = constant for any op-amp.

## 3.6 Derivation of Input Offset Voltage Effect on Output

### Step 1: Define input offset voltage

The input offset voltage $V_{io}$ is the differential DC voltage that must be applied to make $v_o = 0$ with both inputs grounded.

### Step 2: Compute output offset due to $V_{io}$

In open-loop:

$$
V_{o,\text{offset}} = A \cdot V_{io}
$$

For 741: $V_{o,\text{offset}} = (2 \times 10^5)(2 \text{ mV}) = 400 \text{ V}$ — but the output saturates at $V_{sat} \approx 13 \text{ V}$.

### Step 3: In a closed-loop amplifier with gain $A_{CL}$

$$
V_{o,\text{offset}} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{io}
$$

**Example:** Inverting amplifier with gain 100, $V_{io} = 2$ mV:

$$
V_{o,\text{offset}} = 100 \times 2 \text{ mV} = 200 \text{ mV}
$$

This 200 mV DC error is unacceptable for many applications, motivating the use of the **offset null** pins (1 and 5) to trim this out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** $V_{io}$ drifts with temperature, an effect quantified by $\frac{dV_{io}}{dT}$ (typically 5 µV/°C for 741). For precision work, chopper-stabilized op-amps (e.g., ICL7650) with $V_{io} < 1$ µV are used.

## 3.7 Power Consumption Derivation

### Step 1: Quiescent supply current

The 741 draws a quiescent current $I_Q$ from each supply. For ±15 V operation, $I_Q \approx 1.7$ mA per supply.

### Step 2: Total power

$$
P_D = (V_{CC} - V_{EE}) \cdot I_Q
$$

$$
P_D = (15 - (-15))(1.7 \text{ mA}) = 30 \times 1.7 \text{ mA} = 51 \text{ mW}
$$

This matches the typical 50 mW specification.

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Master Summary

| Quantity | Formula | Numerical Value (741) |
|----------|---------|----------------------|
| DC Gain (dB) | $20 \log_{10} A_0$ | 106 dB |
| Unity-gain BW | $A_0 \cdot f_1$ | 1 MHz |
| Max undistorted freq | $SR / (2\pi V_m)$ | ≈ 8 kHz (for 10 V) |
| Output offset (open-loop) | $A_0 \cdot V_{io}$ | Saturates |
| CMRR (dB) | $20 \log(A_d/A_{cm})$ | 90 dB |
| Power dissipation | $(V_{CC} - V_{EE}) I_Q$ | 51 mW |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the 741 Op-Amp (Mermaid Flow)

This diagram shows the four functional blocks and their signal/data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vinp[Input Vplus\nNon-Inverting Pin 3] --> stage1
    vinm[Input Vminus\nInverting Pin 2] --> stage1
    stage1[Stage 1\nDifferential Amp\nwith Active Load\nGilbert Cell] --> stage2
    stage2[Stage 2\nHigh-Gain CE or Darlington\nwith 30 pF Miller Cap] --> stage3
    stage3[Stage 3\nClass AB Push Pull\nOutput Buffer] --> vout[Output Pin 6\nLow Impedance]
    bias[Bias Network\nWidlar Source\nCurrent Mirror] --> stage1
    bias --> stage2
    bias --> stage3
    vcc[VCC Pin 7\nPlus 15 V] --> stage3
    vee[VEE Pin 4\nMinus 15 V] --> stage3
    vcc --> bias
    vee --> bias
    offsetP[Offset Null Pin 1] --> stage1
    offsetN[Offset Null Pin 5] --> stage1
```

**Functional description of the flow:**
- The two differential inputs enter the **input differential amplifier** (Stage 1).
- This stage produces a single-ended output that drives the **high-gain intermediate stage** (Stage 2), which boosts the signal by ~1000×.
- The signal then goes to the **Class-AB output stage** (Stage 3) for current drive capability.
- A **bias network** (right side) provides stable Q-point currents to all three stages.
- The **30 pF Miller capacitor** (on chip, inside Stage 2) establishes the dominant pole at ~5 Hz.

## 4.2 Open-Loop Differential Configuration (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Signal Source v1] --> inplus[Non-Inverting Input Pin 3]
    src2[Signal Source v2] --> inminus[Inverting Input Pin 2]
    inplus --> opamp{{741 Op-Amp\nA equals 2x10^5}}
    inminus --> opamp
    opamp --> vo[Output vo\nequals A times v1 minus v2]
    vccB[VCC = +15 V] -.-> opamp
    veeB[VEE = -15 V] -.-> opamp
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit (VCVS Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[v1 input] --> rin[/"Rin = 2 MΩ"/]
    rin --> node1((Node))
    in2[v2 input] --> node1
    node1 --> dep[/"A times vd\nDependent Source"/]
    dep --> ro[/"Ro = 75 Ω"/]
    ro --> outnode((Output Node))
    outnode --> vo[vo to Load RL]
    outnode --> gnd1((GND))
    ro --> gnd1
```

**Description:**
- $R_{in}$ is the input resistance between the two input pins.
- $A \cdot v_d$ is a voltage-controlled voltage source representing the gain.
- $R_o$ is the output resistance in series with the source.
- The output $v_o$ is taken across the load $R_L$.

## 4.4 Voltage Transfer Curve (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    plot[VTC Plot of vo vs vid]
    plot --> posSat["Positive Saturation Region\nvo equals plus Vsat\nvid greater than plus 65 microV"]
    plot --> linear["Linear Region\nSlope equals Open Loop Gain A equals 2x10^5\nvid between minus 65 microV and plus 65 microV"]
    plot --> negSat["Negative Saturation Region\nvo equals minus Vsat\nvid less than minus 65 microV"]
```

**Mathematical Description of the VTC:**

$$
v_o = \begin{cases} +V_{sat} & \text{if } v_{id} > +\dfrac{V_{sat}}{A} \\[4pt] A \cdot v_{id} & \text{if } \vert v_{id} \vert \leq \dfrac{V_{sat}}{A} \\[4pt] -V_{sat} & \text{if } v_{id} < -\dfrac{V_{sat}}{A} \end{cases}
$$

## 4.5 Frequency Response Curve (Bode Magnitude Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    bode["Bode Magnitude Plot of 20 log A of f vs f"]
    bode --> flat["Flat Region\nGain equals A0 equals 106 dB\nf less than 5 Hz"]
    bode --> pole["Dominant Pole at f1 equals 5 Hz\nSlope changes to minus 20 dB per decade"]
    bode --> rolloff["Rolloff Region\nSlope equals minus 20 dB per decade"]
    bode --> unity["Unity Gain at GBP equals 1 MHz\nA equals 1 or 0 dB"]
```

**Bode equation (asymptotic):**

$$
|A(f)|_{dB} = \begin{cases} 20 \log_{10} A_0 = 106 \text{ dB} & f \ll f_1 \\[4pt] 20 \log_{10} A_0 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{f}{f_1}\right) & f \gg f_1 \end{cases}
$$

**Graphical landmarks:**
- **DC gain:** 106 dB
- **Dominant pole $f_1$:** 5 Hz
- **Slope after $f_1$:** –20 dB/decade
- **Unity-gain frequency (GBP):** 1 MHz
- **Second pole:** ~2–3 MHz (causes extra –40 dB/decade rolloff beyond GBP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The reason the 741 is **unconditionally stable** at unity gain is the –20 dB/decade slope crossing 0 dB *before* the second pole. If the slope were –40 dB/decade at 0 dB, the op-amp would oscillate without compensation.

## 4.6 Summary of Pin Connections (Mermaid Table-Style Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pkg[8-Pin DIP Package] --> p1[Pin 1\nOffset Null A]
    pkg --> p2[Pin 2\nInverting Input Vminus]
    pkg --> p3[Pin 3\nNon-Inverting Input Vplus]
    pkg --> p4[Pin 4\nVEE Negative Supply]
    pkg --> p5[Pin 5\nOffset Null B]
    pkg --> p6[Pin 6\nOutput]
    pkg --> p7[Pin 7\nVCC Positive Supply]
    pkg --> p8[Pin 8\nNC No Connection]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the following parameters of an op-amp: (i) Input offset voltage, (ii) CMRR, (iii) Slew rate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$):** It is the small differential DC voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals of an op-amp to make the output voltage exactly zero when both inputs are grounded. For the 741, $V_{io} \approx 2$ mV.

**(ii) Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** It is the ratio of the differential-mode voltage gain $A_d$ to the common-mode voltage gain $A_{cm}$, expressed in decibels:
$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$
For the 741, CMRR $\approx$ 90 dB. An ideal op-amp has infinite CMRR.

**(iii) Slew Rate (SR):** It is the maximum rate of change of the output voltage in response to a large step input, expressed in V/µs:
$$SR = \left.\frac{dv_o}{dt}\right|_{max}$$
For the 741, $SR = 0.5$ V/µs. An ideal op-amp has infinite slew rate.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any six ideal op-amp characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Infinite open-loop voltage gain** ($A = \infty$)
2. **Infinite input impedance** ($R_{in} = \infty$)
3. **Zero output impedance** ($R_o = 0$)
4. **Infinite bandwidth** (BW = ∞)
5. **Infinite CMRR** (perfect rejection of common-mode signals)
6. **Infinite slew rate** (instantaneous output response)
7. Zero output when $V^+ = V^-$ (perfect balance)
8. Zero power supply rejection ratio (PSRR = 0)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Draw and explain the functional block diagram of the 741 op-amp. Label all stages clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a voltage transfer curve, explain the open-loop operation of an op-amp. Define the linear region, saturation region, and transition points. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

The 741 op-amp consists of **four major functional blocks**:

**1. Input Differential Stage (Stage 1):**
- Built using emitter-coupled NPN transistors (Q1, Q2) with PNP active loads.
- Provides high differential gain and rejects common-mode signals.
- Converts the differential input to a single-ended output.
- Contributes a voltage gain of ≈ 10–50×.

**2. Intermediate Gain Stage (Stage 2):**
- Uses a Darlington pair (Q15–Q16) with current source load.
- Provides the bulk of voltage gain: ≈ 1000× (60 dB).
- The on-chip **30 pF Miller capacitor** sets the dominant pole at ~5 Hz.
- High-impedance node → easily compensated.

**3. Output Stage (Stage 3):**
- Complementary Class-AB push-pull (NPN Q14, PNP Q20) emitter followers.
- Provides low output impedance (75 Ω) and high current drive (±20 mA).
- Includes **short-circuit protection** via Q15, Q21, Q22, and sense resistors.

**4. Bias Network:**
- **Widlar current source** (Q11, Q12) generates the master reference current.
- **Current mirrors** (Q8, Q9, Q10) replicate the bias to all stages.
- Stabilizes Q-points against temperature and supply variations.

**Block diagram (drawn):**
```
   V+ ─┐
       ├──►[Diff Amp]──►[High-Gain Stage]──►[Output Buffer]──► Vo
   V- ─┘       ▲              ▲                  ▲
               │              │                  │
          [Bias Network]──┴───┴──────────────────┘
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **Voltage Transfer Curve (VTC)** plots output voltage $v_o$ versus differential input voltage $v_{id} = v_1 - v_2$.

**Three regions:**

1. **Negative saturation region:** $v_{id} < -\dfrac{V_{sat}}{A}$. Output is held at $-V_{sat}$ (≈ –13 V for 741). The op-amp is OFF; increasing $v_{id}$ in the negative direction has no further effect.

2. **Linear region:** $-\dfrac{V_{sat}}{A} \leq v_{id} \leq +\dfrac{V_{sat}}{A}$. Output is $v_o = A \cdot v_{id}$, a straight line with slope $A$. The width of this region is only $2V_{sat}/A$, which for the 741 is $2 \times 13 / (2 \times 10^5) = 130$ µV.

3. **Positive saturation region:** $v_{id} > +\dfrac{V_{sat}}{A}$. Output is held at $+V_{sat}$ (≈ +13 V).

**Transition points:**
- Upper transition: $v_{id} = +V_{sat}/A \approx +65$ µV
- Lower transition: $v_{id} = -V_{sat}/A \approx -65$ µV

**Key insight:** Because the linear region is so narrow, even microvolt-level signals can saturate the 741 in open-loop. This is why **negative feedback** is essential in practical op-amp circuits — it reduces the effective gain and dramatically widens the linear operating range.

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Define and explain the following op-amp parameters with typical 741 values: (i) Input bias current, (ii) Input offset current, (iii) Open-loop voltage gain, (iv) Gain-bandwidth product. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the maximum undistorted output frequency (full-power bandwidth) of an op-amp in terms of slew rate. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**(i) Input Bias Current ($I_B$):** The average of the DC currents flowing into the two input terminals when the output is nulled:
$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$
For 741: $I_B \approx 80$ nA. It arises from the base bias currents of the input BJTs and must be supplied by the source. It causes output offset in high-impedance circuits.

**(ii) Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$):** The difference between the bias currents of the two input terminals:
$$I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$
For 741: $I_{io} \approx 20$ nA. Even if $I_B$ is large but matched, $I_{io}$ can be small.

**(iii) Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$ or $A_0$):** The differential voltage gain of the op-amp with no external feedback applied:
$$A_0 = \left.\frac{v_o}{v_{id}}\right|_{\text{open loop}}$$
For 741: $A_0 \approx 2 \times 10^5$ V/V (≈ 106 dB). It is frequency-dependent and rolls off above 5 Hz.

**(iv) Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP):** The product of the DC open-loop gain $A_0$ and the dominant pole frequency $f_1$:
$$\text{GBP} = A_0 \cdot f_1$$
For 741: GBP = $(2 \times 10^5)(5 \text{ Hz}) = 1 \text{ MHz}$. It equals the frequency at which the open-loop gain drops to unity (1 V/V). It is also called the **unity-gain bandwidth** $f_t$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Consider a sinusoidal output**

Let the op-amp deliver a sinusoidal output:
$$v_o(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2: Compute the rate of change**

Differentiating:
$$\frac{dv_o}{dt} = 2\pi f V_m \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3: Find the maximum rate of change**

The maximum value of $\cos(2\pi f t)$ is 1, so:
$$\left.\frac{dv_o}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_m$$

**Step 4: Apply the slew rate constraint**

The op-amp cannot change its output faster than the slew rate:
$$2\pi f V_m \leq SR$$

**Step 5: Solve for the maximum frequency**

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$$

This is called the **full-power bandwidth** $f_{FP}$ or **large-signal bandwidth**.

**Numerical substitution (741, $V_m = 10$ V, $SR = 0.5$ V/µs = $0.5 \times 10^6$ V/s):**
$$f_{max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{62.83} \approx 7.96 \text{ kHz}$$

**Conclusion:** A 741 cannot reproduce a 10 V, 10 kHz sine wave without distortion. The output will be triangularized. To handle higher frequencies at large amplitudes, op-amps with higher slew rates (e.g., LF356 with SR = 12 V/µs, or LM318 with SR = 70 V/µs) must be used.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing gain and GBP:** Students often write "GBP = 1 MHz, so the amplifier can amplify signals up to 1 MHz." Wrong — GBP is the gain × bandwidth *product*, not the maximum frequency. At a gain of 10, BW = 100 kHz; at gain of 100, BW = 10 kHz.
> 2. **Confusing slew rate with rise time:** Slew rate is the *maximum* $\frac{dv_o}{dt}$ of the *output*, not the input rise time. A fast input step can still be slowed by the slew rate.
> 3. **Forgetting $V_{sat} \neq V_{CC}$:** Output saturates at ≈ 1–2 V below the supply rail. With ±15 V supplies, $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13$ V, **not** ±15 V.
> 4. **Mixing up inverting and non-inverting:** The inverting configuration feeds the signal into the (–) pin, and output is 180° out of phase. Many students write the opposite.
> 5. **Skipping the bias network:** In block diagram questions, students draw only three stages and forget the bias network. This costs 1–2 marks easily.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] The **741** is a general-purpose, internally compensated, 8-pin DIP op-amp with a **30 pF Miller capacitor** that sets the dominant pole at **5 Hz**.
- [x] Four functional blocks: **Differential Input Stage → High-Gain Intermediate Stage → Class-AB Output Stage → Bias Network**.
- [x] **Pin 2 = Inverting (–)**, **Pin 3 = Non-Inverting (+)**, **Pin 6 = Output**, **Pin 7 = VCC (+15 V)**, **Pin 4 = VEE (–15 V)**, **Pins 1 & 5 = Offset Null**, **Pin 8 = NC**.
- [x] **Ideal op-amp:** $A = \infty$, $R_{in} = \infty$, $R_{out} = 0$, BW = $\infty$, CMRR = $\infty$, SR = $\infty$.
- [x] **Master equation:** $v_o = A(v_1 - v_2) = A \cdot v_{id}$.
- [x] **Typical 741 values to memorize:**
  - $V_{io} = 2$ mV
  - $I_B = 80$ nA
  - $I_{io} = 20$ nA
  - $R_{in} = 2$ MΩ (differential), 200 MΩ (common-mode)
  - $R_o = 75$ Ω
  - $A_0 = 2 \times 10^5$ V/V (106 dB)
  - **CMRR = 90 dB**
  - **SR = 0.5 V/µs**
  - **GBP = 1 MHz**
  - $P_D = 50$ mW
- [x] **Open-loop configurations:** Inverting, Non-Inverting, and Differential.
- [x] **VTC:** Linear region slope = $A_0$; saturation at $\pm V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V; transition at $v_{id} = \pm 65$ µV.
- [x] **Frequency Response:** Single dominant pole at **5 Hz**; rolls off at **–20 dB/decade**; unity gain at **1 MHz**.
- [x] **CMRR formula:** $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$
- [x] **Full-power bandwidth formula:** $f_{FP} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$
- [x] **GBP trade-off:** Gain × Bandwidth = constant = 1 MHz for the 741.
- [x] **Differential vs Common-Mode:** $v_d = v_1 - v_2$ and $v_{cm} = (v_1 + v_2)/2$.
- [x] **Equivalent circuit model:** $R_{in}$ between inputs, dependent source $A \cdot v_d$ in series with $R_o$ to output.
- [x] **Stability:** The 741 is unconditionally stable at unity gain because gain crosses 0 dB at –20 dB/decade slope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked "explain the 741 op-amp," always cover — pin diagram + block diagram + ideal parameters + typical real values + VTC + frequency response. This is the complete recipe for a full-marks answer in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Concept of Voltage Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Concept of Voltage Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage-Series Feedback** (also known as **Series-Shunt Feedback** or **Voltage-Mixing Voltage-Sampling Feedback**) is a negative feedback topology in amplifiers where the **output voltage is sampled** by the feedback network and the resulting **feedback voltage is connected in series** with the input source voltage at the input loop. The fundamental purpose is to stabilize the **voltage gain**, **increase the input impedance**, and **decrease the output impedance** of the amplifier.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this configuration forms the foundational building block of **precision voltage amplifiers, op-amp non-inverting configurations, and regulated voltage control circuits** in Linear Integrated Circuit design.

### Classification of Feedback Topologies (KTU Reference Matrix)

| Sampling (Output) | Mixing (Input) | Feedback Type | Standard Name |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage | Series | **Voltage-Series** | Series-Shunt |
| Voltage | Shunt | Voltage-Shunt | Shunt-Shunt |
| Current | Series | Current-Series | Series-Series |
| Current | Shunt | Current-Shunt | Shunt-Series |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Cruise Control of a Car 🚗**
> 
> Imagine you are driving a car and you set the **cruise control** to **80 km/h**:
> - Your **set value (80 km/h)** = Input source voltage ($V_s$)
> - The **actual speed of the car** = Output voltage ($V_o$)
> - The **error (difference between set value and actual speed)** = Error voltage ($V_i$)
> - The car's control system **continuously compares** the set speed with the actual speed and adjusts the accelerator.
> - This **comparison in a loop** = **Series Mixing** (voltages subtract like $V_s - V_f$)
> - The system **monitors the speed directly** (not current) = **Voltage Sampling**
> 
> Just like cruise control keeps your speed steady even when the road goes uphill, **Voltage-Series Feedback** keeps the output voltage steady even when the load changes!

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Feedback factor ($\beta$)**: Dimensionless ratio, typically $\beta \le 1$ for passive networks
- **Loop gain ($A\beta$)**: Dimensionless, ideally $\ge 1$ for effective feedback
- **Amount of feedback (dB)**: $20 \log_{10}(1 + A\beta)$
- **Standard reference**: $T = A\beta$ is called the **Return Ratio** or **Loop Gain**

## 1.4 Block Schematic of Voltage-Series Feedback

The structure has **four fundamental blocks**:
1. **Source** $V_s$ with source resistance $R_s$
2. **Mixing junction** (series subtraction node): $V_i = V_s - V_f$
3. **Basic Amplifier** with open-loop gain $A = V_o / V_i$
4. **Feedback Network** with feedback factor $\beta = V_f / V_o$
5. **Output** $V_o$ across load $R_L$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The Voltage-Series Feedback amplifier operates on the following **closed-loop principle**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operating Principle**: The feedback network continuously samples the **output voltage** $V_o$ and produces a **proportional feedback voltage** $V_f = \beta V_o$. This $V_f$ is **subtracted in series** from the input source $V_s$ to generate the **error signal** $V_i$. The amplifier acts on this error signal (not directly on $V_s$), thereby forcing the output to be governed entirely by $\beta$ (which is a stable passive parameter) rather than by the variable open-loop gain $A$.

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Input Stage (Series Mixing)**:
   - The source voltage $V_s$ and feedback voltage $V_f$ are connected in **series opposition** (KVL).
   - Error voltage: $V_i = V_s - V_f$
   - Source resistance $R_s$ and amplifier input resistance $R_i$ are now **in series** with $V_s$.

2. **Amplification Stage (Open-Loop Gain $A$)**:
   - The basic amplifier (without feedback) amplifies $V_i$ by gain $A$:
   $$V_o = A \cdot V_i = A(V_s - V_f)$$

3. **Feedback Network (Voltage Sampling)**:
   - The feedback network is typically a **passive, linear two-port** (often a resistive divider).
   - It senses the output voltage $V_o$ (high-impedance sampling, so it does not load the output).
   - It produces: $V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$

4. **Closed-Loop Behavior**:
   - When $A\beta \gg 1$, the system becomes **self-correcting**:
$$V_o = A(V_s - V_f) = A V_s - A \beta V_o$$
$$V_o (1 + A\beta) = A V_s$$
$$A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} \approx \frac{1}{\beta}$$

5. **Stabilization Effect**:
   - The closed-loop gain $A_f$ depends **only on $\beta$** (a stable passive network), not on the variable, temperature-dependent, and non-linear $A$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table: Voltage-Series Feedback — Key Equations (KTU 2024 Module 2 Reference)**

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Effect of Feedback |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open-loop gain | $A$ | $A = V_o / V_i$ | Reference (unfeedback) |
| Feedback factor | $\beta$ | $\beta = V_f / V_o$ | $\beta \le 1$ (passive) |
| Closed-loop gain | $A_f$ | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$ | Reduces gain |
| Loop gain | $T$ | $T = A\beta$ | $T \gg 1$ for ideal |
| Desensitivity factor | $D$ | $D = 1 + A\beta$ | $D > 1$ |
| Input resistance | $R_{if}$ | $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$ | **Increases** by factor $D$ |
| Output resistance | $R_{of}$ | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$ | **Decreases** by factor $D$ |
| Bandwidth product | $BW_f$ | $BW_f = BW \cdot (1+A\beta)$ | **Increases** |
| Feedback voltage | $V_f$ | $V_f = \beta V_o$ | Sensed in series |
| Error voltage | $V_i$ | $V_i = V_s - V_f$ | Reduced by feedback |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for KTU Valuation**: In Voltage-Series feedback, **input impedance goes UP** and **output impedance goes DOWN**. This is the inverse behavior of Current-Shunt feedback. Examiners frequently award zero marks when students swap these relationships — memorize the direction!

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Voltage-Series feedback is the **industry standard** for:
- **Op-amp non-inverting amplifiers** (the canonical example in IC design)
- **Precision voltage regulators** (e.g., LM317, 7805 series)
- **Audio pre-amplifiers** requiring low output impedance to drive speakers
- **Instrumentation front-ends** needing high input impedance to avoid loading sensors
- **Active filters** (Sallen-Key topology) where stable gain and high input impedance are paramount

The phrase **"ideal voltage amplifier"** in any KTU problem statement almost always means: **apply Voltage-Series feedback**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Gain $A_f$

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 2 — be ready to reproduce it cold.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1**: Start with the definition of the open-loop gain.
$$A = \frac{V_o}{V_i}$$

**Step 2**: State the input mixing equation (Series Subtraction / KVL).
$$V_i = V_s - V_f$$

**Step 3**: State the feedback network equation (Voltage Sampling).
$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 4**: Substitute the feedback expression into the gain equation.

The basic amplifier output is:
$$V_o = A \cdot V_i$$

**Step 5**: Substitute $V_i = V_s - V_f$ into the above.
$$V_o = A(V_s - V_f)$$

**Step 6**: Substitute $V_f = \beta V_o$ into Step 5.
$$V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o)$$

**Step 7**: Expand the right-hand side.
$$V_o = A V_s - A \beta V_o$$

**Step 8**: Collect $V_o$ terms on the left-hand side.
$$V_o + A\beta V_o = A V_s$$

**Step 9**: Factor out $V_o$ on the left.
$$V_o(1 + A\beta) = A V_s$$

**Step 10**: Solve for $V_o / V_s$, which is the closed-loop gain $A_f$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_f &= \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11**: Apply the deep negative feedback condition $A\beta \gg 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{A\beta \to \infty} A_f &= \lim_{A\beta \to \infty} \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} \\
&= \lim_{A\beta \to \infty} \frac{1}{\frac{1}{A} + \beta} \\
A_f &\approx \frac{1}{\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that the closed-loop gain is determined **entirely by the feedback network**, which is a passive, stable, and linear element.

## 3.2 Derivation of Input Resistance with Feedback $R_{if}$

**Step 1**: Define input resistance without feedback.
$$R_i = \frac{V_i}{I_i}$$

**Step 2**: With feedback, the input port now has $V_s$ across it (not $V_i$), but the same current $I_i$ flows (since $R_s$, $R_i$, and feedback are all in series).

The current entering the amplifier input is:
$$I_i = \frac{V_i}{R_i}$$

**Step 3**: Substitute $V_i = V_s - V_f$ from the series mixing equation.
$$I_i = \frac{V_s - V_f}{R_i}$$

**Step 4**: Replace $V_f$ with $\beta V_o = \beta A V_i$.
$$I_i = \frac{V_s - \beta A V_i}{R_i}$$

**Step 5**: We also have $V_s = V_i + V_f = V_i + \beta A V_i = V_i(1 + A\beta)$, so:
$$I_i = \frac{V_i(1 + A\beta)}{R_i}$$

**Step 6**: The effective input resistance with feedback is $R_{if} = V_s / I_i$ (voltage source $V_s$ sees this resistance).
$$R_{if} = \frac{V_s}{I_i} = \frac{V_i(1 + A\beta)}{V_i / R_i}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{if} &= R_i (1 + A\beta)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The input resistance is **multiplied** by the desensitivity factor $D = 1 + A\beta$. This is the signature of series mixing — the amplifier appears to have a much **higher** input impedance, making it ideal for voltage sources (which hate being loaded).

## 3.3 Derivation of Output Resistance with Feedback $R_{of}$

This derivation uses **Hypothetical Test Source Method** (also called the **null/short technique**):

**Step 1**: Set the independent source $V_s = 0$ (turn off the input).

**Step 2**: Apply a hypothetical test voltage $V_t$ at the output terminals (with $R_L$ removed).

**Step 3**: The test voltage drives a current $I_t$ back into the output port. The output resistance is:
$$R_{of} = \frac{V_t}{I_t}$$

**Step 4**: With $V_s = 0$, the input signal to the amplifier is $V_i = 0 - V_f = -\beta V_o$.

**Step 5**: The amplifier's response to this input is an output voltage contribution of $A V_i = -A\beta V_o$. So the total output voltage seen at the port is:
$$V_o = V_t + A(-\beta V_t) = V_t(1 - A\beta)$$

**Step 6**: The feedback voltage is then $V_f = \beta V_o = \beta V_t(1 - A\beta)$.

**Step 7**: Apply KCL/KVL at the output node. The current $I_t$ flows through $R_o$ from the amplifier side, and the feedback path adds current $-V_f/R_f$ from the feedback network.

For a properly designed feedback network that does not load the output, the dominant output current is:
$$I_t = \frac{V_t - A(-\beta V_t)}{R_o} = \frac{V_t(1 + A\beta)}{R_o}$$

**Step 8**: Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{of} &= \frac{V_t}{I_t} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The output resistance is **divided** by the desensitivity factor $D = 1 + A\beta$. This is the signature of voltage sampling — the amplifier behaves as a near-ideal voltage source, maintaining $V_o$ constant regardless of load current variations.

## 3.4 Stability and Gain Sensitivity Analysis

**Gain Sensitivity with respect to $A$**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dA_f}{dA} &= \frac{(1 + A\beta) - A(\beta)}{(1+A\beta)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+A\beta)^2} \\
\frac{dA_f}{A_f} &= \frac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fractional change in closed-loop gain** is reduced by the factor $\dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}$ compared to the **fractional change in open-loop gain**. This is the **desensitization principle** — the foundation of all precision amplifier design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram of Voltage-Series Feedback

```mermaid
graph LR
    Source[Source Vs with Rs] -->|Vs| MixerNode
    MixerNode{{Series Mixing Node - Vi = Vs - Vf}}
    MixerNode -->|Vi| AmpBlock[Basic Amplifier Gain A and Ri]
    AmpBlock -->|Vo| OutputNode[Output Node with Ro and RL]
    OutputNode -->|Vo sampled| LoadOut[Load RL gets Vo]
    OutputNode -->|Voltage Sampling| FbNetwork[Feedback Network Factor beta]
    FbNetwork -->|Vf returned| MixerNode
    MixerNode -.->|Vf subtracts from Vs| Source
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Step 1: Apply Source Vs through Rs] --> B[Step 2: Series Mixing subtracts Vf from Vs]
    B --> C[Step 3: Error Vi drives Basic Amplifier of gain A]
    C --> D[Step 4: Amplifier produces Vo across RL]
    D --> E[Step 5: Feedback Network samples Vo and produces Vf = beta times Vo]
    E --> F[Step 6: Vf returns in series to input loop]
    F --> G[Step 7: Closed loop gain Af = A divided by 1 plus A beta approx 1 over beta]
    G --> H[Step 8: Rif increases by 1 plus A beta and Rof decreases by 1 plus A beta]
    H --> I[Step 9: Voltage Amplifier is stabilized ideal voltage source behavior]
```

## 4.3 Input Loop Equivalent Circuit (Series Mixing)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vs[Source Vs] -->|plus terminal| KVLnode((+))
    Rseries[Rs in series with Ri] --> KVLnode
    KVLnode --> Vgen[Error Vi generator]
    Vgen --> Vminus((minus))
    Vfgen[Fb voltage Vf] --> Vminus
    Vminus -->|ground ref| Vs
```

## 4.4 Output Loop Equivalent Circuit (Voltage Sampling)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Aout[Amplifier output A times Vi] --> Rout[Output resistance Ro]
    Rout --> OutNode[Output Node]
    OutNode --> RL[Load RL]
    OutNode -->|parallel sample| FbDivider[Beta divider network]
    FbDivider -->|Vf delivered| FbLine[Feedback line to input]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU**: The word "parallel" or "shunt" at the **output** indicates **voltage sampling**, while the word "series" at the **input** indicates **series mixing**. Combined: **Series-Shunt** = **Voltage-Series Feedback**. This naming convention is the most-tested point in KTU Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Voltage-Series Feedback. Mention any two of its salient features.**

**Model Answer**:
> **Voltage-Series Feedback** is a negative feedback topology in which the **output voltage is sampled** and the **feedback voltage is applied in series** with the input source voltage.
> 
> **Two Salient Features**:
> 1. It **increases the input impedance** by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$.
> 2. It **decreases the output impedance** by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$.
> 3. (Any other valid feature: stabilizes voltage gain, reduces non-linear distortion, extends bandwidth, etc.)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**The closed-loop gain of a voltage-series feedback amplifier is 50 and the open-loop gain is 10,000. Calculate the feedback factor $\beta$ and the amount of feedback in dB.**

**Model Solution**:
Given: $A_f = 50$, $A = 10{,}000$

Using the closed-loop gain formula:
$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

Solving for $1 + A\beta$:
$$1 + A\beta = \frac{A}{A_f} = \frac{10000}{50} = 200$$

$$A\beta = 199$$

$$\beta = \frac{199}{10000} = 0.0199 \approx 0.02$$

Amount of feedback in dB:
$$N_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(1 + A\beta) = 20 \log_{10}(200) = 20 \times 2.301 = 46.02 \text{ dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often write $\beta = A_f / A$ which is dimensionally and algebraically wrong. The correct relation is $\beta = (A/A_f - 1) / A$. One mark is lost for this single error.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) Derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a voltage-series feedback amplifier. State two advantages of this configuration.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

The block diagram of a voltage-series feedback amplifier has a basic amplifier with gain $A$ and a feedback network with factor $\beta$.

**Step 1**: Open-loop gain definition:
$$A = \frac{V_o}{V_i}$$

**Step 2**: Series mixing (KVL at input):
$$V_i = V_s - V_f$$

**Step 3**: Voltage sampling (feedback network):
$$V_f = \beta V_o$$

**Step 4**: Combine:
$$V_o = A V_i = A(V_s - \beta V_o)$$

$$V_o(1 + A\beta) = A V_s$$

$$\boxed{A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$

When $A\beta \gg 1$, $A_f \approx 1/\beta$.

**Two Advantages**:
1. **Voltage gain is stabilized** — depends only on the passive $\beta$ network.
2. **Input impedance is increased**, making it ideal for voltage-source inputs.
3. (Other valid: reduced non-linear distortion, extended bandwidth, reduced output impedance.)

---

**(b) An amplifier with open-loop gain $A = 2000$, input resistance $R_i = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, output resistance $R_o = 40\text{ k}\Omega$ uses voltage-series feedback with $\beta = 0.02$. Find the closed-loop gain, input resistance with feedback, and output resistance with feedback.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Given**: $A = 2000$, $R_i = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_o = 40\text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 0.02$

**Step 1**: Loop gain:
$$A\beta = 2000 \times 0.02 = 40$$

**Step 2**: Desensitivity factor:
$$1 + A\beta = 1 + 40 = 41$$

**Step 3**: Closed-loop gain:
$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{2000}{41} \approx 48.78$$

**Step 4**: Input resistance with feedback (increases):
$$R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta) = 2\text{ k}\Omega \times 41 = 82\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5**: Output resistance with feedback (decreases):
$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{40\text{ k}\Omega}{41} \approx 0.976\text{ k}\Omega \approx 975.6\text{ }\Omega$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> - **1 mark** is reserved for correctly identifying the **direction** of impedance change (input ↑, output ↓). Swapping the formulas (e.g., applying $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$) loses this mark immediately.
> - **0.5 mark** is lost if units ($\text{k}\Omega$, $\Omega$) are not written in the final answer.
> - **1 mark** is reserved for the explicit statement "$A\beta \gg 1$ assumption / desensitivity factor calculation" as a separate step. Skipping directly to the final formula loses this mark.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the general concept of voltage-series feedback. Derive the expression for input and output resistance with feedback.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Block Diagram Description**:
The voltage-series feedback amplifier consists of a basic amplifier of gain $A$ and a feedback network of factor $\beta$. The output voltage $V_o$ is sampled by the feedback network, and the resulting feedback voltage $V_f = \beta V_o$ is subtracted in series from the input source $V_s$, producing the error signal $V_i = V_s - V_f$.

**Input Resistance Derivation**:

From the input loop:
$$I_i = \frac{V_i}{R_i}$$

The voltage across the input port is:
$$V_s = V_i + V_f = V_i + \beta V_o = V_i + \beta A V_i = V_i(1 + A\beta)$$

Therefore:
$$R_{if} = \frac{V_s}{I_i} = \frac{V_i(1+A\beta)}{V_i/R_i}$$

$$\boxed{R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)}$$

**Output Resistance Derivation (using test source method)**:

Set $V_s = 0$, apply a test voltage $V_t$ at the output. The current $I_t$ flowing back is:
$$I_t = \frac{V_t - A(-\beta V_t)}{R_o} = \frac{V_t(1+A\beta)}{R_o}$$

$$\boxed{R_{of} = \frac{V_t}{I_t} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta}}$$

---

**(b) A voltage-series feedback amplifier has $A = 500$, $\beta = 0.05$, $R_i = 1.5\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_o = 20\text{ k}\Omega$. If the source resistance $R_s = 600\text{ }\Omega$, find the overall voltage gain from source to load.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Given**: $A = 500$, $\beta = 0.05$, $R_i = 1.5\text{ k}\Omega = 1500\text{ }\Omega$, $R_o = 20\text{ k}\Omega = 20000\text{ }\Omega$, $R_s = 600\text{ }\Omega$

**Step 1**: Loop gain:
$$A\beta = 500 \times 0.05 = 25$$

**Step 2**: Desensitivity factor:
$$1 + A\beta = 26$$

**Step 3**: Closed-loop gain (intrinsic):
$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{500}{26} \approx 19.23$$

**Step 4**: Input resistance with feedback:
$$R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta) = 1500 \times 26 = 39{,}000\text{ }\Omega = 39\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5**: Output resistance with feedback:
$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} = \frac{20000}{26} \approx 769.23\text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 6**: Voltage gain from source $V_s$ to output $V_o$ (with loading by $R_s$):
The input voltage $V_s$ is divided between $R_s$ and $R_{if}$:
$$V_i = V_s \cdot \frac{R_{if}}{R_s + R_{if}} = V_s \cdot \frac{39000}{600 + 39000} = V_s \cdot 0.9848$$

**Step 7**: Overall source-to-load gain:
$$A_{vs} = A_f \cdot \frac{R_{if}}{R_s + R_{if}} = 19.23 \times 0.9848 \approx 18.94$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes**:
> - **Forgetting the source loading factor** $\dfrac{R_{if}}{R_s + R_{if}}$ is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. The amplifier's intrinsic gain $A_f$ is **not** the source-to-load gain.
> - Confusing voltage-series with current-series (where input is **shunt**, not series) loses **3 marks**.
> - Not stating the **direction of impedance change** explicitly in words loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Voltage-Series Feedback**
> 
> - **Sampling type**: Voltage (shunt at output) — senses $V_o$ directly
> - **Mixing type**: Series — KVL: $V_i = V_s - V_f$
> - **Alternate name**: **Series-Shunt** feedback
> - **Closed-loop gain**: $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$
> - **Ideal case** ($A\beta \gg 1$): $A_f \approx 1/\beta$
> - **Input impedance**: **Increases** to $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$
> - **Output impedance**: **Decreases** to $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$
> - **Bandwidth**: **Increases** by factor $(1 + A\beta)$
> - **Gain stability**: **Improved** by factor $(1 + A\beta)$
> - **Non-linear distortion**: **Reduces** by factor $(1 + A\beta)$
> - **Canonical example**: **Op-amp non-inverting amplifier**
> - **Use case**: **Voltage amplifiers** (high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$)
> - **Block diagram identifiers**: $\beta$ on output side (parallel sample), series subtraction at input
> - **Desensitivity factor**: $D = 1 + A\beta$ — appears in **every** feedback equation
> - **Loop gain**: $T = A\beta$ — must be $\gg 1$ for ideal performance
> - **Sign convention**: **Negative feedback** — feedback **opposes** the input
> - **Exam tip**: Always state the **direction** of $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ change in words before writing the formula — KTU examiners award 1 mark for this.
> - **Memory aid**: **"VS = SS"** — **V**oltage-Series ≡ **S**eries-**S**hunt
> - **Pitfall**: Never confuse with **Current-Series** (which has **current** sampling — direction of impedance changes is **inverted**).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Shunt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Voltage Shunt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Sedra–Smith classification** of negative feedback topologies, the naming convention follows the form **[Output Sampling Method] – [Input Mixing Method]**. Accordingly, the **Voltage–Shunt feedback** topology is defined as a negative feedback configuration in which:

- The **output signal is sampled as a voltage** (the feedback network is connected in **shunt / parallel** with the output port), and
- The **feedback signal is mixed as a current** with the input source (the feedback network is connected in **shunt / parallel** with the input port).

This topology is also referred to as a **Shunt–Shunt** or **Node–Shunt** feedback amplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage–Shunt feedback realizes a Transresistance Amplifier (Current–to–Voltage Converter).** Its ideal closed-loop transfer function is a transresistance $R_{Mf} = \dfrac{V_o}{I_s}$ measured in **ohms (Ω)**.

### The Canonical Realization — The Inverting Op-Amp

The most common implementation of Voltage–Shunt feedback is the **inverting operational amplifier**, in which the input source drives the inverting node through a series resistor $R_1$, and a feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected between the output and the inverting node. Both the input and the output ports are "shunted" by the feedback network, hence the name.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **water tank with an overflow pipe that is controlled by a float valve** (a classic feedback mechanism).

- The **water level in the tank** is the **output voltage** $V_o$ — the thing you want to regulate.
- The **incoming water flow** is the **input current** $I_s$ — the disturbance / signal.
- The **float mechanism senses the water level** (voltage sampling at the output) and **shunts a controlled portion of incoming water back out** before it can raise the level (current mixing at the input).

When the level rises, the float diverts *more* flow away → level stabilizes. When the level falls, the float diverts *less* flow → level rises again. The system always "samples the output voltage" and "mixes a feedback current at the input."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Because the feedback samples a **voltage** and injects a **current**, the ideal gain quantity is a **resistance**. A Voltage–Shunt amplifier is mathematically a *transresistance* converter, exactly what is needed to amplify the tiny current from a photodiode, an instrumentation transducer, or a current-output DAC into a usable voltage.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal voltage | $V_T$ | **25.85 mV** at 300 K |
| Open-loop op-amp gain | $A_{OL}$ | typically **$2 \times 10^5$** (100 dB+) |
| Unity-gain bandwidth | $f_T$ | typically **1 MHz – 100 MHz** |
| Feedback factor | $\beta$ | siemens (S) or **A/V** |
| Transresistance gain | $R_{Mf}$ | **ohms (Ω)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024, Module 2):** Voltage–Shunt feedback is examined under *Effect of feedback on input/output impedance, bandwidth, and gain stability*. Master the impedance formulas — they are the most frequently asked sub-part in KTU ESE questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Output is sampled as a voltage** $\Rightarrow$ the feedback network is connected in **parallel (shunt)** with the load. The voltage across the feedback network is therefore exactly $V_o$ (independent of load current), making the sampled signal a true representation of the output voltage.
2. **Input is mixed as a current** $\Rightarrow$ KCL is applied at the input node. The source current $I_s$ entering the node is **subtracted** by the feedback current $I_f$ flowing out of the node into the feedback network. The "error" signal entering the basic amplifier is a current: $I_{\varepsilon} = I_s - I_f$.
3. **Basic amplifier is a transresistance device** $\Rightarrow$ It converts the input error current into an output voltage with intrinsic gain $R_M$ (ohms).
4. **Feedback network is a transconductance device** $\Rightarrow$ It converts the sampled output voltage into a feedback current with gain $\beta$ (siemens).
5. **Negative feedback is enforced** by the polarity of $I_f$ being opposite to $I_s$, so the loop-gain product $A \beta$ stabilizes the closed-loop transfer function.

## 2.2 The Four Topologies — Master Comparison

| Topology | Output Sampling | Input Mixing | Ideal Gain Quantity | Acts As | $R_{if}$ | $R_{of}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage–Series** | Voltage (shunt) | Series | $A_{vf} = V_o / V_s$ (V/V) | Voltage Amp | Increases | Decreases |
| **Voltage–Shunt** | Voltage (shunt) | Shunt | $R_{Mf} = V_o / I_s$ (Ω) | Transresistance Amp | **Decreases** | **Decreases** |
| **Current–Series** | Current (series) | Series | $G_{Mf} = I_o / V_s$ (S) | Transconductance Amp | Increases | Increases |
| **Current–Shunt** | Current (series) | Shunt | $A_{if} = I_o / I_s$ (A/A) | Current Amp | Decreases | Increases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage–Shunt is the ONLY topology that simultaneously reduces both $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$.** This makes it ideal as an output stage for current-mode sensors.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> **Convention used in all equations:** $A$ = open-loop gain of basic amplifier, $\beta$ = reverse transfer function of feedback network, $D = 1 + A\beta$ = **desensitivity factor** (also called **return difference**).

| # | Parameter | Closed-Loop Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Closed-loop gain (general) | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ | Stabilized by feedback |
| 2 | Transresistance (Voltage–Shunt) | $R_{Mf} = \dfrac{R_M}{1 + R_M \beta}$ | $V_o / I_s$ in ohms |
| 3 | Input resistance (Shunt mixing) | $R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1 + A\beta}$ | **Reduced** by factor $D$ |
| 4 | Output resistance (Voltage sampling) | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$ | **Reduced** by factor $D$ |
| 5 | Inverting op-amp closed-loop voltage gain | $A_{vf} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | Sign inversion included |
| 6 | Feedback factor (resistive divider) | $\beta = -\dfrac{1}{R_f}$ | Sign: shunt mixes opposite |
| 7 | Loop gain (Barkhausen product) | $T = A \beta$ | Must be finite for stability |
| 8 | Bandwidth extension | $f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + A\beta)$ | GBW is conserved |
| 9 | Sensitivity to parameter drift | $\dfrac{dA_f / A_f}{dA / A} = \dfrac{1}{1 + A\beta}$ | $D$-fold reduction |
| 10 | Non-linear distortion reduction | $D_{Nf} = \dfrac{D_N}{1 + A\beta}$ | $D$-fold reduction |

> [!CAUTION]
> When writing $\vert A \beta \vert$ or $\vert 1 + A \beta \vert$ in your answer script, use **$\vert A\beta \vert$** — and **NEVER** use the vertical bar inside any KTU answer-script table. Write inline as `$|A\beta|$` in markdown outside of tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Photodiode / Phototransistor Read-out:** The diode acts as a current source; the op-amp's inverting input is a virtual ground (low $R_{in}$), so the photocurrent is forced entirely through $R_f$, producing $V_o = -I_{photo} \, R_f$.
- **Current-Output DAC Buffers:** Many DACs (e.g., R-2R ladders) output a current. A voltage-shunt stage converts this to a clean voltage.
- **Active Filters (Sallen-Key, Multiple-Feedback):** Multiple-feedback (MFB) topology uses voltage-shunt feedback to realize low-pass and band-pass filters.
- **Shunt Regulators and Protection Circuits:** The low output impedance makes the stage behave as a near-ideal voltage source.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Transresistance Gain

We model the inverting op-amp as the canonical Voltage–Shunt block diagram. The basic amplifier has open-loop transresistance $R_M = V_o / I_{\varepsilon}$ and the feedback network is the resistor $R_f$ producing feedback current $I_f = -V_o / R_f$ (the negative sign indicates current flowing *out* of the input node, opposing the source current $I_s$).

**Step 1: KCL at the input node (the virtual ground of the inverting input).**

$$
I_s = I_{\varepsilon} + I_f
$$

Here $I_s$ is the current delivered by the source through $R_1$, $I_{\varepsilon}$ is the small current entering the op-amp's inverting terminal (essentially $V_{-}/R_{i,A}$), and $I_f$ is the feedback current returning through $R_f$.

**Step 2: Express $I_f$ in terms of $V_o$.**

The voltage at the inverting input is $V_{-}$, and the voltage at the output is $V_o$. The current flowing from output *back* into the node is

$$
I_f = \frac{V_o - V_{-}}{R_f} \;\;\approx\;\; \frac{V_o}{R_f} \quad \text{(since } V_{-} \approx 0 \text{ due to virtual ground)}
$$

> Note: The negative sign convention places the **sampled output voltage** as the *driving* quantity for the feedback network. For a true voltage sampler, we use the magnitude $\vert V_o \vert$.

**Step 3: Apply the op-amp open-loop relation.**

The op-amp produces an output voltage proportional to its differential input:

$$
V_o = -A_{OL} \cdot V_{-}
$$

where $A_{OL}$ is the open-loop voltage gain and the negative sign is the standard inverting input convention. Substituting $V_{-} = I_{\varepsilon} \cdot R_{i,A}$:

$$
V_o = -A_{OL} \cdot I_{\varepsilon} \cdot R_{i,A} \;=\; -R_M \cdot I_{\varepsilon}
$$

This defines the open-loop transresistance $R_M \equiv A_{OL} \cdot R_{i,A}$ of the basic amplifier block.

**Step 4: Form the loop equation.**

From Step 1: $\;I_{\varepsilon} = I_s - I_f = I_s - \dfrac{V_o}{R_f}$

Substituting into Step 3:

$$
V_o = -R_M \left( I_s - \frac{V_o}{R_f} \right)
$$

**Step 5: Solve algebraically for $V_o / I_s$.**

$$
V_o = -R_M \, I_s + \frac{R_M}{R_f} \, V_o
$$

$$
V_o - \frac{R_M}{R_f} V_o = -R_M \, I_s
$$

$$
V_o \left( 1 - \frac{R_M}{R_f} \right) = -R_M \, I_s
$$

$$
\frac{V_o}{I_s} = \frac{-R_M}{1 - \dfrac{R_M}{R_f}} = \frac{-R_M \, R_f}{R_f - R_M}
$$

**Step 6: Factor out the desensitivity factor.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $-1/R_M$:

$$
\frac{V_o}{I_s} = \frac{R_f}{1 - \dfrac{R_f}{R_M}} = \frac{R_f}{1 + \left( -\dfrac{R_f}{R_M} \right)}
$$

Recognizing that the loop gain is $T = A \beta = R_M \cdot (-1/R_f)$, we obtain the standard closed-loop form:

$$
\boxed{\; R_{Mf} \;=\; \frac{V_o}{I_s} \;=\; \frac{R_M}{1 + R_M \beta} \;=\; \frac{-R_f}{1 - R_f / R_M} \;\approx\; -R_f \quad \text{for } R_M \gg R_f\;}
$$

**Physical meaning of the result:** The closed-loop transresistance is set almost entirely by the *passive* feedback resistor $R_f$ — not by the temperature-dependent, nonlinear open-loop gain of the op-amp. This is the heart of feedback's utility.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Input Resistance with Feedback (Shunt Mixing)

In a Shunt-mixed stage, the input resistance looking into the feedback terminal is the resistance $R_1$ of the input resistor in **parallel** with the *reduced* effective resistance of the basic amplifier's input.

**Step 1: Identify the relevant resistance.**

Looking into the inverting input from the source side, the small-signal resistance is the parallel combination of $R_1$ and the input resistance $R_{i,A}$ of the op-amp itself. However, because the feedback forces the inverting input to a virtual ground, the *effective* input resistance is the resistance that the source current $I_s$ "sees":

$$
R_{i,\text{eff}} = \frac{V_{-}}{I_s}
$$

**Step 2: Use the loop equation.**

From the derivation above, $V_{-} = -V_o / A_{OL}$. Substituting $V_o = R_{Mf} \cdot I_s$:

$$
V_{-} = -\frac{R_{Mf} \cdot I_s}{A_{OL}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_{i,\text{eff}} = \frac{V_{-}}{I_s} = -\frac{R_{Mf}}{A_{OL}}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to the standard form.**

Using $R_{Mf} = R_M / (1 + A \beta)$ and $A_{OL} \cdot R_{i,A} = R_M$:

$$
R_{i,\text{eff}} = -\frac{R_M / (1 + A \beta)}{A_{OL}} = -\frac{R_{i,A}}{1 + A \beta}
$$

The magnitude of the input resistance **seen by the source** (including the $R_1$ in parallel path) is:

$$
\boxed{\; R_{if} \;=\; \frac{R_i}{1 + A \beta} \;=\; \frac{R_1 \,\Vert\, R_{i,A}}{1 + A \beta} \;\xrightarrow{R_{i,A} \to \infty}\; \frac{R_1}{1 + A \beta} \;}
$$

> The negative sign in the derivation reflects the **phase inversion**; the **magnitude** of $R_{if}$ is what matters for impedance calculations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Resistance with Feedback (Voltage Sampling)

**Step 1: Open-loop output resistance.**

The op-amp has a finite open-loop output resistance $R_{o,A}$ (typically 50–100 Ω for a 741-type op-amp). The Thevenin equivalent at the output is a voltage source $-A_{OL} V_{-}$ in series with $R_{o,A}$.

**Step 2: Apply a test voltage $V_x$ at the output with input nulled.**

For voltage sampling, we null the input ($I_s = 0$) and inject a test voltage $V_x$ at the output node. The output current $I_x$ supplied by the test source flows partly through $R_{o,A}$ and partly through the feedback network $R_f$ into the (now shorted) input node.

**Step 3: KCL at the output node.**
