By setting $R_f = 0$ (short circuit between output and inverting input) and removing the input resistor $R_1$ (open circuit at inverting input), we obtain the voltage follower configuration. This is the **only configuration** that achieves maximum bandwidth, maximum input impedance, and minimum output impedance simultaneously. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define a voltage follower. Why is it also called a unity-gain buffer?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A voltage follower is an operational amplifier configuration in which the output is **directly connected to the inverting (−) input**, and the input signal is applied to the **non-inverting (+) input**. This provides a closed-loop voltage gain of **unity ($A_v = 1$)**, meaning $V_{out} = V_{in}$. It is called a **unity-gain buffer** because: - **"Unity-gain"** refers to the voltage gain being exactly 1 (the output magnitude equals the input magnitude). - **"Buffer"** refers to its function of isolating a high-impedance source from a low-impedance load, providing current/power gain without affecting the voltage signal. **Key Characteristics:** - Input impedance: Very high (ideally infinite) - Output impedance: Very low (ideally zero) - Function: Impedance transformation with voltage preservation **Marks Distribution:** - 1 mark: Circuit definition with input/output connections - 1 mark: Explanation of "unity-gain" term - 1 mark: Explanation of "buffer" term and impedance transformation --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: List any three advantages of using a voltage follower in electronic circuits.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Three advantages of a voltage follower are:** 1. **High Input Impedance:** The voltage follower presents an extremely high input impedance (ideally infinite), which prevents loading of the source. This is crucial when interfacing with high-impedance sensors or signal sources. 2. **Low Output Impedance:** It provides a very low output impedance (ideally zero), enabling it to drive low-impedance loads without signal degradation. This makes it an excellent current booster. 3. **Maximum Bandwidth:** The voltage follower achieves the highest possible closed-loop bandwidth, equal to the op-amp's gain-bandwidth product ($f_t$). For example, the µA741 voltage follower has a bandwidth of 1 MHz. **Additional Advantages (for extra credit):** - Zero phase shift at low frequencies - Excellent isolation between stages - Simple circuit (only one op-amp, no resistors) - High common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR) preserved **Marks Distribution:** - 1 mark per advantage (3 advantages × 1 mark = 3 marks) --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: (a)** Explain the working of a voltage follower circuit using an operational amplifier. Derive the expression for its closed-loop voltage gain. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An op-amp voltage follower uses an IC with open-loop gain $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, open-loop input impedance $Z_{in,OL} = 2$ MΩ, and open-loop output impedance $Z_{out,OL} = 75$ Ω. Calculate the exact closed-loop voltage gain, input impedance, and output impedance. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks **Working of Voltage Follower:** The voltage follower is constructed using an op-amp where: - The **input signal $V_{in}$** is applied to the **non-inverting (+) input** (Pin 3 of IC 741). - The **output $V_{out}$** (Pin 6) is **directly connected** to the **inverting (−) input** (Pin 2). - This creates a **100% negative feedback** loop with feedback factor $\beta = 1$. **Step-by-Step Working:** **Step 1: Signal Application** (1 Mark) The input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting terminal. Since the op-amp's input impedance is very high, virtually no current flows into the input terminal, preventing source loading. **Step 2: Differential Amplification** (1 Mark) The op-amp amplifies the difference between its two input terminals by the open-loop gain $A_{OL}$: $$V_{out} = A_{OL}(V_+ - V_-)$$ **Step 3: Virtual Short Concept** (1 Mark) Due to the extremely high open-loop gain and negative feedback, the op-amp operates in the linear region and forces the differential input voltage to approach zero: $$V_+ - V_- = 0 \implies V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$$ **Step 4: Direct Feedback** (1 Mark) Since the output is physically wired to the inverting input: $$V_- = V_{out}$$ **Step 5: Output Equation** (1 Mark) Combining Steps 3 and 4: $$V_{out} = V_{in}$$ **Step 6: Derivation of Closed-Loop Gain** (2 Marks) The general closed-loop gain formula for negative feedback amplifiers is: $$A_v = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$$ For the voltage follower, the feedback factor is: $$\beta = \frac{V_{feedback}}{V_{out}} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{out}} = 1$$ Therefore: $$A_v = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}(1)} = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}}$$ Taking the limit as $A_{OL} \to \infty$: $$\lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} A_v = \lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty} \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}} = 1$$ **Conclusion:** The voltage follower provides a closed-loop voltage gain of **unity**, meaning the output voltage exactly tracks the input voltage. --- #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given Data:** - Open-loop gain: $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ - Open-loop input impedance: $Z_{in,OL} = 2$ MΩ $= 2 \times 10^6 \, \Omega$ - Open-loop output impedance: $Z_{out,OL} = 75 \, \Omega$ - Feedback factor: $\beta = 1$ (voltage follower) **Step 1: Exact Closed-Loop Voltage Gain** (2 Marks) $$A_v = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{1 + 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{200000}{200001} = 0.999995$$ **Step 2: Closed-Loop Input Impedance** (2.5 Marks) For non-inverting configuration, the closed-loop input impedance is: $$Z_{in,CL} = Z_{in,OL} \times (1 + A_{OL}\beta)$$ $$Z_{in,CL} = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 + 2 \times 10^5 \times 1)$$ $$Z_{in,CL} = 2 \times 10^6 \times (200001)$$ $$Z_{in,CL} = 4.00002 \times 10^{11} \, \Omega \approx 400 \, \text{G}\Omega$$ **Step 3: Closed-Loop Output Impedance** (2.5 Marks) The closed-loop output impedance is: $$Z_{out,CL} = \frac{Z_{out,OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$$ $$Z_{out,CL} = \frac{75}{1 + 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{75}{200001}$$ $$Z_{out,CL} = 3.7499 \times 10^{-4} \, \Omega \approx 0.375 \, \text{m}\Omega$$ **Summary of Results:** | Parameter | Calculated Value | |---|---| | Closed-Loop Gain $A_v$ | 0.999995 | | Closed-Loop Input Impedance $Z_{in,CL}$ | $\approx 4 \times 10^{11} \, \Omega$ | | Closed-Loop Output Impedance $Z_{out,CL}$ | $\approx 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \, \Omega$ | **Conclusion:** The voltage follower exhibits extremely high input impedance and extremely low output impedance, confirming its role as an ideal buffer/isolation stage. --- ### QUESTION B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the voltage follower configuration. Discuss its input impedance, output impedance, and bandwidth characteristics. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain any **four practical applications** of voltage followers in electronic systems. For each application, justify why a voltage follower is preferred over a direct connection. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks **Circuit Diagram:** (2 Marks) ``` +V_CC | | ____ | | |741 | |____| | | +---------+ | | | +------+ + - +--------+ | \ / | | +--->| +--+ | | | - | | | | | | V_in ----+ | | | | | | +-----+--------+ | V_out | === GND -V_EE ``` **Pin Connections:** - Pin 3 (Non-inverting +): $V_{in}$ - Pin 2 (Inverting −): Connected to Pin 6 - Pin 6: $V_{out}$ - Pin 7: +V_CC - Pin 4: −V_EE **Input Impedance Characteristics:** (1.5 Marks) - **Ideal value:** Infinite ($\infty$) - **Practical value for µA741:** $Z_{in,CL} = Z_{in,OL} \times (1 + A_{OL}) \approx 400$ GΩ - **Reason:** The 100% negative feedback boosts the input impedance by the loop gain factor $(1 + A_{OL}\beta)$. - **Benefit:** Prevents loading of high-impedance sources such as sensors, piezoelectric transducers, and high-value resistive dividers. **Output Impedance Characteristics:** (1.5 Marks) - **Ideal value:** Zero (0 Ω) - **Practical value for µA741:** $Z_{out,CL} = Z_{out,OL} / (1 + A_{OL}) \approx 0.375$ mΩ - **Reason:** The negative feedback reduces the output impedance by the loop gain factor. - **Benefit:** Enables driving of low-impedance loads (speakers, long cables, multiple stages) without signal attenuation. **Bandwidth Characteristics:** (2 Marks) - **Closed-loop bandwidth:** $f_{CL} = f_t / A_v = f_t$ (since $A_v = 1$) - **For µA741:** $f_{CL} = 1$ MHz (maximum possible) - **Reason:** The gain-bandwidth product is constant ($f_t = A_v \times f_{CL}$), so minimum gain (unity) gives maximum bandwidth. - **Benefit:** Suitable for high-frequency signal buffering and wideband applications. **Summary Table:** | Characteristic | Ideal Value | Practical (µA741) | |---|---|---| | Voltage Gain | 1 | 0.99999 | | Input Impedance | $\infty$ | $\approx 400$ GΩ | | Output Impedance | 0 | $\approx 0.375$ mΩ | | Bandwidth | $\infty$ | 1 MHz | --- #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks **Four Practical Applications of Voltage Followers:** **Application 1: Buffer for Piezoelectric Sensors** (2 Marks) Piezoelectric sensors have **extremely high output impedance** (often > 1 GΩ) and cannot directly drive measurement instruments. A voltage follower presents high input impedance to the sensor (no loading) and provides low output impedance to drive the instrument. **Why not direct connection?** Direct connection would cause severe signal attenuation due to the voltage divider formed between the sensor's high impedance and the instrument's input impedance. **Application 2: ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) Driver** (1.75 Marks) ADCs contain a sample-and-hold capacitor that needs to be **charged/discharged rapidly** during acquisition. A voltage follower with low output impedance can source/sink large currents to charge the hold capacitor quickly, ensuring accurate voltage sampling. **Why not direct connection?** The high source impedance would slow down the charging process, causing acquisition errors and non-linear behavior in high-speed ADCs. **Application 3: Cascaded Active Filter Stages** (1.75 Marks) In multi-stage active filters (e.g., Sallen-Key topology), each stage's input impedance loads the previous stage's output, altering the filter's frequency response. Voltage followers inserted between stages provide **isolation**, preserving the designed transfer function. **Why not direct connection?** Without buffering, the loading effect would shift the cutoff frequency, reduce Q-factor, and distort the filter's response. **Application 4: Reference Voltage Distribution** (1.5 Marks) A precision voltage reference (e.g., bandgap reference at 2.5V) often needs to be distributed to **multiple loads** on a PCB. A single voltage follower with low output impedance can drive many loads simultaneously without voltage drop, or multiple voltage followers can be used for individual isolation. **Why not direct connection?** Direct connection to multiple loads would cause voltage droop due to source impedance, and any load variation would reflect back and disturb the reference voltage. **Bonus Application (for extra credit):** **Application 5: Current Boosting in Signal Generators** Function generators often have weak output stages. A voltage follower can boost the current capability (from mA to tens of mA) while maintaining the waveform integrity, enabling direct driving of low-impedance loads like speakers or power amplifiers. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:** ### Mistake 1: Confusing Voltage Follower with Voltage Regulator - **Wrong:** "Voltage follower maintains constant output voltage like a regulator." - **Right:** "Voltage follower provides output that **tracks** the input — it is NOT a regulator. For a regulator, input must be stable, and output is constant." ### Mistake 2: Writing Gain as $A_v = 1$ Without Justification - **Wrong:** "Since output is connected to inverting input, gain is 1." (No derivation) - **Right:** Must show the derivation using virtual short concept OR the negative feedback formula $A_v = A_{OL}/(1+A_{OL}\beta)$ with $\beta = 1$. ### Mistake 3: Forgetting to State $\beta = 1$ Explicitly - **Wrong:** Using $A_v = A_{OL}/(1+A_{OL})$ without explaining why $\beta = 1$. - **Right:** Must state: "Since the output is directly connected to the inverting input, the feedback factor $\beta = V_{out}/V_{out} = 1$." ### Mistake 4: Mixing Up Inverting and Non-Inverting Terminals - **Wrong:** Connecting $V_{in}$ to the inverting input. - **Right:** $V_{in}$ goes to the **non-inverting (+) terminal** for positive feedback configuration to be avoided and proper negative feedback to be established. ### Mistake 5: Not Mentioning Practical vs. Ideal Values - **Wrong:** "Input impedance is infinite, output impedance is zero." (Without context) - **Right:** "Ideally infinite, but practically for µA741, $Z_{in} \approx 400$ GΩ and $Z_{out} \approx 0.375$ mΩ." ### Mistake 6: Skipping Numerical Problems - **Wrong:** Skipping the calculation part in 7-mark numerical questions. - **Right:** Show **every step** with units, substitution, and final boxed answer. Examiners allocate marks for intermediate steps. ### Mistake 7: Not Drawing Circuit Diagram - **Wrong:** Explaining voltage follower without a circuit diagram. - **Right:** Always include a **neat, labeled circuit diagram** with pin numbers, supply connections, and signal flow arrows. This alone can earn 2-3 marks. ### Mistake 8: Confusing Bandwidth with Gain-Bandwidth Product - **Wrong:** "Bandwidth of voltage follower is $f_t$." (Without explanation) - **Right:** "Since $A_v = 1$ and $f_t = A_v \times f_{CL}$, the closed-loop bandwidth $f_{CL} = f_t$." --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist #### ✅ Core Definition - [ ] Voltage follower = Unity-gain buffer with output directly connected to inverting input - [ ] $V_{out} = V_{in}$ (output tracks input) - [ ] Feedback factor: $\beta = 1$ (100% negative feedback) #### ✅ Key Formulas (MUST MEMORIZE) - [ ] $A_v = \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}} \approx 1$ - [ ] $Z_{in,CL} = Z_{in,OL} \times (1 + A_{OL})$ - [ ] $Z_{out,CL} = \dfrac{Z_{out,OL}}{1 + A_{OL}}$ - [ ] $f_{CL} = f_t$ (maximum bandwidth) #### ✅ Circuit Identification - [ ] Input → Pin 3 (Non-inverting +) - [ ] Output → Pin 6 - [ ] Pin 2 (Inverting −) → Connected to Pin 6 (direct wire) - [ ] Pin 7 → +V_CC, Pin 4 → −V_EE - [ ] No external resistors required #### ✅ Performance Characteristics - [ ] Highest input impedance of all op-amp configurations - [ ] Lowest output impedance of all op-amp configurations - [ ] Maximum bandwidth (equal to $f_t$) - [ ] Zero phase shift (inverting input = output, so no inversion) #### ✅ Applications (At Least 4 to Remember) - [ ] Buffer/isolation amplifier - [ ] Impedance matching - [ ] ADC driver - [ ] Active filter cascade isolation - [ ] Reference voltage distribution - [ ] Current booster - [ ] Sample-and-hold circuits #### ✅ Comparison Points (For Exam Questions) - [ ] Voltage follower is a **special case** of non-inverting amplifier ($R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$) - [ ] Has **maximum loop gain** ($T = A_{OL}$) - [ ] Has **maximum bandwidth** ($f_{CL} = f_t$) - [ ] Has **maximum input impedance** and **minimum output impedance** #### ✅ Common Numerical Values (For µA741) - [ ] $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ (106 dB) - [ ] $Z_{in,OL} = 2$ MΩ - [ ] $Z_{out,OL} = 75 \, \Omega$ - [ ] $f_t = 1$ MHz - [ ] $Z_{in,CL} \approx 400$ GΩ - [ ] $Z_{out,CL} \approx 0.375$ mΩ #### ✅ Examiner's Golden Rules - [ ] Always draw circuit diagram first - [ ] State assumptions (ideal vs. practical) - [ ] Show all derivation steps - [ ] Use proper units in numerical answers - [ ] Box the final answer - [ ] Mention practical applications - [ ] Compare with other configurations (if asked) --- ### 📌 Final Exam Tips > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:** > 1. **For 3-mark questions:** Write definition + diagram + 1-2 key formulas. > 2. **For 7-mark sub-questions:** Start with circuit diagram (1-2 marks) → Derivation (3-4 marks) → Numerical/numerical substitution (1-2 marks). > 3. **For 14-mark questions:** Divide answer into clear sections with headings. Use bullet points, tables, and equations for clarity. > 4. **Always relate theory to applications:** Examiners love when students mention real-world uses. > 5. **Practice numerical problems:** At least 5-6 problems on voltage follower with different given parameters. --- ### 🎓 Mastery Level Achieved! 🎓 You now have **complete KTU 2024 Scheme-aligned notes** on the Voltage Follower topic, including: - ✅ Rigorous theoretical foundation - ✅ Intuitive analogies for quick understanding - ✅ All KTU high-yield formulas - ✅ Exhaustive step-by-step derivations - ✅ Professional Mermaid diagrams - ✅ Real KTU-style exam questions with model answers - ✅ Examiner pitfall warnings - ✅ Rapid revision checklist **Best of luck for your KTU examinations!** 🚀

topics.title: Differential and Instrumentation Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential and Instrumentation Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Differential Amplifier (DA) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> A **Differential Amplifier (DA)** is a two-input, two-output analog circuit block that amplifies the *difference* between two input signals applied to its two input terminals while *rejecting* any signal common to both inputs. It is the fundamental building block of every operational amplifier, analog multiplier, mixer, comparator, and active filter used in modern linear IC design.

Mathematically, for an ideal differential amplifier with two inputs $v_1$ and $v_2$ and a single-ended output $v_o$:

$$v_o = A_d (v_1 - v_2)$$

where $A_d$ is the **Differential-Mode Gain**. For an ideal DA, any voltage appearing identically on both inputs (the common-mode signal $v_{cm}$) produces *zero* output.

---

## 1.2 The Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** is a precision, high-gain, fully-differential DC-coupled amplifier built from **three internal op-amps** (two input buffer stages + one difference amplifier stage). It is specifically engineered to extract and amplify small differential signals riding on large common-mode voltages, typically from transducer bridges (strain gauges, thermocouples, biomedical electrodes) where input impedances must be extremely high, gain must be variable through a *single* resistor, and CMRR must remain very high even at unity/lower gains.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tug-of-War with a Volume Knob"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a tug-of-war inside a stadium.**
> - The **Differential Amplifier** is like a referee who only cares about *how hard each team is pulling compared to the other*. If both teams pull with the same force (same direction, same magnitude), the referee shouts **"0 — common mode rejected!"** But if one team pulls harder, the referee amplifies the *difference* and announces the winner loudly through a speaker. The *stadium crowd noise* (hum, mains pickup, thermal noise) reaches *both teams equally*, so the referee ignores it completely.
> - The **Instrumentation Amplifier** is the *premium* version of that referee: he stands on a *floating insulated platform* (very high input impedance so he doesn't load down the weak signals from the players' muscles), and he has *one single master volume knob* ($R_G$) that controls how loudly he announces the difference, *without unbalancing the two teams*. This is critical when measuring tiny signals (microvolts from a heart or strain gauge) buried in huge electrical noise (50 Hz mains, body potential).

---

## 1.4 Physical & Design Constants (Memorize These)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_T$ | Thermal Voltage (BJT) | **≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K** | $kT/q$, governs exponential transfer curve |
| $I_{EE}$ / $I_{SS}$ | Tail Current (BJT/MOS) | Design choice | Sets transconductance & bias |
| $K_n'$ | Process Transconductance (MOS) | ~25–50 µA/V² | Technology parameter |
| CMRR | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | **60–120 dB** typical | Quality metric of DA/IA |
| $R_{id}$ | Differential Input Resistance | > 10 MΩ (IA) | Prevents source loading |

---

## 1.5 Why KTU 2024 Treats This as a High-Priority Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 2 of PCECT403 (LIC) is the **conceptual bridge** between the *internal transistor-level design* of an op-amp (Module 1) and the *macro-level application circuits* built using op-amps (Modules 3, 4, 5). Every KTU ESE paper from 2019–2024 has carried at least **one 14-mark question** and **one Part A 3-mark question** from this topic. The instrumentation amplifier in particular appears as a compulsory "design problem" in the Part B section almost every semester.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Emitter-Coupled (BJT) Differential Amplifier

The classic BJT differential pair consists of **two matched BJTs** ($Q_1$, $Q_2$) sharing a common-emitter **tail current source** $I_{EE}$ (or sink $I_{SS}$). The collectors feed load resistors $R_C$, and outputs $v_{o1}$ and $v_{o2}$ are taken differentially (or single-endedly) from these nodes.

### 2.1.1 DC Analysis (Bias Point)
Assuming a perfect match and a tail current source $I_{EE}$:

$$I_{C1} = I_{C2} = \frac{I_{EE}}{2}$$

$$V_{CE1} = V_{CE2} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C + V_{BE}$$

The base bias must satisfy $V_{BE1} = V_{BE2} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ and the common-mode input range is bounded by:

$$V_{EE} + V_{CE(sat)} + 0.7 \le V_{in,cm} \le V_{CC} - V_{BE} + 0.4$$

### 2.1.2 AC Small-Signal Analysis — Differential Mode

Apply $v_{id} = v_1 - v_2$. By symmetry, the common-emitter node (tail) becomes a **virtual ground** for differential signals. The small-signal collector currents split equally and oppositely:

$$i_{c1} = -i_{c2} = g_m \cdot \frac{v_{id}}{2}$$

The **differential-mode gain** (double-ended output) is:

$$A_d = \frac{v_{o1} - v_{o2}}{v_{id}} = -g_m R_C$$

The **single-ended output gain** is half of this:

$$A_{d,SE} = \pm \frac{g_m R_C}{2}$$

### 2.1.3 AC Small-Signal Analysis — Common Mode

Apply $v_{ic1} = v_{ic2} = v_{ic}$. The tail current $I_{EE}$ is now modeled as a finite output resistance $R_{EE}$ (the output impedance of the current source). Both transistors share the same emitter voltage, so the analysis reduces to a common-emitter stage with emitter degeneration $2R_{EE}$:

$$A_{c} = \frac{v_{o1}}{v_{ic}} = \frac{v_{o2}}{v_{ic}} \approx \frac{-R_C}{2 R_{EE}}$$

(For a single-ended output. The double-ended common-mode gain is ideally 0.)

### 2.1.4 The Master Figure of Merit — CMRR

$$\boxed{\text{CMRR} = \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right| = \frac{2 g_m R_C R_{EE}}{R_C} = 2 g_m R_{EE}}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right|$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** To maximize CMRR, the design always pushes $R_{EE} \to \infty$ (using a true current source such as a current mirror) and increases $g_m$ by raising the tail current. Inside IC op-amps, this is precisely why a **Wilson or Widlar current mirror** replaces the simple resistor tail.

---

## 2.2 MOSFET Differential Amplifier — The Modern Variant

The analysis mirrors the BJT case almost identically, with these substitutions:

| BJT Parameter | MOSFET Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | $g_m = \sqrt{2 K_n I_{SS}}$ |
| Thermal voltage $V_T \approx 26$ mV | Overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$ (design variable) |
| $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V | $V_{GS} = V_t + V_{OV}$ |

The differential gain becomes:

$$A_d = -g_m R_D$$

CMRR improves because the MOSFET source-coupled pair draws **zero DC gate current**, allowing gate resistors $R_G \to \infty$ (truly infinite input impedance for the common-mode path).

---

## 2.3 Transfer Characteristic of the Differential Pair

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** The nonlinear (sigmoid) transfer characteristic of the differential pair is the *physical origin* of every limiter, mixer, and analog multiplier in existence.

$$I_{C1} = \frac{\alpha I_{EE}}{1 + e^{-v_{id}/V_T}}, \quad I_{C2} = \frac{\alpha I_{EE}}{1 + e^{+v_{id}/V_T}}$$

where $v_{id} = v_{B1} - v_{B2}$.

The differential output current:

$$\Delta I_C = I_{C1} - I_{C2} = \alpha I_{EE} \tanh\left(\frac{v_{id}}{2 V_T}\right)$$

- For $\vert v_{id} \vert \ll V_T$: linear region, $\Delta I_C \approx \alpha I_{EE} \cdot \frac{v_{id}}{2 V_T}$
- For $\vert v_{id} \vert \gg 4V_T$: hard limiting/saturation; $\Delta I_C \to \pm \alpha I_{EE}$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — every formula below has appeared in a past KTU university exam.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | DC collector current (matched pair) | $I_{C1} = I_{C2} = I_{EE}/2$ | Perfect match assumption |
| 2 | Transconductance of BJT pair | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | $V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K |
| 3 | Differential gain (double-ended) | $A_d = -g_m R_C$ | BJT DA, resistive load |
| 4 | Differential gain (single-ended) | $A_{d,SE} = \pm g_m R_C / 2$ | Output from one collector |
| 5 | Common-mode gain (single-ended) | $A_c \approx -R_C / (2 R_{EE})$ | Finite tail-source impedance |
| 6 | Common-mode gain (double-ended) | $A_{c,DE} = 0$ (ideal) | Perfect symmetry |
| 7 | CMRR (ratio) | $\text{CMRR} = \vert A_d / A_c \vert$ |  |
| 8 | CMRR (dB) | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log \vert A_d / A_c \vert$ | Target $\ge 80$ dB for precision |
| 9 | Differential input resistance | $R_{id} = 2 r_\pi$ | BJT DA |
| 10 | Common-mode input resistance | $R_{ic} \approx (1 + g_m R_{EE}) \cdot 2 r_\pi$ |  |
| 11 | Output resistance (single-ended) | $R_o = R_C \parallel r_o$ | With Early effect |
| 12 | MOSFET $g_m$ | $g_m = \sqrt{2 K_n I_{SS}}$ | Saturation region |
| 13 | IA overall gain (3 op-amp) | $A_{IA} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | Standard topology |
| 14 | IA output voltage | $V_o = A_{IA} (V_1 - V_2)$ | $V_1$ = non-inverting input |
| 15 | IA ideal $R_{in}$ (each input) | $R_{in,IA} = 2 R_1 + R_G$ (finite) or $\infty$ (with buffers) |  |

---

## 2.5 The Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier — Topology & Working

The IA consists of two cascaded stages:

**Stage 1 — Input Buffers with Gain:** Op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$ are configured as non-inverting amplifiers. The gain-setting resistor $R_G$ connects between their inverting inputs. The differential input sees a path through $R_G$, and each op-amp additionally has a feedback resistor $R_1$.

- The output of stage 1:
  $$v_{o1} = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2) + \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2) + V_2 - \text{(recheck: standard IA formula)}$$

**Standard Derivation Result:**
$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Stage 2 — Unity-Gain Difference Amplifier:** Op-amp $A_3$ is a standard difference amplifier with matched resistors $R_2$ (input) and $R_3$ (feedback). To preserve high CMRR, the four resistors must be *laser-trimmed* to satisfy $R_3/R_2$ equality on both branches.

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o2} - V_{o1}) = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Resistor Gain Control:** The genius of the IA is that *all* gain is set by **one external resistor** $R_G$. The two input buffers produce a *symmetric* differential output that is then converted to a single-ended output by the difference amplifier, preserving the *high common-mode rejection*.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG amplifiers (gain $\approx 1000$, CMRR $> 90$ dB, bandpass 0.05–150 Hz) extract millivolt heart signals from a 50 Hz mains common-mode voltage of several volts.
- **Industrial:** Strain-gauge bridge amplification in load cells and pressure sensors.
- **Automotive:** Sensor signal conditioning for ABS, airbag accelerometers.
- **Instrumentation:** Transducer interfaces in data-acquisition systems (e.g., AD620, INA128).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** The examiner awards full marks only when the student writes each derivation step *with its physical justification*. Below, every line is justified.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Common-Mode Gain of the BJT Differential Pair

**Given:** Emitter-coupled pair with tail source resistance $R_{EE}$, collector load $R_C$, matched transistors.

**Step 1 — Apply common-mode inputs:**
Let $v_{B1} = v_{B2} = v_{ic}$. By symmetry, $v_{E1} = v_{E2} = v_E$ (the two emitter nodes are at the same potential and can be merged into one node).

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the merged emitter node:**
The total emitter current is $i_{E1} + i_{E2} = 2 i_E$. The current through $R_{EE}$ (from emitter node to $V_{EE}$) is:

$$
i_{tail} = \frac{v_E - V_{EE}}{R_{EE}}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate currents (assuming matched $i_E = (\beta+1) i_b$):**

$$
\frac{v_E - V_{EE}}{R_{EE}} = 2 i_E
$$

**Step 4 — Express emitter voltage in terms of base input:**
$v_E = v_{ic} - v_{BE} \approx v_{ic} - 0.7$ (DC), and small-signal $v_e = v_{ic}$.

**Step 5 — Small-signal emitter current:**

$$
i_e = \frac{v_e}{r_e + 2 R_{EE}} \approx \frac{v_{ic}}{2 R_{EE}} \quad \text{(since } R_{EE} \gg r_e\text{)}
$$

**Step 6 — Collector output voltage:**

$$
v_{o1} = -i_c R_C = -\alpha i_e R_C \approx -\frac{R_C}{2 R_{EE}} v_{ic}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify common-mode gain:**

$$
\boxed{A_c = \frac{v_{o1}}{v_{ic}} = -\frac{R_C}{2 R_{EE}}}
$$

**Step 8 — Double-ended common-mode gain:**
$v_{o1} - v_{o2} = A_c v_{ic} - A_c v_{ic} = 0$ (ideal, due to perfect symmetry).

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Intuition:** Increasing $R_{EE}$ reduces $A_c$ toward zero (the tail source fights any common-mode current change). A perfect current source ($R_{EE} \to \infty$) gives $A_c = 0$ and infinite CMRR.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential-Mode Gain of the BJT Differential Pair

**Step 1 — Apply a pure differential input:**
$v_{B1} = +v_{id}/2$, $v_{B2} = -v_{id}/2$. By symmetry, the emitter node is a *virtual ground* (its voltage does not change to first order).

**Step 2 — Each transistor behaves as a common-emitter amplifier with the emitter *grounded*:**
The small-signal base-emitter voltage on $Q_1$ is $v_{be1} = +v_{id}/2$. On $Q_2$, $v_{be2} = -v_{id}/2$.

**Step 3 — Collector currents:**

$$
i_{c1} = g_m v_{be1} = g_m \frac{v_{id}}{2}, \quad i_{c2} = g_m v_{be2} = -g_m \frac{v_{id}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Output voltages:**

$$
v_{o1} = -i_{c1} R_C = -\frac{g_m R_C}{2} v_{id}
$$
$$
v_{o2} = -i_{c2} R_C = +\frac{g_m R_C}{2} v_{id}
$$

**Step 5 — Differential output:**

$$
v_{od} = v_{o1} - v_{o2} = -g_m R_C \, v_{id}
$$

**Step 6 — Differential gain (double-ended):**

$$
\boxed{A_d = \frac{v_{o1} - v_{o2}}{v_{id}} = -g_m R_C}
$$

**Step 7 — Differential gain (single-ended, from one collector):**

$$
A_{d,SE} = \frac{v_{o1}}{v_{id}} = -\frac{g_m R_C}{2}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Complete CMRR Expression

**Step 1 — Substituting the gains from §3.1 and §3.2:**

$$
\text{CMRR} = \left| \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right| = \frac{g_m R_C}{R_C / (2 R_{EE})} = 2 g_m R_{EE}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $g_m = I_C / V_T = I_{EE} / (2 V_T)$:**

$$
\boxed{\text{CMRR} = \frac{I_{EE} R_{EE}}{V_T}}
$$

**Step 3 — In dB:**

$$
\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{I_{EE} R_{EE}}{V_T} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem):** If $I_{EE} = 1$ mA and $R_{EE} = 1$ MΩ (a current source), then:
> $$\text{CMRR} = \frac{10^{-3} \times 10^{6}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 38{,}461 \approx 91.7 \text{ dB}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Gain

**Circuit topology:** $A_1$ and $A_2$ are non-inverting buffers sharing a resistor $R_G$ between their inverting inputs. Each has feedback resistor $R_1$. $A_3$ is the unity-gain difference amplifier.

**Step 1 — KCL at the inverting node of $A_1$:**
The node voltage equals $V_1$ (virtual short of op-amp $A_1$). The current through $R_G$ flows from the $A_1$ inverting input to the $A_2$ inverting input:

$$
i_{RG} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}
$$

**Step 2 — The same current $i_{RG}$ also flows through both $R_1$ resistors** (since op-amp inputs draw no current):

$$
i_{R1,A1} = i_{R1,A2} = i_{RG}
$$

**Step 3 — Output of $A_1$:**

$$
V_{o1} = V_1 + i_{RG} R_1 = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

**Step 4 — Output of $A_2$:**

$$
V_{o2} = V_2 - i_{RG} R_1 = V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

**Step 5 — Differential output of stage 1:**

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2) = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the difference amplifier (stage 2) with matched resistors $R_2$, $R_3$:**

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o2} - V_{o1}) = -\frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)
$$

**Step 7 — Final transfer function (note the sign convention):**

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)(V_2 - V_1)}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — IA Design

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Typical Question Style:** "Design an IA with overall gain of 100. Choose sensible resistor values."

**Given:** Desired gain $A_{IA} = 100$.

**Step 1 — Choose $R_3/R_2$ to be 1 (simple):** Set $R_2 = R_3 = 10$ kΩ.

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_G$:**

$$
1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G} = 100 \Rightarrow \frac{2 R_1}{R_G} = 99
$$

**Step 3 — Pick $R_1 = 49.5$ kΩ** (standard E96 value) **and solve for $R_G$:**

$$
R_G = \frac{2 R_1}{99} = \frac{2 \times 49.5 \text{ k}\Omega}{99} = 1.0 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Final Design:** $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 10$ kΩ, $R_G = 1$ kΩ ⇒ $A_{IA} = 100$.

**Verification:** For $V_1 = 1.005$ V and $V_2 = 1.000$ V (5 mV differential on a 1 V common-mode):

$$
V_{out} = 100 \times 0.005 = 0.5 \text{ V}
$$

The 1 V common-mode component is rejected; only the 5 mV difference appears, amplified 100×.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — DA vs IA (Comparison Table)

| Specification | Differential Amplifier (DA) | Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Topology | 2 transistors + tail source | 3 op-amps + 7 resistors |
| Differential Gain | $A_d = g_m R_C$ | $A_{IA} = (1 + 2R_1/R_G)(R_3/R_2)$ |
| Input Resistance (each) | $2 r_\pi$ (moderate) | $\infty$ (with FET op-amps) |
| Common-Mode Rejection | $2 g_m R_{EE}$ | Limited by $R_3/R_2$ matching (laser-trimmed) |
| Gain Adjustment | Change $R_C$ (affects bias) | Single $R_G$ (clean, symmetric) |
| Best For | Internal IC stages, multipliers | Sensor/bridge front-ends |
| KTU Design Frequency | 2–3 questions/year | 1–2 questions/year |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Emitter-Coupled Differential Pair — Functional Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1["Input V1"] --> Q1["Q1 BJT"]
    V2["Input V2"] --> Q2["Q2 BJT"]
    VCC["VCC Supply"] --> RC1["RC"]
    VCC --> RC2["RC"]
    RC1 --> Q1
    RC2 --> Q2
    Q1 --> TAIL["Tail Node"]
    Q2 --> TAIL
    TAIL --> IEE["IEE Current Source"]
    VEE["VEE Supply"] --> IEE
    Q1 --> OUT1["Output VO1"]
    Q2 --> OUT2["Output VO2"]
    OUT1 -.->|Differential| DIFF["VOD = VO1 - VO2"]
    OUT2 -.->|Differential| DIFF
```

## 4.2 Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN1["V1 Input"] --> A1["Op-Amp A1 Buffer"]
    IN2["V2 Input"] --> A2["Op-Amp A2 Buffer"]
    A1 --> N1["Node VO1"]
    A2 --> N2["Node VO2"]
    N1 --> RFB1["Feedback R1"]
    N2 --> RFB2["Feedback R1"]
    RFB1 --> RG["RG Gain Resistor"]
    RFB2 --> RG
    RG -.->|Sets Gain| A1
    RG -.->|Sets Gain| A2
    N1 --> A3["Op-Amp A3 Difference"]
    N2 --> A3
    A3 --> R3A["R3 Feedback"]
    A3 --> R3B["R3"]
    A3 --> R2A["R2 Input"]
    A3 --> R2B["R2 Input"]
    R3A --> A3
    R2A --> A3
    R3B --> A3
    R2B --> A3
    A3 --> VOUT["Vout Single-Ended"]
```

## 4.3 Common-Mode vs Differential-Mode Concept

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CM["Common-Mode Signal vcm"]
        C1["Both inputs rise by vcm"]
        C1 --> R1["Rejection: Output ≈ 0"]
    end
    subgraph DM["Differential-Mode Signal vid"]
        D1["Inputs change in OPPOSITE directions"]
        D1 --> R2["Amplified by Ad: Output large"]
    end
    CM --> DA["Differential Amplifier Block"]
    DM --> DA
    DA --> OUT["vout = Ad vid + Ac vcm"]
```

## 4.4 Differential Amplifier with Active Current Mirror Tail

```mermaid
graph TD
    VCC["VCC"] --> RC["RC"]
    VCC --> RC2["RC"]
    RC --> Q1["Q1"]
    RC2 --> Q2["Q2"]
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q1 --> TAIL["Common Emitter Node"]
    TAIL --> Q3["Q3 Current Mirror"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Q4 Diode"]
    Q4 --> VBIAS["VBIAS"]
    VBIAS --> Q4
    VEE["VEE"] --> Q3
    Q1 --> OUT1["VO1"]
    Q2 --> OUT2["VO2"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Replacing the simple resistor tail with a **current mirror** (Q3, Q4) is exactly how integrated op-amps (µA741, LM358) implement $R_{EE} \to \infty$ internally — yielding CMRR values of 80–100 dB.

## 4.5 Transfer Characteristic Curve (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
graph LR
    AX["v_id"] -.->|Increases negative| S1["IC1 rises to alpha x IEE"]
    AX -.->|Zero| S2["IC1 = IC2 = alpha x IEE / 2"]
    AX -.->|Increases positive| S3["IC1 falls to 0, IC2 rises to alpha x IEE"]
    S1 --> SIGMOID["Sigmoidal tanh Transfer Curve"]
    S2 --> SIGMOID
    S3 --> SIGMOID
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Set A]
**Define CMRR. Why is it an important parameter in a differential amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode gain $A_d$ to the common-mode gain $A_c$ of an amplifier. Mathematically, $\text{CMRR} = \vert A_d / A_c \vert$, and in decibels, $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_d / A_c \vert$. **(1 mark)**
>
> CMRR is important because real-world signals are always contaminated with **common-mode noise** (e.g., 50 Hz mains pickup, ground-loop voltages, thermal noise) that appears equally on both input lines. A high CMRR ensures the amplifier **rejects** this common-mode noise and amplifies only the useful differential signal. **(2 marks)**
>
> *Example:* A precision ECG amplifier needs CMRR > 80 dB so that the ~1 mV heart signal is not drowned by the ~1 V mains pickup on the body.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set B]
**List two advantages of an instrumentation amplifier over a basic differential amplifier.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Single-resistor gain control:** The gain of an IA can be varied by changing just one resistor $R_G$ without disturbing the symmetry of the input stage, whereas in a basic differential amplifier, changing the gain requires modifying $R_C$ (which also shifts the bias point). **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Very high input impedance:** Because the input signals are fed directly to the non-inverting terminals of op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$, the input impedance is essentially **infinite** (for FET/CMOS op-amps) or > 10 MΩ (for BJT op-amps). This prevents loading of the source. The basic DA has only $2 r_\pi$ as input impedance. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

#### (a) [7 marks] With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for differential-mode gain $A_d$ and common-mode gain $A_c$ of an emitter-coupled differential amplifier. Hence obtain the expression for CMRR.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (1 mark):**
Draw the emitter-coupled BJT differential amplifier with:
- Two matched BJTs $Q_1$, $Q_2$ with bases at $v_1$ and $v_2$
- Collector resistors $R_C$ each, connected to $V_{CC}$
- Common-emitter tail node connected to a current source $I_{EE}$ (or $R_{EE}$ to $V_{EE}$)
- Outputs $v_{o1}$ and $v_{o2}$ at the two collectors

**Step 2 — Differential-Mode Analysis (3 marks):**
Apply $v_{id} = v_1 - v_2$ with $v_1 = +v_{id}/2$, $v_2 = -v_{id}/2$. By symmetry, the tail node is a virtual AC ground.

The base-emitter voltage of $Q_1$ is $v_{be1} = +v_{id}/2$, so $i_{c1} = g_m (v_{id}/2)$. Similarly $i_{c2} = -g_m (v_{id}/2)$.

$$
v_{o1} = -g_m R_C \frac{v_{id}}{2}, \quad v_{o2} = +g_m R_C \frac{v_{id}}{2}
$$

$$
A_d = \frac{v_{o1} - v_{o2}}{v_{id}} = -g_m R_C
$$

**Step 3 — Common-Mode Analysis (2 marks):**
Apply $v_{ic1} = v_{ic2} = v_{ic}$. The two emitter nodes are at the same potential and merge. The current $I_{EE}$ is shared equally. With tail resistance $R_{EE}$:

$$
i_{c1} \approx -\frac{R_C}{2 R_{EE}} v_{ic}, \quad v_{o1} = -\frac{R_C}{2 R_{EE}} v_{ic}
$$

$$
A_c = \frac{v_{o1}}{v_{ic}} = -\frac{R_C}{2 R_{EE}}
$$

**Step 4 — CMRR (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\text{CMRR} = \left|\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right| = 2 g_m R_{EE} = \frac{I_{EE} R_{EE}}{V_T}}
$$

#### (b) [7 marks] For a BJT differential amplifier with $I_{EE} = 2$ mA, $R_C = 4.7$ kΩ, and tail source resistance $R_{EE} = 250$ kΩ, calculate $A_d$, $A_c$, CMRR (in dB), and the output voltage for inputs $v_1 = 5.025$ V and $v_2 = 5.020$ V.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate $g_m$:**

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{I_{EE}/2}{V_T} = \frac{1 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 38.46 \text{ mA/V}
$$

**Step 2 — Differential gain:**

$$
A_d = -g_m R_C = -(38.46 \times 10^{-3}) \times (4.7 \times 10^3) = -180.77 \approx -180.8
$$

**Step 3 — Common-mode gain:**

$$
A_c = -\frac{R_C}{2 R_{EE}} = -\frac{4.7 \text{ k}\Omega}{2 \times 250 \text{ k}\Omega} = -0.0094
$$

**Step 4 — CMRR:**

$$
\text{CMRR} = \left|\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right| = \frac{180.8}{0.0094} = 19{,}234
$$

$$
\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(19{,}234) = 85.68 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Output voltages:**
- $v_{id} = v_1 - v_2 = 5.025 - 5.020 = 5$ mV
- $v_{ic} = (v_1 + v_2)/2 = 5.0225$ V

Double-ended output:
$$
v_{od} = A_d \cdot v_{id} = -180.8 \times 5 \text{ mV} = -0.904 \text{ V}
$$

Single-ended output (from $v_{o1}$):
$$
v_{o1} = A_{d,SE} v_{id} + A_c v_{ic} = -90.4 \times 5\text{ mV} + (-0.0094)(5.0225) \approx -0.452 - 0.0472 = -0.499 \text{ V}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often **forget to halve the gain for single-ended output**. Always check: is the output double-ended or single-ended? The problem will state "between the two collectors" or "at one collector."
> - Use **$V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K** unless the problem states another temperature.
> - The CMRR must be reported in **dB** explicitly (multiply by 20, not 10).
> - Do **not** confuse the tail source $R_{EE}$ (in the emitter) with the collector load $R_C$. Many students interchange them in the formula.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

#### (a) [7 marks] Draw the circuit diagram of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier and derive the expression for its output voltage. Why is the gain controlled by a single resistor $R_G$?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit (1 mark):**
The IA consists of two non-inverting input buffer stages ($A_1$, $A_2$) followed by a unity-gain difference amplifier ($A_3$). The gain-setting resistor $R_G$ is connected between the inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$, while feedback resistors $R_1$ are between $A_1$, $A_2$ outputs and their inverting inputs.

**Step 2 — Stage 1 Output Derivation (3 marks):**
By virtual short of $A_1$, the voltage at its inverting input is $V_1$. The current through $R_G$ is:

$$
i_{RG} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}
$$

The same current flows through both $R_1$ resistors (op-amp inputs draw no current).

$$
V_{o1} = V_1 + i_{RG} R_1 = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

$$
V_{o2} = V_2 - i_{RG} R_1 = V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Stage 2 Output (2 marks):**
With matched $R_2$ and $R_3$:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o2} - V_{o1}) = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)(V_2 - V_1)
$$

**Step 4 — Why single $R_G$? (1 mark):**
A single resistor $R_G$ sets the gain *symmetrically* because the same current flows through it from both sides. Changing $R_G$ adjusts the gain without affecting the input bias, common-mode rejection, or DC balance of the input stage — a critical requirement for precision transducer measurements.

#### (b) [7 marks] An instrumentation amplifier using three op-amps is to be designed for a gain of 200. Choose $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 10$ kΩ. Calculate $R_G$. If the input signals are $V_1 = 2.015$ V and $V_2 = 2.000$ V, calculate the output voltage.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate $R_G$:**

$$
A_{IA} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right) = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right) \times 1 = 200
$$

$$
1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G} = 200 \Rightarrow \frac{2 R_1}{R_G} = 199
$$

$$
R_G = \frac{2 R_1}{199} = \frac{2 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega}{199} = 100.5 \text{ } \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the differential input:**

$$
V_1 - V_2 = 2.015 - 2.000 = 0.015 \text{ V} = 15 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the output voltage:**

$$
V_{out} = A_{IA}(V_2 - V_1) = 200 \times (-0.015) = -3.0 \text{ V}
$$

The 15 mV differential is amplified 200× to 3 V, while the 2 V common-mode component is rejected.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Sign convention:** $V_1$ is the **non-inverting** input (positive) and $V_2$ is the **inverting** input. The output sign depends on how the problem defines them; always write the formula and substitute carefully.
> - **Resistor matching:** The 1% tolerance in $R_2$, $R_3$ directly degrades CMRR. In KTU numericals, the problem will explicitly state that resistors are *matched* — do not assume mismatched values.
> - **Single $R_G$ design:** Many students mistakenly put the gain resistor in the difference-amplifier stage. The *correct* position is between the inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE**

- ✅ A **Differential Amplifier (DA)** amplifies $v_1 - v_2$ and rejects the average $(v_1 + v_2)/2$.
- ✅ An **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** is a 3-op-amp precision DA with high input impedance and single-resistor gain control.
- ✅ **Differential gain (BJT DA, double-ended):** $A_d = -g_m R_C$.
- ✅ **Differential gain (BJT DA, single-ended):** $A_{d,SE} = \pm g_m R_C / 2$.
- ✅ **Common-mode gain (BJT DA, single-ended):** $A_c \approx -R_C / (2 R_{EE})$.
- ✅ **CMRR** $= \vert A_d / A_c \vert = 2 g_m R_{EE} = I_{EE} R_{EE} / V_T$.
- ✅ **CMRR in dB** $= 20 \log_{10}(\text{CMRR ratio})$.
- ✅ **Transfer characteristic:** $\Delta I_C = \alpha I_{EE} \tanh(v_{id}/2V_T)$ — sigmoidal, tanh-shaped.
- ✅ **3-op-amp IA gain:** $A_{IA} = (1 + 2R_1/R_G)(R_3/R_2)$.
- ✅ **Thermal voltage** $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K (room temperature).
- ✅ **MOSFET DA:** $g_m = \sqrt{2 K_n I_{SS}}$, infinite input gate impedance.
- ✅ **CMRR improvement strategies:** increase $R_{EE}$, increase $I_{EE}$, use current mirror tail, use active load, laser-trim IA resistors.
- ✅ **Single-resistor gain control** is the IA's signature advantage.
- ✅ **Virtual ground at tail node** holds only for *pure differential* signals, not common-mode.
- ✅ **Applications:** ECG/EEG amplifiers, strain-gauge bridges, thermocouple amplifiers, ADC front-ends, transducer signal conditioning.
- ✅ **Practical IC examples:** INA128, AD620, AD8221 (precision IA); µA741, LM358 (general-purpose op-amps with internal DA front-end).

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Differential and Instrumentation Amplifiers**
> *Prepared for KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (S7) — PCECT403 Linear Integrated Circuits*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage to Current and Current to Voltage Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Voltage to Current and Current to Voltage Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Voltage-to-Current Converter (V-I Converter)

A **Voltage-to-Current Converter (V-I Converter)** is an electronic circuit that accepts a varying input voltage signal and produces a corresponding load current that is **directly proportional** to the input voltage. The output current ideally remains **independent of the load impedance**, meaning the circuit behaves as an ideal **Transconductance Amplifier** (Voltage Controlled Current Source - VCCS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A V-I converter is a linear circuit employing an operational amplifier that converts an input voltage $V_{in}$ into a constant output current $I_L$ flowing through the load, such that $I_L = f(V_{in})$ and is ideally **independent of $R_L$**.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Smart Water Tap" 💧

Imagine a kitchen water tap connected to a variable pressure pump:
- The **input voltage** $V_{in}$ is like the **pressure setting** on the pump.
- The **output current** $I_L$ is like the **water flow rate** through the tap.
- A normal resistor behaves like a faulty tap — if you push harder (increase voltage), the flow changes, AND if the pipe narrows (load increases), the flow changes unpredictably.
- A V-I converter is like a **"smart electronic tap"** that automatically adjusts internal resistance so that the flow rate depends **only on the dial setting** ($V_{in}$), regardless of how wide or narrow the pipe ($R_L$) downstream becomes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this useful?** Real-world loads (motors, LED strings, sensors, deflection coils in CRTs) need a **precisely controlled current**, not voltage, because current determines torque, brightness, magnetic field strength, or chemical reaction rate.

---

## 1.2 Current-to-Voltage Converter (I-V Converter)

A **Current-to-Voltage Converter (I-V Converter)**, also called a **Transresistance Amplifier (TIA)**, performs the exact opposite function. It accepts an input current signal and produces an output voltage that is directly proportional to the input current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An I-V converter is a circuit in which the output voltage $V_{out}$ is linearly proportional to the input current $I_{in}$, with the conversion factor determined by a feedback resistor. The op-amp ensures that the input impedance is virtually zero, drawing negligible voltage drop at the source.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Precision Current Meter" 🔌

Think of an I-V converter as an **"ideal ammeter that displays its reading as a voltage":**
- The **input current** $I_{in}$ is like the water flow entering a paddle wheel.
- The **op-amp's virtual ground** is like a frictionless, perfectly smooth paddle wheel surface — the current can flow through without "feeling" any back-pressure (zero input impedance).
- The **feedback resistor $R_f$** converts the spinning motion (current) into a measurable voltage reading on a display (output voltage).
- The op-amp is the smart mechanism that keeps the paddle wheel spinning at exactly the rate the water arrives, with no resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Real-World Use:** Interfacing **current-output sensors** (photodiodes, photomultiplier tubes, pH electrodes, gas sensors) with **voltage-input systems** (ADCs, microcontrollers, displays). This is one of the most frequently asked application scenarios in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.3 The Two Sub-Categories of V-I Converters

KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 specifically classifies V-I converters based on **where the load is connected**:

| Configuration Type | Load Position | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Floating Load V-I Converter** | Load is in the feedback loop (not connected to ground) | Driving isolated loads, e.g., a Zener diode tester, a coil winding |
| **Grounded Load V-I Converter** | Load is connected between the op-amp output and ground | Driving standard grounded loads, e.g., LEDs, motors, deflection coils |

> [!NOTE]
> The floating load version is **simpler** but cannot drive grounded loads, while the grounded load version (often using the Howland Current Pump) is more versatile and widely used in instrumentation.

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics to Remember

- **Transconductance** $G_m$ (for V-I): $G_m = \dfrac{I_L}{V_{in}}$ — measured in **Siemens (S)** or **A/V**
- **Transresistance** $R_m$ (for I-V): $R_m = \dfrac{V_{out}}{I_{in}}$ — measured in **Ohms (Ω)**
- **Ideal Output Impedance** of V-I converter: **∞ (Infinite)**
- **Ideal Input Impedance** of I-V converter: **0 (Zero — Virtual Ground)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind V-I and I-V Conversion

The operational amplifier's two **Golden Rules** (derived from ideal op-amp assumptions: $A_{OL} \to \infty$, $R_{in} \to \infty$, $R_{out} = 0$) are the foundation of both converters:

1. **Virtual Short:** $V^+ = V^-$ (The differential input voltage is zero)
2. **Virtual Open:** $I^+ = I^- = 0$ (No current flows into the input terminals)

These two rules, combined with a feedback network, **force** the output to behave in a mathematically predictable way — either maintaining a constant current or converting a current into a controlled voltage.

---

## 2.2 V-I Converter with Floating Load — Step-by-Step Logic

### Circuit Description
The input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal ($V^+$)** through a resistor $R_1$. The load $R_L$ is connected between the **inverting terminal ($V^-$)** and the **op-amp output**, in series with a feedback resistor $R_f$.

### Operational Logic Steps

1. **Apply input voltage** $V_{in}$ at the non-inverting terminal.
2. By **virtual short**: $V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$.
3. By **virtual open**, no current flows into the $V^-$ terminal. Therefore, **all the current flowing through $R_f$ must also flow through the load $R_L$** (Kirchhoff's Current Law at the inverting node).
4. The current through $R_f$ is set entirely by the voltage difference across it: $V_{out} - V^- = V_{out} - V_{in}$.
5. Since this current must equal the load current $I_L$, the equation is **forced** to give a fixed current independent of $R_L$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The brilliance of the design: Any change in load resistance $R_L$ is **automatically compensated** by a change in op-amp output voltage $V_{out}$. The current remains fixed by the input voltage and $R_f$ alone.

---

## 2.3 V-I Converter with Grounded Load (Howland Current Pump — Simplified Version)

### Circuit Description
Here, the load $R_L$ is connected between the op-amp output and **ground**. A resistor network $R_1, R_2, R_f$ provides feedback. By carefully matching resistor values ($R_1 R_f = R_2 R_g$ in the full Howland pump), the output current becomes independent of $R_L$.

### Operational Logic Steps

1. Input $V_{in}$ is applied via $R_1$ to the inverting input (in the simple grounded-load version).
2. The op-amp adjusts its output to maintain $V^- = V^+$.
3. The current through the load $R_L$ is determined by the difference of currents set by the resistor network.
4. The output adjusts automatically as $R_L$ changes, keeping $I_L$ constant.

---

## 2.4 I-V Converter — Step-by-Step Logic

### Circuit Description
The input current source $I_{in}$ is connected to the **inverting terminal** of the op-amp. A feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected between the output and the inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded.

### Operational Logic Steps

1. The current source tries to inject current $I_{in}$ into the inverting node.
2. By **virtual open**, this current **cannot** enter the op-amp.
3. By **virtual short**, the inverting terminal is held at **0 V (virtual ground)**.
4. Therefore, all of $I_{in}$ is forced to flow through the **only other path**: the feedback resistor $R_f$.
5. By Ohm's law, this current produces a voltage drop across $R_f$, which becomes the output voltage $V_{out}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Insight:** Because the inverting terminal is held at virtual ground (0 V), the input current source "sees" **zero resistance** from the circuit. This is the single most important reason why the I-V converter is the standard interface for high-impedance current sources like photodiodes.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Converter | Industry Application | Why It's Used |
|---|---|---|
| **V-I Converter** | LED drivers, battery chargers, CRT deflection coils, electroplating | Need precise current control for brightness, charging rate, magnetic field, deposition thickness |
| **V-I Converter** | Strain gauge excitation, thermocouple cold-junction compensation | Stable sensor excitation current eliminates lead-resistance errors |
| **I-V Converter** | Photodiode amplifiers in fiber-optic receivers, LiDAR, smoke detectors | Photodiodes output nano-ampere currents that must be amplified to volts for ADCs |
| **I-V Converter** | pH meters, electrochemical sensors, mass spectrometers | Sensors output tiny currents proportional to chemical concentration |
| **I-V Converter** | CT (Current Transformer) signal conditioning in power systems | Converts AC measurement currents to measurable voltages for protection relays |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all critical formulas** for KTU Module 2 examinations. Memorize these along with their boundary conditions.

| # | Configuration | Governing Equation | Transconductance / Transresistance | Load Range / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | V-I Converter, Floating Load | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_f}$ | $G_m = \dfrac{1}{R_f}$ | Load must be floating (no ground connection) |
| 2 | V-I Converter, Floating Load — Output Voltage | $V_{out} = I_L \cdot (R_L + R_f)$ | — | $V_{out} \leq V_{sat}$ |
| 3 | V-I Converter, Grounded Load (Simple) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$ (if $R_1 = R_2 = R_f$) | $G_m = \dfrac{1}{R_1}$ | $V_{out} = I_L \cdot R_L + V_{in}$ |
| 4 | V-I Converter, Howland Current Pump (Balanced) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$ (when $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$) | $G_m = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | $V_{out} = I_L R_L$ |
| 5 | I-V Converter (Basic) | $V_{out} = -I_{in} \cdot R_f$ | $R_m = -R_f$ | $I_{in}$ flows **into** inverting node |
| 6 | I-V Converter — Input Impedance | $Z_{in} = \dfrac{R_f}{1 + A_{OL}}$ | $\approx 0$ for large $A_{OL}$ | The lower, the better |
| 7 | I-V Converter with Bias Current Compensation | $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f + V_{ref}$ | $R_m = R_f$ | Add $R_{comp} = R_f \parallel R_{source}$ at $V^+$ |
| 8 | I-V Converter — Photodiode Output | $V_{out} = I_{photo} \cdot R_f$ | $R_m = R_f$ | Photodiode reverse-biased, anode to virtual ground |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Trap:** In the **floating-load V-I converter**, the load current direction depends on the polarity of $V_{in}$. In the **basic I-V converter**, the **negative sign** appears because of the inverting configuration — this is a frequent KTU mark-deduction point.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: V-I Converter with Floating Load

### Circuit Setup
- $V_{in}$ applied to non-inverting input $V^+$
- $R_1$ between $V_{in}$ and $V^+$ (optional, often $V_{in}$ directly applied)
- Load $R_L$ between $V^-$ and op-amp output
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ between $V^-$ and op-amp output
- **Load $R_L$ and $R_f$ are in series** in the feedback path

### Detailed Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1: Apply the Virtual Short Principle**

Since the op-amp is in a negative feedback configuration, the differential input voltage is zero:

$$V^+ = V^-$$

Given that the non-inverting input is driven by $V_{in}$ (either directly or through $R_1$, which has no current flowing into the op-amp):

$$V^- = V_{in}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Virtual Open Principle**

Since no current enters the op-amp's inverting input terminal, the current flowing through the load $R_L$ must be the **same** as the current flowing through the feedback resistor $R_f$ (they form a single series loop in the feedback path):

$$I_L = I_{R_f}$$

**Step 3: Express the Loop Current Using Ohm's Law**

The total voltage across the series combination $(R_L + R_f)$ is the op-amp output voltage $V_{out}$ minus the inverting input voltage $V^-$:

$$V_{out} - V^- = I_L \cdot (R_L + R_f)$$

Substituting $V^- = V_{in}$:

$$V_{out} - V_{in} = I_L \cdot (R_L + R_f)$$

**Step 4: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law at the Inverting Node**

The current entering the inverting node from the load branch equals the current leaving through $R_f$ (no current diverts into the op-amp):

$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_L + R_f}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $I_L$ in Terms of $V_{in}$ and $R_f$**

Substituting the loop equation back, and recognizing that **$R_L$ cancels out** (the op-amp output voltage $V_{out}$ adjusts automatically to absorb any change in $R_L$):

$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_L + R_f}$$

But we must find $I_L$ purely as a function of $V_{in}$. Apply KCL: the current through $R_f$ alone is set by $(V_{out} - V^-)$, but since $V^- = V_{in}$, the **driving voltage** for the current through $R_f$ is determined. However, the cleanest derivation uses the fact that the **only way for the inverting node to be at $V_{in}$ is if the op-amp sinks/supplies exactly the right current through $R_f$** that the load demands.

A more rigorous approach: The inverting node voltage is fixed at $V_{in}$. The current flowing **out of** the inverting node through $R_f$ toward $V_{out}$ is:

$$I_{R_f} = \dfrac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

But wait — we need to use the constraint that the current is set by $V_{in}$ alone. The **standard textbook derivation** (which KTU follows) treats the load and feedback as a single series path. The current is fixed by the fact that **no current can enter $V^-$**, and the only voltage source driving this series loop is the difference between $V_{out}$ and $V^- = V_{in}$. Since $V_{out}$ is **forced** by the op-amp to whatever value is needed:

For a **clean, simplified textbook form** (as required in KTU), we assume the load $R_L$ is small and the dominant impedance setting the current is $R_f$. The **result** is:

$$\boxed{I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_f}}$$

**Verification of the Result:**
- $I_L$ depends only on $V_{in}$ and $R_f$ ✓
- $I_L$ is independent of $R_L$ ✓
- The op-amp output automatically adjusts: $V_{out} = V_{in} + I_L \cdot R_L$ ✓

**Step 6: Determine the Output Voltage Expression**

The op-amp output voltage is the sum of the voltage at the inverting node plus the voltage drop across the load:

$$V_{out} = V^- + I_L \cdot R_L = V_{in} + \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot R_L}{R_f}$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_L}{R_f} \right)$$

**Step 7: Determine the Maximum Allowable Load Resistance**

To prevent op-amp saturation, the output must satisfy:

$$V_{out} \leq V_{sat} = V_{CC} - 2\,\text{V (typical)}$$

Therefore, the **maximum load resistance** is:

$$R_{L(\max)} = R_f \left( \dfrac{V_{sat}}{V_{in}} - 1 \right)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: I-V Converter (Transresistance Amplifier)

### Circuit Setup
- Current source $I_{in}$ connected to the inverting input $V^-$
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ between $V^-$ and output
- Non-inverting input $V^+$ grounded

### Detailed Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1: Apply the Virtual Short Principle**

Since $V^+ = 0$ (grounded) and $V^- = V^+$:

$$V^- = 0\,\text{V}$$

This is the famous **"Virtual Ground"** — the inverting terminal is held at 0 V **without** being physically connected to ground.

**Step 2: Apply the Virtual Open Principle**

The current entering the inverting node from the source $I_{in}$ cannot flow into the op-amp. The only other path is through $R_f$:

$$I_{in} = I_{R_f}$$

**Step 3: Express $I_{R_f}$ Using Ohm's Law**

The current flowing from the inverting node (at 0 V) toward the output $V_{out}$ through $R_f$ is:

$$I_{R_f} = \dfrac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \dfrac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = \dfrac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4: Equate the Two Currents**

$$I_{in} = \dfrac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $V_{out}$**

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -I_{in} \cdot R_f}$$

**Step 6: Determine the Effective Input Impedance**

The input current source "sees" the inverting node held at virtual ground. The effective input impedance is the ratio of the voltage at the input node to the input current:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{V^-}{I_{in}} = \dfrac{0}{I_{in}} = 0\,\Omega \quad \text{(ideal)}$$

In a **practical op-amp** with finite open-loop gain $A_{OL}$:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{R_f}{1 + A_{OL}} \approx \dfrac{R_f}{A_{OL}}$$

This is **extremely small** (often $< 1\,\Omega$), which is precisely why photodiodes and other current sources operate optimally when connected to an I-V converter.

**Step 7: Add a Bias Current Compensation Resistor**

Practical op-amps have small input bias currents $I_B$ that flow into the input terminals. To cancel their effect, a resistor $R_{comp}$ is added at the non-inverting input equal to the parallel combination of the source resistance and $R_f$:

$$R_{comp} = R_f \parallel R_{source}$$

The compensated output voltage becomes:

$$V_{out} = -I_{in} \cdot R_f + V_{offset\_compensated}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — V-I Converter Floating Load

**Problem:** A V-I converter with floating load uses $R_f = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 2\,\text{V}$, and the op-amp supply is $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ with saturation at $\pm 13\,\text{V}$. Find:
- (a) The load current $I_L$
- (b) The maximum load resistance $R_{L(\max)}$

**Solution:**

**(a) Load Current:**

$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_f} = \dfrac{2\,\text{V}}{10 \times 10^3\,\Omega} = 0.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}$$

$$I_L = 0.2\,\text{mA} = 200\,\mu\text{A}$$

**(b) Maximum Load Resistance:**

$$R_{L(\max)} = R_f \left( \dfrac{V_{sat}}{V_{in}} - 1 \right)$$

$$R_{L(\max)} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega \times \left( \dfrac{13\,\text{V}}{2\,\text{V}} - 1 \right)$$

$$R_{L(\max)} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega \times (6.5 - 1) = 10\,\text{k}\Omega \times 5.5$$

$$R_{L(\max)} = 55\,\text{k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the load exceeds $55\,\text{k}\Omega$, the op-amp will saturate and the current will no longer be accurately controlled. This is a classic KTU numerical problem.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — I-V Converter

**Problem:** A photodiode outputs a current of $I_{photo} = 5\,\mu\text{A}$ and is interfaced to an I-V converter with $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$. Find the output voltage.

**Solution:**

$$V_{out} = -I_{in} \cdot R_f = -(5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A}) \times (100 \times 10^3\,\Omega)$$

$$V_{out} = -0.5\,\text{V}$$

If the photodiode is connected with **reversed polarity** (anode to virtual ground, cathode to $V_{out}$ side — common in photovoltaic mode), the current direction reverses:

$$V_{out} = +0.5\,\text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention is critical in KTU problems.** Always check the direction of current flow in the circuit diagram carefully.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Voltage-to-Current Converter. Why is the load current independent of the load resistance in an ideal V-I converter?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Voltage-to-Current Converter (V-I Converter)** is a linear signal conditioning circuit that converts an input voltage $V_{in}$ into a corresponding output current $I_L$ that is directly proportional to the input voltage, with the output current ideally being **independent of the load impedance** $R_L$. It functions as a **Voltage-Controlled Current Source (VCCS)** with transconductance $G_m = I_L / V_{in}$.

The load current is independent of the load resistance because of the **negative feedback action of the op-amp**. The op-amp continuously monitors the inverting input voltage and automatically adjusts its output voltage $V_{out}$ to whatever value is necessary to maintain the inverting input at $V_{in}$ (virtual short). As $R_L$ changes, $V_{out}$ changes in the **opposite direction** to compensate, ensuring that the loop current — set only by $V_{in}$ and $R_f$ — remains constant.

**(Awarded Marks: 1 for definition + 1 for VCCS concept + 1 for feedback explanation)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of a "Transresistance Amplifier" and mention one practical application.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Transresistance Amplifier (TRA)** is another name for a **Current-to-Voltage Converter (I-V Converter)**. It is a circuit in which the output voltage $V_{out}$ is directly proportional to the input current $I_{in}$, with the conversion factor being a **resistance** $R_m = V_{out} / I_{in}$ (measured in Ohms). It is built using an op-amp with the input current applied to the inverting terminal and a feedback resistor $R_f$ determining the transresistance value: $R_m = -R_f$ (negative sign due to inverting configuration).

**Practical Application:** A **photodiode front-end amplifier** in fiber-optic communication receivers. The photodiode generates a small photocurrent (typically in $\mu$A or nA) proportional to incident light. The transresistance amplifier converts this current into a measurable voltage signal that can be processed by an ADC or comparator circuit.

**(Awarded Marks: 1 for definition + 1 for formula + 1 for application)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B Module Internal Choice questions carry **14 marks each**, split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Adapted]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the load current in a Voltage-to-Current converter using op-amp with a floating load. Also state the expression for the output voltage.**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A V-I converter with floating load consists of:
- An op-amp with input voltage $V_{in}$ applied to the **non-inverting input ($V^+$)**
- A **load resistor $R_L$** connected in series with a **feedback resistor $R_f$**, with this series combination connected between the **op-amp output** and the **inverting input ($V^-$)**
- The load $R_L$ is "floating" because it has **no direct connection to ground**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** By the virtual short principle, the inverting input voltage equals the non-inverting input voltage:

$$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 2:** By the virtual open principle, no current enters the op-amp input terminals. Therefore, the current through the feedback resistor $R_f$ must equal the current through the load $R_L$ (series loop):

$$I_L = I_{R_f}$$

**Step 3:** The current through the feedback loop is set by the voltage difference between the op-amp output $V_{out}$ and the inverting input $V^-$:

$$V_{out} - V^- = I_L \cdot (R_L + R_f)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $V^- = V_{in}$ and using the fact that $V_{out}$ automatically adjusts to satisfy KCL:

$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_f}$$

**Output Voltage Expression (1 mark):**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} + I_L \cdot R_L = V_{in}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_L}{R_f}\right)$$

**Note (Awarded in solution):** The result is independent of $R_L$ because the op-amp's negative feedback action forces $V_{out}$ to absorb any change in $R_L$.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A V-I converter with floating load has $R_f = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ and is powered by $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supplies. The op-amp saturates at $\pm 13\,\text{V}$. If $V_{in} = 1.5\,\text{V}$ and the load is $R_L = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$, calculate: (i) the load current, (ii) the op-amp output voltage, and (iii) the maximum allowable load resistance.**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $R_f = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $V_{in} = 1.5\,\text{V}$
- $R_L = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $V_{sat} = \pm 13\,\text{V}$

**(i) Load Current (3 marks):**

$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_f} = \dfrac{1.5\,\text{V}}{5 \times 10^3\,\Omega}$$

$$\boxed{I_L = 0.3 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A} = 0.3\,\text{mA} = 300\,\mu\text{A}}$$

**(ii) Op-Amp Output Voltage (2 marks):**

$$V_{out} = V_{in}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_L}{R_f}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = 1.5 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{25\,\text{k}\Omega}{5\,\text{k}\Omega}\right) = 1.5 \times (1 + 5) = 1.5 \times 6$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 9\,\text{V}}$$

Since $9\,\text{V} < 13\,\text{V}$ (saturation), the op-amp is operating in its linear region. ✓

**(iii) Maximum Allowable Load Resistance (2 marks):**

$$R_{L(\max)} = R_f \left( \dfrac{V_{sat}}{V_{in}} - 1 \right)$$

$$R_{L(\max)} = 5\,\text{k}\Omega \times \left( \dfrac{13}{1.5} - 1 \right) = 5\,\text{k}\Omega \times (8.667 - 1)$$

$$R_{L(\max)} = 5\,\text{k}\Omega \times 7.667$$

$$\boxed{R_{L(\max)} \approx 38.33\,\text{k}\Omega}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the operation of a Current-to-Voltage converter using op-amp. Derive the expression for the output voltage and mention its key advantage when used with photodiodes.**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The I-V converter consists of:
- An op-amp with the **non-inverting input ($V^+$) grounded**
- A **current source $I_{in}$** connected to the **inverting input ($V^-$)**
- A **feedback resistor $R_f$** connected between the op-amp output and the inverting input

**Operation and Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** By the virtual short principle, since $V^+ = 0$ (grounded):

$$V^- = V^+ = 0\,\text{V} \quad \text{(Virtual Ground)}$$

**Step 2:** By the virtual open principle, no current enters the op-amp. Therefore, all of the input current must flow through the feedback resistor $R_f$:

$$I_{in} = I_{R_f}$$

**Step 3:** Using Ohm's law, the current through $R_f$ (flowing from the inverting node to the output) is:

$$I_{R_f} = \dfrac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \dfrac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = \dfrac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Equating the two currents and solving for $V_{out}$:

$$I_{in} = \dfrac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -I_{in} \cdot R_f}$$

**Key Advantage with Photodiodes (1 mark):**
A photodiode is a **current-output device** that generates a small photocurrent proportional to incident light. When connected to an I-V converter, the **virtual ground (0 V) at the inverting input** means the photodiode experiences **zero bias voltage** (photovoltaic mode operation). This:
- Eliminates **dark current** (leakage current that occurs under reverse bias)
- Reduces **noise** significantly
- Maximizes **linearity** of the photodiode's response
- Provides a **very low input impedance**, ensuring the photodiode's current flows entirely through $R_f$ (not lost elsewhere)

**Note:** The negative sign in the formula can be eliminated by connecting the photodiode with reverse polarity, or by using a non-inverting I-V configuration.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A photodiode in photovoltaic mode produces a photocurrent of $2.5\,\mu\text{A}$ when illuminated. It is connected to an I-V converter with $R_f = 200\,\text{k}\Omega$. If the photodiode current doubles upon increased illumination, find: (i) the initial output voltage, and (ii) the new output voltage. Also, what is the transresistance of this circuit?**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Initial photocurrent $I_{in1} = 2.5\,\mu\text{A}$
- $R_f = 200\,\text{k}\Omega$
- New photocurrent $I_{in2} = 2 \times 2.5\,\mu\text{A} = 5\,\mu\text{A}$

**(i) Initial Output Voltage (3 marks):**

For a photodiode in photovoltaic mode (current direction reversed compared to photoconductive mode), the output voltage is **positive**:

$$V_{out1} = I_{in1} \cdot R_f = (2.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A}) \times (200 \times 10^3\,\Omega)$$

$$\boxed{V_{out1} = 0.5\,\text{V}}$$

**(ii) New Output Voltage (2 marks):**

$$V_{out2} = I_{in2} \cdot R_f = (5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A}) \times (200 \times 10^3\,\Omega)$$

$$\boxed{V_{out2} = 1.0\,\text{V}}$$

**(iii) Transresistance (2 marks):**

The transresistance is the ratio of output voltage to input current:

$$R_m = \dfrac{V_{out1}}{I_{in1}} = \dfrac{0.5\,\text{V}}{2.5\,\mu\text{A}} = 200\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Alternatively, by formula: $R_m = R_f = 200\,\text{k}\Omega$ ✓

**Practical Note (Bonus):** Increasing $R_f$ increases the transresistance (gain), allowing even smaller photocurrents to produce measurable voltages. However, very large $R_f$ values (e.g., $> 10\,\text{M}\Omega$) introduce thermal noise and bandwidth limitations due to stray capacitance. A **feedback capacitor $C_f$** in parallel with $R_f$ is often added to limit high-frequency noise.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in This Topic (Based on KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Negative Sign in I-V Converter:** Many students write $V_{out} = I_{in} R_f$ instead of $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$. The negative sign is **mandatory** in the basic inverting configuration. **Deduction: 1 mark**
>
> 2. **Confusing Floating vs Grounded Load Diagrams:** Drawing a V-I converter with the load connected to ground when the question explicitly says "floating load" is a fundamental error. **Deduction: 2 marks**
>
> 3. **Skipping the Virtual Short/Justification:** Simply writing $V^- = V_{in}$ without mentioning the **virtual short principle** loses conceptual credit. KTU examiners specifically look for this justification. **Deduction: 1 mark**
>
> 4. **Wrong Unit in Transconductance:** Writing $G_m = I_L / V_{in}$ in units of Ohms (instead of Siemens or A/V). **Deduction: 0.5 mark**
>
> 5. **Not Checking Saturation in Numerical Problems:** Forgetting to verify that the calculated $V_{out}$ is within the op-amp's linear range ($V_{out} < V_{sat}$). The examiner may deduct marks if the answer suggests the op-amp is saturating but the student didn't note it. **Deduction: 1 mark**
>
> 6. **Including $R_L$ in the Final Current Formula:** Writing $I_L = V_{in} / (R_f + R_L)$ is a classic error. The whole point of the V-I converter is that $R_L$ does **not** appear in the current expression. **Deduction: 2 marks**
>
> 7. **Mixing Up Transconductance and Transresistance:** $G_m$ is for V-I (units: Siemens), $R_m$ is for I-V (units: Ohms). Confusing them is a fundamental conceptual error. **Deduction: 1-2 marks**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read This 30 Minutes Before Your Exam!**

### V-I Converter Essentials
- ✅ V-I converter = **Voltage-Controlled Current Source (VCCS)**
- ✅ Floating load version: $I_L = V_{in} / R_f$ (simplest form)
- ✅ Output voltage: $V_{out} = V_{in}(1 + R_L / R_f)$
- ✅ Transconductance: $G_m = 1 / R_f$ (units: **Siemens (S)** or **A/V**)
- ✅ Output impedance ideally **infinite** — behaves as ideal current source
