- Percentage error: $\eta_{\text{prec}} = \dfrac{V_{TH,\text{eff}}}{V_m} \times 100 = \dfrac{3.5 \times 10^{-6}}{1} \times 100 = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}\%$

**Comparison (1 mark):** The conventional rectifier loses **70%** of the signal, while the precision rectifier loses only **0.00035%** — a dramatic improvement of nearly **5 orders of magnitude**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the role of $D_2$:** Many students omit the clamping diode $D_2$ in diagrams. This diode is **mandatory** for high-frequency operation as it prevents op-amp saturation. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing $V_a$ with $V_o$:** The op-amp output voltage $V_a$ is **not** equal to the circuit output $V_o$. Students often write $V_o = V_i + 0.7$ — this is wrong. $V_a = V_i + 0.7$, but $V_o = V_i$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Sign errors in FWR analysis:** The polarity of $V_{o1}$ in stage 1 of the full-wave rectifier must be carefully tracked. Always state the assumed direction of $I_1$ before applying KCL. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 4. **Using $V_D = 0$ without justification:** If you assume $V_D = 0$ in derivations, explicitly state that the op-amp compensates for the diode drop. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Missing the unit in the formula sheet:** The numerical value of $V_T = 26\text{ mV}$ must include the temperature condition ($T = 300\text{ K}$). **Loss: 0.5 marks.**

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Precision Rectifier = Op-Amp + Diode in Feedback** — exploits op-amp's high $A_{OL}$ to nullify $V_D$.
- ✅ **Effective threshold voltage:** $V_{TH,\text{eff}} = V_D / (1 + A_{OL}) \approx V_D / A_{OL}$.
- ✅ **For 741 op-amp:** $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$, so $V_{TH,\text{eff}} \approx 3.5\ \mu V$ (vs. 0.7 V passive).
- ✅ **Half-wave super diode output:** $V_o = V_i$ for $V_i > 0$, and $V_o = 0$ for $V_i \leq 0$.
- ✅ **Op-amp output pin voltage:** $V_a = V_i + V_D$ (NOT the circuit output $V_o$).
- ✅ **Clamping diode $D_2$** (op-amp output to ground) prevents saturation and enables high-frequency operation.
- ✅ **Full-wave precision rectifier** uses two op-amps: one half-wave stage + one summing stage.
- ✅ **Output equation:** $V_o = \vert V_i \vert$ or $V_o = -\vert V_i \vert$ depending on summing-amplifier polarity.
- ✅ **Applications:** Biomedical (ECG, EEG), instrumentation, true-RMS converters, AM demodulation.
- ✅ **Key advantage:** Faithful rectification of signals **below 0.7 V**, including mV and µV levels.
- ✅ **Shockley diode equation** governs the underlying physics: $I_D = I_S (e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1)$.
- ✅ **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx 26\text{ mV}$ at $T = 300\text{ K}$.
- ✅ **KTU exam hint:** Always include the circuit diagram, state the case ($V_i > 0$ / $V_i < 0$), apply virtual short, and conclude with the transfer characteristic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Comparators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Comparator**, in the context of Linear Integrated Circuits, is an open-loop operational amplifier circuit designed to compare two analog input voltages and produce a **binary digital output** that indicates which of the two inputs is larger. The op-amp is intentionally operated in its **non-linear (saturated) region**, making it fundamentally different from a linear amplifier.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, comparators are classified into the following canonical configurations:

1. **Basic Comparator** (Inverting and Non-Inverting)
2. **Zero-Crossing Detector (ZCD)**
3. **Schmitt Trigger** (Inverting and Non-Inverting) — a comparator with **positive feedback** and **hysteresis**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Verbatim Standard)**
> A comparator is a two-input, single-output analog decision-making circuit. The output is forced to one of the two saturation voltages of the op-amp ($+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$, typically $\pm 13.5$ V for a 741 op-amp powered by $\pm 15$ V) depending on the sign of the differential input voltage $V_d = V_+ - V_-$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Tilt Switch"**
> Imagine a seesaw with a flag attached to its center. You push the seesaw from the left or the right.
> - If the **left side is pushed down even slightly more** than the right, the flag flips to the **LEFT position** (say, $+V_{sat}$).
> - If the **right side is pushed down even slightly more**, the flag flips to the **RIGHT position** ($-V_{sat}$).
> - The slightest imbalance ($\mu$V level) is enough — the op-amp comparator works exactly like this. It amplifies the *difference* between $V_+$ and $V_-$ to its maximum limit, giving a clean digital "high" or "low."

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (741 Op-Amp) | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Supply Voltage ($V_{CC}, -V_{EE}$) | $\pm 15$ | V |
| Output Saturation High ($+V_{sat}$) | $+13.5$ (typ.) | V |
| Output Saturation Low ($-V_{sat}$) | $-13.5$ (typ.) | V |
| Open-Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$) | $2 \times 10^5$ | V/V |
| Switching Time | $\approx 1$ | $\mu$s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 2 mandates thorough coverage of **hysteresis** calculation, **threshold voltage** derivation, and **transfer characteristics** (Voltage Transfer Curve, VTC) of Schmitt Triggers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Basic Comparator (Open-Loop Configuration)

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic

An op-amp in open-loop (no feedback) has a finite open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ (typically $2 \times 10^5$). Therefore, even a millivolt-level differential input drives the output to saturation.

The output expression is:

$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$$

Since $A_{OL}$ is enormous, the output can only physically take two stable values: $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$.

### 2.1.2 Decision Rules

- **Non-Inverting Comparator** ($V_{in}$ at $V_+$, reference at $V_-$):
  - If $V_{in} > V_{ref}$, then $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (Logic HIGH)
  - If $V_{in} < V_{ref}$, then $V_o = -V_{sat}$ (Logic LOW)

- **Inverting Comparator** ($V_{in}$ at $V_-$, reference at $V_+$):
  - If $V_{in} > V_{ref}$, then $V_o = -V_{sat}$ (Logic LOW)
  - If $V_{in} < V_{ref}$, then $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (Logic HIGH)

## 2.2 Zero-Crossing Detector (ZCD)

A ZCD is a special comparator where the reference voltage is **ground (0 V)**. It detects the precise instant a sinusoidal or arbitrary waveform crosses the zero-voltage axis.

- $V_o = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > 0$ V
- $V_o = -V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < 0$ V

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Pitfall:** A basic ZCD suffers from **multiple false transitions** when the input signal is noisy and hovers near 0 V. This is resolved by the **Schmitt Trigger** with hysteresis.

## 2.3 Schmitt Trigger — The Comparator with Hysteresis

The Schmitt Trigger solves the noise problem of a basic comparator by introducing **positive feedback** from the output back to the **non-inverting input**. This creates two distinct switching thresholds, producing a phenomenon called **hysteresis**.

### 2.3.1 Inverting Schmitt Trigger

**Circuit Configuration:**
- Input $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal** ($V_-$)
- A voltage divider ($R_1$ to ground, $R_2$ as feedback) samples the output and feeds it to the **non-inverting terminal** ($V_+$)

**Voltage at Non-Inverting Terminal (using superposition):**

$$V_+ = V_o \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The switching condition occurs when $V_- = V_+$, i.e.:

$$V_{in} = V_o \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### 2.3.2 Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger

**Circuit Configuration:**
- Input $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal** ($V_+$)
- The inverting terminal is connected to a voltage divider ($R_1$ to ground, $R_2$ feedback)

**Voltage at Inverting Terminal:**

$$V_- = V_o \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The switching condition is $V_{in} = V_-$, which gives the same threshold formula structure.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU 2024 "Must-Memorize" Formula Box**

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Open-Loop Output | $V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$ | Basic comparator output |
| Upper Threshold (VUT) | $V_{UT} = +V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Output switches LOW→HIGH |
| Lower Threshold (VLT) | $V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Output switches HIGH→LOW |
| Hysteresis Width | $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_{sat} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Total noise-immunity band |
| Feedback Factor ($\beta$) | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Ratio that sets thresholds |
| Trip Point (Non-Inv) | $V_{th} = \beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Single symmetric threshold |
| Regeneration Condition | $A_{OL} \cdot \beta \geq 1$ | Ensures snap-action (hysteresis exists) |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Comparators are the **bridge between the analog and digital worlds**. They are essential in:

- **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs):** Flash ADCs, successive approximation ADCs
- **Oscilloscope Triggering Circuits**
- **Over-Voltage / Under-Voltage Protection Relays**
- **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) Generators**
- **Window Comparators** (used in battery monitoring systems)
- **Signal Conditioning** in sensor interfaces (e.g., LM393, LM311 ICs)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Threshold Voltages of an Inverting Schmitt Trigger

### Given:
- $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5$ V (using 741 op-amp with $\pm 15$ V supply)
- $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 90$ k$\Omega$

### Step 1 — Express $V_+$ using the Voltage Divider Rule

Since no current flows into the ideal op-amp's non-inverting terminal, the current through $R_1$ equals the current through $R_2$.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_+ &= V_o \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \\
&= V_o \cdot \frac{10\,\text{k}}{10\,\text{k} + 90\,\text{k}} \\
&= V_o \cdot \frac{10}{100} = 0.1 \cdot V_o
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Switching Condition

The comparator switches state when the differential input is zero: $V_+ = V_-$. Since $V_- = V_{in}$:

$$V_{in} = V_+ = 0.1 \cdot V_o$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Two Possible Output States

**Case A: When $V_o = +V_{sat} = +13.5$ V (Upper Threshold)**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{UT} &= 0.1 \cdot (+13.5) \\
&= +1.35 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

This means: as long as $V_{in} > +1.35$ V, the comparator output is forced LOW to $-V_{sat}$.

**Case B: When $V_o = -V_{sat} = -13.5$ V (Lower Threshold)**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{LT} &= 0.1 \cdot (-13.5) \\
&= -1.35 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

This means: as long as $V_{in} < -1.35$ V, the comparator output is forced HIGH to $+V_{sat}$.

### Step 4 — Calculate the Hysteresis Width

$$\begin{aligned}
V_H &= V_{UT} - V_{LT} \\
&= (+1.35) - (-1.35) \\
&= 2.70 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Any noise riding on the input signal with peak-to-peak amplitude less than **2.70 V** will be **completely rejected** by the comparator. The output will not chatter — this is the **noise immunity** advantage of the Schmitt Trigger.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger Thresholds

### Given:
- $V_{in}$ applied to non-inverting terminal through $R_1$ (to ground) and $R_2$ (feedback to inverting)
- $V_{sat} = \pm 12$ V, $R_1 = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 20$ k$\Omega$

### Step 1 — Voltage at Inverting Terminal

$$\begin{aligned}
V_- &= V_o \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \\
&= V_o \cdot \frac{5\,\text{k}}{5\,\text{k} + 20\,\text{k}} \\
&= V_o \cdot 0.2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Switching Condition

For a non-inverting Schmitt trigger, the input is $V_+ = V_{in}$ and switching occurs when $V_+ = V_-$:

$$V_{in} = 0.2 \cdot V_o$$

### Step 3 — Upper Threshold

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{UT} &= 0.2 \cdot (+12) \\
&= +2.4 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Lower Threshold

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{LT} &= 0.2 \cdot (-12) \\
&= -2.4 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Hysteresis Width

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 4.8 \text{ V}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Asymmetric Threshold with Reference Voltage

Often, a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to bias the hysteresis band to a non-symmetric location (e.g., detect over-voltage above 5 V with noise rejection).

### Circuit Modification:
The non-inverting terminal is connected to $V_{ref}$ through $R_3$, and to the output through $R_2$, with $R_1$ to ground.

**Using KCL at the non-inverting node (no current into op-amp):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_+ - V_{ref}}{R_3} + \frac{V_+ - V_o}{R_2} &= 0 \\
V_+ \left(\frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3}\right) &= \frac{V_{ref}}{R_3} + \frac{V_o}{R_2} \\
V_+ &= \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_2 + V_o \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Upper Threshold (with $V_o = +V_{sat}$):**

$$V_{UT} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_2 + V_{sat} \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Lower Threshold (with $V_o = -V_{sat}$):**

$$V_{LT} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_2 - V_{sat} \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Hysteresis:**

$$V_H = \frac{2 \cdot V_{sat} \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Asymmetric Hysteresis Insight:** The $V_{ref}$ term shifts the center of the hysteresis band, while the $V_{sat}$ term controls the **width** of the band. This is a frequent KTU 14-mark problem.

## 3.4 Design Problem Worked Example

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** Design a non-inverting Schmitt trigger with $V_{UT} = +4$ V, $V_{LT} = +1$ V using a 741 op-amp ($V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V).

**Step 1 — Find the feedback factor $\beta$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_H &= V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ V} \\
\beta &= \frac{V_H}{2 \cdot V_{sat}} = \frac{3}{2 \cdot 13} = 0.1154
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Find the reference voltage $V_{ref}$:**

The midpoint of the hysteresis band is $V_M = \dfrac{V_{UT} + V_{LT}}{2} = 2.5$ V.

For the non-inverting configuration with $V_{ref}$:

$$V_M = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3} + 0 = V_{ref} \cdot (1 - \beta)$$

$$V_{ref} = \frac{V_M}{1 - \beta} = \frac{2.5}{1 - 0.1154} = 2.826 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Choose a standard $R_1$ and compute $R_2$:**

Let $R_1 = 1$ k$\Omega$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 0.1154 \\
R_1 + R_2 &= \frac{R_1}{0.1154} = \frac{1000}{0.1154} = 8666 \,\Omega \\
R_2 &= 8666 - 1000 = 7.666 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 7.5 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (standard)}
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Vin Input Source] --> nodeB[Inverting Terminal V-]
    nodeC[Non-Inverting V+ Node] --> nodeD[Comparator Core OpAmp 741]
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Output Stage Vout]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Positive Feedback Network R1 R2]
    nodeF --> nodeC
    nodeG[Ground Reference 0V] --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeH[Load or Next Stage]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Saturation Logic Plus or Minus Vsat]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - `nodeA` represents the input signal $V_{in}$ entering the inverting terminal.
> - `nodeC` and `nodeF` form the **positive feedback loop** — the defining feature of the Schmitt Trigger.
> - `nodeI` captures the binary nature of the output: it is *always* at $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$.

## 4.2 Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) of Inverting Schmitt Trigger

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Vin Very Negative] --> nodeB[Vo Equals Plus Vsat High State]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Vin Rises and Crosses VLT at Negative 1.35 V]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Output Snaps to Minus Vsat Low State]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Vin Keeps Rising Through VUT at Plus 1.35 V]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Output Remains Minus Vsat No Change]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Vin Falls and Crosses VUT at Plus 1.35 V]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Output Snaps Back to Plus Vsat]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Vin Continues to Fall Through VLT]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Output Stays at Plus Vsat]
```

## 4.3 Functional Comparison Block Diagram: Basic Comparator vs. Schmitt Trigger

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Analog Input Vin] --> nodeB{Basic Comparator Open Loop}
    nodeA --> nodeC[Schmitt Trigger with Positive Feedback]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Single Threshold at Vref]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Dual Thresholds VUT and VLT]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Output Chatters on Noise Near Vref]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Output is Hysteresis Immune to Noise]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Unreliable for Slow or Noisy Signals]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Stable Clean Digital Transitions]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Industrial Grade Failure Risk]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Preferred for Production Designs]
```

## 4.4 Zero-Crossing Detector Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Sinusoidal Input Vin equals A sin omega t] --> nodeB[OpAmp in Open Loop]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Vin Greater Than Zero?}
    nodeB --> nodeD{Vin Less Than Zero?}
    nodeC --> nodeE[Output Equals Plus Vsat HIGH]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Output Equals Minus Vsat LOW]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Square Wave Output with Same Frequency]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Feed to Microcontroller or Counter]
```

## 4.5 Component-Level Reference Schematic of Inverting Schmitt Trigger

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[DC Source Vin Connected to Inverting Input Pin 2] --> nodeB[Resistor R1 10k from Pin 2 Optional Input Resistor]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Inverting Input Node]
    nodeD[Resistor R2 90k Feedback from Output to Pin 2] --> nodeC
    nodeE[Resistor R3 10k from Non Inverting Pin 3 to Ground] --> nodeF[Non Inverting Input Node]
    nodeG[Resistor R4 90k Feedback from Output to Pin 3] --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH[OpAmp 741 Triangle Symbol]
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Output Pin 6]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Load Resistor RL to Ground]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Back to R2 and R4 Feedback]
    nodeL[Plus Vcc plus 15V] --> nodeH
    nodeM[Minus VEE minus 15V] --> nodeH
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The triangle `nodeH` is the op-amp. The two feedback resistors (`nodeD` and `nodeG`) inject a fraction of the output back to *both* input nodes in a true bistable configuration. This dual-feedback arrangement is what produces the snap-action behavior essential to hysteresis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What is a comparator? How does it differ from an amplifier?**

**Model Answer:**
A comparator is an open-loop op-amp circuit that compares two input voltages and produces a binary output ($+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$). It operates in the **non-linear (saturated) region** of the op-amp.
In contrast, an amplifier uses **negative feedback** to operate in the **linear region**, where the output is a scaled replica of the input. A comparator has no feedback (or only positive feedback) and behaves as a 1-bit ADC.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define hysteresis voltage in a Schmitt trigger. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**
**Hysteresis voltage** $V_H$ is the difference between the upper threshold voltage $V_{UT}$ and the lower threshold voltage $V_{LT}$ of a Schmitt trigger:

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \frac{2 \cdot V_{sat} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

It is important because it provides **noise immunity**. Any input noise with amplitude less than $V_H/2$ will not cause the output to change state, preventing **false triggering** and ensuring **clean digital transitions**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024):** KTU allows students to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from the same module. Both are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Part (a)** — *For an inverting Schmitt trigger with $R_1 = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 47$ k$\Omega$ and $V_{sat} = \pm 14$ V, calculate the upper and lower threshold voltages, the hysteresis width, and draw the voltage transfer characteristic.* **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the feedback factor $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{4.7}{4.7 + 47} = \frac{4.7}{51.7} = 0.0909$$

**Step 2 — Upper Threshold Voltage:**

$$V_{UT} = +V_{sat} \cdot \beta = 14 \times 0.0909 = +1.273 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Lower Threshold Voltage:**

$$V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \beta = -14 \times 0.0909 = -1.273 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis Width:**

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 1.273 - (-1.273) = 2.546 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — VTC Description:**
- For $V_{in} < V_{LT} = -1.273$ V: $V_o = +V_{sat} = +14$ V
- For $V_{in} > V_{UT} = +1.273$ V: $V_o = -V_{sat} = -14$ V
- Between $-1.273$ V and $+1.273$ V: output retains its previous state (memory property)

---

**Part (b)** — *Explain with a neat circuit diagram the operation of a zero-crossing detector using an op-amp. Mention its limitations.* **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

A **zero-crossing detector (ZCD)** is a comparator with one input tied to ground ($V_{ref} = 0$ V) and the other input receiving the time-varying signal $V_{in} = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Operation:**
- When $V_{in} > 0$: op-amp output saturates to $+V_{sat}$
- When $V_{in} < 0$: op-amp output saturates to $-V_{sat}$

The result is a **square wave** of the same frequency as the input, with transitions aligned to the zero-crossings of the sine wave.

**Limitations:**
1. **Noise-induced false triggering** near the zero-crossing point due to slow signal slope.
2. **Output amplitude is fixed** at $\pm V_{sat}$ regardless of input amplitude — no scaling.
3. **Limited switching speed** of the op-amp (slew rate, ~0.5 V/$\mu$s for 741) introduces **transition delay**.
4. **No threshold adjustment** — the comparator triggers exactly at 0 V, with no noise margin.

**Solution to limitations:** Replace the ZCD with a **Schmitt Trigger** to introduce hysteresis and threshold flexibility.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Part (a)** — *Design a non-inverting Schmitt trigger to detect when a signal exceeds 3.2 V with a hysteresis of 1.0 V. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V.* **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Specifications:**
- Center of hysteresis band $V_M = 3.2$ V (since both thresholds are above ground by this amount)

**Wait — re-reading the problem:** The signal should trigger when it *exceeds* 3.2 V. The hysteresis is 1.0 V. So we want $V_{UT} = 3.2$ V and $V_{LT} = 3.2 - 1.0 = 2.2$ V? Or symmetric about 3.2 V? KTU typically interprets this as **symmetric around the trip point**, so:

- $V_{UT} = 3.2 + 0.5 = 3.7$ V
- $V_{LT} = 3.2 - 0.5 = 2.7$ V

**Step 2 — Calculate Hysteresis:**

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 3.7 - 2.7 = 1.0 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Feedback Factor:**

$$\beta = \frac{V_H}{2 \cdot V_{sat}} = \frac{1.0}{2 \times 13} = 0.03846$$

**Step 4 — Choose $R_1$ and find $R_2$:**

Let $R_1 = 1$ k$\Omega$ (standard). Then:

$$R_2 = R_1 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\beta} - 1\right) = 1000 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.03846} - 1\right) = 1000 \cdot 25 = 25 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Find $V_{ref}$ (for non-inverting with reference):**

The center of the band $V_M = 3.2$ V is set by $V_{ref}$:

$$V_M = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3} = V_{ref} \cdot (1 - \beta)$$

$$V_{ref} = \frac{V_M}{1 - \beta} = \frac{3.2}{1 - 0.03846} = \frac{3.2}{0.9615} = 3.328 \text{ V}$$

---

**Part (b)** — *Derive the expression for the upper and lower threshold voltages of a Schmitt trigger with a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ connected to the non-inverting terminal through $R_3$.* **(7 marks)**

**Solution:** *(This is the asymmetric case covered in Section 3.3)*

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the non-inverting node:**

$$\frac{V_+ - V_{ref}}{R_3} + \frac{V_+ - V_o}{R_2} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_+$:**

$$V_+ = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_2 + V_o \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 3 — Switching condition $V_{in} = V_+$ (inverting input configuration):**

**Upper Threshold** (with $V_o = +V_{sat}$):

$$V_{UT} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_2 + V_{sat} \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Lower Threshold** (with $V_o = -V_{sat}$):

$$V_{LT} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_2 - V_{sat} \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis Width:**

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \frac{2 \cdot V_{sat} \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top Reasons Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing inverting and non-inverting Schmitt trigger formulas** — remember: in both cases the *threshold formula structure* is the same, but the *input is applied to a different terminal*.
> 2. **Using $V_{CC}$ instead of $V_{sat}$** in threshold calculations. $V_{sat}$ is the *actual* output swing, which is ~1.5 V below $V_{CC}$ for a 741.
> 3. **Forgetting to include $V_{ref}$** in asymmetric hysteresis problems — it shifts the center of the band.
> 4. **Not drawing the hysteresis loop** in the VTC — KTU examiners award 2 marks specifically for the transfer characteristic diagram.
> 5. **Units mismatch** — mixing k$\Omega$ and $\Omega$ in the same calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Comparators**

- **Basic Comparator** = Open-loop op-amp = output is always $\pm V_{sat}$.
- **Zero-Crossing Detector** = comparator with $V_{ref} = 0$ V; converts sine to square wave.
- **Schmitt Trigger** = comparator + positive feedback = two thresholds ($V_{UT}$, $V_{LT}$) + hysteresis ($V_H$).
- **Feedback factor:** $\beta = R_1 / (R_1 + R_2)$.
- **Threshold formula (universal):** $V_{th} = \pm V_{sat} \cdot \beta$.
- **Hysteresis width:** $V_H = 2 \cdot V_{sat} \cdot \beta$.
- **Snap-action condition:** $A_{OL} \cdot \beta \geq 1$ — guarantees bistable operation.
- **741 Op-Amp typical:** $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5$ V with $\pm 15$ V supply.
- **Noise immunity** is the **key engineering advantage** of the Schmitt Trigger over the basic comparator.
- **Inverting Schmitt:** input goes to the inverting terminal; output is in anti-phase with the input trend.
- **Non-Inverting Schmitt:** input goes to the non-inverting terminal; output is in phase with the input trend.
- **VTC must show a rectangular hysteresis loop** with width $V_H$ — this is a guaranteed 2-mark item in KTU 14-mark questions.
- **For asymmetric thresholds:** include $V_{ref}$ term; threshold shifts by $V_{ref} \cdot R_2 / (R_2 + R_3)$.
- **Applications to memorize for KTU:** ADC, ZCD, signal conditioning, PWM, window comparator, relaxation oscillator (when combined with RC).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schmitt Triggers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Schmitt Triggers**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Schmitt Trigger (Definition – KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> A **Schmitt trigger** is a regenerative comparator circuit with **positive feedback** (also called a *Schmitt trigger circuit* or *regenerative comparator*). Unlike a simple op-amp comparator that switches state at a single threshold, the Schmitt trigger exhibits **hysteresis** — a deliberate gap between the **Upper Threshold Point (UTP)** and the **Lower Threshold Point (LTP)**. This dual-threshold behavior makes the circuit immune to noisy input transitions, providing clean, sharp, square-wave outputs even when the input signal is slow, noisy, or analog in nature.

**Syllabus Highlight:** Per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCECT403 – Module 2), the Schmitt Trigger is classified under the family of *wave-shaping and wave-generating circuits using op-amps* and is built using the **IC 741** op-amp as the standard reference device.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **light switch in a dark room**:
- A normal switch turns ON when the room gets even slightly bright, and OFF when it gets slightly dim — *annoying, flickers constantly*.
- A **Schmitt-triggered switch** is smarter: it turns ON only when the room is **really dark** (e.g., below 10 lux), and turns OFF only when the room is **really bright** (e.g., above 100 lux). The "in-between" zone is ignored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hysteresis Gap = The "Ignore Zone."**
> This deliberate dead-zone is the heart of a Schmitt trigger. It eliminates **chattering** (false triggering) due to noise, slow signals, or small fluctuations around the threshold — making the output a clean, sharp digital square wave.

In electrical terms:
- **UTP** $\rightarrow$ The input level that forces the output to switch to its **HIGH** state ($+V_{sat}$).
- **LTP** $\rightarrow$ The input level that forces the output to switch to its **LOW** state ($-V_{sat}$).
- **Hysteresis Voltage ($V_H$)** $\rightarrow$ The dead-band between UTP and LTP, $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

For a standard **IC 741 op-amp** with dual supply $\pm 15\,\text{V}$:
- **Output Saturation Voltage:** $V_{sat} \approx \mathbf{\pm 13.5\,V}$ (typical, due to internal transistor drop)
- **Idealized $V_{sat}$** (assumed in most KTU numericals): $V_{sat} = \mathbf{\pm V_{CC} = \pm 15\,V}$
- **Open-Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$):** $\mathbf{2 \times 10^5}$ (typical)
- **Slew Rate:** $\mathbf{0.5\,V/\mu s}$ (for 741)
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** $\mathbf{90\,dB}$
- **Input Offset Voltage:** $\mathbf{2\,mV}$ (negligible in Schmitt trigger design)

> [!NOTE]
> In **KTU exam problems**, unless otherwise stated, assume $V_{sat} = \pm V_{CC}$ (ideal) to simplify threshold calculations. Always read the problem statement carefully to identify whether **±13.5 V** or **±15 V** is to be used.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Schmitt Triggers

| **Type** | **Input Applied To** | **Feedback To** | **Output Polarity vs. Input** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | Inverting terminal (–) | Non-inverting terminal (+) | **Inverted** (HIGH when input below LTP) |
| **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | Non-inverting terminal (+) | Inverting terminal (–) | **Non-inverted** (HIGH when input above UTP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite:** The **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** is the most commonly asked circuit in KTU university exams because it allows symmetric threshold design and clean hysteresis analysis. Be thorough with its derivation.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Positive Feedback? — The "Why" Behind the Design

A **standard open-loop op-amp comparator** has one major flaw: when the input signal hovers near the threshold, even a tiny amount of noise causes the output to **oscillate** rapidly between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$. This is unacceptable in digital systems.

The **Schmitt trigger** solves this by introducing **positive (regenerative) feedback**:
- Once the output begins to swing toward $+V_{sat}$, the feedback reinforces the swing → the output *snaps* to $+V_{sat}$ quickly.
- The same applies for a swing toward $-V_{sat}$.
- The **threshold itself depends on the output state**, creating the hysteresis gap.

> The "regenerative" nature means the switching action is **self-accelerating**, producing nearly **vertical transition edges** on the output.

---

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Inverting Schmitt Trigger

**Circuit Topology (Mental Picture):**
- Input $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting (–) terminal** of the op-amp.
- A resistor **$R_1$** connects output to the **non-inverting (+) terminal** (feedback).
- A resistor **$R_2$** connects the non-inverting (+) terminal to **ground**.
- The voltage at the (+) terminal is a **voltage divider** of the output, often called $V_{+}$ or $V_{ref}$.

**State 1: Output is HIGH (i.e., $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$)**

- The non-inverting terminal sees: $V_{+} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot (+V_{sat}) = \mathbf{+V_{UTP}}$
- Output stays HIGH **as long as** $V_{in} < V_{UTP}$.
- The moment $V_{in}$ exceeds $V_{UTP}$, the op-amp flips → $V_{out} \rightarrow -V_{sat}$.

**State 2: Output is LOW (i.e., $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$)**

- The non-inverting terminal sees: $V_{+} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot (-V_{sat}) = \mathbf{-V_{UTP}}$
- Output stays LOW **as long as** $V_{in} > -V_{UTP}$.
- The moment $V_{in}$ falls below $-V_{UTP}$, the op-amp flips → $V_{out} \rightarrow +V_{sat}$.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are HIGH-YIELD for KTU 2024 university exams. Memorize them.**

| **Parameter** | **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger** |
|---|---|---|
| **Upper Threshold ($V_{UTP}$)** | $+\beta \cdot V_{sat} = +\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ |
| **Lower Threshold ($V_{LTP}$)** | $-\beta \cdot V_{sat} = -\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $-\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ |
| **Feedback Factor ($\beta$)** | $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ (different topology) |
| **Hysteresis Voltage ($V_H$)** | $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \dfrac{2 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $V_H = \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ |
| **Output State Equation** | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ if $V_{in} < V_{LTP}$; $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ if $V_{in} > V_{UTP}$ | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ if $V_{in} > V_{UTP}$; $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ if $V_{in} < V_{LTP}$ |
| **Transfer Characteristic** | Clockwise (inverting) loop | Anti-clockwise (non-inverting) loop |

> **Note on notation:** Some textbooks use $\beta = R_1/R_2$ (inverted) for non-inverting triggers. **Verify the configuration** from the circuit diagram in your KTU question paper before substituting values.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Application Area** | **Use of Schmitt Trigger** |
|---|---|
| **Square Wave / Function Generators** | Forms the heart of the **astable multivibrator** (with an RC timing network). |
| **Wave Shaping** | Converts **sine / triangular** waves into clean **square waves**. |
| **Debouncing Circuits** | Cleans mechanical **switch / relay bounce** in digital inputs. |
| **Zero-Crossing Detectors** | Used in **line voltage sensing** with a defined noise margin. |
| **Pulse Conditioning** | Restores distorted **digital pulses** in communication lines. |
| **Temperature / Light Sensing** | Provides **on-off control** with built-in noise immunity. |
| **555 Timer IC** | Internally uses two **Schmitt trigger comparators** for thresholding. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** Almost every digital input pin in microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino, STM32) internally incorporates a **Schmitt trigger** for noise immunity. This is why the same MCU can reliably read a noisy sensor signal at 5 V logic levels.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Threshold Voltages

**Given:** Op-amp 741 with saturation voltages $\pm V_{sat}$. Input $V_{in}$ to inverting terminal. Feedback through $R_1$ from output to non-inverting terminal. Resistor $R_2$ from non-inverting terminal to ground.

**Step 1: Apply KCL at the non-inverting (+) terminal**

Since the ideal op-amp draws no input current at the (+) terminal, the sum of currents into the node must equal zero:

$$
\frac{V_{+} - V_{out}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{+} - 0}{R_2} = 0
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $V_{+}$ in terms of $V_{out}$**

Multiply through by $R_1 R_2$:

$$
R_2 (V_{+} - V_{out}) + R_1 (V_{+}) = 0
$$

Expand:

$$
R_2 V_{+} - R_2 V_{out} + R_1 V_{+} = 0
$$

Group $V_{+}$ terms:

$$
V_{+} (R_1 + R_2) = R_2 V_{out}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{+} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{out}
$$

**Step 3: Identify the switching condition**

The op-amp switches output state precisely when $V_{in} = V_{+}$ (since input is at the inverting terminal, $V_{out}$ flips when $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{+}$).

**Step 4: Case A — Output is at $+V_{sat}$**

Substitute $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$:

$$
V_{UTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot (+V_{sat}) = +\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**Step 5: Case B — Output is at $-V_{sat}$**

Substitute $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$:

$$
V_{LTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot (-V_{sat}) = -\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**Step 6: Hysteresis Voltage**

$$
V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2} - \left(-\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}\right) = \frac{2 R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** The Schmitt trigger is **symmetric about zero** for the inverting topology — the UTP and LTP are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign. This symmetry is a key assumption in many KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger

**Given:** Input $V_{in}$ applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal. Feedback from output through $R_1$ to the inverting (–) terminal. Resistor $R_2$ from inverting terminal to ground.

**Step 1: Apply KCL at the inverting (–) terminal**

Ideal op-amp → no current into (–) terminal:

$$
\frac{V_{-} - V_{out}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{-} - 0}{R_2} = 0
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $V_{-}$**

Multiply by $R_1 R_2$:

$$
R_2 (V_{-} - V_{out}) + R_1 V_{-} = 0
$$

$$
V_{-} (R_1 + R_2) = R_2 V_{out}
$$

$$
V_{-} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{out}
$$

**Step 3: Switching condition**

For the non-inverting configuration, the op-amp switches when $V_{in} = V_{-}$ (i.e., when the input at (+) crosses the divider voltage at (–)).

**Step 4: Case A — Output is at $+V_{sat}$**

$$
V_{UTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot (+V_{sat}) = +\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**Step 5: Case B — Output is at $-V_{sat}$**

$$
V_{LTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot (-V_{sat}) = -\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Caveat:** The above formulation assumes the input goes directly to the (+) terminal. In some KTU problems, a **separate input resistor $R_3$** is present at the (–) terminal, which changes the formula to $V_{UTP} = \pm \dfrac{R_2}{R_3} V_{sat}$ (a much sharper threshold). **Always redraw the circuit and re-derive** for the exact topology given.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** An inverting Schmitt trigger uses $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, with op-amp saturation voltage $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$. A triangular input of amplitude $\pm 10\,\text{V}$ and $1\,\text{kHz}$ frequency is applied. Find the UTP, LTP, hysteresis, and output duty cycle.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute UTP**

$$
V_{UTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat} = \frac{5\,\text{k}}{10\,\text{k} + 5\,\text{k}} \cdot (+12) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 12 = +4\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 2: Compute LTP**

$$
V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat} = -4\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Hysteresis**

$$
V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 4 - (-4) = 8\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Duty Cycle**

For a symmetric triangular wave (50% rise time, 50% fall time), the Schmitt trigger produces a **symmetric square wave** because UTP and LTP are symmetric about zero. Therefore:

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = 50\% \quad (\text{square wave})
$$

**Step 5: Output Frequency**

Since each half-cycle of the input triangle triggers one transition, the output is a **square wave at the same $1\,\text{kHz}$ frequency**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question:** If the triangular input is **asymmetric** (e.g., $T_{rise} = 2\,T_{fall}$), the duty cycle is **not 50%**. You must compute the time spent above UTP and below LTP separately and take the ratio.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| **Situation** | **Recommended Schmitt Trigger** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Input signal has noise around the threshold | **Inverting (with hysteresis)** | Provides symmetric noise immunity on both sides |
| Need to preserve signal polarity (HIGH = HIGH) | **Non-inverting** | Output is in phase with input |
| Input current is high (source has low impedance) | **Inverting** | Input goes to (–) terminal; high-impedance virtual ground |
| Threshold must be asymmetric ($V_{UTP} \neq \vert V_{LTP} \vert$) | **Inverting with zener / reference** | Add a reference voltage to the (+) terminal |
| Generating square wave from sine wave | **Inverting** | Standard textbook and exam problem |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Block Diagram — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vin["INPUT V_in"]:::inputNode
    R1["FEEDBACK RESISTOR R1"]:::fbResistor
    Amp["OP-AMP 741"]:::opAmpCore
    OutNode["OUTPUT V_out"]:::outputNode
    R2["RESISTOR R2 TO GROUND"]:::gndResistor
    Gnd["GROUND 0V"]:::groundRef
    VplusRef["V_plus NODE"]:::refNode

    Vin -- "to inverting minus terminal" --> Amp
    R1 -- "feedback path" --> VplusRef
    Amp -- "drives" --> OutNode
    VplusRef -- "to non inverting plus terminal" --> Amp
    R2 -- "pull down to ground" --> VplusRef
    R2 --- Gnd
    OutNode -- "feeds back" --> R1

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fbResistor fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef opAmpCore fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef outputNode fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gndResistor fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef groundRef fill:#8B4513,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef refNode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Yellow = Input source; Orange = Reference/feedback node; Red = Feedback resistor $R_1$; Purple = Ground resistor $R_2$; Blue = Op-amp 741; Green = Output node.

---

### 4.2 Transfer Characteristic — Hysteresis Loop (Inverting)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["INPUT V_in LESS THAN V_LTP"] --> B["OUTPUT V_out = PLUS V_sat HIGH"]
    A1["V_in INCREASES ABOVE V_UTP"] --> C["OUTPUT SWITCHES TO MINUS V_sat LOW"]
    B --> A1
    C --> D["V_out REMAINS LOW as V_in DECREASES"]
    D --> E["V_in DROPS BELOW V_LTP"]
    E --> F["OUTPUT SWITCHES BACK TO PLUS V_sat HIGH"]
    F --> B
    A1 -.-> C

    classDef stateHi fill:#00FF00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stateLo fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef switchHi fill:#FFFF00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef switchLo fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A stateHi
    class B stateHi
    class C stateLo
    class D stateLo
    class E switchLo
    class F switchHi
    class A1 switchHi
```

> **Reading the loop:** As $V_{in}$ sweeps upward, the output stays HIGH until $V_{in} = V_{UTP}$, then snaps to LOW. As $V_{in}$ sweeps downward, the output stays LOW until $V_{in} = V_{LTP}$, then snaps to HIGH. This **clockwise loop** is the signature of the inverting Schmitt trigger.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wave Shaping Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    SineStage["STAGE 1: SINE WAVE INPUT f equals 1 kHz"]:::stageOne
    ThreshBlock["STAGE 2: SCHMITT TRIGGER COMPARES V_in WITH V_plus HYSTERESIS BAND"]:::stageTwo
    SwitchDec["STAGE 3: REGENERATIVE POSITIVE FEEDBACK ACCELERATES EDGE TRANSITION"]:::stageThree
    SqrOut["STAGE 4: CLEAN SQUARE WAVE OUTPUT PLUS MINUS V_sat SAME FREQUENCY"]:::stageFour
    NoiseBlock["STAGE 5: NOISE BAND INSIDE V_H DEAD ZONE IS REJECTED"]:::stageFive

    SineStage --> ThreshBlock
    ThreshBlock --> SwitchDec
    SwitchDec --> SqrOut
    ThreshBlock -.-> NoiseBlock
    NoiseBlock -.-> ThreshBlock

    classDef stageOne fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stageTwo fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stageThree fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stageFour fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stageFive fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Pipeline Reading:** A sine wave is fed into the Schmitt trigger. The hysteresis comparison block decides the output state. Regenerative feedback sharpens the edges. Noise within the $V_H$ dead-band is ignored. The final output is a clean square wave at the same fundamental frequency.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a Schmitt trigger. Why is it also called a regenerative comparator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Schmitt trigger is a comparator circuit with **positive feedback** that exhibits **hysteresis**, i.e., two different threshold levels (UTP and LTP) for rising and falling inputs. It is called a **regenerative comparator** because the positive feedback reinforces any small change at the input, causing the output to **regenerate** (accelerate) rapidly toward the saturation level. This produces sharp, noise-immune switching transitions, unlike a simple open-loop comparator.

> *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for hysteresis, 1 mark for regenerative feedback explanation.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. What is hysteresis voltage in a Schmitt trigger? How is it related to the UTP and LTP?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **hysteresis voltage ($V_H$)** is the voltage difference between the **Upper Threshold Point ($V_{UTP}$)** and the **Lower Threshold Point ($V_{LTP}$)**. It is given by:

$$
V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}
$$

It represents the **dead-band** of input voltages for which the output does **not** change state. A larger $V_H$ implies greater noise immunity but also a larger required input swing to cause switching. For a symmetric inverting Schmitt trigger, $V_H = \dfrac{2 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module 2]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **inverting Schmitt trigger** using an op-amp. Derive the expressions for **UTP, LTP, and Hysteresis voltage**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an inverting Schmitt trigger, $R_1 = 15\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5\,\text{V}$. A triangular wave of peak amplitude $\pm 10\,\text{V}$ is applied. **Sketch the output waveform** and calculate the **duty cycle**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Circuit Diagram (Textual Description):**
- Op-amp 741 with $V_{in}$ applied to the **inverting (–) terminal** through input resistor.
- $R_1$ connected between the **output** and the **non-inverting (+) terminal** (positive feedback).
- $R_2$ connected between the **non-inverting (+) terminal** and **ground**.

**Operation Logic:**

1. The voltage at the non-inverting terminal is set by the voltage divider of $R_1$ and $R_2$ acting on $V_{out}$:

$$
V_{+} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{out}
$$

2. When $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$, $V_{+} = +V_{UTP} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$.
3. The output remains at $+V_{sat}$ as long as $V_{in} < V_{UTP}$.
4. When $V_{in}$ crosses above $V_{UTP}$, the differential input becomes negative, and the op-amp switches to $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$.
5. Now $V_{+} = -V_{UTP} = -\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \cdot V_{sat} = V_{LTP}$.
6. The output remains at $-V_{sat}$ until $V_{in}$ falls below $V_{LTP}$.

**Threshold Derivation (already shown in Section 3.1):**

$$
V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}, \quad V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

$$
V_H = \frac{2 R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $R_1 = 15\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1: Compute UTP**

$$
V_{UTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat} = \frac{5}{15 + 5} \cdot 13.5 = \frac{5}{20} \cdot 13.5 = 0.25 \cdot 13.5 = +3.375\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 2: Compute LTP**

$$
V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat} = -3.375\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Hysteresis**

$$
V_H = 3.375 - (-3.375) = 6.75\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 4: Symmetry Analysis**

Since $V_{UTP} = -V_{LTP}$ (symmetric thresholds), and the input triangular wave is **symmetric about zero** (peak $\pm 10\,\text{V}$), the time spent above $+3.375\,\text{V}$ equals the time spent below $-3.375\,\text{V}$.

**Step 5: Duty Cycle**

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{HIGH}}{T_{total}} = 50\%
$$

**Step 6: Output Waveform Description**

- The output is a **square wave** alternating between $+13.5\,\text{V}$ and $-13.5\,\text{V}$.
- **HIGH** ($+13.5\,\text{V}$) when $V_{in} < V_{LTP} = -3.375\,\text{V}$.
- **LOW** ($-13.5\,\text{V}$) when $V_{in} > V_{UTP} = +3.375\,\text{V}$.
- Same frequency as input triangle.

**Output Sketch (Schematic Description):**
A square wave that switches from $-13.5\,\text{V}$ to $+13.5\,\text{V}$ at $V_{in} = -3.375\,\text{V}$ (rising triangle), and switches back to $-13.5\,\text{V}$ at $V_{in} = +3.375\,\text{V}$ (falling triangle).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **non-inverting Schmitt trigger** with a circuit diagram. Derive the expressions for UTP and LTP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a non-inverting Schmitt trigger to generate a square wave from a sine wave of amplitude $5\,\text{V}$ at $1\,\text{kHz}$, with hysteresis voltage of $6\,\text{V}$. Use op-amp with $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Circuit Topology:**
- Input $V_{in}$ applied to the **non-inverting (+) terminal**.
- $R_1$ from output to the **inverting (–) terminal** (feedback).
- $R_2$ from **inverting (–) terminal** to **ground**.

**Operation:**

1. The voltage at the inverting terminal is a divider of $V_{out}$:

$$
V_{-} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{out}
$$

2. The op-amp switches when $V_{in} = V_{-}$:
   - If $V_{in} > V_{-} \rightarrow V_{out} \rightarrow +V_{sat}$.
   - If $V_{in} < V_{-} \rightarrow V_{out} \rightarrow -V_{sat}$.

3. **UTP** (output switches from LOW to HIGH):

$$
V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}
$$

4. **LTP** (output switches from HIGH to LOW):

$$
V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}
$$

5. **Transfer characteristic:** Anti-clockwise loop (non-inverting).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $V_H = 6\,\text{V}$, $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$, $V_{in,peak} = 5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1: Recall the hysteresis formula for non-inverting Schmitt trigger**

$$
V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \frac{2 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
6 = \frac{2 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot 12
$$

**Step 2: Solve for the resistor ratio**

$$
6 = \frac{24 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

$$
R_1 + R_2 = 4 R_2
$$

$$
R_1 = 3 R_2
$$

**Step 3: Choose a standard value**

Let $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, then $R_1 = 30\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 4: Verify the threshold voltages**

$$
V_{UTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat} = \frac{10}{30 + 10} \cdot 12 = \frac{10}{40} \cdot 12 = 0.25 \cdot 12 = +3\,\text{V}
$$

$$
V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat} = -3\,\text{V}
$$

$$
V_H = 3 - (-3) = 6\,\text{V} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Verify with input signal**

The $5\,\text{V}$ sine peak **exceeds** $V_{UTP} = 3\,\text{V}$, so the output switches correctly to $+V_{sat}$ at the positive peak and to $-V_{sat}$ at the negative peak. Design is valid.

**Final Design:** $R_1 = 30\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** in $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$ — many students write $V_{UTP} = V_{sat}$ (without the $\beta$ factor) and lose 2-3 marks instantly.
>
> 2. **Confusing the feedback factor $\beta$** — for the **inverting** Schmitt trigger, $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ (voltage divider across $R_2$ to ground). For the **non-inverting** variant with a separate input resistor, the formula changes. Always re-derive from KCL on the actual circuit shown.
>
> 3. **Wrong assumption for $V_{sat}$** — if the question says $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5\,\text{V}$, do NOT use $\pm 15\,\text{V}$. Read the question's "given" values carefully.
>
> 4. **Not drawing the hysteresis loop** in the transfer characteristic — this is worth **at least 2 marks** in any 7-mark sub-question. Always sketch it.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the direction of switching** — clearly state whether the output is HIGH when $V_{in}$ is above or below the threshold, based on the configuration (inverting vs. non-inverting).
>
> 6. **Forgetting to specify the duty cycle** when asked for a square wave generator — if the input is asymmetric, the duty cycle is NOT 50%.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **Definition:** Schmitt trigger = comparator + positive feedback = hysteresis.
> - **Two key thresholds:** $V_{UTP}$ (Upper) and $V_{LTP}$ (Lower). The gap is $V_H$.
> - **Inverting Schmitt Trigger:** Input at (–) terminal. $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$, $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$, where $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.
> - **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger:** Input at (+) terminal. Same formula for $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$ if topology is symmetric; different for split-resistor configurations.
> - **Hysteresis Formula:** $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \dfrac{2 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ (inverting, standard topology).
> - **Transfer Characteristic:**
>   - *Inverting:* Clockwise hysteresis loop (output LOW when input HIGH, and vice versa).
>   - *Non-inverting:* Anti-clockwise loop (output follows input polarity).
> - **Op-amp 741 standard:** $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5\,\text{V}$ (typical) or $\pm V_{CC}$ (ideal in problems).
> - **Applications:** Square wave generation, wave shaping, switch debouncing, 555 timer internals, line voltage zero-crossing detectors, and digital signal conditioning.
> - **Why positive feedback?** Regenerative action → snap action → clean, fast, noise-immune switching.
> - **Design tip:** To **increase** $V_H$ (more noise immunity) → increase $R_2$ or decrease $R_1$.
> - **Design tip:** To **decrease** $V_H$ (more sensitivity) → decrease $R_2$ or increase $R_1$.
> - **Frequency relation:** For triangular input, output square wave has the **same fundamental frequency**.
> - **Duty cycle:** 50% for symmetric triangular input + symmetric Schmitt trigger. **Not** 50% for asymmetric inputs.
> - **Key visualization:** Always draw the **hysteresis loop** on the $V_{in}$–$V_{out}$ axis. This is worth 2+ marks.
> - **KTU favourite circuit:** Inverting Schmitt trigger with a clean triangular wave input — practice this combination.
> - **Related Module 2 topic (for integration):** The Schmitt trigger is the building block of the **astable multivibrator** (square wave generator) and the **monostable multivibrator** (one-shot) — both covered in subsequent KTU modules.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Log and Antilog Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Log and Antilog Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Log Amplifier (Logarithmic Amplifier):** A non-linear analog signal-processing circuit whose output voltage is *proportional to the natural logarithm* of the input voltage, achieved by exploiting the exponential current–voltage (I–V) characteristic of a PN-junction diode or a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) placed in the negative-feedback path of an operational amplifier.
>
> **Antilog Amplifier (Exponential Amplifier):** A circuit that performs the inverse operation — its output voltage is an *exponential function* (antilogarithm) of the input voltage, constructed by placing the diode/BJT in the *input* path of the inverting op-amp configuration.

The standard mathematical relationship is:
$$V_{o} = -K \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}\right)$$
where $K$ is the scaling factor (Volts/decade) and $V_{ref}$ is a reference voltage.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Volume Compression Studio:**
> Imagine a sound engineer in a recording studio. The human ear and musical instruments produce an enormous *dynamic range* — a whisper may be a million times quieter than a thunderclap. If we simply amplified linearly, the recording would either be too quiet (whispers lost) or distort (loud sounds clip). The engineer uses a **logarithmic compressor**: large signals are "squeezed" down, small signals are gently lifted. After transmission, the listener's amplifier acts as an **antilog expander**, restoring the original dynamic range.
>
> **Log amplifier = Compressor**   |   **Antilog amplifier = Expander**
>
> The PN-junction diode inside the op-amp loop acts as a "natural logarithmic gatekeeper" — it converts every decade of input current into a *fixed, equal step* of output voltage (~60 mV/decade at room temperature).

**Real-World Engineering Applications:**
* **Analog multipliers and dividers** (Log–Antilog technique) — multiply two signals by adding their logs: $V_{out} \propto \log^{-1}(\log V_1 + \log V_2)$.
* **Signal compression** in communication systems (companding — A-law, μ-law).
* **True RMS detection** and **dB meters** in test instruments.
* **V-to-I / I-to-V converters** in instrumentation.
* **Function generators** and **analog computation**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Memorize for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> * **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{26\ mV}$ at $T = 300\ K$ (room temperature)
> * **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
> * **Electronic Charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
> * **Typical Saturation Current:** $I_s \approx 10^{-12}\ \text{A}$ to $10^{-15}\ \text{A}$
> * **Forward Diode Drop:** $V_D \approx 0.6\text{–}0.7\ \text{V}$ (silicon)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Exponential I–V Characteristic — The Heart of the Circuit

Both the log and antilog amplifiers rely on the **Shockley diode equation** (or its BJT counterpart). For a forward-biased PN-junction diode with $V_D \gg V_T$:
$$I_D = I_s \!\left(e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1\right) \;\approx\; I_s\, e^{V_D / \eta V_T}$$

For a BJT in the *transdiode* connection (base shorted to collector, emitter at output), the collector current follows:
$$I_C = I_s\, e^{V_{BE} / V_T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BJT over Diode?** A BJT offers a far wider dynamic range (typically **6 decades**, 1 nA – 1 mA) compared to a simple diode (only **2–3 decades**). The BJT's $V_{BE}$ varies from ~0.4 V to ~0.7 V for this range, providing a more linear logarithmic relationship.

---

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why & How"

**Log Amplifier (Inverting Configuration with PN Element in Feedback):**
1. Input signal $V_{in}$ is applied through a series resistor $R_1$ to the inverting node.
2. The non-inverting input is grounded.
3. Virtual ground at the inverting node forces **all input current** to flow through the feedback element (diode/BJT).
4. The exponential I–V law of the feedback element **forces the output voltage to be the logarithm** of the input current.

**Antilog Amplifier (PN Element in Input Path, Resistor in Feedback):**
1. The input voltage $V_{in}$ drives a diode (or BJT base–emitter) directly.
2. By the exponential law, the resulting current is $I = I_s\, e^{V_{in}/V_T}$.
3. This current is forced by virtual ground to flow through the feedback resistor $R_f$.
4. The output voltage becomes the **exponential (antilog)** of the input.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the core of every KTU numerical/logical question.**

| **Sl. No.** | **Parameter / Quantity** | **Expression** | **Remarks** |
|:-----------:|:-------------------------|:---------------|:------------|
| 1 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = kT / q$ | $V_T \approx 26\ \text{mV}$ at $300\ \text{K}$ |
| 2 | Diode Current (Ideal) | $I_D = I_s\, e^{V_D / V_T}$ | For $V_D \gg V_T$ |
| 3 | BJT Collector Current | $I_C = I_s\, e^{V_{BE} / V_T}$ | Transdiode connection |
| 4 | Log Amplifier Output (Diode) | $V_o = -V_T \ln\!\left(V_{in} / (R_1 I_s)\right)$ | $V_{in} > 0$ for forward bias |
| 5 | Log Amplifier Output (BJT) | $V_o = -V_T \ln\!\left(V_{in} / (R_1 I_s)\right)$ | Wider dynamic range |
| 6 | Log Amp — Base-10 Form | $V_o = -2.303\,V_T \log_{10}\!\left(V_{in} / (R_1 I_s)\right)$ | Standard form for design |
| 7 | Antilog Amplifier Output | $V_o = -R_f I_s\, e^{V_{in} / V_T}$ | $V_{in} > 0$ |
| 8 | Antilog Amp — Base-10 Form | $V_o = -R_f I_s\, \left(10^{V_{in} / 2.303\,V_T}\right)$ | Useful for design |
| 9 | Output Temperature Drift (Slope) | $\partial V_o / \partial T = (V_o / T) - (1.15\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C})$ | Dominant term is $V_o/T$ |
| 10 | Compensated Output (Matched BJT) | $V_o = -\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}\right)$ | Built-in cancellation |
| 11 | Log Conformance Error | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{BE(actual)} - V_{BE(ideal)}}{V_{T}}$ | Ideally 0; typical $\pm 1$% |

> **Conversion Cheat:** $\ln(x) = 2.302585\,\log_{10}(x)$. Hence, $V_T \cdot \ln(\cdot) = 2.302585\,V_T \cdot \log_{10}(\cdot) \approx 59.6\ \text{mV} \cdot \log_{10}(\cdot)$ at $300\ \text{K}$.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

In KTU's outcome-based education framework, this topic maps to **CO2** (Analyze and design non-linear applications of op-amps). The log/antilog pair is the **foundation of analog computation** — any product, quotient, root, or power can be implemented with two log amps, one summing amplifier, and one antilog amp. For example, the analog multiplier:
$$V_1 \times V_2 = \log^{-1}\!\left(\log V_1 + \log V_2\right)$$

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Log Amplifier Using a Diode (Foundational)

**Circuit Topology:** Inverting op-amp with input resistor $R_1$ in series with input $V_{in}$, and a forward-biased diode $D$ in the feedback path between the output and the inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the Inverting (Virtual-Ground) Node.**

Since the op-amp is in the linear region with negative feedback, the inverting input is at *virtual ground* ($V^- = 0$).

**Step 2 — Express the Input Current.**

The current entering the inverting node from the source is:
$$I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 3 — Express the Feedback (Diode) Current.**

The diode is forward-biased (anode at virtual ground, cathode at $V_o$). Therefore, the voltage across the diode is:
$$V_D = V_{anode} - V_{cathode} = 0 - V_o = -V_o$$

By Shockley's equation:
$$I_D = I_s\, e^{V_D / V_T} = I_s\, e^{-V_o / V_T}$$

**Step 4 — Apply KCL (Sum of Currents into Virtual Ground = 0).**

Since the op-amp draws negligible input current:
$$I_{in} = I_D \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} = I_s\, e^{-V_o / V_T}$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $V_o$.**

$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s} = e^{-V_o / V_T}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right) = -\dfrac{V_o}{V_T}$$

**Step 6 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{\,V_o = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right)\,}$$

This proves the output is proportional to the **natural logarithm** of the input voltage. Note: $V_{in}$ must be **positive** to keep the diode forward-biased.

**Step 7 — Convert to Base-10 Form for Design.**

Using $\ln(x) = 2.302585\,\log_{10}(x)$:
$$V_o = -2.302585\,V_T \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right)$$

At $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$:
$$V_o \approx -0.0599\ \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right)\ \text{V}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Log Amplifier Using a BJT (Transdiode Configuration)

**Why BJT is Better:** The diode's series resistance and surface leakage limit accuracy. A BJT in the **transdiode connection** (base tied to collector, forming a 2-terminal device) follows the *ideal* exponential law over 6 decades with predictable $I_s$.

**Circuit Topology:** Resistor $R_1$ at the input, BJT $Q_1$ in feedback with collector connected to the inverting input (virtual ground) and emitter to the output $V_o$. Base is also tied to the collector (transdiode).

**Step 1 — Identify the Operating Condition.**

The collector–base short ensures $V_{BC} = 0$, so the BJT operates in the **forward-active region** with $V_{BE} > 0$. The base is at virtual ground, so the emitter voltage is:
$$V_E = V_o \quad \text{(since } V_{BE} = V_B - V_E = 0 - V_o = -V_o\text{)}$$

Wait — careful: $V_{BE} = V_B - V_E = 0 - V_o = -V_o$. But the BJT emitter is *below* the base for $V_o < 0$, which is exactly what we want for forward-bias.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node.**

$$I_{in} = I_C = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply BJT Exponential Law.**

$$I_C = I_s\, e^{V_{BE} / V_T} = I_s\, e^{-V_o / V_T}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Solve.**

$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} = I_s\, e^{-V_o / V_T} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_o = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right)$$

**The final expression is identical in form to the diode case**, but it is far more accurate over a wider input range.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Antilog (Exponential) Amplifier

**Circuit Topology:** Diode $D$ in the input path (anode to $V_{in}$, cathode to virtual ground), feedback resistor $R_f$ between output and inverting node.

**Step 1 — Voltage Across the Diode.**

Since the inverting node is at virtual ground:
$$V_D = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Diode Current.**

$$I_D = I_s\, e^{V_{in} / V_T}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the Virtual-Ground Node.**

This current flows through $R_f$ from the virtual ground to the output:
$$V_o = 0 - I_D \cdot R_f = -R_f I_s\, e^{V_{in} / V_T}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{\,V_o = -R_f I_s\, e^{V_{in} / V_T}\,}$$

Equivalently, in base-10 form:
$$V_o = -R_f I_s \cdot 10^{\,V_{in} / (2.302585\,V_T)}$$

The negative sign indicates the *inverting* nature. Since $V_{in} > 0$ is required for diode forward bias, $V_o$ is negative and grows in *magnitude* exponentially with $V_{in}$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Temperature Drift Analysis

**The Problem:** $V_T = kT/q$ depends on temperature, and $I_s$ also varies with $T$ as $I_s \propto T^3 \exp(-V_{G0}/V_T)$. Hence, the output drifts with temperature — a major design challenge.

**Step 1 — Differentiate the Output Equation with Respect to Temperature.**

$$V_o = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{\partial V_o}{\partial T} = -\ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right) \dfrac{\partial V_T}{\partial T} - V_T \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\partial V_T / \partial T = V_T / T$ and the $I_s$ temperature law.**

After simplification (see standard texts), the dominant term is:
$$\dfrac{\partial V_o}{\partial T} \approx \dfrac{V_o}{T} - \dfrac{V_{G0} - (4 + n)V_T}{T}$$

At $T = 300\ \text{K}$, the second term evaluates to $\approx -1.15\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Step 3 — Compensation Strategy.**

A matched BJT pair (one as feedback element, one in a temperature-compensation network) cancels the $V_T$ term, leaving only a small residual drift.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Diode Log Amp** | **BJT (Transdiode) Log Amp** | **Antilog Amp** |
|:-------------|:------------------|:------------------------------|:----------------|
| **Element in feedback** | Diode | BJT (C–B shorted) | Resistor $R_f$ |
| **Element in input** | Resistor $R_1$ | Resistor $R_1$ | Diode / BJT |
| **Dynamic Range** | $\sim 2$ decades | $\sim 6$ decades ($\sim 1\ \text{nA}$ – $1\ \text{mA}$) | $\sim 4$ decades |
| **Polarity of $V_{in}$** | Positive only | Positive only | Positive only |
| **Polarity of $V_o$** | Negative | Negative | Negative |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | High | High (needs compensation) | High (needs compensation) |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate (diode non-idealities) | High (matched BJT) | Moderate to high |
| **Typical Application** | Simple compression | Precision instrumentation | Multipliers, RMS, dB meters |
| **Key Equation** | $V_o = -V_T \ln(V_{in}/R_1 I_s)$ | $V_o = -V_T \ln(V_{in}/R_1 I_s)$ | $V_o = -R_f I_s\, e^{V_{in}/V_T}$ |

### 4.4 Signal Processing Flow — Log/Antilog Pair for Analog Multiplication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAA[Input V1 to Log Amp 1] --> nodeAB[Log V1]
    nodeAC[Input V2 to Log Amp 2] --> nodeAD[Log V2]
    nodeAB --> nodeAE[Summing Amplifier: Log V1 plus Log V2]
    nodeAD --> nodeAE
    nodeAE --> nodeAF[Sum equals Log of V1 times V2]
    nodeAF --> nodeAG[Antilog Amplifier: Exponential Conversion]
    nodeAG --> nodeAH[Output: V1 times V2]
    style nodeAA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    nodeAC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeAC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeAB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style nodeAG fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeAH fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note:** The signal flow above demonstrates the classic *Log-Antilog* technique for analog multiplication: $\log V_1 + \log V_2 = \log(V_1 \cdot V_2)$, followed by $\text{antilog}(\log(V_1 \cdot V_2)) = V_1 \cdot V_2$. Division is achieved by *subtraction* at the summing node, and roots/powers are obtained by appropriate scaling.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a logarithmic amplifier. With a neat circuit diagram, derive the output voltage expression for a log amplifier using a diode in the feedback path of an op-amp. State any two limitations of the basic log amplifier.**

**Model Answer:**

A log amplifier is a non-linear op-amp circuit that produces an output voltage proportional to the logarithm of the input voltage, exploiting the exponential I–V characteristic of a PN-junction diode (or BJT) placed in the negative feedback loop.

The standard derivation proceeds as follows. In the inverting configuration, with input $V_{in}$ applied through resistor $R_1$ and a diode in the feedback path, the inverting node is at virtual ground. The input current $I_{in} = V_{in}/R_1$ flows entirely through the diode. By Shockley's equation:
$$I_D = I_s\, e^{V_D / V_T}, \quad V_D = -V_o$$

Equating currents and solving:
$$V_o = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right)$$

This output is the **natural logarithm** of the input.

**Limitations of the basic log amplifier:**
1. The diode operates over a limited current range (only 2–3 decades) before series resistance and leakage degrade the log linearity. *Loss of 1 mark if not stated.*
2. Both $V_T$ and $I_s$ are temperature-dependent, causing significant output drift. *Loss of 1 mark if not stated.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**List any three applications of log and antilog amplifiers. What is the significance of using a BJT in transdiode configuration in a log amplifier?**

**Model Answer:**

**Applications:**
1. **Analog Multiplication and Division** — using the property $\log V_1 + \log V_2 = \log(V_1 \cdot V_2)$.
2. **Signal Compression in Communication Systems** — companding in PCM telephony.
3. **True RMS Measurement and dB Meters** — converting linear signals to decibel scale.

**Significance of BJT Transdiode Configuration:**
* The collector–base shorted BJT provides a near-ideal exponential $I_C$–$V_{BE}$ relationship over **6 decades** of current, vastly superior to a discrete diode (2–3 decades).
* The BJT exhibits better temperature stability, predictable $I_s$, and immunity from surface leakage currents, making it the preferred choice in precision log amplifiers.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign of $V_{in}$:** Students frequently forget that $V_{in}$ must be **positive** for forward bias. The output is **negative**. Drawing an inverting log amp with $V_{in} < 0$ loses **1 mark** instantly.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch in Final Equation:** Forgetting to convert $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ when the problem uses decibels or decades. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Neglecting $V_T$ in Numerical Problems:** At $300\ \text{K}$, $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$. Substituting $V_T = 1\ \text{V}$ (a common error) leads to absurd answers. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Forgetting the Negative Sign:** The inverting configuration *always* produces a negative output. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Polarity of $I_s$:** Always positive; do not insert a negative sign in numerical calculations.

---

### 5.2.1 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q (a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**
**With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a log amplifier using a BJT in transdiode configuration. Derive the expression for the output voltage. Discuss any two limitations of this circuit.**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:**

```
           R1
   VIN ──/\/\/──┬────────── (Virtual GND = 0V)
                │          │
                │         ┌┴┐
                │      B  │ │ Q1 (Transdiode: B-C shorted)
                │      ┌──┤ │   ↑
                │      │  └┬┘   │
                │      │   └────┤
                │      │        │    ┌─────┐
                └──────┴────────┴────┤  −  ├──── VOUT
                                      │ OpAmp│
                          ───────┬────┤  +  ├
                                  │    └─────┘
                                 GND
```

**Operation:**

In the inverting configuration, the BJT $Q_1$ is connected with its base and collector shorted (transdiode). The non-inverting input is grounded. The inverting input is held at virtual ground by negative feedback.

**Derivation of Output Voltage:**

The input current through $R_1$ is:
$$I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

By KCL, this current equals the collector current of $Q_1$:
$$I_C = I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

For a BJT in forward active region, the collector current follows the exponential law:
$$I_C = I_s\, e^{V_{BE} / V_T}$$

Since the base is at virtual ground ($V_B = 0$) and the emitter is at $V_o$:
$$V_{BE} = V_B - V_E = 0 - V_o = -V_o$$

Substituting:
$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} = I_s\, e^{-V_o / V_T}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:
$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right) = -\dfrac{V_o}{V_T}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_o = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right)\,}$$

This shows the output is proportional to the natural logarithm of the input.

**Limitations:**
1. **Temperature Sensitivity:** Both $V_T$ and $I_s$ vary with temperature, causing output drift. Compensation using matched transistor pairs or thermistor networks is required for precise operation.
2. **Limited Bandwidth:** The op-amp's gain-bandwidth product and the BJT's junction capacitance restrict the high-frequency response, making the circuit suitable primarily for low-frequency DC and audio-band applications.

*(Alternative valid limitation: low input impedance, requirement of bipolar supply, sensitivity to op-amp offset voltage.)*

---

**Q (b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**
**Derive the output voltage expression for an antilog amplifier using a diode in the input path. Given $R_f = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$, $I_s = 10^{-14}\ \text{A}$, and $V_{in} = 0.3\ \text{V}$, find the output voltage at $T = 300\ \text{K}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

The antilog amplifier uses a diode in the input path and a resistor $R_f$ in the feedback path. The inverting node is at virtual ground.

The voltage across the diode equals the input voltage:
$$V_D = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$$

The current through the diode is:
$$I_D = I_s\, e^{V_{in} / V_T}$$

This current flows through $R_f$, producing an output:
$$V_o = -I_D \cdot R_f = -R_f I_s\, e^{V_{in} / V_T}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_o = -R_f I_s\, e^{V_{in} / V_T}\,}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
* $R_f = 100\ \text{k}\Omega = 10^5\ \Omega$
* $I_s = 10^{-14}\ \text{A}$
* $V_{in} = 0.3\ \text{V}$
* $T = 300\ \text{K} \Rightarrow V_T = kT/q \approx 26\ \text{mV} = 0.026\ \text{V}$

**Step 1 — Compute the exponent:**
$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_T} = \dfrac{0.3}{0.026} \approx 11.5385$$

**Step 2 — Compute $e^{V_{in}/V_T}$:**
$$e^{11.5385} \approx 1.032 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the output:**
$$V_o = -R_f I_s\, e^{V_{in} / V_T} = -(10^5)(10^{-14})(1.032 \times 10^{5})$$

$$V_o = -(10^5 \times 10^{-14} \times 1.032 \times 10^{5})$$

$$V_o = -(1.032 \times 10^{-4})\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_o \approx -0.1032\ \text{mV} = -103.2\ \mu\text{V}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign indicates the inverting nature. The very small magnitude is due to the low $I_s$ — typically, $V_{in}$ values in the range 0.4–0.7 V are used to get outputs in the millivolt to volt range.

---

### 5.2.2 QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**Q (a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**Design a log amplifier using a BJT such that an input of $V_{in} = 1\ \text{V}$ produces an output of $V_o = -0.5\ \text{V}$. Assume $I_s = 10^{-15}\ \text{A}$ and $V_T = 25\ \text{mV}$. Determine the value of the input resistor $R_1$. Also comment on the effect of temperature on the output.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
* $V_{in} = 1\ \text{V}$
* $V_o = -0.5\ \text{V}$
* $I_s = 10^{-15}\ \text{A}$
* $V_T = 25\ \text{mV} = 0.025\ \text{V}$

**Equation:**
$$V_o = -V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 I_s}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the values:**
$$-0.5 = -0.025 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 \times 10^{-15}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**
$$\dfrac{-0.5}{-0.025} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 \times 10^{-15}}\right)$$
$$20 = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 \times 10^{-15}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the exponential function:**
$$e^{20} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$4.85 \times 10^{8} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 \times 10^{-15}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_1$:**
$$R_1 \times 10^{-15} = \dfrac{1}{4.85 \times 10^{8}} = 2.06 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$R_1 = \dfrac{2.06 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-15}} = 2.06 \times 10^{6}\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{\,R_1 \approx 2.06\ \text{M}\Omega\,}$$

**Effect of Temperature:**

The output $V_o$ is a function of both $V_T$ and $I_s$, both of which depend on temperature:
* $V_T = kT/q$ increases linearly with $T$ (about $+0.085\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ at $300\ \text{K}$).
* $I_s$ increases approximately *doubling every $10\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$* rise, following the empirical relation $I_s = I_{s0} \cdot T^{3-n} \cdot \exp(-V_{G0}/V_T)$.

The net effect is a **drift in output** of approximately **$\pm 1\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$**. Without compensation, the log amplifier can give a $5$–$10$% error over a $50\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ temperature range. **Temperature compensation** using a matched BJT pair (current-mirror arrangement) cancels the $V_T$ term, reducing drift to about $0.01\ \text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$.

---

**Q (b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**Explain with a block diagram how log and antilog amplifiers can be used to perform analog multiplication of two signals $V_1$ and $V_2$. What modification is needed to perform division?**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram for Multiplication:**

