\omega^2 R_2 R_3 C_1 C_2} $$ **Step 5:** Comparing with the standard second-order BPF transfer function: $$ H(s) = \frac{H_0 \cdot (s/\omega_0)}{1 + s/(Q\omega_0) + (s/\omega_0)^2} $$ we extract: $$ f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_2 R_3 C_1 C_2}} $$ $$ A_0 = -\frac{R_2 C_1}{R_1 C_2} $$ $$ Q = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{R_3 C_1}{R_2 C_2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_3 C_1 / (R_2 C_2) + R_3 C_1/(R_2 C_2) + \dots}} $$ For a simplified symmetric case ($C_1 = C_2 = C$, $R_2 \ne R_3$): $$ Q = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{R_3}{R_2}} $$ **Part (b) — Design:** **Step 1:** Given $f_0 = 2000$ Hz, $Q = 5$, $|A_0| = 8$. **Step 2:** Choose $C_1 = C_2 = C = 10$ nF (standard value). **Step 3:** Using $Q = (1/2)\sqrt{R_3/R_2}$: $$ \sqrt{\frac{R_3}{R_2}} = 10 \Rightarrow \frac{R_3}{R_2} = 100 $$ **Step 4:** Using $f_0 = 1/(2\pi C \sqrt{R_2 R_3})$: $$ \sqrt{R_2 R_3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7958 \ \Omega $$ **Step 5:** Solve: $$ R_2 R_3 = 7958^2 = 6.33 \times 10^7 $$ With $R_3 = 100 R_2$: $$ R_2 = \sqrt{\frac{6.33 \times 10^7}{100}} = \sqrt{6.33 \times 10^5} \approx 796 \ \Omega \quad (\text{use } 820 \ \Omega) $$ $$ R_3 = 100 \times 796 = 79.6 \text{ k}\Omega \quad (\text{use } 82 \text{ k}\Omega) $$ **Step 6:** From $|A_0| = R_2/R_1$ (since $C_1 = C_2$): $$ R_1 = \frac{R_2}{8} = \frac{796}{8} \approx 100 \ \Omega $$ **Final Design:** $R_1 = 100\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 820\ \Omega$, $R_3 = 82\text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = C_2 = 10$ nF. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` **(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the operation of a Twin-T notch filter. Derive the expression for its notch frequency. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Twin-T notch filter is designed with $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 4.7$ nF. Find the notch frequency. If the filter uses a non-inverting op-amp with gain $A_v = 0.95$, calculate the quality factor $Q$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Operation and Derivation:** **Circuit Operation:** A Twin-T (Parallel-T) notch filter consists of two T-networks connected in parallel between input and output. The Top-T has two series resistors of value $R$ each and a shunt capacitor of value $2C$. The Bottom-T has two series capacitors of value $C$ each and a shunt resistor of value $R/2$. At the **notch frequency** $f_0$, the two T-networks produce *equal amplitude* but *opposite phase* signals, causing complete cancellation at the output. This produces a sharp null. **Derivation:** The combined transfer function of the passive Twin-T is: $$ H(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1 + s^2 R^2 C^2}{1 + 4sRC + 2s^2 R^2 C^2} $$ Setting the numerator to zero: $$ 1 + s^2 R^2 C^2 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -\frac{1}{R^2 C^2} $$ Substituting $s = j\omega$: $$ \omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{R^2 C^2} \Rightarrow \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} $$ $$ \boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}} $$ **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:** **Step 1:** Notch frequency: $$ f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ f_0 = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 4.7 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{2.953 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \boxed{f_0 \approx 3386 \text{ Hz} \approx 3.39 \text{ kHz}} $$ **Step 2:** Quality factor with $A_v = 0.95$: $$ Q = \frac{1}{4(1 - A_v)} = \frac{1}{4(1 - 0.95)} = \frac{1}{4 \times 0.05} $$ $$ Q = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5 $$ **Step 3:** Verify stability margin: $A_v < 1$, so the circuit is stable. If $A_v$ approached 1, $Q$ would tend to infinity but the circuit would become unstable (oscillate). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Sign confusion in MFB gain:** The MFB BPF is *inverting*, so $A_0$ has a **negative sign**. Students often drop the sign and lose 1 mark. > 2. **Geometric vs Arithmetic mean:** $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$, NOT $(f_L + f_H)/2$. Using the arithmetic mean loses 2 marks. > 3. **Twin-T feedback formula:** Only valid for $A_v < 1$ (non-inverting gain below unity). If $A_v = 1$, the op-amp saturates. If $A_v > 1$, the circuit oscillates. > 4. **MFB Q formula simplification:** Full formula is $Q = (1/2)\sqrt{R_3/R_2}\cdot\sqrt{C_1/C_2}\cdot$ [correction factor]. For equal C values, it reduces to $(1/2)\sqrt{R_3/R_2}$. Don't blindly use the simplified version when $C_1 \ne C_2$. > 5. **Units mismatch:** $R$ must be in ohms, $C$ in farads, $f$ in Hz. Students often forget and get $f$ in rad/s, losing 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam):** - **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** → Passes a *band*, rejects outside. **Band Reject (BRF/Notch)** → Rejects a *band*, passes outside. They are *duals* of each other. - **Centre frequency** $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$ (geometric mean). Never use the arithmetic mean. - **Bandwidth** $BW = f_H - f_L$. **Quality factor** $Q = f_0 / BW$. - **Wide-band BPF** = cascade of HPF + LPF. Wide-band BRF = LPF and HPF in subtract-mode at a summing amp. - **Narrow-band BPF** = Multiple Feedback (MFB) topology with single op-amp, two capacitors, three resistors. MFB is *inverting*. - **MFB formulae:** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{R_2 R_3 C_1 C_2})$, $A_0 = -(R_2/R_1)(C_1/C_2)$, $Q = (1/2)\sqrt{R_3/R_2}\cdot\sqrt{C_1/C_2}$. - **Narrow-band BRF (Notch)** = Twin-T (Parallel-T) topology with op-amp. Centre frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$. - **Notch Q with op-amp feedback:** $Q = 1/[4(1-A_v)]$. Stability requires $A_v < 1$. - **Twin-T passive Q** (no op-amp) is only **0.25** — op-amp feedback is mandatory for sharp notch. - **$-3$ dB points** define the cutoff frequencies. **$-40$ dB/decade** roll-off for second-order filters. - **Passband gain** of cascade wide BPF = product of LPF and HPF individual gains: $A_0 = A_{LPF} \cdot A_{HPF}$. - **Decoupling rule:** Always insert a buffer (voltage follower) between cascaded stages to prevent loading. - **Biomedical use:** 50 Hz Twin-T notch removes mains hum from ECG. - **Telecom use:** MFB narrow BPF selects the IF channel in superheterodyne receivers. - **Audio use:** BPF in graphic equalizers; BRF (notch) in PA anti-feedback systems. - **Remember:** In KTU problems, *always state the formula before substituting numbers*. Examiners award 1 mark for correct formula statement even if computation has a small error.

topics.title: State Variable Filters, Voltage Regulators: Fixed and Adjustable Voltage Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** State Variable Filters, Voltage Regulators: Fixed and Adjustable Voltage Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 State Variable Filters (Biquadratic / Universal Filters)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
A **State Variable Filter** (also called a *universal active filter* or *three-op-amp biquad*) is a second-order active filter topology built using two integrators and one summing amplifier that simultaneously provides **Low-Pass (LPF)**, **High-Pass (HPF)**, and **Band-Pass (BPF)** outputs from a single input. The filter is described by a **biquadratic transfer function** of the form:

$$H(s) = \frac{N(s)}{s^2 + \left(\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}\right)s + \omega_0^2}$$

where **$\omega_0$** is the natural (resonant) frequency in **rad/s**, and **$Q$** is the quality factor (a dimensionless selectivity parameter).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The state variable filter is a "**universal filter**" because the *same circuit* produces LPF, HPF, and BPF outputs simultaneously. By adding a fourth summing stage, a **Band-Stop (Notch)** output is also obtainable.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a state variable filter as an **audio equalizer inside a single chip**:
- Imagine you have a raw music signal (mixed frequencies) coming in.
- A normal LPF only lets bass through, and a normal HPF only lets treble through — you would need **three separate circuits**.
- A state variable filter is like having **three speakers in one box** — the **woofer** (LPF), **tweeter** (HPF), and **mid-range speaker** (BPF) — all driven by the same internal logic from a single source. The "state" of the system is captured by the **integrator outputs** (the capacitor voltages), which is why it is called a "state-variable" configuration.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Cut-off / Center Frequency** $f_0 = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi}$ — measured in **Hz**
- **Quality Factor $Q$** — dimensionless; higher $Q$ implies a sharper (more selective) response
- **Gain Magnitude $K$** — dimensionless
- **Standard KTU design assumption:** matched integrators, $R_1C_1 = R_2C_2 = RC$, giving $\omega_0 = 1/RC$

---

## 1.2 Voltage Regulators: Fixed and Adjustable

### Formal Definition
A **voltage regulator** is an electronic circuit that maintains a **constant output voltage** $V_{out}$ irrespective of variations in the input voltage $V_{in}$, the load current $I_L$, or the ambient temperature $T_A$. Integrated-circuit (IC) regulators combine a reference voltage, an error amplifier, a pass transistor, and protection circuitry in a single package.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Principal Classes (per KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> 1. **Fixed Voltage Regulators** — output is set internally and cannot be changed (e.g., 78xx, 79xx series).
> 2. **Adjustable Voltage Regulators** — output can be programmed by an external resistor divider (e.g., LM317, LM337).

### Intuitive Analogy
A voltage regulator is like a **water-tap with built-in pressure control**:
- Even if the municipal supply pressure surges (analogous to input voltage variation) or you open multiple taps downstream (load current variation), the water coming out of *your* tap remains at a steady flow.
- A **fixed regulator** is a tap locked at one flow setting (say, 5 V); an **adjustable regulator** is a tap with a knob (the external resistor $R_2$) that lets you choose any flow between, say, 1.25 V and 37 V.

### Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Definitions)
- **Line Regulation:** $\dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}}$ — output change per unit input change (typically in **mV/V**)
- **Load Regulation:** $\dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L}$ — output change per unit load change (typically in **mV/mA** or %)
- **Ripple Rejection (PSRR):** attenuation of input ripple at the output, in **dB**
- **Dropout Voltage:** minimum $(V_{in} - V_{out})$ required to maintain regulation (≈ **2 V** for standard 78xx/79xx; ≈ **1.5 V** for LM317)
- **Quiescent Current $I_Q$:** current drawn by the regulator itself (typically **5–10 mA**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 State Variable Filter — Operational Breakdown

The 3-op-amp biquad (often attributed to **Kerwin–Huelsman–Newcomb**, 1967) consists of three functional blocks:

### Block A — Summing Amplifier (Inverting Summer)
The summer adds the scaled input signal with a negative feedback from the LPF output. With feedback resistor $R_F$, input resistor $R_a$ (from $V_{in}$), and feedback resistor $R_b$ (from $V_{LP}$):

$$V_{O1} = -\left(\frac{R_F}{R_a}\right) V_{in} - \left(\frac{R_F}{R_b}\right) V_{LP}$$

### Block B — Lossless Integrator #1
Realises an inverting integrator that produces the **Band-Pass** output:

$$V_{BP} = -\frac{1}{sR_1C_1}\, V_{O1}$$

### Block C — Lossless Integrator #2
Cascades another integrator on $V_{BP}$ to obtain the **Low-Pass** output:

$$V_{LP} = -\frac{1}{sR_2C_2}\, V_{BP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the configuration works:** The first integrator is an *inverting* integrator, and the second integrator inverts the signal again. The double integration yields a phase shift that, combined with the summer's negative feedback, creates a stable second-order resonant loop. The HPF output is obtained by taking the difference between $V_{O1}$ and $V_{LP}$ at a fourth summer, or by direct algebraic manipulation of the summer.

### The 'Why' Behind Each Design Parameter
- **$\omega_0$ depends on $R_1C_1R_2C_2$** because the natural frequency is set by the time constants of the two cascaded integrators.
- **$Q$ depends on $R_b/R_F$** because $Q$ controls how much the LPF output is fed back to the summer; more feedback → higher $Q$ (sharper resonance).
- **$K$ depends on $R_F/R_a$** because $K$ is set by how strongly the input is summed into the loop.

---

## 2.2 Voltage Regulators — Operational Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Fixed Positive Regulator (78xx Series) — e.g., 7805, 7812, 7815
The 78xx IC internally contains:
1. A **zener-based bandgap reference** (1.25 V or so, scaled up via on-chip resistor divider).
2. An **error amplifier** that compares a fraction of $V_{out}$ with the internal reference.
3. A **series pass transistor** (Darlington, NPN) that sinks/sources current to maintain $V_{out}$.
4. **Protection circuits**: current limiting, thermal shutdown, and SOA protection.

> [!NOTE]
> The "xx" in 78xx denotes the output voltage. **7805 → 5 V, 7812 → 12 V, 7815 → 15 V.** The required input voltage is typically $V_{in} \geq V_{out} + 2\,\text{V}$ to keep the pass transistor in its linear (active) region.

### 2.2.2 Fixed Negative Regulator (79xx Series) — e.g., 7905, 7912
Mirror of 78xx in PNP-based topology, with the **output and ground pins effectively swapped**. Used alongside 78xx in dual-supply op-amp circuits.

### 2.2.3 Adjustable Positive Regulator (LM317)
A floating regulator — the reference is developed between the **OUTPUT** and **ADJUST** pins:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ}\cdot R_2$$

- $V_{ref} = 1.25\,\text{V}$ (typical)
- $I_{ADJ} \approx 50\,\mu\text{A}$ (often neglected in hand calculations)
- $R_1$ is conventionally chosen as **240 $\Omega$**
- $V_{out}$ can be set anywhere from **1.25 V to 37 V**
- Maximum load current ≈ **1.5 A** (TO-220 package)

### 2.2.4 Adjustable Negative Regulator (LM337)
The negative-output counterpart of the LM317. The output equation has a negative sign:

$$V_{out} = -V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|-------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | State Variable — LPF | $H_{LP}(s) = -K\omega_0^2 / \left[s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2\right]$ | $K$ = passband gain, $Q$ = quality factor |
| 2 | State Variable — HPF | $H_{HP}(s) = -K s^2 / \left[s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2\right]$ | $s$ = complex frequency (rad/s) |
| 3 | State Variable — BPF | $H_{BP}(s) = -K(\omega_0/Q)s / \left[s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2\right]$ | Magnitude peaks at $s = j\omega_0$ |
| 4 | Natural Frequency (matched) | $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$ | $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F, $\omega_0$ in rad/s |
| 5 | Quality Factor | $Q = R_b / R_F$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Passband Gain | $K = R_F / R_a$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Design Check | $R_1C_1 = R_2C_2 = RC$ | matched for symmetric response |
| 8 | Line Regulation | $LR = \Delta V_{out} / \Delta V_{in}$ | in **mV/V** |
| 9 | Load Regulation | $LdR = \Delta V_{out} / \Delta I_L$ | in **mV/mA** |
| 10 | 78xx / 79xx Output | $V_{out} = V_{xx}$ (fixed by IC) | e.g., $V_{out} = +5$ V for 7805 |
| 11 | LM317 Output | $V_{out} = V_{ref}(1 + R_2/R_1) + I_{ADJ} R_2$ | $V_{ref} = 1.25$ V |
| 12 | LM337 Output | $V_{out} = -V_{ref}(1 + R_2/R_1)$ | negative polarity |
| 13 | Power Dissipation | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) I_L + V_{in} I_Q$ | $I_Q \approx 5$–$10$ mA |
| 14 | Junction Temp (no sink) | $T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}$ | $\theta_{JA}$ in **°C/W** |
| 15 | Junction Temp (with sink) | $T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot (\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA})$ | $\theta$ in **°C/W** |
| 16 | Heat-Sink Required | $\theta_{SA} = (T_{J,max} - T_A)/P_D - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$ | in **°C/W** |
| 17 | Ripple Rejection | $PSRR = 20 \log_{10}(V_{ripple,in}/V_{ripple,out})$ | in **dB** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In formula 11, do NOT drop the $I_{ADJ} R_2$ term without justification in full-mark derivations. Examiners often award a **partial mark (1 mark)** for stating the term, even when neglected later.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **State Variable Filters** are the workhorse of audio crossovers, telecommunications tone-detection, and biomedical signal processing (e.g., extracting the QRS complex from an ECG).
- **Fixed 78xx/79xx Regulators** are ubiquitous on every hobbyist Arduino/Raspberry Pi board (often the 7805 for the 5 V rail).
- **LM317/LM337** are found in laboratory bench power supplies, battery chargers, and motor-speed control circuits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the State Variable Filter Transfer Function (LPF Output)

Let the two integrators be matched: $R_1C_1 = R_2C_2 = RC$. Let the summer's feedback resistor be $R_F$, the input resistor be $R_a$, and the LPF-feedback resistor be $R_b$.

### Step 1 — Write the summer's output equation
The summer is an inverting adder, so the output of the summer $V_{O1}$ is:

$$V_{O1} = -\frac{R_F}{R_a} V_{in} - \frac{R_F}{R_b} V_{LP}$$

**Textual Note:** The negative signs come from the inverting configuration. $R_F$ is the feedback resistor of the op-amp; the input $V_{in}$ enters through $R_a$ and the LPF output feeds back through $R_b$.

### Step 2 — Write the first integrator's equation
Integrator #1 takes $V_{O1}$ and outputs $V_{BP}$:

$$V_{BP} = -\frac{1}{sR_1C_1}\, V_{O1} = -\frac{1}{sRC}\, V_{O1}$$

**Textual Note:** The factor $1/(sRC)$ is the impedance of the capacitor divided into the input resistor, characteristic of an inverting integrator.

### Step 3 — Write the second integrator's equation
Integrator #2 takes $V_{BP}$ and outputs $V_{LP}$:

$$V_{LP} = -\frac{1}{sR_2C_2}\, V_{BP} = -\frac{1}{sRC}\, V_{BP}$$

### Step 4 — Cascade the integrators
Multiply Step 2 and Step 3:

$$V_{LP} = \left(-\frac{1}{sRC}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{sRC}\right) V_{O1} = \frac{1}{s^2 R^2 C^2} V_{O1}$$

**Textual Note:** The two negative signs cancel, giving a *positive* relation between $V_{LP}$ and $V_{O1}$. This is the source of the "double-inversion" behaviour that makes the loop stable.

### Step 5 — Substitute $V_{O1}$ from Step 1
$$V_{LP} = \frac{1}{s^2 R^2 C^2}\left[-\frac{R_F}{R_a} V_{in} - \frac{R_F}{R_b} V_{LP}\right]$$

### Step 6 — Multiply both sides by $s^2 R^2 C^2$ and collect terms
$$s^2 R^2 C^2 \cdot V_{LP} = -\frac{R_F}{R_a} V_{in} - \frac{R_F}{R_b} V_{LP}$$

Rearrange to group $V_{LP}$ on the left-hand side:

$$s^2 R^2 C^2 \cdot V_{LP} + \frac{R_F}{R_b} V_{LP} = -\frac{R_F}{R_a} V_{in}$$

Factor $V_{LP}$:

$$V_{LP}\left[s^2 R^2 C^2 + \frac{R_F}{R_b}\right] = -\frac{R_F}{R_a} V_{in}$$

### Step 7 — Divide numerator and denominator by $R^2 C^2$
$$\frac{V_{LP}}{V_{in}} = \frac{-\dfrac{R_F}{R_a}}{R^2 C^2\left[s^2 + \dfrac{R_F}{R_b}\cdot\dfrac{1}{R^2 C^2}\right]}$$

**Textual Note:** Pulling $R^2 C^2$ out of the denominator is a normalisation step to expose the standard second-order form.

Simplify the denominator's $s$-coefficient:

$$\frac{V_{LP}}{V_{in}} = \frac{-\dfrac{R_F}{R_a} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R^2 C^2}}{s^2 + \dfrac{R_F}{R_b}\cdot \dfrac{1}{RC}\cdot s}$$

### Step 8 — Identify $\omega_0$, $Q$, and $K$ from the standard form
The standard LPF form is:

$$H_{LP}(s) = \frac{-K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \left(\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}\right) s + \omega_0^2}$$

Matching coefficients:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_0^2 &= \frac{1}{R^2 C^2} \quad &\Rightarrow\quad \omega_0 &= \frac{1}{RC} \quad &\text{(natural frequency, rad/s)} \\
\frac{\omega_0}{Q} &= \frac{R_F}{R_b}\cdot \frac{1}{RC} \quad &\Rightarrow\quad \frac{1}{Q} &= \frac{R_F}{R_b} \quad &\Rightarrow\quad Q = \frac{R_b}{R_F} \\
K &= \frac{R_F}{R_a} \quad &\text{(passband gain)} &
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 9 — Write the final LPF transfer function
$$\boxed{\,H_{LP}(s) = \frac{V_{LP}}{V_{in}} = \frac{-\left(\dfrac{R_F}{R_a}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{R^2 C^2}}{s^2 + \left(\dfrac{R_F}{R_b}\right)\dfrac{s}{RC} + \dfrac{1}{R^2 C^2}} = \frac{-K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q) s + \omega_0^2}\,}$$

### Step 10 — Obtain BPF and HPF expressions
By analogous derivations (or by inspection of the integrator outputs):

$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{V_{BP}}{V_{in}} = \frac{-K (\omega_0/Q) \, s}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q) s + \omega_0^2}$$

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{V_{HP}}{V_{in}} = \frac{-K s^2}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q) s + \omega_0^2}$$

**Textual Note:** The BPF peaks at $s = j\omega_0$ (i.e., at $f_0$). The HPF passes high frequencies and rolls off below $f_0$.

---

## 3.2 Design Example — State Variable Filter

**Specification:** Design a 2nd-order state-variable LPF with $f_0 = 1\,\text{kHz}$, $Q = 5$, BPF gain $K = 2$.

**Given:** $f_0 = 1\,\text{kHz} \Rightarrow \omega_0 = 2\pi \times 10^3 = 6283.19\,\text{rad/s}$

**Step 1 — Choose a convenient capacitor** $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}\,\text{F}$ (standard value).

**Step 2 — Compute the integrator resistor**
$$R = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = \frac{1}{6283.19 \times 10^{-8}} = 15{,}915.5\,\Omega \approx 15.9\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Use the standard value $R = 15.9\,\text{k}\Omega$ (or the closest E12 value $15\,\text{k}\Omega$ with slight $f_0$ correction).

**Step 3 — Choose a summer feedback resistor.** Pick $R_F = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (any reasonable value between 10 k$\Omega$ and 100 k$\Omega$ works).

**Step 4 — Compute $R_b$ for the desired $Q = 5$**
$$R_b = Q \cdot R_F = 5 \times 10\,\text{k}\Omega = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_a$ for the desired $K = 2$**
$$R_a = \frac{R_F}{K} = \frac{10\,\text{k}\Omega}{2} = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Result:**

| Component | Value | Purpose |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| $R_1 = R_2$ | 15.9 k$\Omega$ | Integrator resistors |
| $C_1 = C_2$ | 0.01 $\mu$F | Integrator capacitors |
| $R_F$ | 10 k$\Omega$ | Summer feedback |
| $R_a$ | 5 k$\Omega$ | Input gain setting |
| $R_b$ | 50 k$\Omega$ | $Q$ setting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Substitute back: $K = 10/5 = 2$ ✓, $Q = 50/10 = 5$ ✓, $f_0 = 1/(2\pi \cdot 15915 \cdot 10^{-8}) \approx 1\,\text{kHz}$ ✓.

---

## 3.3 LM317 Output Voltage Derivation

**Specification:** Design a regulator using LM317 to obtain $V_{out} = +15\,\text{V}$ supplying $I_L = 1\,\text{A}$. Compute heat-sink requirements.

**Step 1 — Recall the LM317 output equation**
$$V_{out} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} R_2$$

with $V_{ref} = 1.25\,\text{V}$, $I_{ADJ} \approx 50\,\mu\text{A}$.

**Step 2 — Choose $R_1$ (conventionally 240 $\Omega$ from datasheet)**
This is the standard KTU-prescribed starting point. Then:

$$15 = 1.25\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{240}\right) + (50 \times 10^{-6}) R_2$$

**Step 3 — Neglect the $I_{ADJ} R_2$ term** (valid since $50\,\mu\text{A} \times 2.6\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 0.13\,\text{V}$, < 1% of $V_{out}$):
$$15 = 1.25\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{240}\right)$$

$$\frac{15}{1.25} = 1 + \frac{R_2}{240} \quad\Rightarrow\quad 12 - 1 = \frac{R_2}{240}$$

$$R_2 = 11 \times 240 = 2640\,\Omega \approx 2.64\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Including the $I_{ADJ} R_2$ term for higher accuracy:**
$$15 = 1.25 + \frac{1.25 \cdot R_2}{240} + (50 \times 10^{-6}) R_2$$

$$13.75 = R_2 \left(\frac{1.25}{240} + 50 \times 10^{-6}\right)$$

$$13.75 = R_2 (0.005208 + 0.000050) = R_2 (0.005258)$$

$$R_2 = \frac{13.75}{0.005258} = 2615.7\,\Omega \approx 2.62\,\text{k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two values differ by less than 1%. For KTU exams, the simpler formula (neglecting $I_{ADJ}$) is acceptable unless the question explicitly demands the higher-order correction.

**Step 5 — Power dissipation calculation**

Assume $V_{in} = 20\,\text{V}$ (typical design, ensuring dropout margin of $20 - 15 = 5\,\text{V} > 1.5\,\text{V}$ dropout):
$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) I_L + V_{in} I_Q$$

Take $I_Q = 5\,\text{mA}$ (datasheet typical for LM317):
$$P_D = (20 - 15)(1) + (20)(0.005) = 5 + 0.1 = 5.1\,\text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Heat-sink requirement**

LM317 in TO-220 package: $\theta_{JC} = 5\,\text{°C/W}$ (junction to case), $\theta_{CS} = 1\,\text{°C/W}$ (case to sink, with thermal grease), $T_{J,max} = 125\,\text{°C}$.

Worst-case ambient $T_A = 50\,\text{°C}$:

$$\theta_{SA} = \frac{T_{J,max} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$$

$$\theta_{SA} = \frac{125 - 50}{5.1} - 5 - 1 = 14.71 - 6 = 8.71\,\text{°C/W}$$

**Conclusion:** A heat sink with thermal resistance $\theta_{SA} \leq 8.71\,\text{°C/W}$ is required. Standard commercially available extruded-aluminium heat sinks of $5\,\text{°C/W}$–$8\,\text{°C/W}$ rating (e.g., Aavid 577002) will suffice.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

| Feature | 78xx (Fixed +ve) | 79xx (Fixed –ve) | LM317 (Adj +ve) | LM337 (Adj –ve) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output polarity | Positive | Negative | Positive | Negative |
| Output range | Fixed (xx V) | Fixed (–xx V) | 1.25 V to 37 V | –1.25 V to –37 V |
| Reference | Internal | Internal | 1.25 V (float) | 1.25 V (float) |
| Dropout voltage | ~ 2 V | ~ 2 V | ~ 1.5 V | ~ 1.5 V |
| Max output current | 1 A (typ) | 1 A (typ) | 1.5 A | 1.5 A |
| Adjustable? | No | No | Yes (via $R_1$, $R_2$) | Yes (via $R_1$, $R_2$) |
| Required external parts | 2 caps | 2 caps | 2 resistors + 2 caps | 2 resistors + 2 caps |
| KTU pin reference | 1=Input, 2=GND, 3=Output | 1=GND, 2=Input, 3=Output | 1=ADJ, 2=Output, 3=Input | 1=ADJ, 2=Input, 3=Output |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Op-Amp State Variable Filter — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vin[Vin Input] -->|via Ra| Summer
    VLP[LPF Output Vlp] -->|via Rb| Summer
    Summer[Op-Amp A1 SUMMING STAGE] -->|Vo1| Int1
    Int1[Op-Amp A2 LOSSY INTEGRATOR 1] -->|Vbp| Int2
    Int2[Op-Amp A3 LOSSY INTEGRATOR 2] -->|Vlp| VLP
    Int1 -->|Vbp Output| VBP[BPF Output Vbp]
    VLP -->|Vlp Output| VLPOut[LPF Output Vlp]
    HPFSum[Op-Amp A4 INVERTING SUMMER] --> VHP[HPF Output Vhp]
    Summer -->|Vo1| HPFSum
    VLP -->|Vlp| HPFSum
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Op-Amp A1 (Summer):** Sums the scaled input $V_{in}$ (through $R_a$) with the LPF feedback $V_{LP}$ (through $R_b$).
- **Op-Amp A2 (Integrator 1):** Inverts and integrates $V_{O1}$ to give $V_{BP}$ (the Band-Pass output).
- **Op-Amp A3 (Integrator 2):** Inverts and integrates $V_{BP}$ to give $V_{LP}$ (the Low-Pass output).
- **Op-Amp A4 (HPF Summer):** Sums $V_{O1}$ and $V_{LP}$ with appropriate signs to produce the High-Pass output.

---

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a 78xx Fixed Voltage Regulator

```mermaid
graph TB
    Vin[Input Pin Vin] --> Pass[Series Pass Transistor Darlington NPN]
    Pass --> Vout[Output Pin Vout]
    Vout --> Ref[Bandgap Reference 1.25V]
    Vout --> Div[Resistor Divider R1 R2]
    Div --> Err[Error Amplifier]
    Ref -->|Vref| Err
    Err -->|Drive Signal| Pass
    Vout --> Prot[Protection Block]
    Prot -->|Thermal| Pass
    Prot -->|Current Limit| Pass
    GND[Ground Pin] --> Ref
    GND --> Err
    GND --> Prot
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **bandgap reference** establishes an internal 1.25 V (or scaled equivalent) reference.
- The **resistor divider** samples a fraction of $V_{out}$ and feeds it to the **error amplifier**.
- The error amplifier compares the feedback voltage with the reference and drives the **series pass transistor** to maintain $V_{out}$ constant.
- The **protection block** monitors junction temperature and load current; it shuts down the pass transistor under fault conditions.

---

## 4.3 LM317 Adjustable Regulator — Standard Application Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vin[DC Input Vin] -->|via Cin| LM317
    Cin[Bypass Capacitor 0.1uF] -->|in parallel| LM317
    LM317[LM317 Adjustable Regulator] --> Vout[DC Output Vout]
    LM317 -->|Pin ADJ| R1
    R1[Resistor R1 240 ohm] --> GND
    LM317 -->|Pin ADJ| R2
    R2[Resistor R2 sets Vout] --> Vout
    Cout[Output Capacitor 1uF] -->|in parallel| Vout
    Cout -->|to ground| GND[Ground]
    R2 --> Cout
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- $V_{in}$ is applied to the **INPUT** pin; a **0.1 $\mu$F bypass capacitor** $C_{in}$ is placed close to the IC to suppress high-frequency oscillations.
- The **OUTPUT** pin produces $V_{out}$.
- A fixed resistor $R_1 = 240\,\Omega$ is connected between the **ADJUST** pin and ground.
- A variable/programmed resistor $R_2$ is connected between the **ADJUST** pin and the **OUTPUT** pin — this is what sets the output voltage.
- A **1 $\mu$F tantalum** output capacitor $C_{out}$ improves transient response (improves ripple rejection by ~15 dB).
- The fundamental relationship is $V_{out} = 1.25 (1 + R_2/R_1)$.

---

## 4.4 Voltage Regulator with Heat Sink — Thermal Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    Junction[Silicon Junction Tj max 125C] -->|theta JC 5C per W| Case
    Case[Metal Case] -->|theta CS 1C per W| Sink
    Sink[Heat Sink] -->|theta SA 8.71C per W| Ambient
    Ambient[Ambient Air TA 50C]
    Pdis[Power Dissipation 5.1W] --> Junction
    Sink -.->|Forced or Natural Convection| Ambient
```

**Reading the diagram:** Power dissipated in the silicon die flows from the junction through the case and the heat sink into the surrounding air. The total thermal resistance is $\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$. The smaller the thermal resistance, the cooler the device runs for a given power dissipation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Define a state variable filter. List its main advantages over a single-op-amp biquad.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **state variable filter** (or *universal active filter*) is a second-order active filter built from **two integrators and a summing amplifier** that simultaneously provides **Low-Pass (LPF)**, **High-Pass (HPF)**, and **Band-Pass (BPF)** outputs from a single input signal.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. **Simultaneous outputs** — all three filter responses are available concurrently, eliminating the need for separate filter stages.
2. **Independent tuning** — the natural frequency $\omega_0$ and quality factor $Q$ can be tuned **independently** (e.g., $\omega_0$ via integrators, $Q$ via summer feedback).
3. **Low sensitivity** — sensitivities of $\omega_0$ and $Q$ to passive component variations are of order 1 or less, making the design robust.
4. **No positive feedback** — only the standard negative feedback is used, so the circuit is **inherently stable** (unlike the Sallen-Key biquad, which uses positive feedback).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** Compare fixed and adjustable voltage regulators with one example each. Mention one application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Fixed Regulator (e.g., 7805) | Adjustable Regulator (e.g., LM317) |
|---|---|---|
| Output voltage | Permanently set (e.g., +5 V) | Programmable via external resistors (1.25 V to 37 V) |
| External parts | Only bypass capacitors | Two external resistors + bypass capacitors |
| Flexibility | Low (one output per IC) | High (one IC, multiple output voltages) |
| Example IC | 7805 (+5 V), 7912 (–12 V) | LM317 (+ve), LM337 (–ve) |
| Application | Logic supply on digital PCBs | Laboratory bench power supply, battery charger |

(Any reasonable valid answer with example and application earns the 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A(a)** **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Derive the transfer function of the 3-op-amp state variable filter for the LPF output. Identify the expressions for $\omega_0$, $Q$, and $K$ in terms of circuit components.

**Model Solution:**

(Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete derivation. The expected KTU marking scheme is as follows:)

| Step | Marks |
|---|---|
| Writing summer equation $V_{O1} = -(R_F/R_a)V_{in} - (R_F/R_b)V_{LP}$ | 1 |
| Writing integrator #1 equation $V_{BP} = -V_{O1}/(sRC)$ | 1 |
| Writing integrator #2 equation $V_{LP} = -V_{BP}/(sRC)$ | 1 |
| Cascading the two integrators to eliminate $V_{BP}$ | 1 |
| Algebraic manipulation to obtain $H_{LP}(s)$ | 2 |
| Identifying $\omega_0 = 1/RC$, $Q = R_b/R_F$, $K = R_F/R_a$ | 1 |
| **Total** | **7** |

**Final boxed answer:**
$$H_{LP}(s) = \frac{-K \omega_0^2}{s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC},\ Q = \frac{R_b}{R_F},\ K = \frac{R_F}{R_a}$$

---

**Q.A(b)** **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Design a second-order state variable filter (LPF/BPF) with $f_0 = 2\,\text{kHz}$, $Q = 4$, and BPF gain $K = 1$. Assume $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$. Verify by computing the LPF transfer function coefficients.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\omega_0$** (1 mark):
$$\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi \times 2000 = 12{,}566.37\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Choose $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ and compute $R$** (1 mark):
$$R = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = \frac{1}{12566.37 \times 10^{-8}} = 7957.7\,\Omega \approx 7.96\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Use standard $R_1 = R_2 = 7.96\,\text{k}\Omega$ (or closest E12 = 8.2 k$\Omega$).

**Step 3 — Choose summer feedback $R_F$** (1 mark): Pick $R_F = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 4 — Compute $R_b$ for $Q = 4$** (1 mark):
$$R_b = Q \cdot R_F = 4 \times 10\,\text{k}\Omega = 40\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_a$ for $K = 1$** (1 mark):
$$R_a = \frac{R_F}{K} = \frac{10\,\text{k}\Omega}{1} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Verification** (2 marks):
$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} = \frac{1}{7960 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 12{,}562\,\text{rad/s} \approx 2\pi \times 2000\,\text{rad/s} \checkmark$$
$$Q = \frac{R_b}{R_F} = \frac{40\,\text{k}}{10\,\text{k}} = 4 \checkmark$$
$$K = \frac{R_F}{R_a} = \frac{10\,\text{k}}{10\,\text{k}} = 1 \checkmark$$

The LPF transfer function becomes:
$$H_{LP}(s) = \frac{-(1)(12566)^2}{s^2 + (12566/4)s + (12566)^2} = \frac{-1.579 \times 10^8}{s^2 + 3141.6\,s + 1.579 \times 10^8}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B(a)** **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of the **LM317 adjustable voltage regulator**. Derive the expression for $V_{out}$ in terms of the programming resistors.

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the LM317 block diagram in **Section 4.3** of these notes.

**Operation (3 marks):**
The LM317 is a **three-terminal floating adjustable regulator**. Its key internal blocks are:
- A **bandgap reference** of $V_{ref} = 1.25\,\text{V}$ developed between the OUTPUT and ADJUST pins.
- An **error amplifier** that maintains $V_{ref}$ across the programming resistor $R_1$ (connected between ADJUST and ground).
- A **series pass transistor** (Darlington NPN) controlled by the error amplifier.
- **Current limiting** and **thermal overload** protection.

The current through $R_1$ is $I_1 = V_{ref}/R_1$. Since $I_{ADJ} \approx 50\,\mu\text{A}$ is very small compared to $I_1$ (typically a few mA), virtually the same current flows through $R_2$. The voltage across $R_2$ is $I_1 R_2$.

**Derivation of $V_{out}$ (3 marks):**

The output voltage is the sum of the reference voltage and the drop across $R_2$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} + I_1 R_2$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} + \frac{V_{ref}}{R_1} R_2$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} R_2$$

**Final boxed equation:**
$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ}\, R_2\,}$$

**Typical values:** $V_{ref} = 1.25\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 240\,\Omega$, $I_{ADJ} \approx 50\,\mu\text{A}$.

---

**Q.B(b)** **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** A regulated power supply uses an **LM317** to provide $V_{out} = +12\,\text{V}$ at $I_L = 0.8\,\text{A}$. The unregulated input to the LM317 is $V_{in} = 18\,\text{V}$. Calculate the required value of the programming resistor $R_2$. Also determine the power dissipation and the heat sink thermal resistance required. Assume $T_{J,max} = 125\,\text{°C}$, $T_A = 50\,\text{°C}$, $\theta_{JC} = 5\,\text{°C/W}$, $\theta_{CS} = 1\,\text{°C/W}$, and $I_Q = 5\,\text{mA}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $R_2$** (2 marks):

Using $V_{ref} = 1.25$ V and $R_1 = 240\,\Omega$, neglecting $I_{ADJ} R_2$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

$$12 = 1.25\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{240}\right)$$

$$\frac{12}{1.25} = 1 + \frac{R_2}{240} \Rightarrow 9.6 = 1 + \frac{R_2}{240}$$

$$R_2 = 8.6 \times 240 = 2064\,\Omega \approx 2.06\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Power dissipation** (2 marks):
$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) I_L + V_{in} I_Q$$
$$P_D = (18 - 12)(0.8) + (18)(0.005)$$
$$P_D = 4.8 + 0.09 = 4.89\,\text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Heat-sink thermal resistance** (3 marks):

Using the thermal model:

$$T_J = T_A + P_D(\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA})$$

Rearranging for $\theta_{SA}$:

$$\theta_{SA} = \frac{T_{J,max} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$$

$$\theta_{SA} = \frac{125 - 50}{4.89} - 5 - 1$$

$$\theta_{SA} = \frac{75}{4.89} - 6 = 15.34 - 6 = 9.34\,\text{°C/W}$$

**Conclusion:** A heat sink with $\theta_{SA} \leq 9.34\,\text{°C/W}$ is required. A standard aluminium heat sink rated at $8\,\text{°C/W}$ or less would provide adequate cooling margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Hotspots for State Variable Filters:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the summer equation: students often write $V_{O1} = +(R_F/R_a)V_{in} + (R_F/R_b)V_{LP}$. This single sign error propagates through the entire derivation, costing **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $Q$ and $K$ expressions**: $K = R_F/R_a$ (input-related), $Q = R_b/R_F$ (feedback-related). Swapping these earns zero credit on the parameter identification step.
> 3. **Using non-matched integrators** ($R_1C_1 \neq R_2C_2$) in the design: the standard formula $\omega_0 = 1/RC$ only holds for matched stages. A penalty of **1 mark** applies.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert $f_0$ to $\omega_0$**: the design equation $R = 1/(\omega_0 C)$ requires rad/s, not Hz. A common 1-mark error.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Hotspots for Voltage Regulators:**
> 1. **Dropping the $I_{ADJ} R_2$ term in LM317 derivations** without stating the assumption explicitly. Examiners reserve **1 mark** for that term's mention.
> 2. **Wrong polarity** in LM337 calculations (the answer is **negative**, not positive).
> 3. **Forgetting the quiescent current $I_Q$** in $P_D$ calculations — this adds 5–10% error and can be a full-mark deduction.
> 4. **Sign error in the heat-sink formula**: writing $\theta_{SA} = T_A - T_{J,max}$ instead of $T_{J,max} - T_A$ yields a negative answer, indicating conceptual confusion.
> 5. **Not specifying units** for thermal resistance (°C/W) — a small but visible deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### State Variable Filters
- ☐ A **state variable filter** is a *universal 2nd-order filter* with LPF, HPF, BPF outputs from **one circuit**.
- ☐ Built from **two integrators + one summing amplifier** (3 op-amps minimum).
- ☐ Transfer function is **biquadratic**: $H(s) = N(s) / [s^2 + (\omega_0/Q)s + \omega_0^2]$.
- ☐ Key formulas: $\omega_0 = 1/RC$, $Q = R_b/R_F$, $K = R_F/R_a$ (matched integrators assumed).
- ☐ Advantages: **independent tuning** of $\omega_0$ and $Q$; **low sensitivity**; **multiple simultaneous outputs**; **inherently stable**.
- ☐ A 4th op-amp summer can produce a **notch (BSF)** output.

### Voltage Regulators
- ☐ **78xx** = fixed **positive** (7805 = +5 V, 7812 = +12 V, 7815 = +15 V).
- ☐ **79xx** = fixed **negative** (7905 = –5 V, 7912 = –12 V).
- ☐ **LM317** = adjustable **positive** (1.25 V to 37 V); $V_{out} = 1.25(1 + R_2/R_1) + I_{ADJ} R_2$.
- ☐ **LM337** = adjustable **negative**; $V_{out} = -1.25(1 + R_2/R_1)$.
- ☐ $R_1$ is conventionally **240 $\Omega$** in LM317 designs.
- ☐ **Dropout voltage** = 1.5 V (LM317) or 2 V (78xx/79xx).
- ☐ **Line regulation** = $\Delta V_{out}/\Delta V_{in}$ (mV/V).
- ☐ **Load regulation** = $\Delta V_{out}/\Delta I_L$ (mV/mA).
- ☐ **Power dissipation** $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) I_L + V_{in} I_Q$ (don't forget $I_Q$!).
- ☐ **Heat sink** thermal resistance: $\theta_{SA} = (T_{J,max} - T_A)/P_D - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$.
- ☐ Required external components for LM317: $R_1$, $R_2$, $C_{in} \approx 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$, $C_{out} \approx 1\,\mu\text{F}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IC 723 - Low Voltage and High Voltage Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IC 723 - Low Voltage and High Voltage Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **IC 723** is a **monolithic, adjustable, linear voltage regulator** capable of providing both positive and negative regulated output voltages. It is a 14-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package) integrated circuit that contains a **temperature-compensated reference voltage source**, an **error amplifier**, a **series pass transistor**, and **current-limiting/protection circuitry** all on a single silicon chip.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCECT403 - Module 3), the IC 723 is studied under two primary configurable modes:

| Configuration Type | Output Voltage Range | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low Voltage Regulator** | **+2 V to +7 V** | TTL/Logic supplies, Op-Amp rails |
| **High Voltage Regulator** | **+7 V to +37 V** | Analog circuits, Op-Amp ±15 V rails |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The fundamental distinction between the two configurations is the **placement of the voltage divider network** with respect to the internal error amplifier and the 7.15 V Zener reference. The output range pivots around the **internal reference voltage $V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$**.

### 1.1.1 Key Electrical Specifications of IC 723

- **Input Voltage ($V_{IN}$):** Up to **40 V (max)**
- **Output Voltage Range:** **2 V to 37 V** (with proper configuration)
- **Maximum Output Current (without external pass transistor):** **150 mA**
- **Internal Reference Voltage ($V_{REF}$):** **7.15 V (typical)**
- **Line Regulation:** **0.01 % $V_{OUT}$** (typical)
- **Load Regulation:** **0.03 % $V_{OUT}$** (typical)
- **Operating Temperature Range:** **0 °C to 70 °C** (commercial grade)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 7.15 V reference is the *heart* of this IC. Whether the output is below or above this value determines whether we use a **non-inverting** (low V) or **inverting-style feedback** (high V) topology.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ballast Tank" Intuition

Imagine a **water tank on a tall building** that needs to deliver water at a *constant pressure* regardless of how much water is in the overhead reservoir (input variation) or how many taps are open (load variation).

- The **reference voltage (7.15 V)** is the *desired pressure* stamped on a **pressure gauge** at the output pipe.
- The **error amplifier** is the **smart valve operator** who constantly checks the gauge and either tightens or loosens the main valve.
- The **series pass transistor** is the **big main valve** that physically controls water flow.
- The **voltage divider ($R_1, R_2$)** is the **small bypass pipe with a pressure sensor** that reports back to the operator.
- The **low-voltage configuration** is like a tank *below* the sensor height (output < reference), while the **high-voltage configuration** is like a tank *above* the sensor height (output > reference).

In both cases, the operator (error amplifier) maintains the *feedback balance* so that the downstream pressure ($V_{OUT}$) stays rock-solid.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does the divider matter?** The error amplifier forces the voltage at its inverting input to *equal* the reference voltage at its non-inverting input (virtual short principle). By choosing how the divider is connected (attenuating or boosting), we can set the output anywhere from 2 V to 37 V.

---

## 1.3 Functional Block Diagram of IC 723 (Conceptual Overview)

The IC 723 consists of the following internal sub-blocks:

1. **Zener Reference Source** — Produces stable $V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$.
2. **Error Amplifier (Op-Amp)** — Compares feedback with $V_{REF}$.
3. **Series Pass Transistor (Q1)** — The main current-handling element.
4. **Current-Limit Transistor (Q2)** — Protects against short circuits.
5. **Reverse Current Protection Diode** — Prevents latch-up.

The internal connections are arranged such that the user can either:
- **Bypass the internal transistor** and use an external pass transistor for currents > 150 mA, **or**
- **Use the internal transistor directly** for low-current applications.

This modularity is precisely what makes the IC 723 a "Swiss Army knife" of voltage regulation in KTU laboratory experiments and industrial designs.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pin Configuration of IC 723 (14-Pin DIP)

Before diving into the configurations, let us map out the **14 functional pins**:

| Pin No. | Symbol | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $N.C.$ | Not Connected |
| 2 | $V_{IN}$ | Unregulated Input Voltage |
| 3 | $N.C.$ | Not Connected |
| 4 | $V_{REF}$ | Reference Voltage (7.15 V) |
| 5 | $V_{IN}(-)$ | Negative Supply for Op-Amp (Ground) |
| 6 | $V_{OUT}$ | Regulated Output |
| 7 | $C_L$ | Current Limit Sense |
| 8 | $N.C.$ | Not Connected |
| 9 | $V_{IN}(+)$ | Positive Unregulated Supply |
| 10 | $N.C.$ | Not Connected |
| 11 | $N.C.$ | Not Connected |
| 12 | $V_C$ | Compensation / Voltage Control |
| 13 | $I_{SC}$ | Short-Circuit Current Set |
| 14 | $N.C.$ | Not Connected |

> [!NOTE]
> In most simplified textbook schematics, only the **active 6 to 8 pins** are utilized; the rest are NC (No Connection) or used for compensation/advanced features.

---

## 2.2 Low Voltage Regulator Configuration ($2\text{ V} \le V_{OUT} \le 7\text{ V}$)

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology

In the **low voltage configuration**, the output voltage is *less than* the internal reference voltage. The voltage divider $R_1$ and $R_2$ is connected directly across the **output**, and the **center tap** of the divider is fed back to the **inverting input (INV)** of the error amplifier. The **non-inverting input (NON-INV)** is connected to $V_{REF}$.

**Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**

1. A fraction $\beta$ of $V_{OUT}$ is sampled by the divider: $V_{INV} = V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$
2. The error amplifier compares $V_{INV}$ with $V_{REF}$ at its NON-INV input.
3. Through negative feedback, the error amplifier forces: $V_{INV} = V_{REF}$
4. Equating: $V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{REF}$
5. Solving for $V_{OUT}$:

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Counter-intuitive trap for students:** Even though the formula $V_{OUT} = V_{REF}(1 + R_1/R_2)$ looks identical to a non-inverting amplifier gain, in this **low-voltage mode** $R_1$ and $R_2$ are chosen so that the ratio $(1 + R_1/R_2)$ is **less than 1**... Actually, the correct constraint is that the divider **attenuates** the output so that the sampled voltage equals the 7.15 V reference. To get $V_{OUT} < 7.15\text{ V}$, we must use the **alternate divider connection**:
> 
> $$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

This is the **low-voltage formula** (output is a fraction of the reference). The actual KTU convention uses this form.

### 2.2.2 Final Low-Voltage Output Formula

$$\boxed{V_{OUT}(\text{LOW}) = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

The minimum achievable output is approximately **2 V** (limited by the saturation voltage of the internal pass transistor and the headroom of the error amplifier).

---

## 2.3 High Voltage Regulator Configuration ($7\text{ V} \le V_{OUT} \le 37\text{ V}$)

### 2.3.1 Circuit Topology

In the **high voltage configuration**, the output voltage is *greater than* the internal reference. The voltage divider is connected such that $R_1$ is the **feedback resistor** (between $V_{OUT}$ and the INV input) and $R_2$ is the **ground resistor** (between INV input and ground). The NON-INV input is tied to $V_{REF}$.

**Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**

1. Voltage at the INV input: $V_{INV} = V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$
2. Error amplifier enforces: $V_{INV} = V_{REF}$ (virtual short)
3. Therefore: $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$

### 2.3.2 Final High-Voltage Output Formula

$$\boxed{V_{OUT}(\text{HIGH}) = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Both configurations use the same physical divider network, but the **mathematical form** changes based on whether the sampled voltage is *less than* or *greater than* the reference. In university exams, **state explicitly** which formula you are using and why.

---

## 2.4 Current Limit Calculation (Common to Both Configurations)

The IC 723 provides **programmable current limiting** via an external resistor $R_{SC}$ connected between pins 2 and 3 (or 2 and 7, depending on datasheet variant). The current limit is set by:

$$I_{LIMIT} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}} \approx \dfrac{0.7\text{ V}}{R_{SC}}$$

Where $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ is the base-emitter turn-on voltage of the internal current-sense transistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This feature protects the regulator and the load during short-circuit conditions. In KTU lab experiments, students are often asked to design $R_{SC}$ for a specific $I_{LIMIT}$ (e.g., 50 mA or 100 mA).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for the exam hall — these are the high-yield formulas for IC 723 problems.**

| Configuration | Output Voltage Formula | Condition | Current Limit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low Voltage** | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $2\text{ V} \le V_{OUT} \le 7\text{ V}$ | $I_{LIMIT} = \dfrac{0.7}{R_{SC}}$ |
| **High Voltage** | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | $7\text{ V} \le V_{OUT} \le 37\text{ V}$ | $I_{LIMIT} = \dfrac{0.7}{R_{SC}}$ |
| **Foldback Limit** | $I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{OUT} + 0.7}{R_{SC} + R_{CL}}$ | Advanced protection | — |
| **Dropout Voltage** | $V_{DO} \approx 3\text{ V}$ (typical) | Minimum $V_{IN} - V_{OUT}$ | — |

**Key Constants to Memorize:**

- $V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$ (internal Zener reference)
- $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$ (for current-limit calculations)
- $I_{OUT(max, internal)} = 150\text{ mA}$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The IC 723 is widely used in:

1. **Laboratory bench power supplies** (variable +5 V to +30 V).
2. **Industrial control systems** (sensors needing clean rails).
3. **Automotive electronics** (battery-powered regulators).
4. **Battery chargers** (constant-voltage / constant-current sources).
5. **Audio pre-amplifiers** (low-noise supply rails).
6. **Embedded systems** (as a backup to switching regulators for noise-sensitive analog sections).

> [!TIP]
> Modern equivalents include **LM317** (low-power) and **LT1085** (high-current), but the IC 723 remains a pedagogical favorite in KTU curricula because its internal blocks are *transparent* and configurable.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Low-Voltage Configuration Output Equation

**Given:**
- Internal reference $V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$
- Voltage divider $R_1$ (upper, connected to $V_{OUT}$) and $R_2$ (lower, connected to ground)
- The center tap of the divider is fed to the **inverting input** of the error amplifier.
- The **non-inverting input** of the error amplifier is connected to $V_{REF}$.

**To Derive:** Expression for $V_{OUT}$ in terms of $V_{REF}$, $R_1$, and $R_2$.

**Step 1 — Apply Voltage Divider Rule:**

The voltage at the inverting input is a fraction of $V_{OUT}$:

$$V_{INV} = V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Virtual Short Principle of the Error Amplifier:**

The error amplifier, being in a negative feedback loop, forces its two inputs to be at the same potential:

$$V_{INV} = V_{NON-INV}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the NON-INV Input Voltage:**

The NON-INV input is tied directly to the reference:

$$V_{NON-INV} = V_{REF}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions:**

$$V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{REF}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{OUT}$:**

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$

Expanding the fraction:

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical interpretation:** The formula has the structure of a non-inverting amplifier gain $\left(1 + R_1/R_2\right)$. However, for the **low-voltage** mode, the actual sampled signal is the *divided* output. We rewrite it as an attenuation form:
> 
> $$V_{OUT}(\text{LOW}) = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{\beta}, \quad \text{where } \beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} < 1$$

When $\beta < 1$, the ratio $1/\beta > 1$, but the reference itself (7.15 V) is being *scaled* by the divider. The **operational constraint** $V_{OUT} \le 7\text{ V}$ is met by choosing $R_1$ and $R_2$ appropriately.

**Final Compact Form (Low-Voltage):**

$$\boxed{V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — High-Voltage Configuration Output Equation

**Given:**
- Same IC 723 with $V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$
- Voltage divider $R_1$ (feedback, from $V_{OUT}$ to INV input) and $R_2$ (ground, from INV to GND)
- The NON-INV input is connected to $V_{REF}$.

**To Derive:** Expression for $V_{OUT}$ when $V_{OUT} > 7\text{ V}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Voltage Divider Rule at INV Input:**

$$V_{INV} = V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Virtual Short:**

$$V_{INV} = V_{NON-INV} = V_{REF}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and Rearrange:**

$$V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{REF}$$

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Expand for Clarity:**

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The mathematical form is *identical* to the low-voltage case. The difference lies in the **choice of $R_1$ and $R_2$** to satisfy the desired output range:
> - **Low-V mode:** $R_1 \ll R_2$ (so the ratio is small, giving $V_{OUT} < V_{REF}$)
> - **High-V mode:** $R_1 \ge R_2$ (so the ratio is $\ge 1$, giving $V_{OUT} \ge V_{REF}$)

**Final Compact Form (High-Voltage):**

$$\boxed{V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Current Limit Threshold

**Given:**
- Internal transistor $Q_2$ has base-emitter junction connected across $R_{SC}$.
- $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ to turn ON the current-limit transistor.

**To Derive:** The value of $R_{SC}$ for a desired current limit $I_{LIMIT}$.

**Step 1 — Voltage Across $R_{SC}$:**

When the load current $I_L$ flows through $R_{SC}$, the voltage drop is:

$$V_{R_{SC}} = I_L \cdot R_{SC}$$

**Step 2 — Turn-On Condition for Current Limit:**

The current-limit transistor $Q_2$ turns on when:

$$V_{R_{SC}} = V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and Solve:**

$$I_{LIMIT} \cdot R_{SC} = 0.7\text{ V}$$

$$R_{SC} = \dfrac{0.7}{I_{LIMIT}}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $I_{LIMIT} = 50\text{ mA}$:

$$R_{SC} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.050} = 14\text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Power Dissipation in $R_{SC}$:**

$$P_{R_{SC}} = I_{LIMIT}^2 \cdot R_{SC} = (0.050)^2 \cdot 14 = 35\text{ mW}$$

Choose a resistor with at least **2×** the calculated wattage for safety (≥ 70 mW, use a 1/8 W or higher standard).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Low-Voltage Configuration | High-Voltage Configuration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Output Range** | +2 V to +7 V | +7 V to +37 V |
| **Divider Position** | $R_1$ from $V_{OUT}$ to INV; $R_2$ from INV to GND | $R_1$ from $V_{OUT}$ to INV; $R_2$ from INV to GND |
| **Formula Form** | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$ (or $V_{REF} \cdot \beta^{-1}$) | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ |
| **Typical $R_1$ vs $R_2$** | $R_1 \ll R_2$ (e.g., 1 kΩ vs 5 kΩ) | $R_1 \ge R_2$ (e.g., 10 kΩ vs 5 kΩ) |
| **Error Amplifier Mode** | Non-inverting (in feedback loop) | Non-inverting (in feedback loop) |
| **Application** | Logic-level supplies | Analog ±15 V, op-amp rails |
| **Maximum $V_{IN}$** | $V_{OUT} + V_{DO} \approx V_{OUT} + 3\text{ V}$ | $V_{OUT} + V_{DO} \approx V_{OUT} + 3\text{ V}$ |
| **External Pass Needed?** | Optional (internal 150 mA sufficient) | Often required for high current |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Design an IC 723 **high-voltage regulator** for $V_{OUT} = +15\text{ V}$ with a current limit of 100 mA. Choose standard resistor values.

**Given:**
- $V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$
- $V_{OUT} = 15\text{ V}$
- $I_{LIMIT} = 100\text{ mA}$

**Step 1 — Apply the High-Voltage Formula:**

$$15 = 7.15 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Resistor Ratio:**

$$1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{15}{7.15} = 2.0979$$

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = 1.0979$$

**Step 3 — Choose Standard $R_2$:**

Let $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$. Then:

$$R_1 = 1.0979 \cdot 10\text{ k}\Omega = 10.979\text{ k}\Omega$$

Use the standard E12 value $R_1 = 11\text{ k}\Omega$ (closest match).

**Step 4 — Recalculate $V_{OUT}$ with Standard Values:**

$$V_{OUT} = 7.15 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{11}{10}\right) = 7.15 \cdot 2.1 = 15.015\text{ V} \approx 15\text{ V} \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $R_{SC}$ for 100 mA Limit:**

$$R_{SC} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.100} = 7\text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Verify Power Dissipation in $R_{SC}$:**

$$P_{R_{SC}} = (0.100)^2 \cdot 7 = 70\text{ mW}$$

Use a standard **1/4 W (250 mW)** resistor for derating safety.

**Step 7 — Input Voltage Selection:**

$$V_{IN(min)} = V_{OUT} + V_{DO} = 15 + 3 = 18\text{ V}$$

Choose $V_{IN} = 20\text{ V}$ for a comfortable margin.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show all 7 steps explicitly. Examiners reward methodical work — a single skipped step can cost 1-2 marks even if the final answer is correct.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — IC 723 Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Unregulated V_IN (+40V max)"] --> B["Series Pass Transistor Q1"]
    B --> C["Regulated V_OUT"]
    C --> D["Voltage Divider R1-R2"]
    D --> E["Error Amplifier - Inverting Input"]
    F["Zener Reference 7.15V"] --> G["Error Amplifier - Non-Inverting Input"]
    G --> E
    E --> H["Error Amplifier Output"]
    H --> B
    I["Current Sense Resistor R_SC"] --> J["Current Limit Transistor Q2"]
    J --> H
    K["Load Current Path"] --> I
    L["Ground Reference"] --> D
    L --> F
```

**Diagram Interpretation (for KTU exams):**
- The **Zener reference** ($V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$) is the *stable foundation* against which the output is compared.
- The **error amplifier** is the *brain* — it constantly adjusts the series pass transistor to maintain $V_{OUT}$.
- The **divider** ($R_1, R_2$) is the *sensor* — it samples $V_{OUT}$ and sends a feedback signal.
- The **current-limit transistor** is the *guardian* — it cuts off output if current exceeds safe limits.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Low-Voltage Configuration (2 V to 7 V)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN["V_IN Unregulated"] --> Q1["Internal Pass Transistor"]
    Q1 --> VOUT["V_OUT +2V to +7V"]
    VOUT --> R1["R1 Upper Divider"]
    R1 --> N1["Node: INV Input"]
    N1 --> R2["R2 Lower Divider"]
    R2 --> GND["Ground"]
    N1 --> EA1["Error Amplifier"]
    VREF["V_REF 7.15V"] --> EA1
    EA1 --> Q1
    RSC["R_SC Current Sense"] --> Q2["Current Limit Q2"]
    Q2 --> EA1
    VOUT --> LOAD["Load"]
```

**Key Connections in Low-V Mode:**
- $R_1$ is from $V_{OUT}$ to the **inverting input**.
- $R_2$ is from the **inverting input** to **ground**.
- $V_{REF}$ drives the **non-inverting input**.
- The formula: $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$ (with $R_1 \ll R_2$).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — High-Voltage Configuration (7 V to 37 V)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN["V_IN Unregulated +20V"] --> Q1["Internal Pass Transistor"]
    Q1 --> VOUT["V_OUT +7V to +37V"]
    VOUT --> R1["R1 Feedback Resistor"]
    R1 --> N1["Node: INV Input"]
    N1 --> R2["R2 Ground Resistor"]
    N2["Ground"] --> R2
    N1 --> EA1["Error Amplifier"]
    VREF["V_REF 7.15V"] --> EA1
    EA1 --> Q1
    RSC["R_SC Current Sense"] --> Q2["Current Limit Q2"]
    Q2 --> EA1
    VOUT --> LOAD["Load"]
```

**Key Connections in High-V Mode:**
- $R_1$ is from $V_{OUT}$ to the **inverting input** (acts as feedback).
- $R_2$ is from the **inverting input** to **ground**.
- $V_{REF}$ drives the **non-inverting input**.
- The formula: $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ (with $R_1 \ge R_2$).

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: What is the function of the Zener reference voltage in the IC 723, and what is its typical value?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> The Zener reference in the IC 723 provides an **internally generated, temperature-compensated stable voltage** against which the output voltage is compared by the error amplifier. This reference is the **cornerstone of regulation accuracy** — any drift in $V_{REF}$ directly translates to output drift. The typical value is **$V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$** (ranging from 6.8 V to 7.5 V across temperature and process variations). The Zener is biased by an internal current source to maintain its stable breakdown region. *(3 marks: 1 mark for function, 1 mark for typical value, 1 mark for its role in regulation.)*

---

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q: Differentiate between the low-voltage and high-voltage configurations of the IC 723 in terms of output range and divider design.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> | Aspect | Low-Voltage Config | High-Voltage Config |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Output Range** | +2 V to +7 V | +7 V to +37 V |
> | **Divider Ratio** | $R_1 \ll R_2$ (attenuating) | $R_1 \ge R_2$ (boosting) |
> | **Formula** | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$ | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ |
> 
> Both configurations use the **same error amplifier topology**, but the **relative magnitudes** of $R_1$ and $R_2$ are swapped to push the output below or above the 7.15 V reference. *(3 marks: 1 mark per row of comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Format Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student must answer **ONE full question** out of the two choices provided.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: Design an IC 723-based high-voltage regulator to deliver $V_{OUT} = +12\text{ V}$ at a maximum load current of 100 mA with current limiting at 150 mA. Draw the circuit and explain the working.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Design the Resistor Divider for $V_{OUT} = 12\text{ V}$

**Step 1 — Identify the Configuration:**

Since $V_{OUT} = 12\text{ V} > V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$, we use the **high-voltage configuration**.

**Step 2 — Apply the High-Voltage Formula:**

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

$$12 = 7.15 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Ratio:**

$$1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{12}{7.15} = 1.678$$

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = 0.678$$

**Step 4 — Choose Standard $R_2$:**

Let $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (standard E12 value). Then:

$$R_1 = 0.678 \cdot 10\text{ k}\Omega = 6.78\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Round to Standard E12 Value:**

Use $R_1 = 6.8\text{ k}\Omega$ (closest standard value, ±5 % tolerance).

**Step 6 — Verify the Output:**

$$V_{OUT} = 7.15 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{6.8}{10}\right) = 7.15 \cdot 1.68 = 12.012\text{ V} \approx 12\text{ V} \checkmark$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Design the Current Limit at 150 mA and Choose $V_{IN}$

**Step 1 — Calculate $R_{SC}$ for 150 mA Limit:**

$$R_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{I_{LIMIT}} = \dfrac{0.7\text{ V}}{0.150\text{ A}} = 4.67\text{ }\Omega$$

Use standard value $R_{SC} = 4.7\text{ }\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Power Rating of $R_{SC}$:**

$$P_{R_{SC}} = (0.150)^2 \cdot 4.7 = 105.75\text{ mW}$$

Use a **1/4 W (250 mW)** resistor for safety.

**Step 3 — Choose Input Voltage:**

$$V_{IN(min)} = V_{OUT} + V_{DO} = 12 + 3 = 15\text{ V}$$

Use $V_{IN} = 18\text{ V}$ (standard available supply) for a **3 V headroom**.

**Step 4 — Working Explanation:**

The unregulated +18 V is applied to the pass transistor. The divider $R_1 = 6.8\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ samples 12 V and feeds back to the error amplifier. The amplifier compares this with 7.15 V and adjusts the pass transistor to maintain **12 V ±0.01 %** across line and load variations. If load current exceeds 150 mA, the voltage across $R_{SC}$ reaches 0.7 V, turning on the current-limit transistor and folding back the output to protect the regulator.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**Q: With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of the IC 723 in the low-voltage configuration. Design a regulator for $V_{OUT} = +5\text{ V}$ suitable for TTL logic supplies, with current limiting at 80 mA.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Circuit and Working of Low-Voltage Configuration

**Step 1 — Draw the Circuit:**

```
        V_IN (+12V)
            │
            ├── Pin 2 (V_IN) of IC 723
            │
        ┌───┴───┐
        │ IC 723│
        │       │── Pin 6 (V_OUT) ──┬── +5V OUT
        │       │                   │
        │       │              ┌────┴────┐
        │       │              │   R1    │
        │       │              │  3.5kΩ  │
        │       │              └────┬────┘
        │       │                   │
        │       │              ┌────┴────┐
        │       │              │   R2    │
        │       │              │  10kΩ   │
        │       │              └────┬────┘
        │       │                   │
        │       │                  GND
        │       │── Pin 4 (V_REF) ──┘ (via internal connection)
        │       │
        │       │── Pin 2 to Pin 3 (or 7) via R_SC = 8.75Ω
        └───────┘
```

**Step 2 — Working Principle:**

The unregulated input (say +12 V) is applied to the internal pass transistor. The output is sampled by the divider $R_1$-$R_2$ and a fraction of $V_{OUT}$ is fed to the **inverting input** of the internal error amplifier. The **non-inverting input** is tied to the **7.15 V Zener reference**. The error amplifier, in a negative feedback loop, forces:

$$V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_1$ for $V_{OUT} = 5\text{ V}$:**

$$5 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 7.15$$

Wait, this yields $V_{OUT} > V_{REF}$ mathematically. The correct low-voltage formula is the **attenuated reference** form:

$$V_{OUT} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{1 + R_1/R_2}$$

Solving:

$$1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{V_{OUT}} = \dfrac{7.15}{5} = 1.43$$

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = 0.43$$

Let $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_1 = 4.3\text{ k}\Omega$ (use standard **4.3 kΩ E96** or **4.7 kΩ E12** with minor output adjustment).

**Verify with $R_1 = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:**

$$V_{OUT} = \dfrac{7.15}{1 + 4.7/10} = \dfrac{7.15}{1.47} = 4.86\text{ V}$$

Close to 5 V, or use $R_1 = 4.3\text{ k}\Omega$ for **4.99 V**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Current Limit Design and Input Selection

**Step 1 — Compute $R_{SC}$ for 80 mA:**

$$R_{SC} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.080} = 8.75\text{ }\Omega$$

Use standard $R_{SC} = 8.2\text{ }\Omega$ (E12) or $8.87\text{ }\Omega$ (E96) closest.

**Step 2 — Power Rating of $R_{SC}$:**

$$P_{R_{SC}} = (0.080)^2 \cdot 8.75 = 56\text{ mW}$$

Use **1/8 W (125 mW)** resistor.

**Step 3 — Input Voltage Selection:**

$$V_{IN(min)} = V_{OUT} + V_{DO} = 5 + 3 = 8\text{ V}$$

Use standard **+9 V or +12 V** unregulated supply.

**Step 4 — Application Note:**

This configuration is ideal for **TTL/CMOS logic supplies** where a clean **+5 V** rail is needed. The 7.15 V reference ensures excellent temperature stability (typically 0.003 %/°C), and the current limit protects the regulator during short circuits — a common fault in breadboarded digital circuits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Avoid These!):**
> 
> 1. **Formula Confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often write the *same* formula for both configurations and lose marks. **State explicitly** whether you are using the low-V or high-V formula, and show the algebraic rearrangement.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting $V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$ (–1 mark):** Some students approximate it as 7 V or 8 V. Use the **exact** 7.15 V value as specified by the manufacturer.
> 
> 3. **No Headroom Calculation (–1 mark):** Examiners expect $V_{IN(min)} = V_{OUT} + V_{DO}$ to be shown. Omitting this is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 
> 4. **No Resistor Standardization (–1 mark):** Always choose **E12 or E96 standard values** and **verify** the resulting $V_{OUT}$ with the substituted values.
> 
> 5. **Skipping Current Limit (–2 marks):** Current limit design via $R_{SC}$ is a **favourite** Part (b) question. Skipping it costs full marks.
> 
> 6. **No Circuit Diagram (–2 to –3 marks):** Even a hand-drawn schematic with labelled components is mandatory. Examiners deduct heavily for "narrative-only" answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for IC 723 — Low & High Voltage Configurations**

### Core Concepts:
- [x] IC 723 is a **monolithic adjustable voltage regulator** with internal $V_{REF} = 7.15\text{ V}$.
- [x] **Low-Voltage Mode:** Output = +2 V to +7 V (below reference).
- [x] **High-Voltage Mode:** Output = +7 V to +37 V (above reference).
- [x] Same IC, **different external divider design** — that's the only difference!

### Essential Formulas (Memorize!):
- [x] **Low-V:** $V_{OUT} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{1 + R_1/R_2}$ (or equivalent attenuation form)
- [x] **High-V:** $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$
- [x] **Current Limit:** $R_{SC} = \dfrac{0.7\text{ V}}{I_{LIMIT}}$
- [x] **Dropout:** $V_{IN(min)} = V_{OUT} + 3\text{ V}$

### Design Procedure (5 Steps):
- [x] **Step 1:** Identify configuration (Low-V or High-V based on desired $V_{OUT}$ vs 7.15 V).
- [x] **Step 2:** Apply the correct formula and solve for $R_1/R_2$ ratio.
- [x] **Step 3:** Choose standard $R_2$ (typically 1 kΩ to 10 kΩ) and compute $R_1$.
- [x] **Step 4:** Calculate $R_{SC}$ for desired current limit.
- [x] **Step 5:** Choose $V_{IN} = V_{OUT} + 3\text{ V}$ (minimum dropout margin).

### Key Specifications:
- [x] **Maximum Input:** 40 V
- [x] **Internal Transistor Current:** 150 mA
- [x] **Reference Voltage:** 7.15 V (typical)
- [x] **Package:** 14-pin DIP
- [x] **Line Regulation:** 0.01 % (excellent)
- [x] **Load Regulation:** 0.03 % (excellent)

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid:
- [x] Don't confuse the **low-V and high-V formulas** — they look similar but are applied differently.
- [x] Always include the **headroom/dropout** in $V_{IN}$ selection.
- [x] Standardize resistor values to **E12/E96 series**.
- [x] **Show the circuit diagram** — never rely on text alone for a 14-mark question.
- [x] State the **configuration** at the start of your solution (Low-V or High-V).

### Exam Strategy:
- [x] **Part A (3 marks):** Know the reference value, output ranges, and configuration differences.
- [x] **Part B (14 marks):** Expect a design problem with sub-parts (a) for divider design and (b) for current limit / input selection.
- [x] **Time allocation:** ~25 minutes for Part B questions, ~5 minutes for each Part A.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Tip:** When you see "design an IC 723 regulator for X volts", the **first thing** you should write is whether X is greater or less than 7.15 V. This single sentence tells the examiner (and yourself!) which configuration to use — and earns you an immediate **methodology mark** even if the subsequent algebra has a minor slip.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Boosting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Current Boosting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Current Boosting** in the context of Linear Integrated Circuits refers to a class of circuit modification techniques used to enhance the **output current drive capability** of an operational amplifier (op-amp) far beyond its native specification (typically **±20 mA** for standard op-amps like µA741, or **±5–10 mA** for low-power CMOS op-amps). It is achieved by interfacing the op-amp's output with **external discrete power transistors** (BJTs or MOSFETs) configured as emitter-followers, source-followers, or push-pull Class-B/AB stages, while preserving the op-amp's closed-loop voltage gain, bandwidth, and linearity.

The standard op-amp output stage is a **Class-A or Class-AB push-pull transistor pair** with limited quiescent current. When the load demands current exceeding the op-amp's short-circuit output current limit $I_{SC}$, the output stage **saturates** and the op-amp loses its linear transfer characteristic. Current boosting solves this by allowing an external high-power stage to source/sink the bulk of the load current while the op-amp only provides the **base/gate drive** (control signal) to the external transistors.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tour Guide with a Megaphone:**
> Imagine a professor (the op-amp) with a clear, articulate voice that 20 students (a few milliamps) can hear in a small classroom. Now, place the same professor in a stadium with 5,000 students. The professor's voice (the op-amp output current) is **not loud enough** to reach everyone. The solution: give the professor a **megaphone** (the external power transistors). The professor still **controls** what is being said (the op-amp still controls the voltage), but the megaphone's batteries supply the actual acoustic energy to drive the larger audience. The professor never gets hoarse, the audience hears everything clearly, and the message (signal voltage) is unchanged.
>
> Similarly, the op-amp **never delivers large current itself** — it merely dictates the voltage. The external transistors deliver the heavy current to the load, just as the megaphone delivers the loud sound.

### 1.3 Why Current Boosting is Necessary

| Standard Op-Amp | Maximum Output Current | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| µA741 (general purpose) | **±25 mA** | Cannot drive motors, relays, speakers |
| LM358 (low power) | **±40 mA** | Insufficient for high-power audio |
| TL072 (FET input) | **±10 mA** | Very low drive capability |
| OP-27 (precision) | **±20 mA** | Limited for industrial loads |

Applications demanding currents in the range **0.1 A to 10 A** (audio amplifiers, solenoid drivers, motor controllers, heater elements, LED arrays) **require** an external current-boosting stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme Module-3 (Linear Integrated Circuits) emphasizes current boosting as the practical bridge between **low-power signal-processing op-amps** and **high-current industrial/communication loads**. The technique is essential for the design of audio power amplifiers, voltage regulators with high current output, and programmable power supplies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Topology 1 — Simple BJT Emitter Follower Current Booster

**Concept:** Connect an **NPN** transistor (Q1) at the output to **source** current, and a **PNP** transistor (Q2) to **sink** current. The op-amp output drives the **bases** of both transistors, while the **emitters** are tied together and form the boosted output.

**Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):**
- When $V_{in} > 0$ (positive half-cycle): The op-amp output swings positive, forward-biasing the base-emitter junction of NPN Q1. Q1 turns ON and **sources** current $I_L$ to the load.
- When $V_{in} < 0$ (negative half-cycle): The op-amp output swings negative, forward-biasing the base-emitter junction of PNP Q2. Q2 turns ON and **sinks** current $I_L$ from the load.
- The op-amp itself only supplies the tiny **base currents** $I_{B1}$ and $I_{B2}$ (a few mA). The transistors multiply these by the current gain $\beta$ to deliver amperes to the load.
- **Crossover Region Issue:** Near $V_{in} = 0$, both transistors are near cutoff, creating **crossover distortion** because of the $V_{BE}$ threshold (~0.6–0.7 V). This is the main drawback of the simple topology.

### 2.2 Topology 2 — Class-AB Push-Pull Current Booster (Preferred)

**Concept:** Add **biasing diodes** (D1, D2) or a $V_{BE}$ multiplier between the bases of Q1 and Q2. These diodes establish a quiescent bias of about **1.2 V – 1.4 V** between the two bases, keeping both transistors slightly ON at $V_{in} = 0$. This eliminates crossover distortion.

**Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):**
- The two diodes D1, D2 develop a voltage drop equal to $2 \cdot V_D \approx 1.4\text{ V}$, which biases the bases of Q1 (NPN) and Q2 (PNP) such that they are on the **edge of conduction** at quiescence.
- For positive input: Q1 conducts more, sourcing current.
- For negative input: Q2 conducts more, sinking current.
- A **resistor $R_E$** in series with each emitter provides **short-circuit protection** by limiting maximum load current to $I_{L(max)} = V_{BE} / R_E$.
- This is the standard Class-AB output stage found inside virtually all audio power amplifiers (LM386, TDA2003, etc.).

### 2.3 Topology 3 — MOSFET Current Booster

**Concept:** Replace BJTs with **N-channel and P-channel power MOSFETs** (e.g., IRF540, IRF9540). MOSFETs offer:
- Very high input impedance (no gate current drawn from op-amp)
- Faster switching (no charge storage time)
- No thermal runaway issues

The op-amp directly drives the **gates**, and the **sources** form the boosted output. A gate-source bias network (Zener diodes or a small battery) establishes the Class-AB quiescent point.

### 2.4 Current Boosting Inside IC Power Op-Amts

Modern **power op-amps** (e.g., LM675, OPA549, L165) have **current boosting built inside the IC** using the techniques above. Some are even **user-programmable** via an external current-limit resistor pin.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum load current (NPN source) | $I_{L(max)} = \beta_{NPN} \cdot I_{B(max)}$ | Where $I_{B(max)}$ is the maximum base current op-amp can supply (mA) |
| 2 | Base current required from op-amp | $I_B = \dfrac{I_L}{\beta}$ | For a desired load current $I_L$ |
| 3 | Emitter current (load current) | $I_E = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_B \approx \beta \cdot I_B$ | Since $\beta \gg 1$ |
| 4 | Power dissipation in output transistor | $P_D = (V_{CC} - V_L) \cdot I_L$ | Watts (W); $V_L$ is load voltage |
| 5 | Maximum power dissipation | $P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{4 R_L}$ | At $V_L = V_{CC}/2$ (mid-rail), per transfer-to-load theorem |
| 6 | Crossover dead-zone voltage | $V_{cross} = 2 \cdot V_{BE} \approx 1.4 \text{ V}$ | Total range over which both transistors are off (simple topology) |
