The locus is a **line with negative slope** that *folds back* to a lower $I_{SC}$ at $V_{OUT} = 0$. *(1 mark)* 3. **Key advantage:** Fold-back drastically reduces the short-circuit dissipation $P_{SC} = V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC}$, protecting the pass transistor. *(1 mark)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **In a fold-back-protected regulator, derive the expression for the short-circuit current $I_{SC}$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** At $V_{OUT} = 0$, the voltage-divider contribution in the fold-back line equation vanishes. From the KVL of the sense loop: $$V_{BE(on)} = I_{SC} \cdot R_{SC}$$ $$\boxed{I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE(on)}}{R_{SC}}}$$ This is the **y-intercept** of the fold-back line on the $I_L$–$V_{OUT}$ characteristic. *(2 marks for derivation + 1 mark for interpretation)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module-level ESE questions carry 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Either sub-part may be a design or an analysis. The student answers **ONE** of the two full questions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the operation of a **fold-back current limiting** circuit used in linear voltage regulators. Derive the equation of the fold-back locus and identify the *knee* and *short-circuit* points. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A linear regulator supplies $V_{OUT} = 15$ V at a rated current of $I_{FL} = 2.0$ A. The fold-back protection is designed for a short-circuit current $I_{SC} = 0.5$ A using $V_{BE(on)} = 0.7$ V. Determine: (i) The value of the sense resistor $R_{SC}$. (ii) The required divider ratio $R_1/(R_1+R_2)$. (iii) The pass-transistor power dissipation at the fold-back knee and at short circuit, assuming $V_{IN} = 20$ V. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) Schematic + Theory (7 marks)** **Schematic description:** Draw a series-pass BJT $Q_1$ whose emitter feeds a sense resistor $R_{SC}$ in series with the load. A second BJT $Q_{SL}$ has its emitter connected to the load side of $R_{SC}$ and its collector tied to the base of $Q_1$. A divider $R_1$–$R_2$ from $V_{OUT}$ to ground provides a fraction $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ of $V_{OUT}$ to the base of $Q_{SL}$. *(2 marks for schematic)* **Operation:** Under normal load, the voltage across $R_{SC}$ plus the divider contribution is less than $V_{BE(on)}$, so $Q_{SL}$ is OFF and the regulator behaves normally. When an overload increases $I_L$, the voltage across $R_{SC}$ rises; when the sum exceeds $V_{BE(on)}$, $Q_{SL}$ conducts, shunting base drive from $Q_1$ and reducing $I_L$. As $V_{OUT}$ collapses (overload deepens), the divider voltage falls, so the sum at the base of $Q_{SL}$ drops *unless* $I_L$ also drops — hence the negative-slope locus. *(3 marks for theory)* **Derivation of fold-back locus:** Using KVL on the sense loop, $$V_{BE(on)} = I_L R_{SC} + V_{OUT} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$ $$\boxed{I_L = \frac{V_{BE(on)}}{R_{SC}} - \frac{V_{OUT}}{R_{SC}} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}}$$ - **Knee point** at $V_{OUT} = V_{OUT(nom)}$: $I_L = I_{FL}$ - **Short-circuit point** at $V_{OUT} = 0$: $I_L = I_{SC} = V_{BE(on)}/R_{SC}$ *(2 marks for derivation + identification)* --- **(b) Numerical Design (7 marks)** **(i) Sense resistor:** $$R_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE(on)}}{I_{SC}} = \frac{0.7}{0.5} = 1.4 \text{ Ω}$$ Use a standard 1.5 Ω, 2 W resistor. *(2 marks)* **(ii) Divider ratio:** The fold ratio is $K = I_{FL}/I_{SC} = 2.0/0.5 = 4$. $$\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{V_{BE(on)}(K - 1)}{V_{OUT(nom)}} = \frac{0.7 \times 3}{15} = 0.14$$ So $R_1/(R_1+R_2) = 0.14$, i.e., $R_2/R_1 = (1-0.14)/0.14 = 6.14$. *(2 marks)* **(iii) Power dissipation:** At the fold-back knee: $$P_{FL} = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_{FL} = (20 - 15) \times 2.0 = 10 \text{ W}$$ At the short circuit: $$P_{SC} = V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC} = 20 \times 0.5 = 10 \text{ W}$$ In this case both are equal at 10 W — pass transistor must be rated ≥ 15 W with a suitable heatsink. *(3 marks)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module-3 Pattern]** **(a)** Compare **simple current limiting** and **fold-back current limiting** in regulators. Draw the $I_L$–$V_{OUT}$ characteristics for both schemes on a single set of axes and clearly label the knee current $I_{FL}$ and short-circuit current $I_{SC}$. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Design the sense resistor and voltage divider for a fold-back-protected regulator with the following specifications: - $V_{IN} = 24$ V - $V_{OUT(nom)} = 12$ V - $I_{FL} = 1.5$ A - $I_{SC} = 0.3$ A - $V_{BE(on)} = 0.65$ V - Divider current ≈ 5 mA Also verify the worst-case pass-transistor dissipation. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) Comparison + Sketch (7 marks)** | Parameter | Simple Current Limit | Fold-Back Limit | |---|---|---| | Extra components | $R_{SC}$, $Q_{SL}$ | $R_{SC}$, $Q_{SL}$, $R_1$, $R_2$ | | $I_L$–$V_{OUT}$ locus | Horizontal line at $I_{FL}$ | Negative-slope line, $I_{SC} < I_{FL}$ | | Behaviour at $V_{OUT}=0$ | Current remains at $I_{FL}$ | Current drops to $I_{SC}$ | | Power at short | $V_{IN} \cdot I_{FL}$ (large) | $V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC}$ (smaller) | | Application | Low-current supplies | High-current supplies | *(4 marks for tabular comparison + textual explanation)* **Sketch:** On a common $I_L$ (vertical) vs $V_{OUT}$ (horizontal) plot, draw: - A **horizontal line** at $I_L = I_{FL}$ labelled "simple limit". - A **sloping line** from $(0, I_{SC})$ to $(V_{OUT(nom)}, I_{FL})$ labelled "fold-back". - The fold-back line always lies *below* the simple-limit line for $V_{OUT} < V_{OUT(nom)}$. *(3 marks for the correctly drawn, labelled locus)* --- **(b) Design Calculations (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Sense resistor:** $$R_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE(on)}}{I_{SC}} = \frac{0.65}{0.3} = 2.167 \text{ Ω}$$ Use standard value $R_{SC} = 2.2$ Ω, 1 W. *(1.5 marks)* **Step 2 — Fold ratio:** $$K = \frac{I_{FL}}{I_{SC}} = \frac{1.5}{0.3} = 5$$ **Step 3 — Divider ratio:** $$\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{V_{BE(on)}(K-1)}{V_{OUT(nom)}} = \frac{0.65 \times 4}{12} = 0.2167$$ **Step 4 — Divider total resistance** for 5 mA: $$R_1 + R_2 = \frac{V_{OUT(nom)}}{I_{div}} = \frac{12}{0.005} = 2400 \text{ Ω}$$ **Step 5 — Individual resistors:** $$R_1 = 0.2167 \times 2400 = 520 \text{ Ω} \;\;(\text{use } 510 \text{ Ω})$$ $$R_2 = 2400 - 520 = 1880 \text{ Ω} \;\;(\text{use } 1.8 \text{ kΩ})$$ *(3 marks for steps 2–5)* **Step 6 — Worst-case dissipation check:** At fold-back knee: $$P_{FL} = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_{FL} = (24 - 12) \times 1.5 = 18 \text{ W}$$ At short circuit: $$P_{SC} = V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC} = 24 \times 0.3 = 7.2 \text{ W}$$ **Worst case is at the fold-back knee: 18 W.** The pass transistor must be rated ≥ 25 W with adequate heatsink, e.g., 2N3055 in TO-3 case. *(2.5 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to include the divider contribution in $V_{BE}$.** Many students write $V_{BE} = I_L R_{SC}$ alone, which is the *simple* limiter. Always re-derive with $Q_{SL}$ base fed by a divider — that is the fold-back case. > 2. **Inverting $R_1$ and $R_2$.** $R_1$ is the *upper* resistor (between $V_{OUT}$ and tap); $R_2$ is the *lower* (between tap and ground). The fold-back formula uses $\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$, *not* $\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$. > 3. **Worst-case dissipation location.** Students often assume $P_{max}$ is at short circuit. In reality it can be at the *knee*. Always evaluate dissipation at both points and take the larger. > 4. **Using $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V for Ge transistors.** The problem must specify the transistor type. By default in 2024 KTU papers, assume silicon: $V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.65$ – 0.7 V. > 5. **Not specifying the sense-resistor power rating.** $R_{SC}$ dissipates $I_{SC}^2 \cdot R_{SC}$ continuously during a fault — choose at least 1 W rating. > 6. **Skipping the schematic in part (a).** A 7-mark question almost always *requires* a circuit diagram. Draw it even if the question seems conceptual. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Short-circuit protection** clamps the load current at a fixed value $I_{L(max)} = V_{BE}/R_{SC}$ using a sense resistor and a BJT. - [x] **Fold-back protection** adds a resistor divider so that the limit threshold is *load-voltage dependent*, giving a locus of negative slope on the $I_L$–$V_{OUT}$ plane. - [x] **Two key points:** Knee current $I_{FL}$ at the *rated* $V_{OUT}$, and short-circuit current $I_{SC}$ at $V_{OUT} = 0$. Always $I_{SC} < I_{FL}$. - [x] **Fold-back line equation:** $I_L = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_{SC}} - \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{R_{SC}} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$. - [x] **Fold ratio:** $K = I_{FL}/I_{SC} = 1 + \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{V_{BE}} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$. Typical $K$ = 2 to 5. - [x] **Design start:** compute $R_{SC}$ from $I_{SC}$, then the divider from the desired $K$. - [x] **Worst-case dissipation:** Evaluate $P_{pass} = (V_{IN}-V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$ at *both* the knee and the short — the maximum is the design constraint. - [x] **Sense-resistor rating:** must handle $I_{SC}^2 \cdot R_{SC}$ continuously. - [x] **Heatsink rule of thumb:** pick a pass transistor whose $P_{D(max)} \geq 1.5 \times$ the calculated worst-case dissipation. - [x] **Always draw the schematic** in KTU answers — it is worth 2–3 marks even when the question appears analytical. - [x] **Sign convention:** $V_{BE(on)}$ is positive (~0.65 V for Si); do not introduce negative signs into the magnitude formulas. - [x] **Real-world link:** Fold-back is the standard protection in the LM723, LM317 (with external pass transistor), and most industrial 5 V / 12 V / 24 V linear supplies.

topics.title: Timer IC 555 - Functional Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Timer IC 555 - Functional Diagram ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **IC 555 Timer** is a highly stable, monolithic integrated circuit capable of producing accurate time delays or oscillation. It is essentially an 8-pin analog/digital hybrid device whose internal architecture consists of two precision comparators, a bistable SR flip-flop (latch), a discharge transistor, a resistive voltage divider, and a totem-pole output driver. The functional diagram defines the **internal signal flow** between these sub-blocks, dictating the timing, thresholding, and pulse-generation behavior of the device. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The functional block diagram is a **CO4 / CO5 mapped** topic. Examiners expect students to draw the block diagram, label all internal nodes, and explain the role of the voltage divider, comparators, flip-flop, discharge transistor, and output stage in controlling the output state. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Dual-Gate Valve" Model) Imagine a **water tank with two sensors** — a *low-level sensor* at 1/3 full and a *high-level sensor* at 2/3 full. A mechanical latch controls a valve: - When water drops **below the low sensor (1/3)**, the latch **opens the inlet valve** (sets the output HIGH). - When water rises **above the high sensor (2/3)**, the latch **closes the inlet and opens the drain** (resets the output LOW). - A small drainage tap (the *discharge transistor*) is mechanically tied to the latch — it is shut when the output is HIGH and open when the output is LOW. This is exactly how the 555 works — only instead of water levels, it senses **voltage levels at the Trigger (Pin 2) and Threshold (Pin 6)**, and instead of a mechanical latch, it uses an **SR flip-flop**. ### 1.3 Standard Physical & Electrical Constants - **Resistor value of internal voltage divider:** **5 kΩ** (three equal resistors — hence the name *"555"*) - **Number of pins (DIP package):** **8** - **Supply voltage range (VCC):** **+4.5 V to +15 V (standard 555)**; up to **+18 V (CMOS variants)** - **Maximum output current:** **±200 mA** (totem-pole driver) - **Default threshold voltages:** Upper = **(2/3) VCC**, Lower = **(1/3) VCC** - **Inventor:** **Hans Camenzind**, 1972 (Signetics) > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology used by KTU paper setters:** > *Comparator, Voltage Divider, SR Flip-Flop (Latch), Discharge Transistor, Totem-Pole Output, Threshold Level, Trigger Level, Control Voltage, Reset Input.* --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Internal Functional Blocks (Block-by-Block) #### Block 1: Internal Voltage Divider (Three 5 kΩ Resistors) - A chain of **three equal 5 kΩ resistors** connected between VCC (Pin 8) and GND (Pin 1). - Produces two stable reference voltages without external biasing: - **(2/3) VCC** at the non-inverting input of Comparator 1 - **(1/3) VCC** at the inverting input of Comparator 2 - The midpoint is brought out to **Pin 5 (Control Voltage)** for external modulation. #### Block 2: Upper Threshold Comparator (Comparator 1) - **Non-inverting (+)** input: tied to the **(2/3) VCC** node. - **Inverting (−)** input: tied to **Pin 6 (THRESHOLD)**. - Output goes **HIGH** whenever $V_{\text{pin 6}} > \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$. - Its output feeds the **R (Reset)** input of the SR flip-flop. #### Block 3: Lower Threshold Comparator (Comparator 2) - **Inverting (−)** input: tied to the **(1/3) VCC** node. - **Non-inverting (+)** input: tied to **Pin 2 (TRIGGER)**. - Output goes **HIGH** whenever $V_{\text{pin 2}} < \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$. - Its output feeds the **S (Set)** input of the SR flip-flop. #### Block 4: SR Flip-Flop (Bistable Latch) - A level-triggered bistable memory element. - **Set (S) input** ← output of Comparator 2. - **Reset (R) input** ← output of Comparator 1. - **External Reset (Pin 4)** → asynchronous master reset, active LOW (forces Q = 0). - **Q̄ output** drives the **base of the discharge transistor**. - **Q output** drives the **output driver stage**. #### Block 5: Discharge Transistor (Pin 7) - An **open-collector NPN transistor** whose base is fed by Q̄. - When Q̄ = HIGH → transistor **ON** → Pin 7 is effectively shorted to ground (discharge path active). - When Q̄ = LOW → transistor **OFF** → Pin 7 is high-impedance (open). #### Block 6: Output Stage (Totem-Pole Driver) - A **push-pull complementary output** (one NPN pulling LOW, one PNP pulling HIGH). - Inverted relative to the Q output of the flip-flop. - Capable of sourcing/sinking **~200 mA**, sufficient to drive LEDs, relays, and TTL loads. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) | Quantity | Expression | Boundary Condition | Unit | |----------|------------|--------------------|------| | Upper internal reference | $V_{TH1} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ | When Control Voltage pin is open | Volts | | Lower internal reference | $V_{TH2} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ | When Control Voltage pin is open | Volts | | Modified upper reference | $V_{TH1}' = V_{CTRL}$ | When external $V_{CTRL}$ applied at Pin 5 | Volts | | Modified lower reference | $V_{TH2}' = \frac{1}{2} V_{CTRL}$ | When external $V_{CTRL}$ applied at Pin 5 | Volts | | Voltage divider total resistance | $R_{div} = 3 \times 5\,\text{k}\Omega = 15\,\text{k}\Omega$ | Always | Ohms | | Current through divider | $I_{div} = \frac{V_{CC}}{15\,\text{k}\Omega}$ | Quiescent | mA | | Comparator 1 output condition | $V_{\text{out,Comp1}} = 1$ if $V_{\text{pin6}} > V_{TH1}$ else 0 | Threshold sense | Logic | | Comparator 2 output condition | $V_{\text{out,Comp2}} = 1$ if $V_{\text{pin2}} < V_{TH2}$ else 0 | Trigger sense | Logic | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Monostable Multivibrator (One-shot)** → used in touch switches, missing-pulse detectors, debouncers. - **Astable Multivibrator (Oscillator)** → square-wave clocks, LED flashers, PWM generation. - **Schmitt Trigger** → by feeding Pin 6 from Pin 3, the IC becomes a precision inverting Schmitt trigger with adjustable hysteresis. - **Linear Ramp Generator** → by replacing the discharge resistor with a current source. - **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** → by varying Pin 5 (Control Voltage), the threshold levels shift, modulating the output frequency. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Whenever a question asks *"With the help of the functional block diagram, explain the working of the 555 timer"*, the expected answer flow is: **(1) State the six sub-blocks → (2) Explain the voltage divider and reference levels → (3) Describe comparator action → (4) Describe flip-flop state transitions → (5) Relate flip-flop output to discharge transistor and output pin.** --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Internal Reference Voltages The 555 IC contains **three identical resistors** in series, each of value $R = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, connected between $V_{CC}$ and ground. The total resistance is therefore: $$ R_{total} = R + R + R = 3R $$ **Step 1 — Identify the node voltages:** Let us call the two tap points **Node A** (junction of the upper 5 kΩ and middle 5 kΩ) and **Node B** (junction of the middle 5 kΩ and lower 5 kΩ). **Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider rule for Node A:** Node A is the junction between two 5 kΩ resistors (the upper one) and a stack of two 5 kΩ resistors (the middle and lower). The voltage at Node A referenced to ground is: $$ V_{A} = V_{CC} \times \frac{R_{below\,A}}{R_{total}} = V_{CC} \times \frac{R + R}{R + R + R} $$ **Step 3 — Simplify:** $$ V_{A} = V_{CC} \times \frac{2R}{3R} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the voltage divider rule for Node B:** Node B has only one 5 kΩ resistor below it (the lower one) and a stack of two above it: $$ V_{B} = V_{CC} \times \frac{R}{3R} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC} $$ **Step 5 — Mapping to the 555 functional diagram:** $$ V_{A} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Non-inverting input of Comparator 1} $$ $$ V_{B} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Inverting input of Comparator 2} $$ These are the **default upper and lower threshold voltages** of the device. ✔ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Modified Thresholds When Control Voltage is Externally Set When an external DC voltage $V_{CTRL}$ is applied to **Pin 5 (Control Voltage)**, it overrides the internal **(2/3) VCC** node. The two internal comparators are biased as follows: - Comparator 1 reference: now $V_{CTRL}$ - Comparator 2 reference: the midpoint of an internal 5 kΩ–5 kΩ sub-divider fed by $V_{CTRL}$ **Step 1 — Comparator 1 upper threshold becomes:** $$ V_{TH1}' = V_{CTRL} $$ **Step 2 — Comparator 2 lower threshold is half of the Control Voltage (due to the internal 5 kΩ–5 kΩ sub-divider feeding the (1/3) VCC node):** $$ V_{TH2}' = \frac{1}{2} V_{CTRL} $$ **Step 3 — Practical implication:** A KTU problem may state: *"If the Control Voltage pin is connected to 4 V, calculate the new threshold levels."* Plugging in $V_{CTRL} = 4\,\text{V}$: $$ V_{TH1}' = 4\,\text{V}, \qquad V_{TH2}' = 2\,\text{V} $$ > This is a **standard KTU sub-question** in Module 4 past papers. --- ### 3.3 KTU Analytical Matrix — Flip-Flop State Transition Logic Let $S$ = output of Comparator 2, $R$ = output of Comparator 1, $\overline{RST}$ = external reset (Pin 4), and $Q$ = the flip-flop's stored state. | $\overline{RST}$ (Pin 4) | $S$ (Trigger Comp) | $R$ (Threshold Comp) | Flip-Flop Action | $Q$ | Pin 3 (Output) | Pin 7 (Discharge) | |---------------------------|--------------------|----------------------|-------------------|-----|----------------|--------------------| | 0 | X | X | Direct Reset | 0 | LOW | ON (shorted to GND) | | 1 | 1 ($V_{\text{pin2}} < V_{TH2}$) | 0 | Set | 1 | HIGH | OFF (open) | | 1 | 0 ($V_{\text{pin2}} > V_{TH2}$) | 1 ($V_{\text{pin6}} > V_{TH1}$) | Reset | 0 | LOW | ON (shorted to GND) | | 1 | 0 | 0 | Hold (Memory) | $Q_{prev}$ | Unchanged | Unchanged | > [!NOTE] > **Note the "Hold" State:** When both comparator outputs are LOW (i.e., the trigger voltage is between (1/3) VCC and (2/3) VCC AND the threshold voltage is also between them), the flip-flop **retains its previous state**. This memory behavior is what makes the 555 function as a monostable or bistable element, depending on external connections. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of IC 555 ```mermaid flowchart TB VCCNODE["VCC Pin 8"]:::supply GNDNODE["GND Pin 1"]:::ground RSTNODE["Reset Pin 4"]:::resetNode CTRLNODE["Control Voltage Pin 5"]:::controlNode TRIGNODE["Trigger Pin 2"]:::inputA THRHNODE["Threshold Pin 6"]:::inputB OUTNODE["Output Pin 3"]:::output DISNODE["Discharge Pin 7"]:::output R1["R1 5kOhm"]:::resistor R2["R2 5kOhm"]:::resistor R3["R3 5kOhm"]:::resistor NODEA["Node A 2 by 3 VCC"]:::ref NODEB["Node B 1 by 3 VCC"]:::ref COMP1["Comparator 1 Upper Threshold"]:::comp COMP2["Comparator 2 Lower Threshold"]:::comp FLIPFLOP["SR Flip Flop Latch"]:::flipflop QBAR["QBAR Inverted Output"]:::qbar DRVTRAN["Discharge NPN Transistor"]:::transistor OUTDRV["Totem Pole Output Driver"]:::driver VCCNODE --> R1 --> NODEA NODEA --> R2 --> NODEB NODEB --> R3 --> GNDNODE NODEA -- non inverting plus of Comp1 --> COMP1 THRHNODE -- inverting minus of Comp1 --> COMP1 COMP1 -- R Reset input --> FLIPFLOP NODEB -- inverting minus of Comp2 --> COMP2 TRIGNODE -- non inverting plus of Comp2 --> COMP2 COMP2 -- S Set input --> FLIPFLOP RSTNODE -- asynchronous master reset --> FLIPFLOP CTRLNODE -- overrides Node A --> NODEA FLIPFLOP -- Q output --> OUTDRV OUTDRV --> OUTNODE FLIPFLOP -- QBAR --> QBAR --> DRVTRAN DRVTRAN --> DISNODE classDef supply fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ground fill:#e2e2e2,stroke:#555,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a87,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ref fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#36c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef comp fill:#d4edda,stroke:#2a7,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef flipflop fill:#e0c3fc,stroke:#73c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef qbar fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c83,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef transistor fill:#f9d5e5,stroke:#a47,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef driver fill:#cde7ff,stroke:#357,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef inputA fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#a85,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef inputB fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#a85,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#d1f0ff,stroke:#058,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resetNode fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c44,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef controlNode fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#248,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Flow — Astable State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> PowerOn PowerOn --> CapacitorCharging: Power applied
Capacitor initially LOW CapacitorCharging --> BelowLowerThreshold: Vc rising from 0V BelowLowerThreshold --> ChargingPhase1: Vc below 1 by 3 VCC ChargingPhase1 --> TriggerComparatorHigh: Vc crosses 1 by 3 VCC TriggerComparatorHigh --> SetFlipFlop: S becomes 1 SetFlipFlop --> OutputHigh: Q becomes 1
Discharge OFF OutputHigh --> CapacitorContinuesCharging: Output Pin 3 HIGH CapacitorContinuesCharging --> AboveUpperThreshold: Vc reaches 2 by 3 VCC AboveUpperThreshold --> ResetFlipFlop: R becomes 1 ResetFlipFlop --> OutputLow: Q becomes 0
Discharge ON OutputLow --> CapacitorDischarges: Pin 7 shorts cap to GND CapacitorDischarges --> BelowLowerThreshold: Vc falls below 1 by 3 VCC BelowLowerThreshold --> TriggerComparatorHigh: Cycle repeats ResetFlipFlop --> ForcedReset: External Reset Pin 4 LOW ForcedReset --> OutputLow: Asynchronous reset ``` ### 4.3 Pin Configuration Reference (8-Pin DIP) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DIP8[8 Pin DIP Top View] P1["Pin 1 GND"]:::pin P2["Pin 2 TRIGGER"]:::pinin P3["Pin 3 OUTPUT"]:::pinout P4["Pin 4 RESET"]:::pinrst P5["Pin 5 CTRL VOLT"]:::pinctrl P6["Pin 6 THRESHOLD"]:::pinin P7["Pin 7 DISCHARGE"]:::pinout P8["Pin 8 VCC"]:::pinsup end P1 --- P8 classDef pin fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef pinin fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#a85,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pinout fill:#d1f0ff,stroke:#058,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pinrst fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c44,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pinctrl fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#248,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pinsup fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Compilation Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `COMP1`, `NODEA`, `FLIPFLOP`). The reserved word `end` is never used as a node name. All labels are plain uppercase text without any bold or markdown formatting characters. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the term "Threshold Voltage" in the context of IC 555. List the default values of the two internal threshold levels used by the 555 timer.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Threshold Voltage** is the analog voltage applied to Pin 6 (THRESHOLD) of the 555 timer, which is continuously compared against the internal reference to determine the flip-flop state. **[1 mark]** - The two default internal threshold levels are derived from the three 5 kΩ resistors forming a voltage divider: **[1 mark]** - **Upper Threshold:** $V_{TH1} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ (used by Comparator 1) - **Lower Threshold:** $V_{TH2} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ (used by Comparator 2) - These levels can be modified by applying an external Control Voltage $V_{CTRL}$ at Pin 5, in which case the thresholds become $V_{CTRL}$ and $\frac{1}{2} V_{CTRL}$ respectively. **[1 mark]** --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the role of the discharge transistor (Pin 7) in the functional block diagram of IC 555?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - The discharge transistor is an **open-collector NPN transistor** whose base is connected to the **Q̄ (inverted) output** of the internal SR flip-flop. **[1 mark]** - When the flip-flop is in the **Reset** state (Q = 0, Q̄ = 1), the transistor is turned **ON**, providing a low-resistance path from Pin 7 to ground. This is used to **discharge an external timing capacitor** through an external resistor. **[1 mark]** - When the flip-flop is in the **Set** state (Q = 1, Q̄ = 0), the transistor is **OFF**, and Pin 7 behaves as an open circuit, allowing the external capacitor to **charge** through the external resistor. **[1 mark]** --- ### 📗 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of the 555 timer IC. Label all eight pins and all internal sub-blocks. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the upper and lower internal threshold voltages of the 555 timer. What happens to these levels when the Control Voltage pin (Pin 5) is externally forced to a voltage of 6 V? **(7 Marks)** ##### ✅ Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 Marks** - **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw all six internal blocks — the 3 × 5 kΩ voltage divider, two comparators, the SR flip-flop, the discharge transistor, and the totem-pole output stage — connected exactly as shown in the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1. Label **Pin 1 (GND), Pin 2 (TRIGGER), Pin 3 (OUTPUT), Pin 4 (RESET), Pin 5 (CONTROL VOLTAGE), Pin 6 (THRESHOLD), Pin 7 (DISCHARGE), Pin 8 (VCC)**. - **Operation (4 marks):** 1. The three internal 5 kΩ resistors form a divider producing **(2/3) VCC** and **(1/3) VCC** as references. **[1 mark]** 2. Comparator 1 (Upper Threshold) compares $V_{\text{pin 6}}$ with $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ and **resets** the flip-flop when threshold input exceeds this value. **[1 mark]** 3. Comparator 2 (Lower Threshold) compares $V_{\text{pin 2}}$ with $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ and **sets** the flip-flop when the trigger input falls below this value. **[1 mark]** 4. The flip-flop's Q output drives the totem-pole output buffer (Pin 3) and its Q̄ output drives the base of the discharge transistor (Pin 7). An active-LOW external reset (Pin 4) can override the flip-flop asynchronously. **[1 mark]** **Part (b) — 7 Marks** The three internal 5 kΩ resistors form a series chain between $V_{CC}$ and ground: $$ V_{A} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{2R}{3R} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} $$ $$ V_{B} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R}{3R} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC} $$ Hence $V_{TH1} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ and $V_{TH2} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$. **[2 marks]** **With $V_{CTRL} = 6$ V externally applied to Pin 5:** **[2 marks]** $$ V_{TH1}' = V_{CTRL} = 6\,\text{V} $$ $$ V_{TH2}' = \frac{1}{2} V_{CTRL} = 3\,\text{V} $$ Thus the new upper threshold is **6 V** and the new lower threshold is **3 V**. This is useful in **voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO)** and **PWM modulator** applications where the Control Voltage is dynamically varied. **[3 marks]** --- #### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE]** **[KTU University Exam — Supplementary June 2024]** **(a)** Draw the internal functional block diagram of the 555 timer and explain the function of each of the following sub-blocks: (i) Voltage Divider, (ii) SR Flip-Flop, (iii) Totem-Pole Output. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With reference to the functional diagram, construct the truth table showing the relationship between Trigger, Threshold, Reset, Output, and Discharge states. Clearly indicate the "hold" condition. **(7 Marks)** ##### ✅ Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 Marks** - **Functional Block Diagram (1 mark):** Standard 555 internal architecture as per Section 4.1. - **(i) Voltage Divider (2 marks):** Three equal 5 kΩ resistors in series between VCC and GND produce reference voltages $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ and $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$. The midpoint is externally accessible via Pin 5 (Control Voltage) for modulating the threshold levels. - **(ii) SR Flip-Flop (2 marks):** A bistable memory element. It is **Set** when Comparator 2 output is HIGH (trigger below $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$) and **Reset** when Comparator 1 output is HIGH (threshold above $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$). It maintains its previous state ("hold") when neither comparator output is asserted. An asynchronous master reset (Pin 4) directly clears it. - **(iii) Totem-Pole Output (2 marks):** A push-pull complementary output stage driven by the flip-flop's Q output. It can source or sink up to 200 mA, making the 555 capable of directly driving LEDs, relays, and TTL loads. It is essentially an inverter of the flip-flop Q state. **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Truth Table (5 marks):** | Reset (Pin 4) | Trigger (Pin 2) | Threshold (Pin 6) | Output (Pin 3) | Discharge (Pin 7) | |---------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|--------------------| | 0 | X (Don't care) | X (Don't care) | LOW (0) | ON (transistor ON) | | 1 | $< \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ | $< \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ | HIGH (1) | OFF (transistor OFF) | | 1 | $> \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ | $> \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ | LOW (0) | ON (transistor ON) | | 1 | $> \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ | $< \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ | Previous State (Hold) | Previous State (Hold) | **Explanation of "Hold" Condition (2 marks):** When both the trigger voltage lies above $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ **and** the threshold voltage lies below $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$, both comparator outputs are LOW. The SR flip-flop has no active set/reset input, so it **retains its previous state** — the output and discharge pin remain unchanged. This is the **memory behavior** that enables stable oscillation in astable mode and one-shot triggering in monostable mode. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to label Pin 5** as Control Voltage — many students omit Pin 5 entirely in the block diagram, losing 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing the comparator inputs** — the upper threshold comparator's non-inverting input is at $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ and its inverting input is Pin 6. Students frequently swap these. (-1 mark) > 3. **Not mentioning the "Hold" condition** in the truth table — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss in KTU 2024 Scheme questions. > 4. **Confusing the totem-pole stage with the flip-flop output** — the totem-pole is an **inverted buffer** of Q. The output is **NOT** Q itself. > 5. **Writing $V_{TH2}' = \frac{1}{3} V_{CTRL}$** instead of $\frac{1}{2} V_{CTRL}$ when Control Voltage is applied. The (1/3) VCC node is derived from a 5 kΩ–5 kΩ sub-divider fed by Pin 5, giving exactly $\frac{V_{CTRL}}{2}$. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - 🔸 The **555 timer** is built around **6 internal blocks**: 3 × 5 kΩ divider, 2 comparators, 1 SR flip-flop, 1 discharge transistor, and 1 totem-pole output driver. - 🔸 The **name "555"** comes from the **three 5 kΩ resistors** in the internal voltage divider. - 🔸 **Default thresholds:** Upper = $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$, Lower = $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$. - 🔸 **External Control Voltage** at Pin 5 changes thresholds to $V_{CTRL}$ and $\frac{1}{2} V_{CTRL}$. - 🔸 **Pin 2 (TRIGGER)** is the **non-inverting** input of the lower comparator — a **LOW signal below (1/3) VCC sets** the flip-flop. - 🔸 **Pin 6 (THRESHOLD)** is the **inverting** input of the upper comparator — a **HIGH signal above (2/3) VCC resets** the flip-flop. - 🔸 **Pin 4 (RESET)** is **active-LOW asynchronous master reset** — forces output LOW and turns ON the discharge transistor. - 🔸 **Pin 7 (DISCHARGE)** is an **open-collector NPN** driven by **Q̄**, not Q. - 🔸 **Pin 3 (OUTPUT)** is the **Q output through an inverting totem-pole buffer**, capable of **±200 mA** drive. - 🔸 The **"Hold" state** of the SR flip-flop (when both comparators output LOW) is what gives the 555 its **memory** — enabling astable and monostable operations. - 🔸 The **8-pin DIP** package is the standard form factor. CMOS variants (e.g., LMC555, TLC555) have the same pinout but offer lower quiescent current and rail-to-rail output swing. - 🔸 **Pin 1 is always GND** and **Pin 8 is always VCC** — never interchange them. - 🔸 The **maximum source/sink current of 200 mA** is a common exam question; exceeding it will damage the output stage. - 🔸 Always draw the **block diagram FIRST** in a 7-mark question, then narrate the signal flow — examiners award partial credit for a correctly drawn diagram even if the explanation is incomplete. ---

topics.title: Astable and Monostable Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable Operations**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The 555 Timer — The Foundation Block

The **IC 555 Timer**, introduced by Hans Camenzind in 1972, is a highly stable, monolithic timing circuit capable of producing accurate time delays or oscillation. In **Module 4**, we study the 555 as a building block for two crucial multivibrator circuits — **Astable** and **Monostable** — both of which fall under the *non-linear (bistable/astable) applications* of the device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO4** — *“Design multivibrator circuits and waveform generators using op-amps and special function ICs.”* Expect direct 7–14 mark derivations in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Astable Multivibrator (Free-Running Oscillator)

**Formal Definition:**
An **Astable Multivibrator** using the 555 timer is a *free-running* oscillator that has **no stable state**. It continuously switches between HIGH and LOW states without any external trigger, producing a continuous train of rectangular pulses.

**Real-World Analogy — The Automatic Traffic Signal 🚦**
Imagine a traffic light at a busy junction that nobody manually controls. It automatically cycles: **Green → Yellow → Red → Green**... without ever stopping. Nobody "presses a button" to start it — it simply oscillates by itself. That is exactly what an astable does — it self-oscillates between two quasi-stable states.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Has **TWO quasi-stable states** (no true stable state) and is **trigger-free**.

---

## 1.3 Monostable Multivibrator (One-Shot)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Monostable Multivibrator** using the 555 timer has **one stable state** and **one quasi-stable state**. It remains in the stable state indefinitely until an **external trigger pulse** is applied, after which it flips to the quasi-stable state for a precisely timed duration $t_W$ and then automatically returns to the stable state.

**Real-World Analogy — The Staircase Light in a Building 🪜**
You walk into a dark stairwell, press the switch (TRIGGER), the light turns ON. After a fixed time (say 30 seconds), it automatically turns OFF — regardless of whether you are still in the stairwell. You must press the switch AGAIN to turn it back on. That is a **one-shot** behavior — one trigger → one timed output pulse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Has **ONE stable state** + **ONE quasi-stable state**, requires an **external negative-going trigger** at pin 2.

---

## 1.4 Why Study These Two Together?

| Feature | Astable | Monostable |
|---|---|---|
| External Trigger | Not required | Mandatory |
| Stable States | 0 (quasi-stable only) | 1 |
| Quasi-Stable States | 2 | 1 |
| Output Waveform | Continuous square wave | Single pulse per trigger |
| Primary Use | Clock generators, LED flashers, tone generators | Pulse stretching, missing pulse detection, debouncing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of the 555 Timer (Quick Recap)

The 555 contains:
- A **voltage divider** (three $5\text{ k}\Omega$ resistors) creating $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ reference levels.
- **Two comparators** — Upper Comparator (C1, threshold input) and Lower Comparator (C2, trigger input).
- An **SR Flip-Flop** that latches the comparator outputs.
- A **discharge transistor** (open-collector) connected to pin 7.
- An **output stage** providing up to **200 mA** of sink/source current.

---

## 2.2 Astable Operation — Phased Analysis

### Phase I: Capacitor Charging (Output is HIGH)
- Initially, discharge transistor Q1 is **OFF**, so the external capacitor $C$ charges through the series combination of $R_A$ and $R_B$ from $V_{CC}$.
- The output remains **HIGH** as long as the voltage across $C$ has not reached $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$.
- Charging path: $V_{CC} \rightarrow R_A \rightarrow R_B \rightarrow C$.

### Phase II: Threshold Trip
- When $V_C \uparrow = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, the **upper comparator fires**, **RESET** the flip-flop.
- Output goes **LOW**, and the discharge transistor Q1 turns **ON**.

### Phase III: Capacitor Discharging (Output is LOW)
- $C$ now discharges through $R_B$ and the discharge transistor (pin 7) to ground.
- Output stays **LOW** as long as $V_C > \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$.

### Phase IV: Trigger Trip
- When $V_C \downarrow = \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$, the **lower comparator fires**, **SET** the flip-flop.
- Output returns **HIGH**, Q1 turns **OFF**, and the cycle repeats indefinitely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cycle repeats perpetually — this is the *free-running* nature of the astable.

---

## 2.3 Monostable Operation — Phased Analysis

### Stable State (Resting Condition)
- No trigger applied → $V_{\text{trigger}} > \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ → Lower comparator OFF → Flip-flop RESET → Output **LOW** → Discharge transistor **ON** → Pin 7 shorts capacitor to ground → $V_C \approx 0$.

### Quasi-Stable State (After Trigger)
- A **negative-going trigger** drops pin 2 below $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ → Lower comparator fires → Flip-flop **SET** → Output goes **HIGH** → Discharge transistor **OFF** → $C$ begins charging through $R_A$ only.

### Return to Stable State
- When $V_C = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, the upper comparator fires → Flip-flop RESET → Output returns **LOW** → Discharge transistor **ON** → $C$ rapidly discharges to 0 V → circuit returns to its stable (resting) state.

> [!NOTE]
> The output pulse width $t_W$ is determined **solely by the external $R_A C$ network**, completely independent of the trigger pulse width (provided $t_{\text{trigger}} < t_W$).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these equations appear in nearly every 555 ESE paper.**

### Astable Mode Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Charging Time (HIGH), $t_c$ | $0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$ | seconds |
| Discharging Time (LOW), $t_d$ | $0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$ | seconds |
| Total Time Period, $T$ | $0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$ | seconds |
| Frequency of Oscillation, $f$ | $\dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$ | Hz |
| Duty Cycle, $D$ | $\dfrac{(R_A + R_B)}{(R_A + 2R_B)} \times 100\%$ | percent |
| Frequency of Output | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$ | Hz |

### Monostable Mode Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Output Pulse Width, $t_W$ | $1.1 \cdot R_A \cdot C$ | seconds |
| Trigger Pulse Width | $t_{\text{trigger}} < t_W$ | seconds |
| Recovery Time, $t_r$ | $\approx 100$ ns (typical) | seconds |
| Charging Threshold | $\frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ to $\frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ | volts |

### Critical Design Constraints

- The constant **0.693** comes from $\ln(2) = 0.6931$ (since $V_C$ charges from $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, ratio = 2).
- The constant **1.1** comes from $\ln(3) = 1.0986 \approx 1.1$ (charging from **0** to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ through the same network — ratio = 3).
- In astable mode, $D$ can **never** be exactly 50% unless $R_A \ll R_B$ (then $D \rightarrow 50\%$ asymptotically). For true 50% duty cycle, use a diode across $R_B$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Astable:** LED flashers, PWM generators, tone generators for alarms, clock pulse source for digital logic, Morse code keyers.
- **Monostable:** Switch debouncing in digital inputs, missing-pulse detectors, pulse delay lines, frequency dividers, sequential timing controllers, automotive turn-signal circuits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Astable Time Period

### 3.1.1 Charging Equation (Output HIGH)

During the charging phase, the capacitor $C$ charges from its initial voltage $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ toward the final voltage $V_{CC}$ through the total resistance $(R_A + R_B)$. Using the standard first-order RC charging equation:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{final} - (V_{final} - V_{initial}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_c}
$$

Substituting $V_{final} = V_{CC}$, $V_{initial} = \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$, and time constant $\tau_c = (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}\right) e^{-t/((R_A + R_B)C)}
$$

$$
V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3}V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/((R_A + R_B)C)}
$$

The charging phase ends when $V_C(t) = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3}V_{CC} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3}V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_c/((R_A + R_B)C)}
$$

Rearranging by moving terms to isolate the exponential:

$$
\frac{2}{3}V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_c/((R_A + R_B)C)} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}
$$

$$
\frac{2}{3}V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_c/((R_A + R_B)C)} = \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}
$$

Dividing both sides by $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$:

$$
e^{-t_c/((R_A + R_B)C)} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$
-\frac{t_c}{(R_A + R_B)C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_c = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C
$$

> **Conclusion:** The HIGH time is directly proportional to the sum of both resistors, multiplied by $C$ and the natural log of 2.

### 3.1.2 Discharging Equation (Output LOW)

During the discharging phase, the capacitor discharges from $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ toward $0$ V through $R_B$ only (since the discharge transistor shorts pin 7 to ground and bypasses $R_A$). The time constant is $\tau_d = R_B \cdot C$:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{initial} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_d} = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/(R_B C)}
$$

The discharge ends when $V_C(t) = \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$:

$$
\frac{1}{3}V_{CC} = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_d/(R_B C)}
$$

Dividing both sides by $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$:

$$
e^{-t_d/(R_B C)} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
-\frac{t_d}{R_B C} = -\ln(2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_d = R_B C \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C
$$

### 3.1.3 Total Time Period and Frequency

$$
T = t_c + t_d = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C + 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C
$$

$$
T = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C
$$

Frequency is the reciprocal:

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}
$$

### 3.1.4 Duty Cycle

By definition, duty cycle is the fraction of time the output is HIGH:

$$
D = \frac{t_c}{T} = \frac{0.693(R_A + R_B)C}{0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C} = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
D\% = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Monostable Pulse Width

In the monostable mode, the capacitor charges from **0 V** (since the discharge transistor keeps it grounded in the stable state) up to the upper threshold $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, through $R_A$ only. The final voltage is $V_{CC}$, initial voltage is $0$ V, and time constant is $\tau = R_A C$:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{CC} - V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/(R_A C)} = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t/(R_A C)}\right)
$$

The pulse width $t_W$ is the time taken to reach $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3}V_{CC} = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t_W/(R_A C)}\right)
$$

Dividing by $V_{CC}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-t_W/(R_A C)}
$$

Isolating the exponential:

$$
e^{-t_W/(R_A C)} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
-\frac{t_W}{R_A C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln(3)
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_W = R_A C \cdot \ln(3) = 1.0986 \cdot R_A C \approx 1.1 R_A C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\ln(3)$ here and $\ln(2)$ in astable?** Because the monostable capacitor starts from **0 V** and charges to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (ratio of 3:1), while the astable capacitor oscillates between $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (ratio of 2:1).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** Design an astable multivibrator using 555 timer for a frequency of **1 kHz** with a duty cycle of **60%**. Assume $C = 0.1\,\mu F$ and $V_{CC} = 12\,V$.

**Solution:**

Given: $f = 1\text{ kHz} = 1000\text{ Hz}$, $D = 60\% = 0.6$, $C = 0.1\,\mu F = 10^{-7}\text{ F}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the time period:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\text{ ms} = 10^{-3}\text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the HIGH and LOW times using duty cycle:**

$$
t_c = D \cdot T = 0.6 \times 10^{-3} = 0.6\text{ ms}
$$

$$
t_d = T - t_c = 1\text{ ms} - 0.6\text{ ms} = 0.4\text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in formula form:**

$$
t_c = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C = 0.6 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
t_d = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_B$ from the discharge equation:**

$$
R_B = \frac{t_d}{0.693 \cdot C} = \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{0.693 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{6.93 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
R_B = 5772\,\Omega \approx 5.77\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R_A$ from the charging equation:**

$$
R_A + R_B = \frac{t_c}{0.693 \cdot C} = \frac{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.693 \times 10^{-7}} = 8658\,\Omega
$$

$$
R_A = 8658 - 5772 = 2886\,\Omega \approx 2.89\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Final Answer:** $R_A \approx 2.89\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B \approx 5.77\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu F$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 555 Timer — Astable Mode Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    VCC1[VCC PIN 8] --> R1A[RA]
    R1A --> NODE_A[NODE A]
    NODE_A --> R1B[RB]
    R1B --> NODE_B[NODE B]
    NODE_B --> CAP[C TIMER CAPACITOR]
    CAP --> GND1[GND PIN 1]
    
    NODE_B --> PIN7[PIN 7 DISCHARGE]
    PIN7 --> Q1[DISCHARGE TRANSISTOR NPN]
    Q1 --> GND2[GROUND]
    
    PIN2[PIN 2 TRIGGER] --> CMP1[LOWER COMPARATOR]
    PIN6[PIN 6 THRESHOLD] --> CMP2[UPPER COMPARATOR]
    CMP1 --> FF[SR FLIP FLOP]
    CMP2 --> FF
    FF --> Q1
    FF --> PIN3[PIN 3 OUTPUT]
    
    VCC2[VCC] --> DIV[VOLTAGE DIVIDER 5K 5K 5K]
    DIV --> REF1[1 DIVIDE 3 VCC REF]
    DIV --> REF2[2 DIVIDE 3 VCC REF]
    REF1 --> CMP1
    REF2 --> CMP2
    
    PIN8[VCC] --> CTRL[CIRCUIT SUPPLY]
    PIN4[PIN 4 RESET] --> CTRL
    PIN5[PIN 5 CTRL VOLTAGE] --> REF2
```

## 4.2 Astable Waveform Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PHASE1[CHARGING PHASE]
        A1[OUTPUT HIGH] --> B1[CAPACITOR CHARGES THROUGH RA AND RB]
        B1 --> C1[VC RISES FROM 1 DIV 3 VCC TO 2 DIV 3 VCC]
    end
    
    subgraph PHASE2[DISCHARGING PHASE]
        A2[OUTPUT LOW] --> B2[CAPACITOR DISCHARGES THROUGH RB ONLY]
        B2 --> C2[VC FALLS FROM 2 DIV 3 VCC TO 1 DIV 3 VCC]
    end
    
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE1
```

## 4.3 555 Timer — Monostable Mode Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    VCC3[VCC PIN 8] --> R2A[RA EXTERNAL]
    R2A --> NODE_C[NODE C TIMER JUNCTION]
    NODE_C --> CAP2[C TIMER CAPACITOR]
    CAP2 --> GND3[GND PIN 1]
    
    NODE_C --> PIN7B[PIN 7 DISCHARGE TRANSISTOR]
    PIN7B --> Q2[DISCHARGE SWITCH]
    Q2 --> GND4[GROUND]
    
    TRIG[EXTERNAL NEGATIVE TRIGGER PIN 2] --> CMP3[LOWER COMPARATOR]
    THRESH[PIN 6 CONNECTED TO NODE C] --> CMP4[UPPER COMPARATOR]
    CMP3 --> FF2[SR FLIP FLOP]
    CMP4 --> FF2
    FF2 --> Q2
    FF2 --> PIN3B[PIN 3 SINGLE PULSE OUTPUT]
    
    VCC4[VCC] --> DIV2[VOLTAGE DIVIDER 5K 5K 5K]
    DIV2 --> REFL[1 DIV 3 VCC REFERENCE]
    DIV2 --> REFH[2 DIV 3 VCC REFERENCE]
    REFL --> CMP3
    REFH --> CMP4
```

## 4.4 Monostable State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STABLE
    STABLE --> QUASI : TRIGGER BELOW 1 DIV 3 VCC
    QUASI --> STABLE : VC REACHES 2 DIV 3 VCC AFTER TIME TW
    STABLE --> STABLE : NO TRIGGER
```

## 4.5 Signal Level Summary Table

| State | Pin 2 Trigger | Pin 6 Threshold | Pin 3 Output | Pin 7 Discharge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Astable HIGH** | Above $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ | Below $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ | HIGH | OFF |
| **Astable LOW** | Below $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ | Above $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ | LOW | ON |
| **Monostable Stable** | Above $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ | $V_C \approx 0$ | LOW | ON |
| **Monostable Quasi** | Trigger active | $V_C$ rising through $R_A C$ | HIGH | OFF |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List the three internal comparators' reference voltages in a 555 timer. Explain how these thresholds are used to determine the charging and discharging limits in an astable multivibrator.*

**Model Answer:**

The internal voltage divider in the 555 timer creates **two reference voltages** at exactly $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ from the supply rail. These are non-adjustable (unless pin 5 — the Control Voltage terminal — is externally driven).

In astable mode, the timing capacitor $C$ charges and discharges **between these two thresholds**:
- When $V_C$ rises to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, the **upper comparator** resets the internal flip-flop → output goes LOW.
- When $V_C$ falls to $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$, the **lower comparator** sets the flip-flop → output goes HIGH.

This switching behavior between the two thresholds is what produces the rectangular output waveform, and the **ratio of the thresholds (2:1)** is what gives rise to the $\ln(2) = 0.693$ constant in the time equations.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare astable and monostable multivibrators using 555 timer in terms of stable states, trigger requirement, and output waveform.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Astable Multivibrator | Monostable Multivibrator |
|---|---|---|
| Stable States | **None** (two quasi-stable) | **One stable + one quasi-stable** |
| External Trigger | **Not required** — self-oscillates | **Mandatory** — negative trigger at pin 2 |
| Output Waveform | Continuous square wave (free-running) | Single pulse per trigger (one-shot) |
| Time Period / Width | $T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$ | $t_W = 1.1 R_A C$ |
| Frequency Formula | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$ | Not periodic (event-triggered) |
| Components | $R_A$, $R_B$, $C$ | $R_A$, $C$ only |
| Typical Use | Clock generators, oscillators | Pulse stretching, delay generation |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **astable multivibrator using 555 timer IC**. Derive the expressions for charging time, discharging time, total time period, and frequency of oscillation.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Circuit Description:**
In astable configuration, the 555 timer is wired as follows:
- Pin 8 (VCC) and Pin 4 (RESET) connected to $V_{CC}$.
- Pin 1 (GND) to ground.
- Pin 5 (CONTROL VOLTAGE) decoupled to ground through a **0.01 µF** capacitor to filter noise.
- **External timing network:** $R_A$ between VCC and pin 7, $R_B$ between pin 7 and pin 6/pin 2 junction, and $C$ from pin 6 to ground.
- Pin 2 (TRIGGER) and pin 6 (THRESHOLD) are **shorted together** and connected to the $R_B$–$C$ junction.
- Pin 3 is the output.

**Operation Phases:**

**Phase 1 — Capacitor Charging (Output HIGH):**
Assume initially $C$ is discharged. The discharge transistor Q1 is OFF. $C$ charges through the series path $V_{CC} \rightarrow R_A \rightarrow R_B \rightarrow C$ with time constant $\tau_c = (R_A + R_B)C$. The output at pin 3 is **HIGH**.

**Phase 2 — Upper Threshold Trip:**
When $V_C = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, the upper comparator fires, resetting the internal SR flip-flop. This causes:
- Output at pin 3 → **LOW**
- Discharge transistor Q1 → **ON** (pin 7 effectively grounded)

**Phase 3 — Capacitor Discharging (Output LOW):**
$C$ now discharges through $R_B$ into pin 7 (and into the ON transistor) to ground. Time constant $\tau_d = R_B C$. Output remains **LOW**.

**Phase 4 — Lower Threshold Trip:**
When $V_C = \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$, the lower comparator fires, setting the flip-flop:
- Output → **HIGH**
- Q1 → **OFF**
- The cycle restarts with charging.

**Derivations:**

Starting with the standard RC charging equation and applying the boundary conditions (as derived in **Section 3.1.1** of these notes):

**Charging Time:**

$$
t_c = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C
$$

**Discharging Time:**

$$
t_d = R_B C \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693 \cdot R_B C
$$

**Total Time Period:**

$$
T = t_c + t_d = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C + 0.693 R_B C
$$

$$
T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C
$$

**Frequency of Oscillation:**

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}
$$

These are the four key KTU-marked equations required for full credit.

---

**(b)** Design an astable multivibrator using 555 timer IC to generate a square wave with **frequency 500 Hz** and **duty cycle 75%**. Take $C = 0.5\,\mu F$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $f = 500$ Hz
- $D = 75\% = 0.75$
- $C = 0.5\,\mu F = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ F

**Step 1 — Time Period:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{500} = 2\text{ ms} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — HIGH and LOW Times:**

$$
t_c = D \times T = 0.75 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 1.5\text{ ms}
$$

$$
t_d = T - t_c = 2\text{ ms} - 1.5\text{ ms} = 0.5\text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_B$ using $t_d$:**

$$
t_d = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C
$$

$$
0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.693 \times R_B \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
R_B = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.693 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{0.693 \times 10^{-6}} = 1443\,\Omega \approx 1.44\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_A$ using $t_c$:**

$$
t_c = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C
$$

$$
1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.693 \times (R_A + 1443) \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
R_A + 1443 = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.693 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 4329\,\Omega
$$

$$
R_A = 4329 - 1443 = 2886\,\Omega \approx 2.89\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Duty Cycle:**

$$
D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = \frac{2886 + 1443}{2886 + 2886} = \frac{4329}{5772} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $R_A \approx 2.89\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B \approx 1.44\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.5\,\mu F$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **monostable multivibrator using 555 timer** with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the output pulse width $t_W$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Circuit Description:**
- Pin 8 (VCC) and Pin 4 to $V_{CC}$.
- Pin 5 decoupled to ground via 0.01 µF.
- **External network:** $R_A$ between VCC and pin 7, and $C$ between pin 7 and ground.
- **Crucial difference from astable:** Pin 6 (THRESHOLD) and Pin 7 (DISCHARGE) are **shorted together** (no separate $R_B$).
- Pin 2 is the **TRIGGER input** — receives a negative-going pulse (must drop below $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$).
- Pin 3 is the output.

**Stable State (Quiescent):**
With no trigger applied, $V_{\text{pin2}} > \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$, lower comparator is OFF, flip-flop is in **RESET** state, output at pin 3 is **LOW**, and the discharge transistor is **ON** — keeping the capacitor $C$ clamped at 0 V.

**Trigger Event:**
A negative-going trigger pulse pulls pin 2 below $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$. The lower comparator fires and **SETS** the flip-flop:
- Output goes **HIGH**
- Discharge transistor turns **OFF**
- Capacitor $C$ begins charging through $R_A$ from 0 V toward $V_{CC}$

**Quasi-Stable State:**
During this state, the output is HIGH. The capacitor voltage rises exponentially:
$$
V_C(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t/(R_A C)}\right)
$$

**Return to Stable:**
When $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, the upper comparator fires and **RESETS** the flip-flop:
- Output returns to **LOW**
- Discharge transistor turns **ON**
- Capacitor rapidly discharges to 0 V

The circuit is now back in its stable state, awaiting the next trigger.

**Derivation of Pulse Width:**

Setting $V_C(t_W) = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$:

$$
\frac{2}{3}V_{CC} = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t_W/(R_A C)}\right)
$$

$$
e^{-t_W/(R_A C)} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

$$
-\frac{t_W}{R_A C} = -\ln(3)
$$

$$
\boxed{t_W = R_A C \cdot \ln(3) = 1.1 R_A C}
$$

---

**(b)** A monostable 555 circuit uses $R_A = 100\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 1\,\mu F$. Calculate the output pulse width. If the trigger pulse applied has a width of **200 µs**, will the circuit function correctly? Justify your answer.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the pulse width:**

$$
t_W = 1.1 \times R_A \times C = 1.1 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
t_W = 1.1 \times 0.1 = 0.11\text{ s} = 110\text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with trigger pulse width:**

Trigger width: $t_{\text{trigger}} = 200\,\mu s = 0.2\text{ ms}$

Required condition for **correct operation**:

$$
t_{\text{trigger}} < t_W
$$

$$
0.2\text{ ms} < 110\text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The condition is **satisfactorily met**. The trigger pulse (200 µs) is much narrower than the generated output pulse (110 ms). The circuit will function correctly and will produce a clean 110 ms output pulse per trigger.

**Step 3 — Additional check — Recovery time:**

The recovery time of a standard 555 is approximately **100 ns** (negligible). After $t_W$ elapses, the capacitor discharges rapidly through pin 7's transistor, and the circuit becomes ready for the next trigger almost immediately.

> [!NOTE]
> If $t_{\text{trigger}} > t_W$, the comparator remains triggered throughout — the output may stay HIGH or behave erratically. The condition $t_{\text{trigger}} < t_W$ is **mandatory**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (avoid these to score full marks):**
> 1. **Mixing up the constants:** Astable uses **0.693** ($\ln 2$); Monostable uses **1.1** ($\ln 3$). Writing 1.1 in astable or 0.693 in monostable = **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Capacitor values are typically in µF, nF, or pF — failing to convert to Farads before plugging into equations is a **frequent, costly error** worth 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the 2× on $R_B$ in the time period formula:** Writing $T = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$ instead of $T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$ is a **classic blunder** costing 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not stating boundary conditions:** Examiners want to see "$V_C$ charges from $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$" explicitly written — skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing pin numbers:** Pin 7 = Discharge, Pin 6 = Threshold, Pin 2 = Trigger, Pin 3 = Output. Drawing them wrongly on the circuit diagram costs marks.
> 6. **Astable duty cycle cannot reach 50%** without a diode steering network — if the question asks for 50%, the standard 555 circuit cannot achieve it; mention the diode modification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] The **555 timer** has two comparators, an SR flip-flop, a discharge transistor, and a 3-resistor voltage divider giving $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ references.
- [x] **Astable** = no stable state, self-oscillates, **two quasi-stable states**, output is a continuous square wave.
- [x] **Monostable** = one stable state, one quasi-stable state, requires an **external negative trigger**, output is a single timed pulse.
- [x] **Astable charging time:** $t_c = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$ — capacitor charges through **both** resistors.
- [x] **Astable discharging time:** $t_d = 0.693 \cdot R_B C$ — capacitor discharges through $R_B$ only.
- [x] **Astable total period:** $T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$.
- [x] **Astable frequency:** $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$.
- [x] **Astable duty cycle:** $D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ — always greater than 50%.
- [x] **Monostable pulse width:** $t_W = 1.1 R_A C$ — independent of trigger pulse width.
- [x] **Trigger condition for monostable:** $t_{\text{trigger}} < t_W$ (trigger must be **narrower** than the output pulse).
- [x] **Recovery time** of monostable ≈ 100 ns — the minimum wait before the next trigger.
- [x] **The constant 0.693** comes from $\ln 2$ (ratio of upper to lower threshold in astable = 2:1).
- [x] **The constant 1.1** comes from $\ln 3$ (ratio of $V_{CC}$ to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ in monostable = 3:1).
- [x] **Pin 4 (RESET)** must be tied HIGH for normal operation — pulling it LOW forces the output to LOW.
- [x] **Pin 5 (CONTROL VOLTAGE)** can modulate thresholds externally — useful for VCO and PWM applications.
- [x] Standard supply range: **+5 V to +15 V** (bipolar 555); CMOS 555 can operate down to **+1.5 V**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Voltage Controlled Oscillator and Application of VCO IC LM566, Phase Locked Loop: Basic Building Block
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Voltage Controlled Oscillator and Application of VCO IC LM566, Phase Locked Loop: Basic Building Block

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) and Phase Locked Loop (PLL)

## 1.1 Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) — Core Definition

A **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** is an electronic oscillator circuit whose instantaneous output frequency $f_o$ is a linear function of an externally applied DC control voltage $V_C$. It is the frequency-domain counterpart of a voltage-to-frequency converter, and it forms the heart of every frequency-modulation, frequency-synthesis, and phase-locked system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *“A VCO is an oscillator in which the output frequency is linearly proportional to an externally applied control voltage. When the control input is zero, the VCO operates at its free-running (center) frequency.”*

Mathematically, the ideal transfer characteristic of a VCO is expressed as:

$$
f_o = f_{o(\text{center})} + K_{VCO} \cdot V_C
$$

where:
- $f_o$ = instantaneous output frequency (Hz)
- $f_{o(\text{center})}$ = free-running (quiescent) frequency when $V_C = 0$
- $K_{VCO}$ = **VCO gain / sensitivity** (Hz/V)
- $V_C$ = input control voltage (V)

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Voltage-Operated Piano"

Imagine a piano where, instead of striking keys, you twist a single big knob. Turning the knob slightly makes the piano emit a *low* musical note; turning it more makes the note *higher*. The knob is your **control voltage** $V_C$, and the musical note is the **output frequency** $f_o$. The piano always produces a steady note, but its pitch (frequency) obediently follows the knob position. That is exactly what a VCO does in the electrical world — it converts a steady DC voltage into a sine/square/triangular wave whose frequency rises and falls with the knob voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The VCO is *not* a voltage-to-amplitude converter. It is a **voltage-to-frequency** converter. A larger $V_C$ does **not** make the output larger — it makes the oscillations **faster** (i.e., more cycles per second).

### Key Electrical Parameters of a Practical VCO

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range (LM566) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Center frequency | $f_o$ | Hz | 1 Hz to 1 MHz |
| VCO gain | $K_{VCO}$ | Hz/V | 6.6 kHz/V (fixed) |
| Control voltage range | $V_C$ | V | 0.75 V to $(V^+ - 0.75 V)$ |
| Frequency linearity error | — | % | ±0.5 % (typ.) |
| Power supply | $V^+$ | V | ±5 V to ±12 V (dual) |

---

## 1.2 The LM566 VCO Monolithic IC

The **LM566** (originally introduced by National Semiconductor) is an 8-pin monolithic VCO designed for high linearity, wide frequency range, and excellent temperature stability. It is one of the most frequently asked-about VCO ICs in KTU examinations.

### Pin Configuration (8-Pin DIP)

| Pin | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | GND | Ground (or $V^-$ for dual supply) |
| 2 | NC | No connection |
| 3 | VCO_OUT (square) | Square-wave output |
| 4 | VCO_OUT (triangular) | Triangular-wave output |
| 5 | $R_1$ | External timing resistor terminal |
| 6 | $C_1$ | External timing capacitor terminal |
| 7 | $V_C$ | Control voltage input (modulating signal) |
| 8 | $V^+$ | Positive supply (typically +12 V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The LM566 simultaneously provides **two outputs** from pin 3 (square wave) and pin 4 (triangular wave). The control voltage at pin 7 modulates **both** outputs simultaneously.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Dual-Speaker Siren"

Think of the LM566 as a fire-truck siren that has two speakers stacked on top of each other — one is a *buzzer* (square-wave output) and the other is a *whistle* (triangular-wave output). Both are driven by the same internal motor (the current-controlled multivibrator), so when you push the accelerator pedal (control voltage $V_C$), **both** speakers get louder **and** faster together. You never have to control them separately.

### Internal Functional Architecture

The LM566 is built around a **current-controlled multivibrator** core, with precision current mirrors and a Schmitt-trigger comparator. The two main sub-circuits are:

1. **Current Source Network:** Produces a charging current $I$ proportional to $(V^+ - V_C)$, which is mirrored into the timing capacitor $C_1$.
2. **Bistable Comparator (Schmitt Trigger):** Switches state when the capacitor voltage crosses predefined upper and lower thresholds, generating the square wave; its inverted output is integrated to form the triangular wave.

---

## 1.3 Phase Locked Loop (PLL) — Core Definition

A **Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a negative-feedback control system that forces the phase of a locally generated output signal to track the phase of an incoming reference signal. Once "locked," the output frequency becomes *exactly* equal to the input frequency (or an integer multiple/divisor of it).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *“A PLL is a closed-loop frequency-control system that synchronizes the output of a VCO with an input reference signal in frequency as well as in phase.”*

The PLL is essentially an *electronic marionette*: it pulls the VCO’s string (voltage) so that the VCO’s dance (phase) exactly mimics the reference dancer’s steps.

### Conceptual Analogy — Two Children Walking in Step

Imagine two children holding hands. The first child is the **reference signal** (input), walking at a steady pace. The second child is the **VCO** (output), who must walk in step. The **Phase Detector** is the "speed judge" who screams *"faster!"* or *"slower!"* depending on whether the VCO is behind or ahead. The **Low-Pass Filter** is the calm coach who converts those shouts into smooth, gentle tugs on the VCO’s hand. After a brief adjustment period (the **capture** phase), the VCO walks in perfect lockstep with the reference — and from then on, every stumble of the VCO is corrected immediately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PLL does **not** directly compare frequencies. It compares **phases**. A phase difference automatically implies a frequency difference, but the system’s error-correction variable is *phase*, not frequency. This is the most common conceptual pitfall in KTU viva-voce questions.

### Three Operating States of a PLL

| State | Description | Signal Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Free-running** | VCO oscillates at its own center frequency; no input applied | $f_{out} = f_o$ |
| **Capture (Acquisition)** | Input is applied but lock is not yet established; loop is hunting | Erratic beat pattern at PD output |
| **Lock (Tracking)** | Output frequency is *exactly* equal to input frequency; phase error is small and constant | $f_{out} = f_{in}$, $\Delta\phi = \text{constant}$ |

> [!TIP]
> A KTU favorite: *“Distinguish between lock range and capture range.”* See the formula sheet below.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 VCO Operating Principle — How Does a VCO Work?

The VCO internally contains a **current source** that charges/discharges a **timing capacitor** $C_1$ at a rate determined by a control voltage. A **Schmitt trigger** senses the capacitor voltage and toggles the polarity of the current source whenever the voltage reaches preset upper and lower thresholds.

### Step-by-Step Operation

1. **Apply control voltage $V_C$** at the modulation input.
2. The current source produces a current $I$ that is **proportional to $(V^+ - V_C)$** (for LM566).
3. This current charges $C_1$ linearly, producing a **triangular ramp** across the capacitor.
4. A **Schmitt-trigger comparator** monitors this ramp and flips state at threshold $V_{TH+}$ and $V_{TH-}$.
5. The comparator’s output is a **square wave** at the *same frequency* as the ramp.
6. When $V_C$ rises → current $I$ rises → ramp slope rises → frequency rises.
7. The relation is **linear** over a wide operating range.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the "why" behind the linear $f_o \propto V_C$ relationship: because the charging current is *linear* in $V_C$, and the ramp slope is *linear* in current, the period $T$ is *linear* in $1/V_C$, giving $f_o \propto V_C$.

---

## 2.2 LM566 — Detailed Functional Architecture

### Output Frequency Equation

For the LM566, the output frequency is given by the **manufacturer’s equation** (which students must memorize):

$$
f_o = \frac{2 \cdot (V^+ - V_C)}{R_1 \cdot C_1 \cdot V^+}
$$

where:
- $f_o$ = output frequency (Hz)
- $V^+$ = positive supply voltage (V)
- $V_C$ = control voltage at pin 7 (V)
- $R_1$ = external timing resistor (Ω) — connected between pin 5 and $V^+$
- $C_1$ = external timing capacitor (F) — connected between pin 6 and ground

> [!WARNING]
> Common KTU error: students often write $f_o = 2(V_C)/R_1C_1$ **forgetting** the $V^+$ term in the denominator. The correct form has the **difference** $(V^+ - V_C)$ in the numerator.

### VCO Sensitivity (Gain)

Differentiating the LM566 frequency equation with respect to $V_C$:

$$
K_{VCO} = \left|\frac{\partial f_o}{\partial V_C}\right| = \frac{2}{R_1 \cdot C_1 \cdot V^+}
$$

So the sensitivity is **inversely proportional to $R_1$, $C_1$, and $V^+$**. Choosing a small $R_1$ and $C_1$ yields a high $K_{VCO}$ (frequency changes rapidly with $V_C$).

### Frequency Modulation using LM566

When a low-frequency message signal $v_m(t)$ is superimposed on the DC control voltage $V_C$, the instantaneous output frequency becomes:

$$
f_o(t) = \frac{2 \cdot [V^+ - (V_{C,DC} + v_m(t))]}{R_1 C_1 V^+}
$$

This is precisely the **Frequency Modulation (FM)** operation, with the FM modulator sensitivity:

$$
K_{FM} = \frac{2}{R_1 C_1 V^+} \quad \text{(Hz/V)}
$$

### LM566 Applications

- **FM modulator** for communication transmitters
- **FSK (Frequency Shift Keying) generator** for digital modems
- **Tone generator** in touch-tone telephones
- **Signal generator** in laboratory instruments
- **Function generator** block in analog computers

---

## 2.3 Phase Locked Loop (PLL) — Detailed Functional Architecture

The PLL is a closed-loop negative-feedback system composed of **three basic building blocks** (and an optional fourth):

| Block | Symbol | Function | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase Detector | PD | Multiplies/compares two input phases | Error voltage $V_d$ proportional to $\Delta\phi$ |
| Low-Pass Filter | LPF | Smooths the PD output, removes high-frequency components | Filtered control voltage $V_C$ |
| Voltage Controlled Oscillator | VCO | Generates output whose frequency tracks input | Output signal with $f_o \propto V_C$ |
| Frequency Divider | ÷N | Divides VCO output to allow frequency synthesis | Sub-multiple of $f_o$ (only in frequency synthesizers) |

### 2.3.1 Phase Detector (PD)

The phase detector produces an output **proportional to the phase difference** $\Delta\phi$ between the reference input $f_{in}$ and the VCO output $f_o$:

$$
V_d = K_{PD} \cdot \Delta\phi
$$

where $K_{PD}$ is the **phase detector gain** (V/rad).

**Types of Phase Detectors (KTU-most-asked):**
1. **XOR gate PD** — simplest, output is high when phases differ
2. **Edge-triggered JK flip-flop PD** — gives triangular output, sensitive to both phase and frequency
3. **Phase Frequency Detector (PFD)** — modern, gives unambiguous lock indication over full $\pm 2\pi$ range

### 2.3.2 Low-Pass Filter (LPF)

The PD output contains a high-frequency component at $2f_{in}$ (sum frequency from the multiplication), which must be removed. The LPF also sets the **loop bandwidth** — the speed at which the PLL can respond to frequency changes.

Three common filter topologies:
- **First-order passive RC filter** (most common in basic KTU problems)
- **Active proportional-integral filter** (better tracking and zero steady-state error)
- **Lead-lag filter** (compromise between noise and acquisition speed)

### 2.3.3 VCO (Inside the PLL)

The VCO inside the PLL has the transfer characteristic:

$$
f_o = f_{o(\text{center})} + K_{VCO} \cdot V_C
$$

In the phase domain, this is equivalent to:

$$
\theta_o(t) = 2\pi \int_0^t f_o(\tau) \, d\tau = 2\pi f_{o(\text{center})} t + 2\pi K_{VCO} \int_0^t V_C(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

---

## 2.4 Lock Range and Capture Range

These two terms are the **most-confused pair** in KTU PLL questions. They are *not* the same.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lock Range ($\Delta f_L$):** the range of input frequencies over which the PLL can **maintain** lock once it is already locked. Depends on the **maximum VCO control voltage swing**.
>
> **Capture Range ($\Delta f_C$):** the range of input frequencies over which the PLL can **acquire** lock from a free-running state. Depends on the **LPF bandwidth**. **Always:** $\Delta f_C \le \Delta f_L$.

$$
\Delta f_L = \pm K_{VCO} \cdot V_{d,\max}
$$

$$
\Delta f_C = \Delta f_L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{f_{LPF}}{f_{in}}}
$$

(for a first-order RC LPF, where $f_{LPF} = 1/(2\pi R_1 C_1)$).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Role of VCO/PLL |
|---|---|
| FM radio receiver | PLL locks to the carrier → FM demodulation |
| Mobile phone | PLL frequency synthesizers generate all channel frequencies from a single crystal |
| Digital modems | FSK demodulation via PLL |
| Microprocessor clock | PLL multiplies a low-frequency crystal to a high CPU clock |
| Spacecraft telemetry | Coherent demodulation of weak signals buried in noise |
| Motor speed control | PLL synchronizes motor rotation to a reference |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is a must-memorize summary for the KTU University Exam. Cover it before entering the exam hall.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_o = \frac{2(V^+ - V_C)}{R_1 C_1 V^+}$ | LM566 output frequency | Hz |
| 2 | $K_{VCO} = \frac{2}{R_1 C_1 V^+}$ | VCO sensitivity | Hz/V |
| 3 | $f_o = f_{o(\text{center})} + K_{VCO} V_C$ | General VCO characteristic | Hz |
| 4 | $V_d = K_{PD} \cdot \Delta\phi$ | Phase detector output | V |
| 5 | $\theta_o(t) = 2\pi \int f_o \, dt$ | Phase is integral of frequency | rad |
| 6 | $\Delta f_L = \pm K_{VCO} \cdot V_{d,\max}$ | Lock range | Hz |
| 7 | $\Delta f_C = \Delta f_L \sqrt{f_{LPF}/f_{in}}$ | Capture range (1st-order LPF) | Hz |
| 8 | $f_{LPF} = 1/(2\pi R_1 C_1)$ | LPF cutoff frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $V_{FSK,1} = 8V^+ / 4$ | LM566 upper FSK frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $V_{FSK,0} = 5V^+ / 4$ | LM566 lower FSK frequency | Hz |
