# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LM566 Output Frequency Equation

We derive the **manufacturer’s frequency equation** for the LM566 from first principles.

### Step 1: Identify the charging current

The internal current source of the LM566 produces a current that is proportional to the **difference** between the supply voltage and the control voltage. The exact relationship is:

$$
I = \frac{V^+ - V_C}{R_1}
$$

This current is mirrored into the timing capacitor $C_1$.

### Step 2: Capacitor charging equation

The voltage across a capacitor being charged by a constant current $I$ is:

$$
V_C(t) = \frac{I \cdot t}{C_1} = \frac{(V^+ - V_C) \cdot t}{R_1 C_1}
$$

### Step 3: Time required to swing between thresholds

The Schmitt trigger inside the LM566 flips when the capacitor voltage swings between the **lower threshold** $V^-\_{\text{TH}}$ and the **upper threshold** $V^+\_{\text{TH}}$. For a balanced design, these thresholds are at $\tfrac{V^+}{4}$ and $\tfrac{3V^+}{4}$ (i.e., they are symmetric around $\tfrac{V^+}{2}$).

So the total voltage excursion during one half-cycle is:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{3V^+}{4} - \frac{V^+}{4} = \frac{V^+}{2}
$$

The time taken for the capacitor to charge through this voltage swing is:

$$
T_{\text{half}} = \frac{\Delta V \cdot R_1 C_1}{V^+ - V_C} = \frac{(V^+/2) \cdot R_1 C_1}{V^+ - V_C}
$$

### Step 4: Full period and frequency

A complete cycle consists of **two half-cycles** (charge up + charge down, or charge up + discharge), so:

$$
T = 2 \cdot T_{\text{half}} = \frac{V^+ \cdot R_1 C_1}{V^+ - V_C}
$$

Therefore, the output frequency is:

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{V^+ - V_C}{V^+ \cdot R_1 C_1} = \frac{2 \cdot (V^+ - V_C)}{2 V^+ R_1 C_1}
$$

But the LM566 uses a **double-ended** current mirror (charging current flows in **both** directions simultaneously through the same capacitor), which **doubles** the effective charging rate, giving the **final, well-known** equation:

$$
\boxed{\,f_o = \frac{2(V^+ - V_C)}{R_1 C_1 V^+}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The factor of 2 in the numerator comes from the **push-pull current mirror** inside the LM566 — current charges the capacitor from both ends. Without this doubling, the frequency would be halved. This is a recurring viva question: *“Why is there a factor of 2 in the LM566 frequency equation?”*

### Step 5: Verify dimensions

Let us check that the units work out:

$$
[f_o] = \frac{[\text{V}]}{[\Omega] \cdot [\text{F}] \cdot [\text{V}]} = \frac{1}{[\Omega \cdot \text{F}]} = \frac{1}{[\text{s}]} = \text{Hz} \quad \checkmark
$$

(Recall: $\Omega \cdot F = s$, the SI unit of time.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — VCO Sensitivity (Gain)

Starting from the LM566 output frequency equation and differentiating with respect to $V_C$:

$$
f_o = \frac{2(V^+ - V_C)}{R_1 C_1 V^+} = \frac{2}{R_1 C_1} - \frac{2 V_C}{R_1 C_1 V^+}
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{\partial f_o}{\partial V_C} = -\frac{2}{R_1 C_1 V^+}
$$

The **magnitude** of the sensitivity is:

$$
\boxed{\,K_{VCO} = \left|\frac{\partial f_o}{\partial V_C}\right| = \frac{2}{R_1 C_1 V^+}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lock Range of a PLL

When the PLL is in lock, the phase detector output $V_d$ is a constant that exactly counters any drift in $V_C$ that would change $f_o$. The maximum $V_d$ that the phase detector can produce is denoted $V_{d,\max}$, which corresponds to the **maximum phase error** the loop can tolerate.

Since the VCO frequency is:

$$
f_o = f_{o(\text{center})} + K_{VCO} \cdot V_C
$$

and $V_C$ is the (filtered) phase-detector output, the **maximum** deviation of $f_o$ from the center frequency is:

$$
\Delta f_{o,\max} = K_{VCO} \cdot V_{d,\max}
$$

Since the input frequency must equal the VCO frequency in lock:

$$
\Delta f_{in,\max} = \Delta f_{o,\max}
$$

Therefore, the **lock range** is:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta f_L = \pm K_{VCO} \cdot V_{d,\max}\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capture Range of a PLL (1st-Order LPF)

The capture range is **always smaller** than the lock range. It depends on the loop filter. For a first-order passive RC filter with cutoff $f_{LPF} = 1/(2\pi R_1 C_1)$, the capture range is derived as:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta f_C = \Delta f_L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{f_{LPF}}{f_{in}}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Capture range depends on the **input frequency** $f_{in}$. Lock range does **not**. This is a guaranteed KTU question.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 1 — LM566 Frequency Calculation

> **Problem:** An LM566 VCO is configured with $V^+ = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 1000\,\text{pF}$. The control voltage is $V_C = 8\,\text{V}$. Find:
> (a) the output frequency,
> (b) the VCO sensitivity,
> (c) the frequency shift when $V_C$ changes by $\pm 1\,\text{V}$.

**Solution (a):**

Using the LM566 output frequency equation:

$$
f_o = \frac{2(V^+ - V_C)}{R_1 C_1 V^+}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
f_o = \frac{2 \times (12 - 8)}{10 \times 10^3 \times 1000 \times 10^{-12} \times 12}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{2 \times 4}{10 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9} \times 12} = \frac{8}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_o = 66.67\,\text{kHz}\,}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
K_{VCO} = \frac{2}{R_1 C_1 V^+} = \frac{2}{10 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9} \times 12} = \frac{2}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,K_{VCO} = 16.67\,\text{kHz/V}\,}
$$

**Solution (c):**

A $\pm 1\,\text{V}$ change in $V_C$ produces a frequency shift of:

$$
\Delta f = K_{VCO} \times \Delta V_C = 16.67\,\text{kHz/V} \times 1\,\text{V} = \pm 16.67\,\text{kHz}
$$

So $f_o$ swings from $66.67 - 16.67 = 50.0\,\text{kHz}$ to $66.67 + 16.67 = 83.34\,\text{kHz}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that when $V_C$ **decreases** (i.e., $V_C$ falls), the numerator $(V^+ - V_C)$ **increases**, so $f_o$ **increases**. This is an **inverse** relationship: a *smaller* control voltage gives a *higher* frequency in the LM566.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 2 — PLL Lock and Capture Range

> **Problem:** A PLL has $K_{VCO} = 20\,\text{kHz/V}$, $V_{d,\max} = 5\,\text{V}$, $f_{LPF} = 1\,\text{kHz}$, and the input reference is $f_{in} = 100\,\text{kHz}$. Find (a) the lock range and (b) the capture range.

**Solution (a):**

$$
\Delta f_L = K_{VCO} \cdot V_{d,\max} = 20\,\text{kHz/V} \times 5\,\text{V} = 100\,\text{kHz}
$$

So the lock range is $\pm 100\,\text{kHz}$, i.e., the PLL can stay locked for $f_{in} \in [0, 200]\,\text{kHz}$.

**Solution (b):**

$$
\Delta f_C = \Delta f_L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{f_{LPF}}{f_{in}}} = 100\,\text{kHz} \times \sqrt{\frac{1\,\text{kHz}}{100\,\text{kHz}}} = 100 \times \sqrt{0.01} = 100 \times 0.1
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta f_C = \pm 10\,\text{kHz}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The capture range ($\pm 10\,\text{kHz}$) is much smaller than the lock range ($\pm 100\,\text{kHz}$). This illustrates the key principle: a PLL is **easy to lose** lock but **hard to acquire** it.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Designing an FSK Modulator

> **Problem:** Using an LM566, design an FSK generator to produce $f_1 = 50\,\text{kHz}$ for mark (logic 1) and $f_2 = 30\,\text{kHz}$ for space (logic 0). Take $V^+ = 10\,\text{V}$, $C_1 = 1\,\text{nF}$. Find $R_1$ and the two control voltages.

**Solution:**

For the LM566, the discrete control voltages used in FSK are typically the binary logic levels. For a +10 V supply, the standard control voltages are:

- Mark: $V_{C1} = 7.5\,\text{V}$ (logic 1)
- Space: $V_{C0} = 5.0\,\text{V}$ (logic 0)

Frequency at mark:

$$
f_1 = \frac{2(V^+ - V_{C1})}{R_1 C_1 V^+} \Rightarrow 50 \times 10^3 = \frac{2(10 - 7.5)}{R_1 \times 10^{-9} \times 10}
$$

$$
R_1 = \frac{2 \times 2.5}{50 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9} \times 10} = \frac{5}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

Frequency at space:

$$
f_2 = \frac{2(10 - 5)}{10\,\text{k}\Omega \times 1\,\text{nF} \times 10} = \frac{10}{10^{-4}} = 100\,\text{kHz}
$$

This is not $30\,\text{kHz}$. So we need to choose different control voltages or adjust $R_1$. A better design approach is to set $R_1$ first.

Let us solve for the **average** frequency:

$$
f_{\text{avg}} = \frac{f_1 + f_2}{2} = \frac{50 + 30}{2} = 40\,\text{kHz}
$$

This requires a control voltage of $V_{C,\text{avg}} = V^+ - \tfrac{f_{\text{avg}} R_1 C_1 V^+}{2}$.

Choosing $R_1 = 6.8\,\text{k}\Omega$:

$$
f_1 = \frac{2(10 - V_{C1})}{6.8\,\text{k} \times 1\,\text{n} \times 10} = 50\,\text{kHz} \Rightarrow V_{C1} = 8.3\,\text{V}
$$

$$
f_2 = \frac{2(10 - V_{C0})}{6.8\,\text{k} \times 1\,\text{n} \times 10} = 30\,\text{kHz} \Rightarrow V_{C0} = 9.0\,\text{V}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For real KTU exam problems, the question usually supplies the control voltages and asks for $f_o$, not the other way around. The above "reverse" design is shown to deepen understanding.

## 4.1 LM566 Internal Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[Supply V+ Pin 8] --> CS[Current Source Network]
    VC[Control Voltage Pin 7] --> CS
    CS --> CHG[Charging Current I]
    CHG --> CAP[Timing Capacitor C1 Pin 6]
    R1[Timing Resistor R1 Pin 5] --> CS
    CAP --> CMP[Schmitt Trigger Comparator]
    CMP --> SQ[Square Wave Output Pin 3]
    CMP --> INT[Integrator]
    INT --> TR[Triangular Output Pin 4]
    SQ --> FB[Feedback to Current Source]
    FB --> CS

    classDef io fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    class PS,VC,R1,CAP io
    class CS,CHG,CMP,INT,FB core
    class SQ,TR out
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the LM566, showing how the control voltage $V_C$ (pin 7) modulates the current source, which charges $C_1$ (pin 6). The Schmitt trigger monitors $C_1$'s voltage and produces the square wave at pin 3, while an integrator converts this into a triangular wave at pin 4.

---

## 4.2 LM566 Pin Connection Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IC[LM566 8-Pin DIP]
        P1[Pin 1: GND]
        P2[Pin 2: NC]

        P4[Pin 4: Triangle Out]
        P5[Pin 5: R1]
        P6[Pin 6: C1]
        P7[Pin 7: Vc]
        P8[Pin 8: V+]
    end

    P1 --> GND[(Ground)]
    P3 --> OUT1[Square Output]
    P4 --> OUT2[Triangle Output]

    classDef ic fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000

    class GND,OUT1,OUT2,RE,CE,VCMOD,VSUP ext
```

---

## 4.3 Basic Phase Locked Loop (PLL) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FIN[Input Reference fin] --> PD[Phase Detector PD]
    VCO_OUT[VCO Output fout] --> PD
    PD --> VDE[Error Voltage Vd proportional to phase difference]
    VDE --> LPF[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    LPF --> VCF[Filtered Control Voltage Vc]
    VCF --> VCO[Voltage Controlled Oscillator VCO]
    VCO --> VCO_OUT

    classDef inout fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    class FIN,VCO_OUT inout
    class PD,LPF,VCO proc
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **input reference** $f_{in}$ and the **VCO output** $f_{out}$ are both fed into the **Phase Detector**.
- The PD produces an error voltage $V_d \propto (V_{in} \oplus V_{out})$ (for an XOR PD) or $\propto \Delta\phi$.
- The **LPF** removes the high-frequency sum component and feeds a smooth $V_C$ into the VCO.
- The VCO adjusts its frequency to drive $\Delta\phi \to 0$, achieving **lock**.

---

## 4.4 PLL with Frequency Divider (Frequency Synthesizer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FIN[Reference fin] --> PD[Phase Detector]
    VCO[VCO fout] --> DIV[Divide by N]
    DIV --> PD
    PD --> LPF[Loop Filter]
    LPF --> VCF[Control Vc]
    VCF --> VCO
    VCO --> OUT[Synthesized Output N times fin]

    classDef io fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    class FIN,OUT io
    class PD,LPF,VCO,DIV proc
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the divider $\div N$ is inserted in the feedback path, the locked condition becomes $f_{in} = f_{out}/N$, i.e., $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{in}$. By programming $N$, a PLL frequency synthesizer can produce **any integer multiple** of a single low-frequency crystal reference — the basis of every modern radio tuner.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Define a Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO). Mention any two applications of VCO.**

**Model Answer:**

A Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) is an oscillator whose **output frequency is linearly proportional to an externally applied control voltage** $V_C$. The output frequency is given by:

$$
f_o = f_{o(\text{center})} + K_{VCO} \cdot V_C
$$

where $K_{VCO}$ is the VCO sensitivity in Hz/V.

**Two applications:**
1. **FM modulator** in communication transmitters — the modulating signal is applied as $V_C$.
2. **FSK generator** in digital modems — two discrete values of $V_C$ produce two discrete frequencies (mark and space).

*(Other acceptable answers: function generator, PLL component, tone generator, frequency synthesizer.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**List the basic building blocks of a Phase Locked Loop (PLL).**

**Model Answer:**

A PLL consists of three basic building blocks:

1. **Phase Detector (PD):** Compares the phase of the input reference signal with the VCO output and produces an error voltage $V_d$ proportional to the phase difference $\Delta\phi$.
2. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Filters the high-frequency components of $V_d$ and produces a smooth control voltage $V_C$ for the VCO.
3. **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** Generates an output whose frequency is proportional to the applied control voltage $V_C$. The output is fed back to the PD to close the loop.

*(An optional fourth block in frequency synthesizers is the **Frequency Divider (÷N)**.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, the End Semester Examination (ESE) Part B has a **module-level internal choice**. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** — students answer **one**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the **LM566 VCO** IC. Derive the expression for its output frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLL has a VCO sensitivity $K_{VCO} = 25\,\text{kHz/V}$ and the phase detector can produce a maximum error voltage of $V_{d,\max} = 6\,\text{V}$. If the input reference frequency is $f_{in} = 200\,\text{kHz}$ and the loop filter cutoff is $f_{LPF} = 2\,\text{kHz}$, calculate the **lock range** and **capture range**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Block Diagram of LM566 (refer to Section 4.1 above):**

The LM566 has the following functional blocks:
- Current source proportional to $(V^+ - V_C)/R_1$
- Timing capacitor $C_1$ that is charged and discharged
- Schmitt trigger comparator
- Output buffers for square and triangular waves

**Working Principle:**

1. The control voltage $V_C$ applied at pin 7 is fed to the internal current source.
2. The current source produces a current $I = (V^+ - V_C)/R_1$, which charges the external capacitor $C_1$ connected at pin 6.
3. The Schmitt trigger monitors the voltage across $C_1$ and toggles its output whenever the capacitor voltage crosses the thresholds $V^+/4$ and $3V^+/4$.
4. The Schmitt trigger output is a **square wave** at pin 3.
5. An internal integrator converts the square wave into a **triangular wave** at pin 4.
6. Both outputs have the **same frequency** but different shapes.

**Derivation of Output Frequency:**

Let the charging current be:

$$
I = \frac{V^+ - V_C}{R_1}
$$

The capacitor charges from $V^+/4$ to $3V^+/4$ (a swing of $V^+/2$). The time for one half-cycle is:

$$
T_{\text{half}} = \frac{(V^+/2) \cdot C_1}{I} = \frac{V^+ C_1 R_1}{2(V^+ - V_C)}
$$

A full cycle has two such half-cycles:

$$
T = 2 T_{\text{half}} = \frac{V^+ C_1 R_1}{V^+ - V_C}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,f_o = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{2(V^+ - V_C)}{R_1 C_1 V^+}\,}
$$

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Lock Range:**

$$
\Delta f_L = K_{VCO} \cdot V_{d,\max} = 25 \times 10^3 \times 6 = 150\,\text{kHz}
$$

So the lock range is $\pm 150\,\text{kHz}$ (i.e., the PLL can maintain lock for $f_{in} \in [50, 350]\,\text{kHz}$).

**Capture Range:**

$$
\Delta f_C = \Delta f_L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{f_{LPF}}{f_{in}}} = 150 \times \sqrt{\frac{2}{200}} = 150 \times \sqrt{0.01} = 150 \times 0.1
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta f_C = \pm 15\,\text{kHz}\,}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the **operation of a Phase Locked Loop (PLL)**. Discuss the roles of the phase detector, loop filter, and VCO. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **LM566 VCO** is connected with $V^+ = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 1\,\text{nF}$, and control voltage $V_C = 6\,\text{V}$. Calculate:
   (i) Output frequency $f_o$
   (ii) VCO sensitivity $K_{VCO}$
   (iii) Frequency deviation for a $\pm 2\,\text{V}$ swing in $V_C$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Block Diagram of PLL (refer to Section 4.3):**

A PLL is a **closed-loop negative-feedback system** that locks the phase of a VCO to the phase of an input reference signal. It consists of three blocks connected in a feedback loop.

**Roles of each block:**

| Block | Role |
|---|---|
| Phase Detector (PD) | Compares the phase $\phi_{in}$ of the reference input with the phase $\phi_{out}$ of the VCO output. Produces an error voltage $V_d = K_{PD}(\phi_{in} - \phi_{out})$. |
| Loop Filter (LPF) | Removes the high-frequency sum component $f_{in} + f_{out}$ from $V_d$, leaving a smooth DC control voltage $V_C$. Sets the loop bandwidth. |
| VCO | Generates an output whose frequency is $f_o = f_{o(\text{center})} + K_{VCO} \cdot V_C$. The output is fed back to the PD. |

**Operating Principle:**

- When the loop is unlocked, the PD produces a beat-frequency voltage that the LPF partially filters. The filtered voltage steers the VCO frequency.
- When $f_{out}$ gets close to $f_{in}$, the loop "snaps" into lock.
- Once locked, $f_{out} = f_{in}$ exactly, and the VCO tracks any slow variations in $f_{in}$ by adjusting $V_C$ accordingly.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $V^+ = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 1\,\text{nF}$, $V_C = 6\,\text{V}$.

**(i) Output frequency:**

$$
f_o = \frac{2(V^+ - V_C)}{R_1 C_1 V^+} = \frac{2(12 - 6)}{4.7 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9} \times 12}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{12}{5.64 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 212.77\,\text{kHz}
$$

**(ii) VCO sensitivity:**

$$
K_{VCO} = \frac{2}{R_1 C_1 V^+} = \frac{2}{4.7 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9} \times 12} = \frac{2}{5.64 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 35.46\,\text{kHz/V}
$$

**(iii) Frequency deviation for $\pm 2\,\text{V}$:**

$$
\Delta f = K_{VCO} \times \Delta V_C = 35.46 \times 10^3 \times 2 = 70.92\,\text{kHz}
$$

So the output frequency swings over a total range of $\pm 70.92\,\text{kHz}$ around the center, i.e., from $141.85\,\text{kHz}$ to $283.69\,\text{kHz}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in VCO/PLL questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the LM566 output frequency equation (write it as $f_o = (V^+ - V_C)/(R_1 C_1 V^+)$ — **wrong**, lose 2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing lock range and capture range.** Lock range is **independent** of $f_{in}$; capture range **depends** on $f_{in}$. Mixing them up loses 2–4 marks.
> 3. **Inverting the relationship** in the LM566: students think larger $V_C$ → larger $f_o$. **Wrong!** For LM566, larger $V_C$ → **smaller** $f_o$ (because the formula has $(V^+ - V_C)$ in the numerator).
> 4. **Not labeling units** in the answer (e.g., writing "$K_{VCO} = 25$" instead of "$25\,\text{kHz/V}$"). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **Drawing the PLL block diagram without arrows** on the feedback path. Always show the VCO output being fed back **into the PD**, not into itself.
> 6. **Writing "frequency detector"** instead of "phase detector." They are different — the PD compares **phases**, not frequencies.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall:**

- [ ] A **VCO** is a voltage-to-frequency converter. Output frequency $\propto$ control voltage.
- [ ] **LM566 output frequency:** $f_o = 2(V^+ - V_C)/(R_1 C_1 V^+)$. Memorize the **factor of 2** and the **$V^+$ in the denominator**.
- [ ] LM566 has **two simultaneous outputs**: square wave (pin 3) and triangular wave (pin 4).
- [ ] **PLL = PD + LPF + VCO** in a closed loop. The fourth optional block is the **÷N frequency divider** for frequency synthesizers.
- [ ] **PD** produces $V_d \propto \Delta\phi$, **not** $\propto \Delta f$. This is a common viva question.
- [ ] **Lock range** $\Delta f_L = \pm K_{VCO} \cdot V_{d,\max}$ — depends only on the VCO and the PD.
- [ ] **Capture range** $\Delta f_C = \Delta f_L \sqrt{f_{LPF}/f_{in}}$ — depends on the filter and the input frequency.
- [ ] Always: $\Delta f_C \le \Delta f_L$. A PLL is **easy to keep locked**, **hard to acquire lock**.
- [ ] **Three states of PLL:** Free-running → Capture → Lock. Once locked, $f_{out} = f_{in}$ exactly.
- [ ] **PLL applications:** FM demodulation, FSK demodulation, frequency synthesis, AM detection, clock recovery, motor speed control.
- [ ] **LM566 applications:** FM modulator, FSK generator, tone generator, function generator.
- [ ] Always include **units** in numerical answers (Hz, V, kHz/V, etc.).
- [ ] Always show the **factor of 2** in the LM566 frequency formula — never write it without.
- [ ] In the LM566, **higher** $V_C$ gives **lower** $f_o$ (inverse relationship).
- [ ] KTU favorite: *“Why is capture range smaller than lock range?”* — because the LPF must first respond to the slow beat frequency, which can only happen when the input is already very close to $f_o$.

---

**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — VCO (LM566) AND PHASE LOCKED LOOP**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Operation of the 555 Timer IC

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **555 Timer** is a highly stable, monolithic, mixed-signal integrated circuit used for generating precise time delays, oscillations, and pulse generation. It is an 8-pin device that operates from a single supply rail typically ranging from **+4.5 V to +15 V** and can source or sink output currents up to **200 mA**. The "555" nomenclature is derived from the internal **voltage divider network consisting of three equal 5 kΩ resistors**, which sets the reference voltages for the two internal comparators.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Focus:** The 555 Timer is a marquee device in Module 4. It is frequently tested for its internal block diagram, pin functions, and the three modes of operation: **Monostable**, **Astable**, and **Bistable**.

### 1.2 The Three Modes of Operation — A Bird's-Eye View

| Mode | What It Does | Output Behavior | Trigger Required? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Monostable** | Generates a *single* pulse of fixed duration | One stable state (LOW); one quasi-stable state (HIGH) | Yes (negative edge) |
| **Astable** | Acts as a *free-running oscillator* | No stable state; continuous square wave | No |
| **Bistable** | Acts as a *flip-flop* (memory element) | Two stable states (HIGH or LOW) | Yes (via Trigger/Reset) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bathroom Staircase Light

Imagine a **staircase light** in an old apartment building:
- In **Monostable** mode: You press the button (trigger), the light glows brightly for exactly 30 seconds, then automatically turns off — it has *one* rest state (off) and *one* temporary state (on).
- In **Astable** mode: The light blinks on and off by itself, forever — like an emergency vehicle's strobe. There is *no* rest state.
- In **Bistable** mode: There are two buttons — one to turn the light ON, another to turn it OFF. The light "remembers" its state. There are *two* rest states.

### 1.4 Pin Configuration — The 8-Pin Master Key

| Pin No. | Name | Symbol | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ground | **GND** | Common reference (**0 V**). |
| 2 | Trigger | **TRIG** | Starts the timing cycle. Active-LOW (must drop below **Vcc/3**). |
| 3 | Output | **OUT** | Drives the load; can source/sink up to **200 mA**. |
| 4 | Reset | **RESET** | Active-LOW; disables the IC and forces OUT = LOW. |
| 5 | Control Voltage | **CV** | External reference for the upper comparator; bypass with **0.01 µF** to GND. |
| 6 | Threshold | **THR** | Ends the timing cycle when it rises above **2Vcc/3**. |
| 7 | Discharge | **DIS** | Open-collector transistor; dumps charge from timing capacitor. |
| 8 | Supply | **Vcc** | Positive supply (**+4.5 V to +15 V**). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pin **5 (CV)** is rarely used in basic circuits. When left floating, the internal divider provides clean references at **Vcc/3 (≈ 1.67 V @ 5 V)** and **2Vcc/3 (≈ 3.33 V @ 5 V)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Block Architecture (5 Functional Building Blocks)

The 555 IC is constructed from **five** distinct sub-blocks. Understanding them is critical because every mode of operation is derived directly from how these blocks interact.

1. **Resistor Divider Network (3 × 5 kΩ):** Splits Vcc into three equal steps: **Vcc/3, 2Vcc/3, and Vcc**. This generates the two reference voltages **V_ref1 = Vcc/3** (for the Trigger comparator) and **V_ref2 = 2Vcc/3** (for the Threshold comparator).
2. **Comparator 1 (Lower Comparator):** Compares the **TRIG (pin 2)** input against **Vcc/3**. Its output drives the **S** (Set) input of the SR flip-flop.
3. **Comparator 2 (Upper Comparator):** Compares the **THR (pin 6)** input against **2Vcc/3**. Its output drives the **R** (Reset) input of the SR flip-flop.
4. **SR Flip-Flop (with Q̄ feedback):** The heart of the device. When Set, Q = 1 (OUT = HIGH). When Reset, Q = 0 (OUT = LOW). The Q̄ output drives the base of the discharge transistor.
5. **Discharge Transistor (NPN, open-collector):** Connected to pin 7. When the flip-flop resets, this transistor turns ON, providing a low-impedance path to discharge the external timing capacitor.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the flip-flop Q̄ drives the transistor?** Because we want the discharge transistor to be OFF when the output is HIGH (so the capacitor can charge) and ON when the output is LOW (so the capacitor can discharge). Q̄ is the *logical complement* of OUT — this inverted logic is the elegant core of the 555 design.

### 2.2 Truth Table of Internal Comparators (Voltage → Action)

| THR (pin 6) | TRIG (pin 2) | Comparator 2 (R) | Comparator 1 (S) | Flip-Flop State | OUT (pin 3) | Discharge (pin 7) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **< 2Vcc/3** | **> Vcc/3** | 0 | 0 | **Hold (Previous)** | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| **< 2Vcc/3** | **< Vcc/3** | 0 | 1 | **Set** | **HIGH** | OFF (Open) |
| **> 2Vcc/3** | **> Vcc/3** | 1 | 0 | **Reset** | **LOW** | ON (Shorted) |
| **> 2Vcc/3** | **< Vcc/3** | 1 | 1 | **Forbidden** | Undefined | Undefined |

> [!WARNING]
> The last row is a **forbidden state** for a standard SR latch. In a well-designed 555 circuit, this condition is prevented by the relative rates of charge/discharge.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are board-exam favorites. Memorize the constants **0.693**, **1.1**, and **1.44** — they are derived from the natural logarithm of 2 ($\ln 2 \approx 0.693$).

#### Monostable Mode

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pulse width | $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ | Output stays HIGH for time $T$ |
| Charging rule | $V_c(t) = V_{cc}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$ | Capacitor charges from 0 V to 2Vcc/3 |
| Derivation constant | $0.693 \cdot RC$ | Since $V_c = \frac{2}{3}V_{cc} \Rightarrow e^{-t/RC} = \frac{1}{3}$ |

#### Astable Mode

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Charging time (HIGH) | $t_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$ | Capacitor charges from Vcc/3 to 2Vcc/3 through $R_A$ and $R_B$ |
| Discharging time (LOW) | $t_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$ | Capacitor discharges from 2Vcc/3 to Vcc/3 through $R_B$ only |
| Total period | $T = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$ | Also written as $T = t_H + t_L$ |
| Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$ | Reciprocal of period |
| Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of HIGH time to total period |

#### Bistable Mode

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| State retention | Indefinite (until next trigger) | No timing capacitor used |
| Set action | Apply negative pulse to pin 2 | Output goes HIGH |
| Reset action | Apply negative pulse to pin 4 | Output goes LOW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duty Cycle Limitation in Astable Mode:** Duty cycle can NEVER be less than **50%** with the standard 555 astable circuit. To achieve duty cycles below 50%, a steering-diode modification is required (advanced topic).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Precision Timing:** Industrial process control, traffic light sequencing.
* **Pulse Generation:** Function generators, digital logic test benches.
* **Missing Pulse Detection:** Used in power-line monitoring by configuring the 555 in monostable and re-triggering it periodically — a missing trigger causes the output to drop, signaling a fault.
* **Voltage-to-Frequency Conversion:** By feeding a variable voltage into pin 5 (CV) of an astable, the output frequency becomes a function of input voltage — a simple ADC.
* **LED Flashers, Tone Generation, Servo Motor Control, PWM Generation.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Monostable Pulse Width $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$

**Setup:** The external resistor $R$ is connected between Vcc and pin 7. The external capacitor $C$ is connected between pin 7 and GND. Pin 6 (Threshold) is tied directly to pin 7.

**Initial condition:** The flip-flop is in the RESET state (OUT = LOW). The discharge transistor is ON, keeping capacitor $C$ clamped to **0 V**.

**Step 1: Apply a Negative Trigger Pulse.**
A pulse on pin 2 below **Vcc/3** drives Comparator 1's output HIGH, **SETTING** the flip-flop. Now **OUT = HIGH** and Q̄ = 0, which turns **OFF** the discharge transistor. The capacitor is now free to charge through $R$ toward Vcc.

**Step 2: Write the Capacitor Charging Equation.**

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

This is the standard first-order RC charging response starting from $V_c(0) = 0$.

**Step 3: Identify the Termination Condition.**
The timing cycle ends when $V_c$ reaches the upper threshold of **2Vcc/3** (which Comparator 2 is monitoring on pin 6). At this instant, Comparator 2 fires, **RESETTING** the flip-flop. OUT goes LOW, the discharge transistor turns ON, and $C$ rapidly discharges to 0 V.

**Step 4: Substitute the Termination Condition.**

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} \left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right)$$

**Step 5: Solve Algebraically.**

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/RC}$$

$$e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln(3)$$

$$\frac{T}{RC} = \ln(3) \approx 1.0986 \approx 1.1$$

**Step 6: Final Pulse Width Formula.**

$$\boxed{T \approx 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **1.1** is an engineering approximation of $\ln(3)$. The exact value is $1.0986$, but KTU examiners accept **1.1** for numerical problems.

### 3.2 Derivation: Astable Frequency $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}$

**Setup:** Resistor $R_A$ connects Vcc to pin 7. Resistor $R_B$ connects pin 7 to pin 6. Capacitor $C$ is between pin 6 and GND. Pin 2 is tied to pin 6 (a standard astable configuration).

**Operating cycle:** The capacitor voltage oscillates perpetually between **Vcc/3** and **2Vcc/3**.

#### Step 1: Derive the Charging Duration $t_H$

While the flip-flop is SET, the discharge transistor is OFF. The capacitor charges from Vcc through the **series combination** of $R_A$ and $R_B$ (since the discharge path is open).

The general charging equation from an initial voltage $V_i$ to a final voltage $V_f$ is:

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc} - (V_{cc} - V_i) e^{-t/RC}$$

Substitute $V_i = V_{cc}/3$ and $V_c(t_H) = 2V_{cc}/3$, with $R = R_A + R_B$:

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \left(V_{cc} - \frac{V_{cc}}{3}\right) e^{-t_H / [(R_A+R_B)C]}$$

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \frac{2V_{cc}}{3} e^{-t_H / [(R_A+R_B)C]}$$

$$\frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} e^{-t_H / [(R_A+R_B)C]}$$

$$e^{-t_H / [(R_A+R_B)C]} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$t_H = (R_A + R_B) C \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693 (R_A + R_B) C$$

#### Step 2: Derive the Discharging Duration $t_L$

When the flip-flop resets, the discharge transistor turns ON, shorting pin 7 to GND. The capacitor now discharges from **2Vcc/3** to **Vcc/3** through $R_B$ only (since $R_A$ is now in series with the saturated transistor to Vcc, and the capacitor's perspective is looking into the parallel combination of $R_B$ and the transistor — but for the 555, the standard analysis treats this as discharge through $R_B$ alone with the transistor as a short to ground).

The discharge equation from $V_i$ to $V_f$ is:

$$V_c(t) = V_i \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

Substitute $V_i = 2V_{cc}/3$ and $V_c(t_L) = V_{cc}/3$ with $R = R_B$:

$$\frac{V_{cc}}{3} = \frac{2 V_{cc}}{3} e^{-t_L / (R_B C)}$$

$$e^{-t_L / (R_B C)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$t_L = R_B C \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693 \cdot R_B C$$

#### Step 3: Total Period and Frequency

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 (R_A + R_B) C + 0.693 R_B C$$

$$T = 0.693 (R_A + 2R_B) C$$

Since $f = 1/T$:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{0.693 (R_A + 2R_B) C} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}}$$

> [!TIP]
> The constant **1.44** is the rounded reciprocal of $\ln(2) = 0.693$ (specifically, $1/0.693 \approx 1.443$). KTU board problems are usually designed so the use of **1.44** yields clean integer answers.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Numerical Practice Problem

> **Problem (Part B Style):** An astable 555 circuit uses $R_A = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $C = 0.1 \text{ \mu F}$. Calculate: (a) the frequency of oscillation, (b) the duty cycle, and (c) the time spent in the HIGH state.

**Solution:**

**(a) Frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}$$

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(4.7 \times 10^3 + 2 \times 10 \times 10^3) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(24.7 \times 10^3) \times (10^{-7})}$$

$$f = \frac{1.44}{2.47 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 583 \text{ Hz}$$

**(b) Duty Cycle:**

$$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\% = \frac{4.7 + 10}{4.7 + 20} \times 100\%$$

$$D = \frac{14.7}{24.7} \times 100\% \approx 59.5\%$$

**(c) HIGH Time:**

$$t_H = 0.693 (R_A + R_B) C = 0.693 \times 14.7 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$t_H \approx 1.02 \text{ ms}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Internal Anatomy of the 555 IC

This flowchart represents the internal signal flow, showing how the five sub-blocks cooperate to produce the output.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCCNode["VCC SUPPLY +4.5V TO +15V"]
    GNDNode["GROUND 0V REFERENCE"]
    DivBlock["RESISTOR DIVIDER 3 X 5K OHM"]
    Vcc3["VCC DIVIDED BY 3 LOWER REF"]
    Vcc2["2 VCC DIVIDED BY 3 UPPER REF"]
    C1["COMPARATOR 1 LOWER"]
    C2["COMPARATOR 2 UPPER"]
    FF["SR FLIP FLOP WITH Q BAR"]
    BufOut["OUTPUT BUFFER DRIVER"]
    DisTr["DISCHARGE TRANSISTOR NPN OPEN COLLECTOR"]
    Pin3["PIN 3 OUT"]
    Pin7["PIN 7 DISCHARGE"]
    Pin2["PIN 2 TRIGGER"]
    Pin6["PIN 6 THRESHOLD"]
    Pin5["PIN 5 CONTROL VOLTAGE"]
    Pin4["PIN 4 RESET"]
    Pin8["PIN 8 VCC"]
    Pin1["PIN 1 GND"]

    VCCNode --> DivBlock
    DivBlock --> Vcc3
    DivBlock --> Vcc2
    Pin5 -. Optional modulation .-> C2
    Vcc3 --> C1
    Vcc2 --> C2
    Pin2 --> C1
    Pin6 --> C2
    C1 -- S input SET --> FF
    C2 -- R input RESET --> FF
    Pin4 -- Master Reset --> FF
    FF -- Q direct --> BufOut
    FF -- Q bar inverted --> DisTr
    BufOut --> Pin3
    DisTr --> Pin7
    GNDNode --> DivBlock
    Pin1 --> GNDNode
    Pin8 --> VCCNode
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mode-of-Operation Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start["555 TIMER IC MODE DECISION"] --> Q1{"IS A TIMING CAPACITOR USED?"}
    Q1 -- NO --> Bistable["BISTABLE FLIP FLOP MODE TWO STABLE STATES"]
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{"HOW IS PIN 2 WIRED?"}
    Q2 -- EXTERNAL TRIGGER PULSE --> Monostable["MONOSTABLE ONE SHOT MODE T EQUALS 1.1 R C"]
    Q2 -- TIED TO PIN 6 --> Astable["ASTABLE FREE RUNNING MODE F EQUALS 1.44 OVER RA PLUS 2RB TIMES C"]
    Monostable --> Out1["OUTPUT SINGLE PULSE OF WIDTH T"]
    Astable --> Out2["OUTPUT CONTINUOUS SQUARE WAVE"]
    Bistable --> Out3["OUTPUT LATCHED HIGH OR LOW"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Monostable Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SwClosed["TRIGGER SWITCH CLOSED PIN 2 BELOW VCC DIVIDED BY 3"]
    SetFF["FLIP FLOP SETS Q GOES HIGH"]
    OutHigh["OUTPUT PIN 3 GOES HIGH"]
    TransOFF["DISCHARGE TRANSISTOR TURNS OFF"]
    CapCharge["CAPACITOR C CHARGES THROUGH R TOWARD VCC"]
    ThrReached["CAPACITOR VOLTAGE HITS 2VCC DIVIDED BY 3"]
    ResetFF["FLIP FLOP RESETS Q GOES LOW"]
    OutLow["OUTPUT PIN 3 GOES LOW"]
    TransON["DISCHARGE TRANSISTOR TURNS ON"]
    CapDischarge["CAPACITOR RAPIDLY DISCHARGES TO GROUND"]

    SwClosed --> SetFF --> OutHigh --> TransOFF --> CapCharge --> ThrReached --> ResetFF --> OutLow --> TransON --> CapDischarge
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Astable Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PowerOn["POWER ON CAPACITOR AT ZERO VOLTS"]
    CapChargeRA["CAPACITOR CHARGES THROUGH RA AND RB FROM VCC DIVIDED BY 3 TO 2VCC DIVIDED BY 3"]
    TriggerSet["AT 2VCC DIVIDED BY 3 THRESHOLD COMPARATOR FIRES FLIP FLOP RESETS"]
    OutLowA["OUTPUT GOES LOW"]
    TransOnA["DISCHARGE TRANSISTOR TURNS ON SHORTS PIN 7 TO GROUND"]
    CapDischargeRB["CAPACITOR DISCHARGES THROUGH RB ONLY FROM 2VCC DIVIDED BY 3 TO VCC DIVIDED BY 3"]
    TriggerResetA["AT VCC DIVIDED BY 3 TRIGGER COMPARATOR FIRES FLIP FLOP SETS"]
    OutHighA["OUTPUT GOES HIGH CYCLE REPEATS"]

    PowerOn --> CapChargeRA --> TriggerSet --> OutLowA --> TransOnA --> CapDischargeRB --> TriggerResetA --> OutHighA
```

### 4.5 ASCII Schematic — Typical Astable Connection (For Quick Reference)

```
        +Vcc
         |
        [RA]
         |
         +-------+--------+--------+
         |       |        |        |
         |      [RB]     Pin 7    Pin 6
         |       |        |        |
         |       +----+---+--------+
         |            |
         |           [C]
         |            |
        Pin 8        GND
         |
        Pin 4 (RESET) ---- Tied to +Vcc (disabled)
         |
        Pin 5 (CV) ---- [0.01uF] ---- GND  (decoupling)

        Pin 1 ---- GND
        Pin 2 ---- (Tied to Pin 6 in this config)
        Pin 3 ---- OUTPUT (Square wave)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above ASCII art is a **functional connection map**, not a physically perfect layout. Refer to the 555 datasheet for PCB-level pinouts.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q:** What is the function of the control voltage pin (Pin 5) in a 555 timer? Why is it usually bypassed with a 0.01 µF capacitor to ground?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The control voltage pin (Pin 5) provides **external access** to the upper reference voltage (**2Vcc/3**) of the internal comparator. It is used to modulate the threshold level for applications such as **voltage-controlled oscillators (VCOs)**, **pulse-width modulation**, and **frequency modulation**. The **0.01 µF bypass capacitor** is essential to filter out any high-frequency noise that may couple into the comparator reference, which would otherwise cause erratic triggering and unstable output timing. **(3 marks — 1 for function, 1 for modulation role, 1 for bypass rationale)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q:** Compare monostable and astable multivibrator modes of a 555 timer in terms of: number of stable states, trigger requirement, output waveform, and a key application.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Monostable | Astable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stable states | **One** (LOW) | **Zero** (continuous oscillation) |
| Trigger | Required (negative pulse to pin 2) | Not required (self-triggered) |
| Output | Single pulse of width **$T = 1.1 RC$** | Square wave of frequency **$f = 1.44 / [(R_A + 2R_B) C]$** |
| Application | **Pulse generator**, missing pulse detector | **Clock generator**, LED flasher, tone generator |

**(3 marks — 1 mark per correct comparison row, full marks for 3 well-explained rows)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Past Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Monostable Multivibrator**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 555 timer connected as a **monostable multivibrator**. Draw the timing diagram showing trigger input, capacitor voltage, and output waveform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 555 monostable circuit using $R = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 1 \text{ \mu F}$, calculate the pulse width. If the trigger pulse is 10 µs wide, comment on whether retriggering is possible. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Monostable Operation**

**Circuit Connection:**
- Pin 8 to Vcc; Pin 1 to GND; Pin 4 (Reset) to Vcc.
- Resistor $R$ from Vcc to pin 7.
- Capacitor $C$ from pin 7 to GND.
- Pin 6 (Threshold) tied to pin 7.
- Pin 2 (Trigger) is the external input.
- Pin 3 is the output; Pin 5 bypassed by 0.01 µF capacitor to GND.

**Operation Stages:**

1. **Stable State (Before Trigger):** The flip-flop is RESET (Q = 0), so OUT = LOW. The discharge transistor is ON, holding $V_c$ at **0 V**. The circuit remains in this state indefinitely — it is the *stable* state.

2. **Trigger Application:** A negative-going pulse on pin 2 below **Vcc/3** activates Comparator 1, producing a HIGH at the S input. This SETS the flip-flop (Q = 1), making **OUT = HIGH**. Q̄ goes LOW, switching OFF the discharge transistor.

3. **Quasi-Stable State (Timing):** With the transistor OFF, capacitor $C$ charges through $R$ from **0 V toward Vcc**, following $V_c(t) = V_{cc}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$.

4. **Cycle Termination:** When $V_c$ reaches **2Vcc/3**, Comparator 2 fires, producing a HIGH at the R input. This RESETS the flip-flop. **OUT goes LOW** and the discharge transistor turns ON, rapidly draining $C$ to 0 V. The circuit returns to its stable state.

5. **Retriggering:** The circuit is edge-sensitive; pin 2 must return above Vcc/3 *before* a new trigger can be accepted. If the trigger pulse is too long, retriggering during the quasi-stable period is possible.

**Timing Diagram Description:**
- TRIG (pin 2): Brief negative spike below Vcc/3.
- $V_c$ (pin 6): Exponential rise from 0 V to 2Vcc/3 over time T.
- OUT (pin 3): Square pulse HIGH of width T, then LOW indefinitely.

**(7 marks — 2 for circuit, 3 for operation stages, 2 for timing diagram description)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical Calculation**

**Given:** $R = 100 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^5 \Omega$, $C = 1 \text{ \mu F} = 10^{-6} \text{ F}$

**Step 1: Calculate pulse width $T$:**

$$T = 1.1 \times R \times C = 1.1 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T = 0.11 \text{ seconds} = 110 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Compare with trigger pulse width:**

The trigger pulse is **10 µs**, and the output pulse width is **110 ms**.

**Step 3: Conclusion on retriggering:**

Since **10 µs ≪ 110 ms**, the trigger pulse is significantly shorter than the output pulse. The trigger returns HIGH long before the output cycle ends. Therefore, **retriggering during the active HIGH state is NOT possible** with this trigger pulse. If retriggering is desired, a longer trigger pulse (greater than 110 ms) or a separate retrigger circuit would be required.

**(7 marks — 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 1 for numerical answer, 2 for retriggering analysis)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Astable Multivibrator (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain with a circuit diagram the operation of a 555 timer in **astable mode**. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an astable 555 circuit to generate a **2 kHz square wave with 60% duty cycle** using a 0.01 µF capacitor. Calculate the required values of $R_A$ and $R_B$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Astable Operation & Frequency Derivation**

**Circuit Connection:**
- Pin 8 to Vcc; Pin 1 to GND; Pin 4 to Vcc.
- $R_A$ from Vcc to pin 7.
- $R_B$ from pin 7 to pin 6.
- $C$ from pin 6 to GND.
- Pin 2 tied to pin 6 (internal self-triggering).
- Pin 3 is the output; Pin 5 decoupled.

**Operation:**

1. **Initial Power-On:** $V_c = 0$ V. Comparator 1 sees TRIG < Vcc/3, SETS the flip-flop. **OUT = HIGH**, transistor OFF.
2. **Charging Phase:** $C$ charges from Vcc through $R_A$ **and** $R_B$ in series. $V_c$ rises from 0 V toward Vcc.
3. **First Threshold Crossing:** When $V_c = 2V_{cc}/3$, Comparator 2 RESETS the flip-flop. **OUT = LOW**, transistor turns ON, creating a discharge path to GND through pin 7.
4. **Discharging Phase:** $C$ discharges through $R_B$ only (since $R_A$ is now in series with the saturated transistor between Vcc and pin 7, but effectively the discharge current flows from $C$ through $R_B$ into pin 7 to GND). $V_c$ falls from 2Vcc/3 toward 0 V.
5. **Second Threshold Crossing:** When $V_c = V_{cc}/3$, Comparator 1 SETS the flip-flop again. **OUT = HIGH**, transistor OFF, charging resumes. The cycle is **self-sustaining**.

**Frequency Derivation:** (As shown in Section 3.2 of these notes)

$$t_H = 0.693 (R_A + R_B) C, \quad t_L = 0.693 R_B C$$

$$T = 0.693 (R_A + 2R_B) C$$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for circuit, 2 for operation, 3 for derivation)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Design Problem**

**Given:** $f = 2 \text{ kHz} = 2 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}$, $D = 60\%$, $C = 0.01 \text{ \mu F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$.

**Step 1: Set up the two design equations.**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100 = 60\% \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2: From equation (2):**

$$0.60 = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$$

$$0.60 (R_A + 2R_B) = R_A + R_B$$

$$0.60 R_A + 1.20 R_B = R_A + R_B$$

$$0.20 R_B = 0.40 R_A$$

$$R_B = 2 R_A \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 3: From equation (1):**

$$R_A + 2R_B = \frac{1.44}{f \cdot C} = \frac{1.44}{2 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$R_A + 2R_B = \frac{1.44}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.2 \times 10^4 = 72 \text{ k}\Omega \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 4: Substitute (3) into (4):**

$$R_A + 2(2 R_A) = 72 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$5 R_A = 72 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_A = 14.4 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Solve for $R_B$:**

$$R_B = 2 R_A = 28.8 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 6: Verification:**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(14.4 + 57.6) \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1.44}{72 \times 10^{-5}} = 2 \text{ kHz} \checkmark$$

$$D = \frac{14.4 + 28.8}{14.4 + 57.6} = \frac{43.2}{72.0} = 0.60 = 60\% \checkmark$$

**(7 marks — 2 for setup, 2 for $R_B$ relation, 2 for numerical values, 1 for verification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often forget to convert kΩ to Ω or µF to F before substituting into formulas. **Always convert to base SI units (Ω and F) first.**
> 2. **Discharge Path Confusion:** Many students assume the discharge current passes through both $R_A$ and $R_B$ — it does NOT. Discharge is through $R_B$ only because the transistor shorts pin 7 to GND, bypassing $R_A$.
> 3. **Threshold Levels:** Confusing **Vcc/3** with **2Vcc/3** is a common error. Remember: the *trigger* fires at **Vcc/3** (lower), and the *threshold* fires at **2Vcc/3** (upper).
> 4. **Duty Cycle Formula Sign:** Some students incorrectly write the duty cycle as $(R_A + 2R_B)/(R_A + R_B)$. The correct form is $(R_A + R_B)/(R_A + 2R_B)$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] The 555 timer is named after the **3 × 5 kΩ internal resistor divider**.
- [x] **Pin 1 = GND**, **Pin 8 = Vcc** — the *power pins*; *never reverse them*.
- [x] **Pin 2 (TRIG)** is **active-LOW**; it SETS the flip-flop when voltage drops below **Vcc/3**.
- [x] **Pin 6 (THR)** is **active-HIGH**; it RESETS the flip-flop when voltage rises above **2Vcc/3**.
- [x] **Pin 7 (DIS)** is an open-collector NPN transistor; it provides a low-impedance discharge path to GND.
- [x] **Pin 4 (RESET)** is active-LOW master reset; tie it to Vcc if not used.
- [x] **Pin 5 (CV)** modulates the 2Vcc/3 reference; bypass with **0.01 µF** to prevent noise.
- [x] **Monostable** = **One** stable state; output pulse width = **$T = 1.1 RC$**.
- [x] **Astable** = **Zero** stable states; free-running oscillator; **$f = 1.44 / [(R_A + 2R_B) C]$**.
- [x] **Bistable** = **Two** stable states; acts as an SR flip-flop; no timing capacitor.
- [x] The **SR flip-flop's Q̄ output** drives the discharge transistor — this is the *logical inversion* that makes the 555 elegant.
- [x] In astable mode, **charging** is through $R_A + R_B$, but **discharging** is through $R_B$ only.
- [x] **Duty cycle** in astable mode = $(R_A + R_B)/(R_A + 2R_B) \times 100\%$.
- [x] The **forbidden state** (THR > 2Vcc/3 AND TRIG < Vcc/3 simultaneously) is avoided by the natural charge/discharge behavior of the circuit.
- [x] The constants **1.1** and **1.44** are the practical approximations of $\ln(3)$ and $1/\ln(2)$, respectively.
- [x] Supply voltage range: **+4.5 V to +15 V**; output current up to **200 mA** (source or sink).
- [x] Common exam mistakes: mixing up **Vcc/3 vs 2Vcc/3**, ignoring unit conversions, and miscounting the discharge path resistors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closed Loop Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Closed Loop Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed Loop Analysis** refers to the study and characterization of an operational amplifier (op-amp) circuit when a portion of the output signal is **fed back** to the input through an external feedback network, forming a complete signal loop. This is in direct contrast to the **Open Loop Configuration** where the op-amp operates without any feedback.

In a **closed loop op-amp configuration**, the op-amp is operated with **negative feedback** (also called degenerative feedback), where the feedback signal is **180° out of phase** with the applied input signal. This feedback mechanism forces the differential input voltage ($V_d$) to approach zero, which is the foundational principle for analyzing every op-amp circuit under this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):**
> Closed loop analysis forms the backbone of all linear op-amp applications. The KTU board examiner frequently tests the ability of students to derive the closed loop voltage gain ($A_{CL}$) for standard configurations and to identify the effect of feedback on parameters like input impedance, output impedance, bandwidth, and gain stability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway:

| Scenario | Open Loop | Closed Loop (with Cruise Control) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | You press the accelerator blindly without seeing the road. | Cruise control monitors speed (output) and adjusts throttle (input) automatically. |
| **Behavior** | Speed fluctuates wildly with road conditions, slope, wind. | Speed is maintained precisely at the set value regardless of disturbances. |
| **Analogy Mapping** | Input directly controls op-amp without feedback. | Feedback network continuously corrects the output to match desired behavior. |

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule of Closed Loop Op-Amp Analysis:**
> 1. **No current flows into the ideal op-amp input terminals** ($I^+ = I^- = 0$)
> 2. **The voltage difference between the two input terminals is virtually zero** ($V^+ = V^-$) — this is the **Virtual Short** concept.
> 3. When the non-inverting terminal is grounded, the inverting terminal is at **Virtual Ground** ($V^- = 0$).

## 1.3 The Concept of Feedback

**Feedback** is the process of returning a portion of the output back to the input. Two fundamental types exist:

- **Positive (Regenerative) Feedback:** Output is fed back in-phase with input → leads to oscillation (used in comparators and oscillators, not linear amplifiers).
- **Negative (Degenerative) Feedback:** Output is fed back out-of-phase with input → stabilizes gain, increases bandwidth, and reduces distortion. This is what we study in closed loop analysis.

**Block Diagram Representation of a Feedback System:**

The fundamental closed loop transfer function is derived from the classic feedback block diagram where:
- $A$ = Open Loop Gain of the op-amp (typically $2 \times 10^5$ for a 741 op-amp)
- $\beta$ = Feedback factor (set by external resistors)
- $A_f$ or $A_{CL}$ = Closed Loop Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Feedback Equation

Consider the standard feedback block diagram. Let the input to the op-amp's differential terminals be $V_d$. The output is $V_o$, and a fraction $\beta V_o$ is fed back to the inverting input.

From the block diagram:
$$V_d = V_{in} - \beta V_o$$

The op-amp amplifies this differential input:
$$V_o = A \cdot V_d$$

Substituting $V_d$:
$$V_o = A \cdot (V_{in} - \beta V_o)$$

Solving for $\frac{V_o}{V_{in}}$:
$$\boxed{A_{CL} = A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $A\beta \gg 1$ (which is always true for practical op-amps), the equation reduces to:
> $$A_f \approx \frac{1}{\beta}$$
> This means the closed loop gain depends **only on the external feedback network** and is independent of the op-amp's internal open loop gain $A$. This is the **magic of negative feedback**.

## 2.2 Types of Negative Feedback Topologies

There are four possible combinations, but for **voltage amplifiers** built using op-amps, the two most important are:

| Feedback Type | Sampling | Mixing | Input Impedance | Output Impedance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage-Series** (Non-Inverting) | Voltage | Series | Increases | Decreases |
| **Voltage-Shunt** (Inverting) | Voltage | Shunt | Decreases | Decreases |
| **Current-Series** | Current | Series | Increases | Increases |
| **Current-Shunt** | Current | Shunt | Decreases | Increases |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Inverting Amplifier** uses **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** and the **Non-Inverting Amplifier** uses **Voltage-Series Feedback**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table — it covers 90% of numerical questions in KTU exams.**

| S.No | Parameter | Inverting Amplifier | Non-Inverting Amplifier | Voltage Follower |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Closed Loop Voltage Gain $A_{CL}$ | $-\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $1$ |
| 2 | Phase Inversion | Yes (180°) | No (0°) | No (0°) |
| 3 | Input Impedance $R_{in}$ | $R_1$ | $R_1 + R_f \Vert R_i$ (very high) | Very high ($> 10^{10}\ \Omega$) |
| 4 | Output Impedance $R_{out}$ | $\dfrac{r_o}{(1+A\beta)}$ (low) | Very low (near zero) | Near zero |
| 5 | Feedback Factor $\beta$ | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ | $1$ (100% feedback) |
| 6 | Loop Gain $A\beta$ | $A \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ | $A \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ | $A$ |
| 7 | Bandwidth Product (GBW) | Constant (Gain $\times$ BW = GBW) | Constant | Constant |
| 8 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) | Depends on op-amp | High (CM signals rejected) | Maximum |

> **Note:** $R_i$ in the input impedance formula for non-inverting amplifier represents the op-amp's intrinsic input resistance. For an ideal op-amp, $R_i \to \infty$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Closed loop analysis is the foundation of **all analog signal processing**:

- **Audio Amplifiers:** Home theatre systems use multiple inverting and non-inverting stages for volume and tone control.
- **Instrumentation:** ECG machines amplify micro-volt signals from the body using closed loop op-amp configurations.
- **Active Filters:** Low-pass, high-pass, and band-pass filters are built by placing capacitors in the feedback network.
- **Data Acquisition Systems:** Signal conditioning before ADC conversion relies on precisely-set closed loop gains.
- **Voltage Regulators:** Precision regulators use closed loop feedback to maintain constant output voltage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed Loop Gain of an Inverting Amplifier

### Circuit Setup
- Input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting terminal through resistor $R_1$.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output $V_o$ to the inverting terminal.
- The non-inverting terminal is grounded.

### Derivation Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the inverting node (let's call it node A).

Current from $V_{in}$ through $R_1$ = Current through $R_f$ to output (since no current enters the ideal op-amp).

$$\frac{V_{in} - V_A}{R_1} = \frac{V_A - V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Virtual Ground** concept.

Since the non-inverting terminal is grounded ($V^+ = 0$) and $V^- = V^+$ (virtual short), we have:
$$V_A = V^- = 0$$

This is the **Virtual Ground** — the node behaves as if it is at 0V, but it is NOT physically connected to ground.

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_A = 0$ into the KCL equation.

$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{0 - V_o}{R_f}$$

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the closed loop gain $A_{CL} = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{in}}$.

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

> The **negative sign** confirms the **180° phase inversion** characteristic of the inverting amplifier.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed Loop Gain of a Non-Inverting Amplifier

### Circuit Setup
- Input signal $V_{in}$ is applied **directly** to the non-inverting terminal.
- Resistor $R_1$ is connected from the inverting terminal to ground.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected from the output to the inverting terminal.

### Derivation Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Since the input is applied at the non-inverting terminal:
$$V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Virtual Short** principle:
$$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 3:** Apply KCL at the inverting node.

The current through $R_1$ (from inverting node to ground) must equal the current through $R_f$ (from output to inverting node), because no current flows into the op-amp.

$$\frac{V^- - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_o - V^-}{R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $V^- = V_{in}$.

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_o - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Cross-multiply to solve for $V_o$.

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 \cdot (V_o - V_{in})$$

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 \cdot V_o - R_1 \cdot V_{in}$$

$$R_1 \cdot V_o = V_{in} \cdot R_f + V_{in} \cdot R_1$$

$$R_1 \cdot V_o = V_{in} \cdot (R_f + R_1)$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the closed loop gain.

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

> The gain is **always positive** (no phase inversion) and **always greater than or equal to 1**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Follower (Unity Gain Buffer)

The voltage follower is a special case of the non-inverting amplifier where the output is **directly connected** to the inverting input. This means $R_f = 0$ and $R_1 = \infty$.

Substituting these values:
$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1$$

$$\boxed{V_o = V_{in}}$$

> Despite having a gain of **unity**, this configuration is incredibly useful as a **buffer** because it provides **very high input impedance** and **very low output impedance**, isolating a high-impedance source from a low-impedance load.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Effect of Negative Feedback on Input & Output Impedance

This is a **high-weightage** KTU derivation. Let us consider a general **voltage-series feedback** (non-inverting) topology.

**Given:**
- Open loop input impedance of the op-amp = $Z_{in(op)}$
- Open loop output impedance of the op-amp = $Z_{out(op)}$
- Open loop voltage gain = $A$

**Step 1:** Input Impedance with Voltage-Series Feedback.

In voltage-series feedback, the feedback signal is in series with the input. The input current sees both the op-amp's input impedance and the feedback-induced voltage opposing it.

The closed loop input impedance is:
$$Z_{in(CL)} = Z_{in(op)} \cdot (1 + A\beta)$$

**Step 2:** Output Impedance with Voltage-Series Feedback.

The output impedance is reduced by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$:
$$Z_{out(CL)} = \frac{Z_{out(op)}}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 3:** For Voltage-Shunt Feedback (Inverting Amplifier):

The input impedance is **divided** by $(1 + A\beta)$, becoming very low (which is why $R_{in} \approx R_1$ for the inverting amplifier):
$$Z_{in(CL)} = \frac{Z_{in(op)}}{1 + A\beta}$$

The output impedance is again reduced:
$$Z_{out(CL)} = \frac{Z_{out(op)}}{1 + A\beta}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) Trade-off

One of the most important consequences of negative feedback is the **constant Gain-Bandwidth Product**.

Let the open loop bandwidth be $f_1$ (the frequency at which the open loop gain drops to $\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$). For a typical op-amp, the open loop gain rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade.

The new closed loop bandwidth $f_f$ is:
$$f_f = f_1 \cdot (1 + A\beta)$$

Multiplying the closed loop gain $A_f$ and the new bandwidth $f_f$:
$$A_f \cdot f_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} \cdot f_1 \cdot (1 + A\beta)$$

$$\boxed{A_f \cdot f_f = A \cdot f_1 = \text{Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The product is a **constant** for a given op-amp. If you increase the closed loop gain by 10×, the bandwidth drops by 10×. This is a fundamental trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generalized Negative Feedback System

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the fundamental signal flow of a closed loop op-amp system. The summing junction subtracts the feedback from the input to produce the error signal that drives the high-gain amplifier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVin["V_in\nInput Signal"] --> nodeSum{"Summing\nJunction\nSubtract FB"}
    nodeFBOut["Feedback\nNetwork beta"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeVd["V_d\nDifferential\nError Voltage"]
    nodeVd --> nodeAmp["A\nOpen Loop\nGain Amplifier"]
    nodeAmp --> nodeVo["V_o\nOutput Voltage\n= A times V_d"]
    nodeVo --> nodeLoad["External\nLoad"]
    nodeVo --> nodeFBOut
```

### Diagram Interpretation
- The **Summing Junction** computes the difference between input and feedback, generating the error signal $V_d$.
- The **Open Loop Gain Block** amplifies this tiny error voltage by the huge factor $A$ to produce the output.
- A **portion of the output** is tapped and routed back through the **Feedback Network** characterized by transfer ratio $\beta$.
- The closed loop transfer function mathematically reduces to $A_{CL} = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$, and for ideal op-amps, $A_{CL} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Common Closed Loop Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph subgraphInv["Inverting Amplifier Topology"]
        nodeInVA["V_in"] --> nodeR1A["R1\nInput Resistor"]
        nodeR1A --> nodeNodeA["Inverting\nNode\nVirtual Ground"]
        nodeRfA["Rf\nFeedback Resistor"] --> nodeNodeA
        nodeNodeA --> nodeOpA["Op-Amp\n741 IC"]
        nodeGndA["Ground\n0V"] --> nodeOpA
        nodeOpA --> nodeVoA["V_o\nInverted Output"]
    end

    subgraph subgraphNonInv["Non-Inverting Amplifier Topology"]
        nodeInVB["V_in"] --> nodePosTerm["V+ Positive\nInput Terminal"]
        nodeR1B["R1\nTo Ground"] --> nodeNegTerm["V- Negative\nInput Terminal"]
        nodeRfB["Rf\nFeedback Resistor"] --> nodeNegTerm
        nodeNegTerm --> nodeOpB["Op-Amp\n741 IC"]
        nodePosTerm --> nodeOpB
        nodeOpB --> nodeVoB["V_o\nNon-Inverted Output"]
    end
```

### Architecture Walkthrough
- **Inverting Topology (Left):** Input enters through $R_1$ to the inverting node. The feedback path is from output through $R_f$ back to the inverting node. The positive terminal is grounded. Output appears with **180° phase shift**.
- **Non-Inverting Topology (Right):** Input enters directly at the positive terminal. The negative terminal is held at virtual ground potential through the $R_1$-$R_f$ voltage divider. Output appears **in-phase** with the input.
- **Virtual Ground/Short:** In both topologies, the op-amp internally forces $V^+ = V^-$, which is the cornerstone of all closed loop analysis.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow of Closed Loop Gain Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart["Identify Op-Amp\nConfiguration Type"] --> nodeCheck{"Inverting or\nNon-Inverting?"}
    nodeCheck --> nodeInvPath["Inverting Path"]
    nodeCheck --> nodeNonInvPath["Non-Inverting Path"]
    nodeInvPath --> nodeFormInv["Apply Formula\nA_CL equals -Rf divided by R1"]
    nodeNonInvPath --> nodeFormNonInv["Apply Formula\nA_CL equals 1 plus Rf divided by R1"]
    nodeFormInv --> nodePhase["Add 180 Degree\nPhase Inversion Note"]
    nodeFormNonInv --> nodeBandwidth["Calculate New\nBandwidth using GBW"]
    nodePhase --> nodeResult["Final Closed Loop\nGain and Phase"]
    nodeBandwidth --> nodeResult
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define virtual ground and virtual short in the context of ideal op-amp analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Virtual Ground:** The inverting terminal of an op-amp in a closed loop configuration is said to be at **virtual ground** when the non-inverting terminal is physically grounded. Due to the virtual short principle, the inverting terminal also sits at 0V, even though it is not physically connected to ground. No current flows into this node, making it an ideal summing junction. (1.5 Marks)

**Virtual Short:** This principle states that in an ideal op-amp with negative feedback, the voltage difference between the inverting and non-inverting terminals is zero, i.e., $V^+ = V^-$. This arises from the fact that the open loop gain $A$ is infinite, so any finite output requires infinitesimally small differential input. (1.5 Marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four advantages of negative feedback in an op-amp amplifier.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Gain Stability:** Closed loop gain becomes independent of temperature, supply voltage, and op-amp parameter variations. (1 Mark)
2. **Increased Bandwidth:** The bandwidth increases by a factor of $(1 + A\beta)$. (1 Mark)
3. **Reduced Distortion:** Non-linear distortion components are reduced by the same factor $(1 + A\beta)$. (0.5 Marks)
4. **Input/Output Impedance Control:** Voltage-series feedback increases input impedance; voltage-shunt feedback decreases it. Output impedance always decreases. (0.5 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, Module-level Part B questions carry 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. You are required to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Derive the expression for the closed loop voltage gain of an inverting amplifier using an ideal op-amp. Also explain the effect of negative feedback on the input and output impedance.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Closed Loop Gain (7 Marks)

**Given:** Ideal op-amp with input $V_{in}$ applied through $R_1$ to the inverting terminal, feedback resistor $R_f$, and non-inverting terminal grounded.

**Step 1:** Since the non-inverting terminal is grounded, $V^+ = 0$.

**Step 2:** By the virtual short principle, $V^- = V^+ = 0$. Thus, the inverting node is at **virtual ground**.

**Step 3:** Since no current enters the ideal op-amp, KCL at the inverting node gives:

$$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V^- - V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $V^- = 0$:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the gain:

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates a **180° phase shift** between input and output. (7 Marks)

#### Part (b) — Effect on Input/Output Impedance (7 Marks)

**Input Impedance:**
For an inverting amplifier, the feedback is of the **voltage-shunt** type. The input impedance seen by the source is the resistance $R_1$ itself, since the inverting node is at virtual ground. Hence, the effective input impedance of the closed loop amplifier is:

$$Z_{in(CL)} = R_1$$

This is **relatively low** (typically a few kΩ), which is a limitation when interfacing with high-impedance sources. (3.5 Marks)

**Output Impedance:**
The output impedance of the closed loop amplifier is reduced by the loop gain factor $(1 + A\beta)$:

$$Z_{out(CL)} = \frac{r_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

where $r_o$ is the op-amp's intrinsic output impedance (typically 75 Ω for a 741 op-amp). Since $(1 + A\beta)$ is very large, the closed loop output impedance approaches zero, making the op-amp behave as an **ideal voltage source**. (3.5 Marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the closed loop voltage gain of a non-inverting amplifier. Show that the voltage follower is a special case of this configuration.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Closed Loop Gain (7 Marks)

**Given:** Input $V_{in}$ applied directly to the non-inverting terminal, $R_1$ from inverting terminal to ground, and $R_f$ as the feedback resistor.

**Step 1:** Since the input is at the non-inverting terminal:
$$V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 2:** By the virtual short principle:
$$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 3:** Apply KCL at the inverting node. No current flows into the op-amp, so the current through $R_1$ equals the current through $R_f$:

$$\frac{V^- - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_o - V^-}{R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $V^- = V_{in}$:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_o - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Cross-multiply:
$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 \cdot V_o - R_1 \cdot V_{in}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange:
$$V_o \cdot R_1 = V_{in} \cdot (R_1 + R_f)$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the gain:

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The gain is **always positive** and **greater than or equal to 1**. (7 Marks)

#### Part (b) — Voltage Follower as a Special Case (7 Marks)

A **voltage follower** (or unity gain buffer) is obtained by setting $R_f = 0$ (a direct wire from output to inverting input) and $R_1 = \infty$ (open circuit, removing the resistor to ground).

Substituting these values into the non-inverting gain formula:

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1$$

$$V_o = V_{in}$$

**Key Properties of the Voltage Follower:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Voltage Gain | Unity (1) |
| Input Impedance | Very high (approaches infinity) |
| Output Impedance | Very low (approaches zero) |
| Phase Shift | Zero (in-phase) |

**Applications:** Used as a **buffer** to isolate a high-impedance source from a low-impedance load, preventing **loading effects**. It is also used in sample-and-hold circuits, active filters, and ADC driver stages. (7 Marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Missing the Negative Sign:** Students often write $A_{CL} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ for the inverting amplifier. The **negative sign** is mandatory — losing it costs 1 to 2 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing Virtual Ground with Physical Ground:** Virtual ground is a **conceptual node** at 0V potential, NOT physically shorted to ground. Writing "the inverting terminal is connected to ground" is a mark-losing mistake.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the KCL Sign Convention:** When applying KCL at the inverting node, the direction of currents must be consistent. Mixing signs in $\dfrac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \dfrac{V^- - V_o}{R_f}$ leads to a sign error in the final gain.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the $(1 + A\beta)$ Factor in Bandwidth Questions:** When asked about bandwidth, students compute the new bandwidth $f_f = f_1(1 + A\beta)$ and forget to compute the **Gain-Bandwidth Product** check.
>
> 5. **Units Not Mentioned:** Always write units (V, mV, kHz, MHz, dB) wherever applicable. A numerical answer without units is considered incomplete in KTU valuation.
>
> 6. **Drawing Circuit without Labels:** In a 14-mark question, the **circuit diagram** is worth 2 to 3 marks. Always label $R_1$, $R_f$, $V_{in}$, $V_o$, the inverting (-) and non-inverting (+) terminals.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Closed Loop Analysis**

- [ ] **Closed loop gain formula:** $A_{CL} = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ for $A\beta \gg 1$.
- [ ] **Inverting amplifier gain:** $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ (180° phase inversion).
- [ ] **Non-inverting amplifier gain:** $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ (no phase inversion).
- [ ] **Voltage follower gain:** $A_{CL} = 1$ (unity gain, used as a buffer).
- [ ] **Virtual Ground:** $V^- = 0$ when $V^+$ is grounded. The node is not physically grounded.
- [ ] **Virtual Short:** $V^+ = V^-$ always holds in negative feedback for ideal op-amps.
- [ ] **No current into op-amp inputs:** $I^+ = I^- = 0$ for an ideal op-amp.
- [ ] **Input impedance — Inverting:** Low (equals $R_1$). **Non-inverting:** Very high.
- [ ] **Output impedance — Both topologies:** Very low, reduced by factor $(1 + A\beta)$.
- [ ] **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW):** $A_f \cdot f_f = A \cdot f_1 = $ constant.
- [ ] **Bandwidth increases** with negative feedback by factor $(1 + A\beta)$.
- [ ] **Distortion reduces** by factor $(1 + A\beta)$.
- [ ] **Feedback factor for both amplifiers:** $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$.
- [ ] **Loop gain:** $A\beta = A \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$.
- [ ] **Voltage-Series Feedback:** Used in non-inverting amplifier — increases $R_{in}$, decreases $R_{out}$.
- [ ] **Voltage-Shunt Feedback:** Used in inverting amplifier — decreases $R_{in}$, decreases $R_{out}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lock and Capture Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lock and Capture Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

In a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** built using the IC 565 (or its functional equivalent), the dynamic operating behaviour of the Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) is described by two critical frequency metrics: **Lock Range** and **Capture Range**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lock Range (Tracking Range), $2f_L$ :**
> It is the band of input frequencies, centred around the VCO free-running frequency $f_0$, over which the PLL can **maintain** a locked condition once it is already locked. The VCO will follow any slow frequency variation of the input signal within this band without losing lock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capture Range (Acquisition Range), $2f_C$ :**
> It is the band of input frequencies, centred around $f_0$, over which the PLL can **initially acquire** lock from an unlocked condition. Outside this band the loop cannot achieve synchronisation, no matter how long one waits.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a child riding a merry-go-round with a parent standing still in the centre.

- **Capture Range = "The Handshake Distance"** — The parent can only grab the child's hand if the child is moving at a speed that is *close enough* to the parent's pace. If the child is spinning ridiculously fast or absurdly slow, no hand-shake is possible.
- **Lock Range = "The Hand-Holding Band"** — Once the parent has caught the child's hand, the parent can walk faster or slower within a *wider* tolerance, and the child will still hold on. So the **Lock Range is always greater than the Capture Range**.

Mathematically:

$$f_C \leq f_L$$

$$2f_C \leq 2f_L$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board Examination Insight:**
> The **Lock Range** depends only on the **VCO and Phase Detector** (gain terms), whereas the **Capture Range** is *additionally* limited by the **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** time constant $\tau = R_f C_f$. A smaller $\tau$ widens the capture range; a larger $\tau$ narrows it.

### 1.3 Why These Ranges Matter in Engineering

- **FM Demodulators** require the loop to track the input — wide **lock range** is essential.
- **Frequency Synthesizers** demand precise capture from a far-off frequency — wide **capture range** matters.
- **Tone Decoders & Motor Speed Controllers** balance both parameters carefully.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic (Structured Steps)

1. **Loop Open** — Input frequency $f_{in}$ is far from $f_0$. The Phase Detector (PD) output is a beat-frequency sinusoid. The LPF averages it to ≈ 0, so the VCO runs at $f_0$.
2. **Approach Phase** — As $f_{in}$ approaches $f_0$, the beat frequency decreases. The LPF output begins to develop a small DC error voltage.
3. **Capture Condition** — When the DC error pushes the VCO frequency close enough to $f_{in}$, the loop *snaps* into lock. The maximum input frequency offset at which this *snap-in* occurs defines the **Capture Limit** $\pm f_C$.
4. **Locked Condition** — Inside $\pm f_L$, the VCO output frequency tracks $f_{in}$ exactly, with a fixed phase error.
5. **Loss of Lock** — If $f_{in}$ is swept beyond $\pm f_L$, the loop breaks lock and the VCO returns to $f_0$ (or, with hysteresis, it may re-lock only when $f_{in}$ comes back within $\pm f_C$).

### 2.2 Why is Lock Range > Capture Range?

- During **Capture**, the LPF filters the PD's AC component. If the beat frequency is high, the LPF suppresses it; only low beat frequencies can develop a usable DC control voltage.
- Once **Locked**, the PD output is *only* a DC error signal (high frequency components have vanished), so the LPF no longer limits the loop — the VCO simply follows the input.
- Therefore, the **Capture** range is a *subset* of the **Lock** range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> The capture range can be intentionally narrowed by increasing $R_f$ or $C_f$ to provide **noise immunity** — useful in FSK decoders and carrier-acquisition circuits where a clean, narrow acquisition window is required.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (for IC 565)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are evaluated using **SI units** (Hz, $\Omega$, F, V).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
