# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks each, short answer)

### Question 1: Define the term "Capture Range" of a PLL. How does it differ from the Lock Range? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Capture Range** of a PLL is the range of input frequencies over which the PLL can *initiate* lock acquisition starting from a free-running (unlocked) state. It is denoted as $2 \Delta f_C$ or $\Delta f_C$ (half-range).

**Key Differences:**

| **Lock Range** | **Capture Range** |
|---|---|
| Set by VCO sensitivity and maximum control voltage | Set by the Low-Pass Filter (LPF) cutoff frequency |
| Independent of LPF capacitor $C$ | Strongly dependent on LPF capacitor $C$ |
| Always wider than capture range | Always narrower than lock range |
| Refers to *tracking* an already-locked input | Refers to *initial acquisition* of lock |
| Formula: $2 \Delta f_L = 8 f_o$ | Formula: $2 \Delta f_C = \tfrac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{2 \pi \Delta f_L / (3.6 \times 10^3 C)}$ |

---

### Question 2: Define Resolution and Accuracy of a Digital-to-Analog Converter. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Resolution** is the smallest change in the analog output that can be distinguished as the digital input changes by 1 LSB. It is also called the **step size**:

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

It is often expressed as a percentage of full-scale: $\% \text{Resolution} = \dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100$.

**Accuracy** is the deviation of the actual DAC output from the ideal theoretical value for a given input code. A common specification is:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \left\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \right\vert \le \pm \tfrac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}$$

Resolution is *set by the architecture* (number of bits), while accuracy is *set by component tolerances, op-amp offsets, and switch resistances* (i.e., by manufacturing realities).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Format:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer *one* complete question from the choice given.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Phase Locked Loop using IC 565. Derive the expression for its free-running frequency and lock range.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 for the Mermaid architecture.)

**Working Explanation:**

A PLL is a closed-loop negative feedback system that synchronizes the VCO output with an incoming reference signal. The IC 565 integrates the Phase Detector, LPF, and VCO in a single package.

**1. Phase Detector (PD):** The PD is a balanced multiplier implemented using a Gilbert cell (four-quadrant multiplier). It multiplies the input signal $v_{in} = V_s \sin(2 \pi f_{in} t + \theta_{in})$ with the VCO output $v_{VCO} = V_o \cos(2 \pi f_{VCO} t + \theta_{VCO})$. Using the product-to-sum identity:

$$v_d = K_m \cdot v_{in} \cdot v_{VCO} = \dfrac{K_m V_s V_o}{2} \left[ \sin(\theta_{in} - \theta_{VCO}) + \sin(2 \pi (f_{in} + f_{VCO}) t + \dots) \right]$$

The first term is the **DC error voltage** proportional to phase difference; the second is a high-frequency component at $f_{in} + f_{VCO}$ which the LPF removes.

**2. Low-Pass Filter:** A first-order RC filter (R internal ≈ 3.6 kΩ, C external) extracts the DC component $V_d = K_d \sin(\theta_e)$, where $\theta_e = \theta_{in} - \theta_{VCO}$.

**3. VCO:** The control voltage $V_d$ shifts the VCO frequency. A positive $V_d$ raises the frequency; negative lowers it. The relationship is:

$$f_{VCO} = f_o + K_o \cdot V_d$$

The VCO is built around a current-controlled multivibrator with an external $R_T$ and $C_T$.

**4. Feedback:** The VCO output is fed back to the PD, closing the loop.

**Free-Running Frequency Derivation:**

The current source charges $C_T$ linearly. The Schmitt trigger thresholds are at $\tfrac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ and $\tfrac{2}{3} V_{CC}$. The charge time is:

$$T_1 = \dfrac{C_T \cdot (V_{CC}/3)}{(V_{CC}-V_C)/R_T} \approx \dfrac{R_T C_T}{3}$$

The discharge time $T_2$ equals $T_1$ by symmetry. Total period:

$$T = 2 T_1 = \dfrac{2 R_T C_T}{3}$$

The IC 565 datasheet gives the empirical formula:

$$\boxed{f_o = \dfrac{1.2}{4 R_T C_T}}$$

**Lock Range Derivation:**

In the locked state, $f_{VCO} = f_{in}$, and the phase error is held constant. The phase detector sensitivity is $K_d = V_{CC}/\pi$ (V/rad). The maximum control voltage is achieved at $\theta_e = \pi/2$:

$$V_{d,max} = K_d \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{2} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2}$$

The VCO sensitivity is $K_o = 8 f_o / V_{CC}$ (Hz/V). The maximum frequency deviation (half lock range):

$$\Delta f_L = K_o \cdot V_{d,max} = \dfrac{8 f_o}{V_{CC}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2} = 4 f_o$$

**Total Lock Range:**

$$\boxed{2 \Delta f_L = 8 f_o}$$

This is independent of the supply voltage and is a clean KTU favorite result.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** An IC 565 PLL has $R_T = 10$ k$\Omega$ and $C_T = 0.01$ $\mu$F. The supply voltage is $V_{CC} = \pm 10$ V. The LPF capacitor is $C = 1$ $\mu$F. Calculate: (i) Free-running frequency, (ii) Lock range, (iii) Capture range.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$R_T = 10$ k$\Omega = 10^4$ $\Omega$
$C_T = 0.01$ $\mu$F $= 10^{-8}$ F
$V_{CC} = 10 - (-10) = 20$ V
$C = 1$ $\mu$F $= 10^{-6}$ F

**(i) Free-running frequency:**

$$f_o = \dfrac{1.2}{4 R_T C_T} = \dfrac{1.2}{4 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{1.2}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 3 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Lock Range (total):**

$$2 \Delta f_L = \dfrac{8 f_o}{V_{CC}} \cdot V_{CC} = 8 f_o = 8 \times 3 = 24 \text{ kHz}$$

Half lock range: $\Delta f_L = 12$ kHz. So the PLL can track inputs from $f_o - \Delta f_L = -9$ kHz (clipped to 0) to $f_o + \Delta f_L = 15$ kHz. The lock range in the *meaningful positive frequency* band is $f_o \pm 12$ kHz, i.e., **0 to 15 kHz** (one-sided interpretation, depending on the sign of $V_d$).

**(iii) Capture Range (total):**

Using the formula:

$$2 \Delta f_C = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi \Delta f_L}{3.6 \times 10^3 \cdot C}}$$

Substituting $\Delta f_L = 12 \times 10^3$ Hz and $C = 10^{-6}$ F:

$$2 \Delta f_C = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi \times 12 \times 10^3}{3.6 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$2 \Delta f_C = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{24 \pi \times 10^3}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$2 \Delta f_C = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{6.667 \pi \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \times 2582.0 \times \sqrt{\pi} = \dfrac{2582.0 \times 1.7725}{3.1416}$$

$$2 \Delta f_C \approx 1456.6 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.457 \text{ kHz}$$

**Half capture range:** $\Delta f_C \approx 728$ Hz.

**Summary:**

| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| $f_o$ | 3 kHz |
| $2 \Delta f_L$ | 24 kHz (half: 12 kHz) |
| $2 \Delta f_C$ | ≈ 1.457 kHz (half: ≈ 728 Hz) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the operation of a 4-bit R-2R ladder Digital-to-Analog Converter with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for its output voltage.

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Operation (Refer to Section 4.3 diagram):**

The R-2R ladder uses only two resistor values: $R$ and $2R$. Each bit of the 4-bit digital input $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$ controls a single-pole double-throw switch. The switch connects the corresponding 2R vertical resistor to either the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ (when bit = 1) or to ground (when bit = 0).

The ladder operates on the principle of **current division at every node**. Consider the MSB side (right end) of the ladder:

- Looking into the ladder from any node, the equivalent resistance is $R$ (a key property of the R-2R topology).
- The current at the MSB node is $I_3 = V_{ref} / (2R + R_{eq}) = V_{ref} / (3R)$. But since the op-amp inverting input is at *virtual ground*, the equivalent resistance looking into the ladder is exactly $R$. So the MSB current is $V_{ref} / 2R$.
- At the next node, the current splits: half goes to the bit-2 branch, half continues leftward. So $I_2 = V_{ref} / 4R$.
- Similarly, $I_1 = V_{ref} / 8R$ and $I_0 = V_{ref} / 16R$.

Each current is gated by its corresponding bit switch — the current exists in the bit branch *only if* the bit is 1.

**Total current flowing into the op-amp virtual ground:**

$$I_{total} = b_3 \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2R} + b_2 \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{4R} + b_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{8R} + b_0 \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{16R}$$

**Output voltage (op-amp converts current to voltage with feedback $R_f = R$):**

$$v_o = -R_f \cdot I_{total} = -R \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{R} \left( \dfrac{b_3}{2} + \dfrac{b_2}{4} + \dfrac{b_1}{8} + \dfrac{b_0}{16} \right)$$

$$\boxed{v_o = -V_{ref} \left( \dfrac{b_3}{2} + \dfrac{b_2}{4} + \dfrac{b_1}{8} + \dfrac{b_0}{16} \right)}$$

For input word $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1000$ (only MSB on), $v_o = -V_{ref}/2$.
For input word $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 0001$ (only LSB on), $v_o = -V_{ref}/16$.

**Generalization to $n$-bits:**

$$v_o = -V_{ref} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{b_i}{2^{n-i}}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a 12-bit DAC, find the percentage resolution and the step size if the full-scale output is 10 V. If the input is $100100110101_2$, calculate the analog output voltage. Comment on the importance of monotonicity.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V, $V_{ref} = 10$ V (assumed equal to FS for a unity-gain DAC).

**Percentage Resolution:**

$$\% \text{Resolution} = \dfrac{1}{2^{12} - 1} \times 100 = \dfrac{1}{4095} \times 100 \approx 0.0244 \text{ %}$$

**Step Size (Resolution in volts):**

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{10}{4095} \approx 2.442 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

**Input Word:** $b_{11} b_{10} \dots b_0 = 100100110101_2$

**Decimal equivalent:**

$$1 \cdot 2^{11} + 0 \cdot 2^{10} + 0 \cdot 2^9 + 1 \cdot 2^8 + 0 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 1 \cdot 2^5 + 1 \cdot 2^4 + 0 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0$$

$$= 2048 + 0 + 0 + 256 + 0 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 1$$

$$= 2048 + 256 + 32 + 16 + 4 + 1 = 2357$$

**Analog Output Voltage:**

$$v_o = \Delta V \times \text{Decimal Value} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3} \times 2357 \approx 5.755 \text{ V}$$

(Or, equivalently: $v_o = V_{ref} \times \dfrac{2357}{4095} = 10 \times 0.5756 = 5.756$ V.)

**Monotonicity:**

A DAC is **monotonic** if its output never *decreases* as the input code increases from zero to full-scale. In other words, $V_{out}(k+1) \ge V_{out}(k)$ for all codes $k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monotonicity is a critical specification in control systems** (e.g., a motor driven by a DAC must not run *backwards* if the digital code increments by 1). The R-2R ladder is naturally monotonic if the resistor matching is good, but the weighted resistor DAC can be non-monotonic if the MSB resistor is slightly larger than its ideal value (an error of 1 LSB in the MSB can cause a *drop* in output when the MSB is turned on).

**Effect of Monotonicity Failure:**

If a 4-bit weighted DAC has the MSB resistor $R/8$ accidentally as $R/9$ (≈ 11% error), then when the input changes from $0111$ to $1000$, the output *decreases* instead of increasing. This is a non-monotonic behavior and is unacceptable in feedback control systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (PLL):**
> 1. **Mixing up the lock and capture range formulas.** Lock range is $8 f_o$ and *does not* depend on $C$. Capture range depends on $C$ and is *always smaller* than the lock range.
> 2. **Forgetting the $V_{CC}$ factor in the lock range formula.** When supply voltage changes, the lock range *per Hz* changes, but the total $8 f_o$ expression already absorbs this when the standard formula is used. Students often write $\Delta f_L = 8 f_o / V_{CC}$ (per V) and forget to multiply by $V_{CC}$.
> 3. **Confusing $R_T$ and $R_f$.** In the PLL, $R_T$ is the VCO timing resistor (sets $f_o$). $R_f$ in op-amp circuits is feedback. Don't mix them up.
>
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (DAC):**
> 1. **Wrong indexing of bits.** The LSB ($b_0$) corresponds to the *smallest* weight ($2^0 = 1$), the MSB ($b_{n-1}$) to the *largest* weight ($2^{n-1}$). Reversing these is an instant 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Sign of output.** The op-amp summer produces a *negative* output. The minus sign is essential — the KTU examiner always checks.
> 3. **% Resolution = 1/2^n − 1, NOT 1/2^n.** The −1 in the denominator is a KTU classic trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **PLL IC 565 core formula:** $f_o = 1.2 / (4 R_T C_T)$ — memorize in both SI and practical (k$\Omega$, $\mu$F) units.
- **Lock range (total):** $2 \Delta f_L = 8 f_o$ — independent of supply voltage and LPF components.
- **Capture range (total):** $2 \Delta f_C = (1/\pi)\sqrt{2 \pi \Delta f_L / (3.6 \times 10^3 C)}$ — narrower than lock range, shrinks with larger LPF capacitor.
- **VCO sensitivity:** $K_o = 8 f_o / V_{CC}$ (Hz/V).
- **Phase detector sensitivity:** $K_d = V_{CC}/\pi$ (V/rad).
- **DAC resolution:** Step size $\Delta V = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$.
- **% Resolution:** $1/(2^n - 1) \times 100$ — note the −1.
- **4-bit Weighted DAC output:** $v_o = -V_{ref}(b_0 + 2 b_1 + 4 b_2 + 8 b_3)$ when $R_f = R$.
- **4-bit R-2R Ladder output:** $v_o = -V_{ref}(b_0/16 + b_1/8 + b_2/4 + b_3/2)$ when $R_f = R$.
- **Monotonicity:** Output must increase (or stay constant) for monotonically increasing input. Essential for control applications.
- **PLL Applications:** FM demodulation, FSK demod, frequency synthesis, motor speed control, tone decoding.
- **DAC Applications:** Audio playback, video graphics, function generators, motor control, instrumentation.
- **Always include a block diagram** in your answer to PLL questions — examiners expect it (1–2 marks reserved for it).
- **Always show the bit indexing** ($b_0$ = LSB, $b_{n-1}$ = MSB) in DAC derivations.
- **Operational states of PLL:** Free-running → Acquisition → Locked → (break lock if $f_{in}$ drifts beyond lock range).
- **IC 565 frequency range:** 0.001 Hz to 500 kHz.
- **SNR for ideal n-bit DAC:** $6.02n + 1.76$ dB.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Sanity Check Before Submission:** For any DAC, verify (a) the step size × maximum code = $V_{FS}$ (or very close, off by one LSB), and (b) the output sign matches the op-amp configuration. For any PLL problem, verify $\Delta f_C < \Delta f_L$ and both are smaller than $f_o$ in typical cases.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **Linear Integrated Circuits (LIC)**, *Specifications* refer to the set of quantitatively measurable performance parameters that characterize the behaviour, accuracy, speed, and operating limits of an operational amplifier (op-amp) or any analog IC. These parameters are the bridge between the **Ideal Op-Amp Model** (a theoretical construct) and the **Practical / Real Op-Amp** (the manufactured silicon device).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Specifications are manufacturer-defined numerical limits and measured parameters which describe how closely a real op-amp approximates the ideal device under specified conditions of supply voltage, load, temperature, and frequency.*

### 1.2 Classification of Op-Amp Specifications

Op-amp specifications are broadly classified into three categories based on the operating condition under which they are measured:

| Category | Description | Examples |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **DC Specifications** | Measured under steady-state (quiescent) conditions | Input bias current, Input offset current, Input offset voltage |
| **AC Specifications** | Measured under dynamic / time-varying conditions | Slew rate, Gain-bandwidth product, Settling time |
| **Absolute Maximum Ratings** | Limits beyond which the IC may be permanently damaged | Maximum supply voltage, Maximum differential input voltage, Operating temperature range |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Showroom" Analogy

Imagine you walk into a car showroom. The salesman reads a *specification sheet* (0–100 km/h in 8 seconds, mileage 18 kmpl, 5-star safety, etc.). These numbers tell you how the car **deviates from a "perfect car"**.

- The **ideal car** = infinite speed, zero fuel, zero cost. (Theoretical.)
- The **real car** = Maruti, BMW, Tesla. (Has actual numbers.)
- The **specifications** = the printed data sheet that quantifies the difference.

> [!NOTE]
> An op-amp **datasheet** is the engineer's "showroom specification sheet." Every deviation from the ideal — offset, noise, slow response — is quantified as a specification.

### 1.4 Why These Specifications Matter in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes understanding **non-ideal effects** because real circuit performance (like integrator drift, comparator accuracy, filter Q-factor) depends entirely on these parameters. The university frequently tests the student's ability to:

1. Identify which parameter is responsible for a given **practical limitation** (drift → thermal drift, slow response → slew rate, common-mode pickup → CMRR).
2. Compute numerical values like offset voltage at output, or required compensation current.

### 1.5 The Ideal Op-Amp — The Reference Point

Before studying specifications, the **ideal op-amp** must be defined because every specification is a *deviation* from it:

- **Open-loop voltage gain** $A_{OL} = \infty$
- **Input impedance** $R_{in} = \infty$
- **Output impedance** $R_{out} = 0$
- **Bandwidth** $BW = \infty$
- **Zero output** when $V_1 = V_2$ (zero offset)
- **Infinite Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**
- **Infinite Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)**
- **Infinite Slew Rate**

> [!TIP]
> A student should memorize this list. **Every single specification answers the question: "How is the real op-amp less perfect than this list?"**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC (Static) Specifications

#### 2.1.1 Input Bias Current ($I_B$)

**Definition:** The average of the two DC bias currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting input terminals of the op-amp, measured with the output at zero volts.

$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$

**Why it exists:** The input stage of an op-amp is a **BJT differential pair** (or FET pair). BJTs require a base current to keep the transistor ON. Even FETs leak a small gate current.

**Typical value:** BJT input op-amp (µA741) → **80 nA to 500 nA**. FET input (LF351) → **50 pA**.

**Why it matters in circuits:** $I_B$ flowing through the input resistors produces an unwanted DC voltage that gets amplified. This is why **bias compensation resistors** are added.

#### 2.1.2 Input Offset Current ($I_{OS}$)

**Definition:** The algebraic difference between the two input bias currents.

$$I_{OS} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$

**Why it exists:** No two transistors in the input stage are perfectly matched. One always draws slightly more current than the other.

**Typical value:** For µA741 → **20 nA to 50 nA**. Generally, $I_{OS} \approx 0.1 \times I_B$.

#### 2.1.3 Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$)

**Definition:** The small DC voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals to force the output voltage to zero.

**Why it exists:** Mismatch in the input transistor pair's $V_{BE}$ (BJT) or $V_{GS}$ (FET) due to slight differences in geometry, doping, or temperature.

**Typical value:** µA741 → **2 mV to 6 mV**. Precision op-amp OP-07 → **25 µV to 100 µV**.

**Effect on output:** For a non-inverting amplifier with gain $A_{CL}$:

$$V_{out,offset} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_{IO}$$

#### 2.1.4 Thermal Drift (Temperature Coefficient)

**Definition:** The rate of change of input offset voltage (or bias current) with temperature, measured in **µV/°C**.

**Formulas:**

$$\text{Thermal drift of } V_{IO} = \frac{\Delta V_{IO}}{\Delta T} \quad [\mu V/°C]$$

$$\text{Thermal drift of } I_{OS} = \frac{\Delta I_{OS}}{\Delta T} \quad [pA/°C]$$

**Total offset at any temperature:**

$$V_{IO}(T) = V_{IO}(25°C) + \left(\frac{\Delta V_{IO}}{\Delta T}\right)(T - 25°C)$$

#### 2.1.5 Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR) / Supply Sensitivity

**Definition:** The ability of the op-amp to reject variations in the supply voltage. Defined as the ratio of change in supply voltage to the equivalent change in input offset voltage, with output held constant.

$$PSRR = \frac{\Delta V_{S}}{\Delta V_{IO}} \quad \text{(commonly expressed in dB)}$$

$$PSRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\Delta V_{S}}{\Delta V_{IO}}\right)$$

### 2.2 AC (Dynamic) Specifications

#### 2.2.1 Slew Rate (SR)

**Definition:** The maximum rate of change of output voltage per unit time, when a large step signal is applied at the input. Measured in **V/µs**.

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max}$$

**Why it exists:** Internal compensation capacitor and current source limits exist. When the input tries to swing faster than SR, the output becomes a **ramp** instead of a step.

**Limitation for sinusoidal output:** For an undistorted sine wave of peak amplitude $V_p$ at frequency $f$:

$$SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$$

**Full-power bandwidth:**

$$f_{FPBW} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{p,max}}$$

**Typical value:** µA741 → **0.5 V/µs**. LF351 → **13 V/µs**. LM318 → **70 V/µs**.

#### 2.2.2 Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)

**Definition:** The product of the closed-loop voltage gain and the -3 dB bandwidth of the closed-loop amplifier. Constant for a given op-amp.

$$GBW = A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB} = A_{OL} \times f_c$$

Where $f_c$ is the dominant pole frequency.

**Typical value:** µA741 → **1 MHz**. LM318 → **15 MHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the trade-off: "If you double the gain, your bandwidth halves."

#### 2.2.3 Settling Time ($t_s$)

**Definition:** The time required for the output to reach and remain within a specified error band (e.g., 0.1% or 0.01%) of its final value, after a step input.

**Critical for:** A/D converter driver circuits, sample-and-hold amplifiers.

#### 2.2.4 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

**Definition:** The ability of the op-amp to reject signals that appear simultaneously on both inputs (common-mode signals), relative to the differential gain.

$$CMRR = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Equivalent input offset due to common-mode noise:**

$$V_{IO,cm} = \frac{V_{cm}}{CMRR}$$

**Typical value:** µA741 → **90 dB**. Precision op-amp OP-07 → **120 dB**.

#### 2.2.5 Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$)

**Definition:** The differential voltage gain of the op-amp with no external feedback.

$$A_{OL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{id}}$$

**Typical value:** µA741 → **200,000 (106 dB)**. Note that $A_{OL}$ is **frequency-dependent** and rolls off at -20 dB/decade above $f_c$.

### 2.3 Other Critical Specifications

| Specification | Symbol | Definition | Typical (µA741) |
|---------------|--------|------------|-----------------|
| Input resistance | $R_{in}$ | Resistance seen between differential inputs | **2 MΩ** |
| Output resistance | $R_{out}$ | Thevenin resistance at output | **75 Ω** |
| Output voltage swing | $V_{o,sw}$ | Maximum peak output before clipping | **±13 V** (on ±15 V supply) |
| Power consumption | $P_D$ | Quiescent power drawn from supplies | **85 mW** |
| Common-mode input range | $V_{ICR}$ | Range of $V_{cm}$ where op-amp operates linearly | **±13 V** |
| Differential input range | $V_{ID,max}$ | Maximum allowed $V_{id}$ before damage | **±30 V** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU 2024 University Exams.**

| # | Specification | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---------------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Input Bias Current | $I_B = (I_{B1}+I_{B2})/2$ | A, nA | Average |
| 2 | Input Offset Current | $I_{OS} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$ | A, nA | Difference |
| 3 | Output Offset Voltage (Non-Inv) | $V_{out} = (1 + R_f/R_1)V_{IO}$ | V | Due to $V_{IO}$ |
| 4 | Total Input Offset at $T$ | $V_{IO}(T) = V_{IO}(25°C) + (\Delta V_{IO}/\Delta T)(T-25)$ | V | Drift included |
| 5 | Slew Rate | $SR = dV_{out}/dt \vert_{max}$ | V/µs | Time domain |
| 6 | Slew Rate Limit (Sine) | $SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$ | V/µs | Sine wave |
| 7 | Full-Power Bandwidth | $f_{FPBW} = SR / (2\pi V_{p,max})$ | Hz | Sine full power |
| 8 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBW = A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB}$ | Hz | Constant |
| 9 | CMRR (dB) | $CMRR = 20\log(A_d/A_{cm})$ | dB | Ratio |
| 10 | PSRR (dB) | $PSRR = 20\log(\Delta V_S/\Delta V_{IO})$ | dB | Ratio |
| 11 | Output Offset due to $I_{OS}$ | $V_{out} = I_{OS} \times R_f$ | V | Inverting amp |
| 12 | Bias Compensation Resistor | $R_{comp} = R_1 \vert\mid R_f$ | Ω | In non-inv |
| 13 | Open-Loop Gain (dB) | $A_{OL,dB} = 20\log(A_{OL})$ | dB | Standard |
| 14 | Time Period of Triangle (SR) | $T = 4V_p/SR$ | s | Triangle wave |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These specifications are not abstract — they directly constrain **every analog design**:

- **Audio pre-amplifiers:** Need high CMRR (≥ 80 dB) to reject mains hum.
- **Sensor signal conditioning:** Need low $V_{IO}$ and low thermal drift (precision op-amps like OP-07, INA128).
- **High-speed pulse processing:** Need high SR (LM318, AD8000).
- **Data acquisition systems:** Need high GBW and low settling time.
- **Battery-powered IoT devices:** Need low power consumption (LPV801, MCP6041).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Input Bias Current on Output Voltage

Consider a practical **non-inverting amplifier** with the input bias current $I_B$ flowing into the non-inverting terminal. The inverting terminal draws a current $I_B$ (approximately) through the resistor network $R_1$ and $R_f$.

**Step 1: Voltage at the inverting terminal due to $I_B$**

The Thevenin resistance seen from the inverting terminal to ground is the parallel combination of $R_1$ and $R_f$:

$$R_{th} = R_1 \Vert R_f = \frac{R_1 R_f}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 2: Voltage drop due to bias current**

$$V^- = I_B \cdot R_{th} = I_B \cdot \frac{R_1 R_f}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 3: Since the op-amp is in linear region, $V^+ = V^-$**

Assuming $V_{in}$ is grounded (worst-case for offset analysis), $V^+ = 0$, so $V^- = 0$. But because of the current flow, the output must produce the negative of this voltage scaled by the noise gain:

$$V_{out} = -I_B \cdot R_f + V^- \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 4: Substitute $V^-$ and simplify**

$$V_{out} = -I_B \cdot R_f + I_B \cdot \frac{R_1 R_f}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= -I_B R_f + I_B \cdot \frac{R_1 R_f}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} \\
&= -I_B R_f + I_B R_f \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Surprise result!** If we add a compensation resistor $R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f$ at the non-inverting terminal, the output offset due to $I_B$ is **cancelled**. This is the origin of the famous compensation resistor.

### 3.2 Derivation: Output Offset due to $I_{OS}$ in an Inverting Amplifier

**Step 1:** With $R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f$, the bias currents $I_{B1}$ and $I_{B2}$ produce equal voltage drops, so they cancel.

**Step 2:** But $I_{B1} \neq I_{B2}$, so a residual exists. The difference is $I_{OS}$:

$$I_{OS} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$

**Step 3:** This $I_{OS}$ flows through $R_f$ (because the path through $R_{comp}$ is balanced), producing:

$$V_{out,IOS} = I_{OS} \cdot R_f$$

> [!TIP]
> This is why $I_{OS}$ is the *more fundamental* parameter than $I_B$ for designers — it represents the *un-cancellable* part.

### 3.3 Derivation: Slew Rate Required for an Undistorted Sine Wave

**Step 1:** Sine wave output: $V_{out}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$

**Step 2:** Compute derivative:

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = 2\pi f V_p \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3:** Maximum rate occurs when $\cos(2\pi f t) = 1$:

$$\left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

**Step 4:** For undistorted output, op-amp slew rate must satisfy:

$$SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$$

**Step 5:** Solve for maximum undistorted frequency:

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
For µA741, $SR = 0.5$ V/µs = $0.5 \times 10^6$ V/s. If $V_p = 10$ V:

$$f_{max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10} = \frac{500000}{62.83} = 7957 \text{ Hz} \approx 7.96 \text{ kHz}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Frequency of Triangle/Square Output under Slew Rate Limit

When a square wave of amplitude $\pm V_p$ is applied, the output becomes a triangle wave (if the op-amp is slew-limited). The slope on each half-cycle is SR.

**Step 1:** One half-cycle: voltage changes from $-V_p$ to $+V_p$ → total change = $2V_p$.

**Step 2:** Time taken = (voltage change) / (rate of change):

$$\frac{T}{2} = \frac{2V_p}{SR} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{4V_p}{SR}$$

**Step 3:** Frequency:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{SR}{4V_p}$$

### 3.5 Derivation: Total Output Offset Voltage (Combined Effects)

For a non-inverting amplifier, the output offset has contributions from $V_{IO}$, $I_{OS}$, and thermal drift.

**Step 1: Output offset from input offset voltage:**

$$V_{out1} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{IO}$$

**Step 2: Output offset from $I_{OS}$ (unbalanced even with $R_{comp}$):**

$$V_{out2} = I_{OS} \cdot R_f$$

**Step 3: Thermal drift contribution at temperature $T$:**

$$V_{IO,drift}(T) = \left(\frac{\Delta V_{IO}}{\Delta T}\right) (T - 25°C)$$

$$V_{out3} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot \frac{\Delta V_{IO}}{\Delta T} (T - 25°C)$$

**Step 4: Total worst-case output offset:**

$$V_{out,total} = V_{out1} + V_{out2} + V_{out3}$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Op-Amp Based on Application

| Application | Critical Specification | Required Value | Suitable Op-Amp Family |
|-------------|------------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| Precision DC Instrumentation | Low $V_{IO}$, Low Drift | $V_{IO} < 100$ µV, Drift < 1 µV/°C | OP-07, OP-27, INA128 |
| High-Speed Pulse Processing | High SR, High GBW | $SR > 100$ V/µs, GBW > 50 MHz | LM318, AD8000, THS4631 |
| Audio Preamplifier | High CMRR, Low Noise | $CMRR > 80$ dB, $e_n < 10$ nV/√Hz | NE5532, OPA2134 |
| Battery Powered IoT | Low $P_D$ | $I_Q < 1$ µA | LPV801, MCP6041 |
| Integrator / Long Time Constant | Low $I_B$, Low $I_{OS}$ | $I_B < 50$ pA | LF355, AD549 |
| Comparator Replacement (Fast) | High SR | $SR > 1000$ V/µs | AD8561, TLV3501 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Op-Amp Specifications (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Op-Amp Specifications]:::root --> B[DC Specifications]:::branch
    A --> C[AC Specifications]:::branch
    A --> D[Absolute Maximum Ratings]:::branch
    A --> E[Thermal Specifications]:::branch

    B --> B1[Input Bias Current IB]
    B --> B2[Input Offset Current IOS]
    B --> B3[Input Offset Voltage VIO]
    B --> B4[PSRR]

    C --> C1[Slew Rate SR]
    C --> C2[Gain-Bandwidth Product GBW]
    C --> C3[Settling Time]
    C --> C4[CMRR]

    D --> D1[Max Supply Voltage]
    D --> D2[Max Differential Input]
    D --> D3[Max Common Mode Input]
    D --> D4[Operating Temperature Range]

    E --> E1[Thermal Drift of VIO]
    E --> E2[Thermal Drift of IOS]
    E --> E3[Junction Temperature]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Specifications Affect Real Circuits

```mermaid
graph LR
    INP[Input Signal Source]:::input --> LIMIT[Specification Limiter]:::core
    LIMIT --> IDEAL[Ideal Op-Amp Block]:::ideal
    IDEAL --> OUT[Output Stage]:::output

    LIMIT -.-> DC[DC Errors:
    VIO
    IB
    IOS
    PSRR]:::err
    LIMIT -.-> AC[AC Errors:
    Slew Rate
    GBW
    Settling Time
    CMRR]:::err
    LIMIT -.-> TH[Thermal Errors:
    Drift of VIO
    Drift of IOS]:::err

    DC -.-> IDEAL
    AC -.-> IDEAL
    TH -.-> IDEAL

    OUT --> LOAD[Load Circuit]:::load
    LOAD -. Feedback .-> INP

    classDef input fill:#10b981,stroke:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    classDef core fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    classDef ideal fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#ffffff
    classDef err fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    classDef load fill:#6b7280,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Slew Rate Limitation — Functional Block View

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Step or Sine Wave] --> B{Is dV/dt within SR Limit?}
    B -- YES --> C[Faithful Reproduction at Output]:::good
    B -- NO --> D[Slew Rate Limitation Active]:::bad
    D --> E[Output becomes Triangular Ramp]
    D --> F[Amplitude Compression in Sine]
    D --> G[Edge Rounding in Square]

    style C fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#fca5a5,color:#000000
    style F fill:#fca5a5,color:#000000
    style G fill:#fca5a5,color:#000000
```

### 4.4 Thermal Drift Behaviour Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Room Temp 25C]:::start --> M1[VIO at 25C]
    M1 --> SUM[Add Thermal Drift Term]:::sum
    T2[Operating Temp T]:::start --> DRIFT[Drift Coefficient x Delta T]:::drift
    DRIFT --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[VIO at T]:::result

    OUT --> OUTF[Amplified to Output:
    Vout offset equals
    1 plus Rf over R1
    times VIO at T]:::final

    classDef start fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    classDef sum fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000
    classDef drift fill:#f97316,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    classDef result fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#ffffff
    classDef final fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the following terms with respect to op-amp specifications:**
(a) Input bias current
(b) Input offset voltage
(c) Slew rate

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Input Bias Current ($I_B$):** The average of the DC currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting input terminals of the op-amp, with the output at zero. It arises because the input transistors require a base/gate bias current to operate. For µA741, $I_B$ is typically 80–500 nA. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$):** The small DC voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals to make the output voltage exactly zero. It is caused by the mismatch in the input differential pair. For µA741, $V_{IO}$ is typically 2–6 mV. **(1 mark)**

**(c) Slew Rate (SR):** It is the maximum rate of change of output voltage per unit time, expressed in V/µs, measured when a large step input is applied. It arises due to internal current source and compensation capacitor limits. For µA741, SR = 0.5 V/µs. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**What is CMRR? Mention the typical value of CMRR for µA741 op-amp. How is it related to op-amp performance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CMRR stands for **Common Mode Rejection Ratio**. It is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to the common-mode gain ($A_{cm}$) of the op-amp, expressed in dB. **(1.5 marks)**

$$CMRR = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

For µA741, the typical CMRR value is **90 dB**. **(0.5 mark)**

A higher CMRR indicates better rejection of common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum), thus better noise immunity and accuracy. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Define the following op-amp specifications and explain their significance:
(i) Input offset current
(ii) Slew rate
(iii) PSRR
(iv) Thermal drift

**(b) [7 marks]** For an inverting amplifier with $R_1 = 1$ kΩ and $R_f = 10$ kΩ, the op-amp has $V_{IO} = 5$ mV, $I_{B} = 200$ nA, and $I_{OS} = 30$ nA. Calculate:
(i) Output offset voltage due to $V_{IO}$.
(ii) Output offset voltage due to $I_{OS}$.
(iii) The required value of the bias compensation resistor.

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**(i) Input Offset Current ($I_{OS}$): [2 marks]**
It is the algebraic difference between the two input bias currents:
$$I_{OS} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$
**Significance:** It represents the un-cancellable part of bias current and produces a residual output offset voltage. Typical value for µA741 is 20–50 nA.

**(ii) Slew Rate (SR): [2 marks]**
It is the maximum rate of change of output voltage:
$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max} \quad \text{V/µs}$$
**Significance:** It limits the maximum frequency of undistorted large-signal output. For sine wave: $SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$.

**(iii) PSRR: [1.5 marks]**
Power Supply Rejection Ratio is the ability of the op-amp to reject variations in supply voltage:
$$PSRR_{dB} = 20\log\left(\frac{\Delta V_S}{\Delta V_{IO}}\right)$$
**Significance:** Indicates how immune the op-amp is to ripple and noise on power supply lines.

**(iv) Thermal Drift: [1.5 marks]**
The rate of change of input offset voltage (or bias/offset current) with temperature, measured in µV/°C or pA/°C.
**Significance:** Critical for precision applications over wide temperature ranges.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $R_1 = 1$ kΩ, $R_f = 10$ kΩ, $V_{IO} = 5$ mV, $I_{OS} = 30$ nA.

**(i) Output offset due to $V_{IO}$: [2 marks]**

For an inverting amplifier, the noise gain (1 + R_f/R_1) also amplifies $V_{IO}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out,IO} &= \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_{IO} \\
&= \left(1 + \frac{10k}{1k}\right) \cdot 5\text{ mV} \\
&= 11 \times 5\text{ mV} \\
&= 55\text{ mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Output offset due to $I_{OS}$: [2 marks]**

$$V_{out,IOS} = I_{OS} \times R_f = 30 \text{ nA} \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$V_{out,IOS} = 30 \times 10^{-9} \times 10 \times 10^3 = 300 \text{ µV} = 0.3 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Compensation resistor: [1 mark]**

$$R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f = \frac{R_1 \times R_f}{R_1 + R_f} = \frac{1 \times 10}{1 + 10} = 0.909 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 909 \text{ }\Omega$$

**(iv) Total output offset (optional – for bonus understanding): [2 marks]**

$$V_{out,total} = V_{out,IO} + V_{out,IOS} = 55 + 0.3 = 55.3 \text{ mV}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the slew rate limitation of an op-amp. Derive the condition for an undistorted sinusoidal output of peak amplitude $V_p$ at frequency $f$. A certain op-amp has SR = 0.5 V/µs. Determine the maximum frequency at which it can deliver an output of 10 V peak amplitude.

**(b) [7 marks]** Define and explain the following with units:
(i) Gain-Bandwidth Product
(ii) Common Mode Rejection Ratio
(iii) Open-loop voltage gain
(iv) Input resistance

A non-inverting amplifier has gain = 50. The op-amp has open-loop gain = $2 \times 10^5$ and unity-gain bandwidth = 1 MHz. Find the closed-loop bandwidth.

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**(a) [7 marks]**

Slew rate is the maximum rate of change of output voltage with time. It is caused by the limited current available to charge/discharge the internal compensation capacitor. **[2 marks]**

**Diagram (text-based):** Input: large amplitude step / high-frequency sine → Op-amp internal current limit → Output: triangular ramp (if step) or amplitude-clipped sine. **[1 mark]**

**Derivation [3 marks]:**

Let $V_{out}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} &= 2\pi f V_p \cos(2\pi f t) \\
\left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{max} &= 2\pi f V_p
\end{aligned}$$

For undistorted output, the op-amp slew rate must satisfy:

$$SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$$

**Numerical: [1 mark]**

Given $SR = 0.5$ V/µs $= 0.5 \times 10^6$ V/s, $V_p = 10$ V.

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10} = 7957.7 \text{ Hz} \approx 7.96 \text{ kHz}$$

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW): [2 marks]**
The product of closed-loop gain and the corresponding -3 dB bandwidth. It is constant for a given op-amp and equals the unity-gain frequency.
$$GBW = A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB}$$
**Unit:** Hz (or MHz).

**(ii) Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR): [2 marks]**
Ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain.
$$CMRR = 20\log(A_d / A_{cm}) \text{ dB}$$
**Unit:** dB. Higher is better.

**(iii) Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$): [1.5 marks]**
The differential voltage gain of the op-amp with no external feedback.
$$A_{OL} = V_{out} / V_{id}$$
**Unit:** V/V (or dB). For µA741: ~200,000 (106 dB).

**(iv) Input Resistance: [1.5 marks]**
The resistance seen between the differential input terminals. For µA741 (BJT input) ~ 2 MΩ. For FET input op-amps, it can exceed $10^{12}$ Ω.
**Unit:** Ω.

**Closed-loop bandwidth calculation: [1 mark]** (often part of the 7 marks)

Given: $A_{CL} = 50$, Unity-gain BW = 1 MHz.
Since $GBW = A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB} = 1$ MHz:
$$f_{-3dB} = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}} = \frac{1\text{ MHz}}{50} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_B$ and $I_{OS}$:** Many students swap the two. Remember: $I_B$ is the *average* (cancelable), $I_{OS}$ is the *difference* (uncancelable).
> 2. **Forgetting the noise gain factor:** In an inverting amplifier, $V_{IO}$ is multiplied by $(1 + R_f/R_1)$, NOT by $R_f/R_1$. KTU examiners check this carefully.
> 3. **Slew rate units:** $V/\mu s$ must be converted to $V/s$ when plugging into $f = SR/(2\pi V_p)$ formula. Many students skip this conversion and lose a mark.
> 4. **CMRR vs PSRR:** Both are in dB, both are ratios, both reject a *type* of noise. The mnemonic: "**C**MRR → **C**ommon-mode noise; PSRR → **P**ower **S**upply noise."
> 5. **GBW is constant:** Even if you change feedback, GBW does not change. If gain increases, bandwidth decreases proportionally.
> 6. **Thermal drift unit:** µV/°C, not mV/°C. Precision op-amps have much smaller drift than general-purpose.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Specifications** = quantitative deviations of a real op-amp from the ideal.
- [x] **Three categories:** DC, AC, and Absolute Maximum Ratings.
- [x] **Input Bias Current ($I_B$):** Average of the two input currents. (nA for BJT, pA for FET input.)
- [x] **Input Offset Current ($I_{OS}$):** Difference; produces uncancellable offset at output: $V_{out} = I_{OS} \cdot R_f$.
- [x] **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$):** Mismatch-induced DC voltage; amplified by noise gain to output.
- [x] **Bias Compensation Resistor:** $R_{comp} = R_1 \Vert R_f$, placed in series with the non-inverting input.
- [x] **Thermal Drift:** µV/°C; critical for precision DC circuits.
- [x] **Slew Rate (SR):** V/µs; limits maximum rate of output change. Sine wave: $SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$.
- [x] **Full-Power Bandwidth:** $f_{FPBW} = SR / (2\pi V_{p,max})$.
- [x] **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW):** $A_{CL} \cdot f_{-3dB}$ = constant.
- [x] **CMRR:** Rejects common-mode noise; typical 90 dB for µA741.
- [x] **PSRR:** Rejects supply-rail noise; typical 90 dB for µA741.
- [x] **Open-Loop Gain ($A_{OL}$):** Very high (~ $10^5$ for µA741); frequency-dependent.
- [x] **Total Output Offset:** Sum of contributions from $V_{IO}$, $I_{OS}$, and thermal drift.
- [x] **Slew-rate-limited triangle wave frequency:** $f = SR / (4V_p)$.
- [x] **Selecting an op-amp:** Always match the dominant specification to the application (low $V_{IO}$ for precision, high SR for speed, low $I_Q$ for battery).
- [x] **µA741 reference values:** $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, $SR = 0.5$ V/µs, GBW = 1 MHz, $CMRR = 90$ dB, $V_{IO} = 2$ mV, $I_B = 80$ nA.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weighted Resistor Type and R-2R Ladder Type, Analog to Digital Converters: Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Weighted Resistor Type and R-2R Ladder Type, Analog to Digital Converters: Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Digital-to-Analog Converters (DACs) — A Foundational Refresher

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a mixed-signal integrated circuit that converts a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code (typically a binary word) into a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog voltage or current proportional to the digital input.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, two architectures dominate the study of monolithic DACs in this module:
> **1. Weighted Resistor DAC** (Binary-Weighted architecture)
> **2. R-2R Ladder DAC** (R-2R Ladder network architecture)

Both architectures belong to the broad family of **summing-amplifier-based current-steering DACs** because they exploit the linear superposition principle of operational amplifier inverting summing configurations.

---

## 1.2 Weighted Resistor DAC — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):**
> A *Weighted Resistor DAC* is a parallel-binary current-summing network in which the input binary bits $b_0, b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_{N-1}$ (where $b_0$ is the **MSB** and $b_{N-1}$ is the **LSB**) are connected through a set of precision resistors whose values follow a strict binary progression — $R, 2R, 4R, 8R, \ldots, 2^{N-1}R$ — to the virtual-ground summing node of an inverting op-amp.

The op-amp, configured with a feedback resistor $R_f$, sums currents produced by those input branches whose corresponding bit is logic-1 (i.e., the branch is switched to the reference voltage $V_{REF}$).

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Volume Knob Family":**

Imagine you are in a sound-mixing studio. The studio engineer has **N** sliders on a console. The first slider controls a giant subwoofer (very large influence on output sound), the second slider controls a slightly smaller woofer, the third a mid-range speaker, and so on, with each successive slider controlling progressively smaller speakers. The "weight" of each slider on the final output sound is *not* equal — it follows a binary weighting (twice as powerful as the next). The "MSB slider" has the largest speaker; the "LSB slider" has the tiniest tweeter. By pushing up a specific combination of sliders (your *digital word*), you create an output sound (your *analog voltage*) whose loudness is the weighted sum of all speakers. The **Weighted Resistor DAC** does exactly this with *electrical currents instead of sound*.

---

## 1.3 R-2R Ladder DAC — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):**
> An *R-2R Ladder DAC* is a current-divider network that uses only **two resistor values — $R$ and $2R$** — arranged in a cascaded, ladder-like topology. Each rung of the ladder terminates at a digitally controlled SPDT switch that steers the node current either into a virtual-ground summing junction (for "1") or to ground (for "0"). The Thévenin-equivalent resistance seen at every node is exactly $R$, which makes the structure inherently self-scaling and free of the wide-resistor-ratio problem that plagues the weighted-resistor design.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Tapering Waterfall":**

Picture a long, stepped waterfall with $N$ steps. The water flowing over the *first* (top) step is enormous; at every subsequent step, the water is exactly *halved*. After $N$ steps, the residual water at the bottom is incredibly tiny. Now, place a *valve* at each step. If the valve is open (logic-1), that step's water flows into the collection bucket (the summing amplifier); if closed (logic-0), that water spills harmlessly to the ground. The bucket's total water level is a *binary-weighted sum* — even though every "step component" is built from the *same two sizes* of bricks ($R$ and $2R$). This manufacturing elegance is precisely why the R-2R ladder is the *industry standard* in IC fabrication.

---

## 1.4 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) — Specifications Overview

An **ADC** performs the inverse operation: it samples a continuous-time analog signal and produces a discrete binary code. KTU examiners test the *static and dynamic performance metrics* that quantify an ADC's fidelity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core ADC Specifications (Exam-Relevant):**
> - **Resolution** (number of bits $N$)
> - **Step Size / LSB weight** ($\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1}$)
> - **Quantization Error** ($\pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$)
> - **Accuracy** vs **Precision** distinction
> - **Conversion Time** ($T_c$)
> - **Dynamic Range** ($\text{dB}$)
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** and **SNR + Distortion (SINAD)**
> - **Aperture Time** and **Aperture Jitter**
> - **Integral Non-Linearity (INL)** and **Differential Non-Linearity (DNL)**
> - **Power Dissipation**

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Digital Thermometer":**

A digital thermometer converting room temperature (analog) into a digital reading is an ADC. Its **resolution** is how fine the decimal display is (0.1 °C vs 1 °C). Its **accuracy** is whether the reading is *correct* (calibrated against a standard). Its **conversion time** is how long it takes to display a stable value. Its **SNR** is the readability of the digits in a noisy environment. All these together form its **specification sheet**.

---

## 1.5 Resolution & Step Size — The Backbone Metric

If an ADC has $N$ bits and full-scale input range $V_{FS} = V_{REF}$ (or $V_{REF}^+ - V_{REF}^-$), then:

$$\text{Number of discrete output codes} = 2^N$$

The **resolution** is the smallest analog change that can be distinguished, called the **step size** or **LSB voltage**:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} \quad \text{(unipolar, including zero code)}$$

or equivalently,

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1} \quad \text{(unipolar, full-scale = max code)}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** A 1-bit ADC is a light switch — it can only say "dark" or "bright" (two states). An 8-bit ADC is a dimmer with 256 brightness levels. A 16-bit ADC is a 65,536-step precision dimmer.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher bits ⇒ Finer resolution ⇒ Smaller step size ⇒ Larger circuit complexity (resistor count, switch count, comparator count).**
> This trade-off is the central engineering question KTU examiners love to ask.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Weighted Resistor DAC — Operational Principle (Step-by-Step)

The circuit consists of:
1. A reference voltage source $V_{REF}$ (stable, low-impedance).
2. $N$ single-pole double-throw (SPDT) analog switches, one per bit.
3. $N$ binary-weighted precision resistors connected from each switch output to the **virtual ground** (summing node) of the op-amp.
4. An inverting op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f$.

### Logic of Operation
- When bit $b_i = 1$ (logic HIGH), the switch connects its resistor to $V_{REF}$.
- When bit $b_i = 0$ (logic LOW), the switch connects its resistor to **ground** (0 V).
- Because the op-amp's inverting input is at **virtual ground**, the current through the resistor for bit $b_i$ is:

$$I_i = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{REF}}{R_i}$$

where $R_i$ is the binary-weighted resistor.

### Why "Binary-Weighted" Resistor?
The MSB must contribute **twice** the current of the next bit, **four times** that of the third bit, and so on. By Ohm's law ($I = V/R$), to halve the current, you must **double** the resistance. Thus:

$$R_{\text{MSB}} : R_{\text{MSB-1}} : \cdots : R_{\text{LSB}} = R : 2R : 4R : \cdots : 2^{N-1}R$$

The MSB resistor is $R$ (smallest), LSB is $2^{N-1}R$ (largest).

### Current Summation at Virtual Ground
By **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the inverting node (which is at 0 V because of virtual short):

$$I_f = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} I_i = V_{REF} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i R}$$

where $i = 0$ corresponds to the MSB (resistor $R$) and $i = N-1$ corresponds to the LSB (resistor $2^{N-1}R$).

### Output Voltage
Since $I_f$ flows through $R_f$ and the non-inverting terminal is grounded, the output is:

$$V_o = -I_f R_f = -\frac{R_f}{R} V_{REF} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If we choose the standard design condition $R_f = R$, then:

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

The output is **inverted** and **proportional to the binary-weighted sum** of the input bits.

### Practical Drawback
For $N = 10$ bits, the resistor spread is $2^{9} = 512$. The MSB resistor must be $\approx 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ while the LSB must be $\approx 512\,\text{k}\Omega$. Fabricating such a wide range with matched temperature coefficients is **extremely difficult in IC technology** → motivates the R-2R design.

---

## 2.2 R-2R Ladder DAC — Operational Principle (Step-by-Step)

The R-2R ladder uses only two resistor values: $R$ and $2R$, arranged in a repeating pattern.

### Circuit Topology
- A **vertical (rung) resistor** of value $2R$ exists at each bit node, connected to an SPDT switch.
- A **horizontal (series) resistor** of value $R$ exists between adjacent bit nodes.
- The leftmost (MSB) end of the ladder terminates in a resistor $2R$ to ground.
- The rightmost end of the ladder terminates with a $2R$ resistor to ground.

### The "Equivalent Resistance Theorem"
The key insight is that **looking into any node of the ladder from the right**, the equivalent Thévenin resistance is exactly $R$ (because the series $R$ adds to the parallel combination of $2R \mid 2R = R$, then $R + R = 2R$, then $2R \mid 2R = R$, etc.). This makes the ladder self-similar and easy to analyze.

### Current Division at Each Node
When the MSB switch is at $V_{REF}$ and all others at ground, the current entering the MSB node is $I_{\text{tot}} = V_{REF} / (2R)$ (because the MSB node sees $2R$ to $V_{REF}$ and $2R$ to ground in parallel = $R$, and this $R$ is in series with another $R$ → total $2R$).

This current splits equally at every node, so:
- At the MSB node: $I_{\text{MSB}} = V_{REF} / (2R)$.
- At the next node: $I_{\text{MSB-1}} = V_{REF} / (4R)$.
- At the next: $V_{REF} / (8R)$.
- ...
- At the LSB: $V_{REF} / (2^N \cdot R)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each successive bit contributes *exactly half* the current of the previous bit** — the binary weighting emerges *automatically* from the ladder geometry, even though only two resistor values are used.

### Final Output Voltage
With op-amp feedback resistor $R_f$ and the ladder's Thevenin output taken at the summing node:

$$V_o = -R_f \cdot \frac{V_{REF}}{2R} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

If $R_f = R$:

$$V_o = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

---

## 2.3 Comparison Table: Weighted Resistor vs R-2R Ladder

| Property | Weighted Resistor DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Resistor values required** | $N$ distinct values ($R$ to $2^{N-1}R$) | Only 2 values ($R$, $2R$) |
| **Resistor spread ratio** | $2^{N-1} : 1$ (very large) | $2 : 1$ (constant) |
| **IC fabrication difficulty** | High for $N \ge 8$ | Easy and standard |
| **Accuracy at high $N$** | Poor (resistor mismatch) | Excellent |
| **Switch requirements** | One SPDT per bit | One SPDT per bit |
| **Speed / Settling time** | Slower (parasitic at large $R$) | Faster (smaller parasitic) |
| **Output type** | Current summing | Current dividing + summing |
| **KTU exam weight** | Theoretical + derivation | Numerical + design |
| **Typical application** | Low-bit precision DACs (4–6 bit) | High-resolution audio, video DACs |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — DAC Architectures

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below assume $b_0$ is the MSB and $b_{N-1}$ is the LSB. $V_o$ is the analog output, $V_{REF}$ is the reference voltage.

| # | Quantity | Weighted Resistor DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MSB resistor | $R$ | $2R$ (vertical) |
| 2 | LSB resistor | $2^{N-1}R$ | $2R$ (vertical) |
| 3 | Current from bit $b_i$ | $\dfrac{b_i V_{REF}}{2^i R}$ | $\dfrac{b_i V_{REF}}{2 \cdot 2^i R}$ |
| 4 | Output voltage ($R_f = R$) | $-V_{REF} \sum \dfrac{b_i}{2^i}$ | $-\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2} \sum \dfrac{b_i}{2^i}$ |
| 5 | Full-scale output (all 1s) | $-V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{2^N - 1}{2^{N-1}}$ | $-\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2^N - 1}{2^{N-1}}$ |
| 6 | Step size $\Delta$ | $\dfrac{\vert V_{FS} \vert}{2^N - 1}$ | $\dfrac{\vert V_{FS} \vert}{2^N - 1}$ |
| 7 | % Resolution | $\dfrac{1}{2^N - 1} \times 100$ | $\dfrac{1}{2^N - 1} \times 100$ |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — ADC Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part-A and Part-B question on Module 4.**

| # | Specification | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of discrete codes | $2^N$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Step size (LSB weight) | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Full-scale range (unipolar) | $V_{FS} = (2^N - 1) \cdot \Delta$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Quantization error | $\pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Resolution (% of FS) | $\dfrac{1}{2^N} \times 100\,\%$ | percentage |
| 6 | Dynamic range | $20 \log_{10}(2^N) = 6.02\,N \text{ dB}$ | decibels (dB) |
| 7 | Theoretical SNR (sinusoidal input) | $\text{SNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ | dB |
| 8 | Effective number of bits (from SNR) | $N_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\text{SNR} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits |
| 9 | Conversion time (single-slope, dual-slope) | $T_c = 2^N \cdot T_{\text{clock}}$ | seconds |
| 10 | Aperture jitter error (RMS) | $\Delta V_{\text{jitter}} = 2\pi f_{in} V_{FS} \cdot t_j$ | Volts |
| 11 | Maximum sample rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 12 | INL / DNL | expressed in LSB units | LSB |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test **SNR $= 6.02N + 1.76$ dB** and **Dynamic Range $= 6.02N$ dB** — note the **+1.76 dB offset** in SNR comes from the RMS-to-peak conversion of a sine wave. **Do not confuse them.**

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Area | DAC Architecture | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Audio CD players (16-bit) | R-2R Ladder / Segmented | High resolution, monotonicity, low distortion |
| Function generators (low-cost) | Weighted Resistor | Simple, low N |
| Microcontroller PWM-alternative | R-2R Ladder | Compact, easy to interface to GPIO |
| Video DACs in graphics cards | Segmented / Current-Steering (advanced) | High speed (>100 MHz), low glitch |
| Industrial process control (4–20 mA) | R-2R + V-to-I converter | Accurate, linear, low drift |
| Digital attenuators in RF | R-2R | Wide range, fast settling |

**Real-World Utility:** Every smartphone, every audio interface, every digital oscilloscope, and every SDR (software-defined radio) board uses a DAC. The choice between weighted-resistor and R-2R is dictated by **resolution, IC fabrication cost, and linearity** trade-offs. For resolutions $\ge 8$ bits, R-2R is essentially always chosen.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of an $N$-Bit Weighted Resistor DAC

### Circuit Setup
- $N$ binary-weighted resistors: $R_0 = R$ (MSB), $R_1 = 2R$, $R_2 = 4R$, ..., $R_{N-1} = 2^{N-1}R$ (LSB).
- Each resistor $R_i$ is connected via SPDT switch to either $V_{REF}$ (if $b_i = 1$) or ground (if $b_i = 0$).
- The common node of all resistors is the **inverting input** of an op-amp.
- Non-inverting input is grounded. Feedback resistor is $R_f$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Current from Each Branch
Since the inverting input is a **virtual ground** (held at 0 V by the op-amp feedback), the voltage across each resistor $R_i$ is exactly $b_i \cdot V_{REF}$ (where $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$). By Ohm's law:

$$I_i = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{REF}}{R_i} = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{REF}}{2^i R}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the Virtual-Ground Node
The algebraic sum of all currents entering the node must equal the current leaving through $R_f$:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} I_i = I_f$$

$$\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i \cdot V_{REF}}{2^i R} = \frac{-V_o}{R_f}$$

*Conversion Logic:* The negative sign appears because the conventional current direction for the feedback current is from the output node back toward the summing junction; the op-amp forces $V_o$ to be negative to sink the input currents through the virtual ground.

### Step 3 — Solve for $V_o$
Multiply both sides by $R_f$:

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f \cdot V_{REF}}{R} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Design Convention $R_f = R$
This is the **standard KTU-exam assumption**. With $R_f = R$:

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

### Step 5 — Full-Scale Output (All Bits = 1)
Set $b_i = 1$ for all $i$:

$$V_{o,\text{FS}} = -V_{REF} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{1}{2^i} = -V_{REF} \cdot \frac{1 - (1/2)^N}{1 - 1/2} = -V_{REF} \cdot (2 - 2^{1-N})$$

Using the geometric-series identity $\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} x^i = \frac{1 - x^N}{1 - x}$ with $x = 1/2$:

$$V_{o,\text{FS}} = -V_{REF} \cdot \frac{1 - 1/2^N}{1/2} = -V_{REF} \left( 2 - \frac{1}{2^{N-1}} \right)$$

For large $N$, $V_{o,\text{FS}} \approx -2 V_{REF}$ (the LSB-term is negligible). For $N = 4$, $V_{o,\text{FS}} = -V_{REF} \cdot (2 - 1/8) = -1.875\,V_{REF}$.

### Step 6 — Step Size (Resolution)
The change in $V_o$ for a 1-LSB increment in the digital word (e.g., $0111 \to 1000$):

$$\Delta V_o = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{1}{2^{N-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the discrepancy: the smallest *bit-weighted* increment is $V_{REF}/2^{N-1}$ because the MSB weight is $V_{REF}$ (not $V_{REF}/2$). The textbook *step size* in terms of full-scale fraction is:
> $$\Delta = \frac{V_{o,\text{FS}}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{V_{REF}(2 - 2^{1-N})}{2^N - 1} \approx \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{N-1}} \quad \text{for large } N$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Output Voltage of an $N$-Bit R-2R Ladder DAC

### Circuit Setup
- A series of $N$ nodes. Between adjacent nodes, a horizontal resistor of value $R$.
- At each node, a vertical resistor of value $2R$ connects to an SPDT switch.
- The rightmost node terminates with $2R$ to ground.
- The leftmost (MSB) node is fed from $V_{REF}$ via $2R$ (or directly through a switch that connects to $V_{REF}$ / ground).

### Step 1 — Establish the Equivalent Resistance at Each Node
Look into node $k$ from the *right*. The rightward ladder beyond node $k$ consists of $R$ in series with the parallel combination of the terminating $2R$ and the next-stage equivalent. By induction, this equivalent is always $R$. So at every node:
- The resistor looking right = $R$
- The vertical $2R$ to the switch

At the switch, when in position $V_{REF}$: the $2R$ vertical resistor sees $V_{REF}$ at the top, and the equivalent $R$ from the right. These two are in series (with node 0V at the junction by virtual ground? No — the summing node is the *only* virtual-ground node; intermediate nodes are not virtual ground in R-2R).

Actually, the R-2R analysis uses a slightly different framework: each ladder bit, when selected, contributes a current that is **halved** at the next node.

### Step 2 — Current at the MSB Node
With all other switches at ground, the MSB node has $2R$ to $V_{REF}$, and the rest of the ladder (looking right) is equivalent to $R$. The total resistance from $V_{REF}$ to ground through this branch is $2R + R = 3R$? No — the right equivalent $R$ is in *parallel* with the next vertical $2R$ which is grounded, so they form $R \mid 2R = 2R/3$. Hmm — this is the standard analysis that yields the result.

Let me redo this cleanly using the canonical approach: **Thevenin Equivalent at Each Node.**

Consider the network to the right of node $i$ (looking right). Call its Thevenin equivalent $V_{th,i}$ and $R_{th,i}$.

**Base case (rightmost):** The rightmost branch is a $2R$ to ground. So $R_{th,N-1} = 2R$, $V_{th,N-1} = 0$.

**Induction step:** At node $i$, we have a series $R$ in series with $(R_{th,i+1} \mid 2R_{\text{vert at }i+1})$. If $R_{th,i+1} = R$ (base case gives this, and we show it inductively), then $(R \mid 2R) = 2R/3$. Add the series $R$: $R_{th,i} = R + 2R/3 = 5R/3$.

Wait — this doesn't give $R$. Let me re-examine the topology.

The correct canonical R-2R ladder analysis assumes the ladder is connected to an op-amp virtual ground at the *output* node (the right end), not that each bit is independently switched. There are two equivalent configurations:

**Configuration A (most common in textbooks):** The output of the ladder is connected directly to the op-amp inverting input (virtual ground). Each bit switch connects its vertical $2R$ to either $V_{REF}$ (for 1) or to ground (for 0). The *leftmost* end of the ladder is terminated in $2R$ to ground.

In this configuration, the Thevenin equivalent of the right end (toward virtual ground) is $R$ (inductively), and the analysis proceeds as follows:

### Step 1' — Equivalent Resistance
By the standard ladder-reduction, looking from *any* node into the ladder toward the op-amp virtual ground, the equivalent resistance is exactly $R$. (Proof by induction: at the rightmost node, $2R$ is in parallel with the virtual ground's zero — but actually the rightmost is just $2R$ to the virtual ground, so $R_{eq} = 2R$ from that node? The standard result depends on the exact connection.)

**Let me use the universally accepted result:**
> The current contributed by bit $i$ (with $i = 0$ = MSB) at the op-amp summing node is:
> $$I_i = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{REF}}{2R} \cdot \frac{1}{2^i}$$

This result is derived by recognizing that the ladder performs *binary current division* — each rung divides the available current by 2.

### Step 3 — Total Current at Summing Node
Summing all bit currents:

$$I_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} I_i = \frac{V_{REF}}{2R} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

### Step 4 — Output Voltage
With feedback resistor $R_f$ and inverting amplifier:

$$V_o = -I_{\text{total}} \cdot R_f = -\frac{R_f \cdot V_{REF}}{2R} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

If $R_f = R$:

$$V_o = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compare this with the weighted-resistor result $V_o = -V_{REF} \sum b_i/2^i$. The R-2R output is **half** of the weighted-resistor output for the same digital input. This is because in R-2R, the MSB has resistance $2R$ (not $R$), so the MSB current is half.

### Step 5 — Full-Scale Output
For all bits = 1:

$$V_{o,\text{FS}} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2} \cdot \left(2 - \frac{1}{2^{N-1}}\right) = -V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^N}\right) = -\frac{V_{REF}(2^N - 1)}{2^N}$$

For large $N$, $V_{o,\text{FS}} \to -V_{REF}$.

### Step 6 — Step Size
The change in $V_o$ per 1-LSB increment:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{o,\text{FS}}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$$

(after simplification, since $(2^N-1)/2^N / (2^N-1) = 1/2^N$).

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical textbook step size $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$, which is **the same as the ADC step size** — a key unifying concept. *A DAC and an ideal ADC of the same $N$ bits have the same step size*, because they share the same quantization grid.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Weighted Resistor DAC

**Problem [KTU Pattern]:** A 4-bit weighted resistor DAC uses $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $V_{REF} = 5\,\text{V}$. The op-amp feedback resistor is $R_f = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Find the analog output for the input word $b_0 b_1 b_2 b_3 = 1010$ (MSB to LSB).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify resistor values: $R_0 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 40\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 80\,\text{k}\Omega$.

Step 2 — Identify active bits: $b_0 = 1$, $b_1 = 0$, $b_2 = 1$, $b_3 = 0$.

Step 3 — Apply the formula:

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f V_{REF}}{R} \sum_{i=0}^{3} \frac{b_i}{2^i} = -\frac{(10)(5)}{10} \left( \frac{1}{1} + \frac{0}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{0}{8} \right)$$

Step 4 — Compute the inner sum:

$$\sum = 1 + 0 + 0.25 + 0 = 1.25$$

Step 5 — Final result:

$$V_o = -5 \times 1.25 = -6.25 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Trap:** KTU examiners specifically watch for the *sign* of the output. A weighted-resistor DAC with an inverting op-amp configuration *always produces a negative output for a positive $V_{REF}$*. If the question says "find the output voltage" and you give +6.25 V, you **lose 1 mark**.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — R-2R Ladder DAC

**Problem [KTU Pattern]:** A 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC has $R = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $2R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{REF} = 10\,\text{V}$, and $R_f = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$. Compute the output for digital input $b_0 b_1 b_2 b_3 = 1101$ (MSB to LSB).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the R-2R output formula:

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f \cdot V_{REF}}{2R} \sum_{i=0}^{3} \frac{b_i}{2^i} = -\frac{(5)(10)}{2(5)} \left( \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{0}{4} + \frac{1}{8} \right)$$

Step 2 — Simplify the coefficient:

$$-\frac{50}{10} = -5$$

Step 3 — Compute the binary-weighted sum:

$$\sum = 1 + 0.5 + 0 + 0.125 = 1.625$$

Step 4 — Final result:

$$V_o = -5 \times 1.625 = -8.125 \text{ V}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — ADC Specifications Derivation Walk-Throughs

### 3.5.1 Quantization Error Derivation
A real ADC maps a continuous input $V_{\text{in}}$ to the *nearest* quantization level $V_q$. The **quantization error** is:

$$e_q = V_{\text{in}} - V_q$$

Since $V_q$ lies in the half-open interval $\left[ V_q - \Delta/2, \, V_q + \Delta/2 \right)$, the error lies in:

$$e_q \in \left[ -\frac{\Delta}{2}, \, +\frac{\Delta}{2} \right)$$

Hence the **peak quantization error** is $\Delta/2$ and the **RMS quantization error** is:

$$e_{q,\text{rms}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

### 3.5.2 SNR Derivation for an Ideal $N$-bit ADC
Assume a **full-scale sinusoidal** input of amplitude $V_{FS}/2$. The signal power is:

$$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

The quantization-noise power (treating it as white noise uniformly distributed over the Nyquist band) is:

$$P_{\text{noise}} = \frac{e_{q,\text{rms}}^2}{1} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}$$

The signal-to-noise ratio:

$$\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} \right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{V_{FS}^2 / (12 \cdot 2^{2N})} \right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}{8} \right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left( 1.5 \cdot 2^{2N} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 N \log_{10}(2)$$

Using $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ and $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$:

$$\boxed{\text{SNR (dB)} = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$

### 3.5.3 Dynamic Range Derivation
Dynamic Range is the ratio of the largest representable signal to the smallest (1 LSB), expressed in dB:

$$\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{V_{FS}}{V_{LSB}} \right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{V_{FS}}{V_{FS}/2^N} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(2^N) = 20 N \log_{10}(2)$$

$$\boxed{\text{DR (dB)} = 6.02\,N \text{ dB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The 1.76 dB difference between SNR and DR** comes from the fact that DR compares peak values (V/V) while SNR compares RMS signal power to RMS noise power, plus the 1.5-factor from quantization noise bandwidth vs signal power.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example — ADC Resolution & SNR

**Problem:** A 12-bit ADC has a unipolar input range of $0$ to $10\,\text{V}$. Calculate (a) the resolution in volts, (b) the percentage resolution, (c) the dynamic range in dB, and (d) the theoretical SNR in dB.

**Solution:**

**(a) Step size:**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.441 \text{ mV}$$

**(b) Percentage resolution:**

$$\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^N} \times 100 = \frac{1}{4096} \times 100 \approx 0.0244\,\%$$

**(c) Dynamic Range:**

$$\text{DR} = 6.02 \times 12 = 72.24 \text{ dB}$$

**(d) Theoretical SNR:**

$$\text{SNR} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 73.00 \text{ dB}$$

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Weighted Resistor DAC — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DIGITAL INPUT b0 MSB b1 b2 ... bN1 LSB"] --> B["SPDT SWITCH ARRAY"]
    B --> C["BINARY WEIGHTED RESISTOR NETWORK R 2R 4R ... 2N1R"]
    C --> D["SUMMING JUNCTION VIRTUAL GROUND"]
    D --> E["OP AMP INVERTING SUMMER Rf"]
    E --> F["ANALOG OUTPUT Vo NEGATIVE POLARITY"]
    V1["Vref STABLE REFERENCE"] --> B
    G["ANALOG GROUND"] --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-by-block reading:**
> - `DIGITAL INPUT`: $N$-bit binary word.
> - `SPDT SWITCH ARRAY`: One switch per bit, steers branch to $V_{REF}$ or GND.
> - `BINARY WEIGHTED RESISTOR NETWORK`: Each branch resistance is doubled for each successive lower bit.
> - `SUMMING JUNCTION`: Virtual ground (0 V) at op-amp inverting input.
> - `OP AMP INVERTING SUMMER`: Performs $I \to V$ conversion with gain $-R_f/R$.
> - `Vref STABLE REFERENCE`: Reference voltage (e.g., 5 V, 10 V, 2.5 V bandgap).

---

## 4.2 R-2R Ladder DAC — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DIGITAL INPUT b0 MSB b1 b2 ... bN1 LSB"] --> B["SPDT SWITCH ARRAY AT EACH LADDER RUNG"]
    B --> C["R2R LADDER NETWORK ONLY TWO RESISTOR VALUES R AND 2R"]
    C --> D["THEVENIN EQUIVALENT OUTPUT Vo_series = R AT EVERY NODE"]
    D --> E["OP AMP SUMMER WITH FEEDBACK RESISTOR Rf"]
    E --> F["ANALOG OUTPUT Vo NEGATIVE POLARITY"]
    V1["Vref STABLE REFERENCE"] --> B
    G1["GROUND TERMINATION 2R ON LEFT END"] --> C
    G2["GROUND TERMINATION 2R ON RIGHT END"] --> C
    G3["ANALOG GROUND"] --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key visual takeaways:**
> 1. Only **two resistor values** are present in the entire network.
> 2. The terminations (left and right) use $2R$ to ground.
> 3. Every node's Thevenin equivalent *looking into the ladder* is exactly $R$ — the self-similar property.
> 4. The output magnitude is **half** that of a weighted-resistor DAC for the same digital input.

---

## 4.3 ADC Specification Map — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["ANALOG INPUT Vin CONTINUOUS TIME CONTINUOUS AMPLITUDE"] --> B["SAMPLE AND HOLD S/H"]
    B --> C["QUANTIZER N BITS"]
    C --> D["ENCODER BINARY GRAY OR 2S COMPLEMENT"]
    D --> E["DIGITAL OUTPUT CODE"]
    F["REFERENCE VOLTAGE Vref"] --> C
    G["CLOCK"] --> B
    G --> C
    H["SPECIFICATION METRICS"] --> I["STATIC METRICS"]
    H --> J["DYNAMIC METRICS"]
    I --> I1["RESOLUTION N BITS"]
    I --> I2["QUANTIZATION ERROR DELTA OVER 2"]
    I --> I3["INL DNL IN LSB UNITS"]
    I --> I4["OFFSET GAIN ERROR"]
    J --> J1["SNR 6.02N PLUS 1.76 dB"]
    J --> J2["SINAD"]
    J --> J3["ENOB"]
    J --> J4["APERTURE TIME AND JITTER"]
    J --> J5["CONVERSION TIME TC"]
    J --> J6["DYNAMIC RANGE 6.02N dB"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> The top half shows the *signal path* (analog in → sample → quantize → encode → digital out).
> The bottom half shows the *two families of specifications* — **static** (DC-style measurements) and **dynamic** (time/frequency-domain measurements). KTU examiners often ask students to *classify* a given specification into static or dynamic.

---

## 4.4 Quantitative Trade-off Map — Resolution vs Speed vs Power

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HIGHER RESOLUTION N INCREASES"] --> B["QUANTIZATION FIDELITY"]
    A --> C["DYNAMIC RANGE 6.02N dB"]
    A --> D["SNR 6.02N PLUS 1.76 dB"]
    A --> E["CONVERSION TIME 2N TIMES Tclock"]
    A --> F["CIRCUIT COMPLEXITY"]
    A --> G["POWER DISSIPATION"]
    B --> H["BETTER ANALOG ACCURACY"]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> I["LOWER THROUGHPUT"]
    F --> J["LARGER CHIP AREA"]
    G --> K["HIGTHER BATTERY DRAIN"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This diagram illustrates the **fundamental trade-off** in ADC/DAC design. You cannot increase $N$ (resolution) without paying a cost in *speed, area, and power*. KTU Module-4 problems often test this trade-off quantitatively — e.g., "If you double the resolution from 8 to 9 bits, by what factor does the conversion time of a successive-approximation ADC change?" (Answer: doubles, since $T_c \propto 2^N$.)
