# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: List any three advantages of an R-2R ladder DAC over a binary-weighted resistor DAC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The R-2R ladder DAC has the following advantages over a binary-weighted resistor DAC:

1. **Only two resistor values are required ($R$ and $2R$)**, regardless of the number of bits $N$. In contrast, a binary-weighted DAC needs $N$ distinct resistor values ranging from $R$ to $2^{N-1}R$. (1 mark)

2. **The resistor spread ratio is constant at $2:1$** for the R-2R ladder, whereas the binary-weighted design has a spread of $2^{N-1}:1$. This makes R-2R much easier to fabricate in IC technology and to match across temperature. (1 mark)

3. **The R-2R ladder offers better linearity and lower harmonic distortion** at high resolutions (8 bits and above) because the small resistor spread minimizes temperature-coefficient mismatch errors. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> For full marks, students should also mention that R-2R has faster settling time due to smaller parasitic capacitances and can be easily extended to higher bit counts by appending identical ladder sections.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the following ADC specifications: (i) Resolution, (ii) Quantization Error, (iii) Dynamic Range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Resolution (1 mark):**
Resolution is the smallest change in the analog input that an ADC can detect and convert into a distinct digital output code. It is determined by the number of bits $N$. The resolution in volts is given by the step size:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$

For example, a 10-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 5\,\text{V}$ has a resolution of $5/1024 \approx 4.88\,\text{mV}$.

**(ii) Quantization Error (1 mark):**
Quantization error is the inherent uncertainty introduced when a continuous analog input is mapped to the nearest discrete digital level. For an ideal ADC, this error is bounded within $\pm \Delta/2$, i.e., the analog value is always within half a step size of the true value. The RMS quantization error is $\Delta/\sqrt{12}$.

**(iii) Dynamic Range (1 mark):**
Dynamic Range (DR) is the ratio, expressed in decibels (dB), between the largest representable input signal (full-scale) and the smallest resolvable signal (1 LSB). For an $N$-bit ADC:

$$\text{DR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(2^N) = 6.02\,N \text{ dB}$$

For a 16-bit ADC, DR $= 6.02 \times 16 = 96.32\,\text{dB}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part-B questions on a single module topic provide **internal choice** (either/or). Below are two fully independent alternative question papers.

---

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**A (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **4-bit binary-weighted resistor DAC**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. State one major drawback of this architecture. **(7 marks)**

**A (b)** A **4-bit R-2R ladder DAC** has $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{REF} = 5\,\text{V}$, and feedback resistor $R_f = R$. Calculate:
- (i) the analog output for the input word $b_0 b_1 b_2 b_3 = 1011$, and
- (ii) the percentage resolution of the DAC.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**Circuit Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The 4-bit binary-weighted resistor DAC consists of:
- A reference voltage source $V_{REF}$.
- Four SPDT analog switches $S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3$ controlled by bits $b_0, b_1, b_2, b_3$ (where $b_0$ = MSB).
- Four precision resistors with values $R, 2R, 4R, 8R$ connected to the inverting input of an op-amp.
- An op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f$ and grounded non-inverting input.
- The output is taken from the op-amp output terminal.

**Operation Explanation (2 marks):**

When a bit $b_i = 1$, the corresponding switch routes the reference voltage $V_{REF}$ to the binary-weighted resistor. When $b_i = 0$, the switch grounds that resistor. The op-amp's inverting input acts as a **virtual ground**, so the current through each branch is determined solely by $V_{REF}$ and that branch's resistor value. These currents sum at the virtual-ground node and flow through $R_f$, producing an output voltage $V_o$ that is the **negative, binary-weighted sum** of the input bits scaled by $V_{REF}$ and the resistor ratio.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Current from bit $b_i$: $I_i = b_i V_{REF} / (2^i R)$.

KCL at virtual ground: $\sum I_i = -V_o / R_f$.

Solving with $R_f = R$:

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \sum_{i=0}^{3} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

**Major Drawback (1 mark):**

The resistor spread grows as $2^{N-1}:1$. For $N = 4$, the spread is $8:1$; for $N = 10$, it is $512:1$. Fabricating and matching such a wide range of precision resistors in monolithic IC technology is **extremely difficult**, leading to significant linearity errors and temperature-coefficient mismatches. This motivates the R-2R ladder architecture.

---

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**(i) Output voltage for input $b_0 b_1 b_2 b_3 = 1011$ (4 marks):**

For an R-2R ladder DAC with $R_f = R$:

$$V_o = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2} \sum_{i=0}^{3} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

Identify the active bits: $b_0 = 1$, $b_1 = 0$, $b_2 = 1$, $b_3 = 1$.

Compute the binary-weighted sum:

$$\sum = \frac{1}{1} + \frac{0}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{8} = 1 + 0 + 0.25 + 0.125 = 1.375$$

Substitute:

$$V_o = -\frac{5}{2} \times 1.375 = -2.5 \times 1.375 = -3.4375 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Percentage resolution (3 marks):**

For a 4-bit DAC, the total number of discrete output levels is:

$$2^4 = 16 \text{ levels}$$

The percentage resolution is:

$$\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^N} \times 100 = \frac{1}{16} \times 100 = 6.25\,\%$$

Equivalently, in terms of the full-scale output $V_{o,\text{FS}} = -V_{REF} \cdot (2^4 - 1)/2^4 = -5 \cdot 15/16 = -4.6875\,\text{V}$, the step size is:

$$\Delta = \frac{\vert V_{o,\text{FS}} \vert}{2^N - 1} = \frac{4.6875}{15} = 0.3125 \text{ V} \quad \text{(this equals } V_{REF}/2^N\text{)}$$

As a percentage of full scale: $0.3125 / 4.6875 \times 100 = 6.67\,\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (Valuation Pitfall):** Many students write the percentage resolution as $\frac{1}{2^N - 1} \times 100$. Both $\frac{1}{2^N}$ and $\frac{1}{2^N - 1}$ are accepted by most KTU examiners, but the **conventional textbook form** is $\frac{1}{2^N} \times 100$ (this gives the *fraction of full scale* per step). If the examiner is strict, $\frac{1}{2^N - 1}$ is for *fractional full-scale ratio per step* — clarify with your working. KTU officially uses $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$ and $\% = 100/2^N$.

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4 — Alternative]**

**B (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **4-bit R-2R ladder DAC**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. Mention two advantages over a binary-weighted resistor DAC. **(7 marks)**

**B (b)** Define the following ADC specifications and give the formula for each:
- (i) Resolution (step size)
- (ii) Quantization error (peak)
- (iii) Dynamic range
- (iv) Theoretical SNR

A **14-bit ADC** has a full-scale input range of $\pm 5\,\text{V}$. Calculate (i) the step size in mV, (ii) the percentage resolution, (iii) the dynamic range in dB, and (iv) the theoretical SNR in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**Circuit Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The R-2R ladder DAC consists of:
- A reference voltage $V_{REF}$ connected to the leftmost end of the ladder through a $2R$ resistor.
- A cascade of $R$ (horizontal/series) and $2R$ (vertical/rung) resistors.
- SPDT switches at each rung that connect the $2R$ to either $V_{REF}$ (bit = 1) or ground (bit = 0).
- The right end of the ladder is connected to the inverting input of an op-amp (virtual ground).
- The op-amp has feedback resistor $R_f$ and produces the analog output $V_o$.

**Operation Explanation (2 marks):**

The R-2R ladder functions as a **binary current divider**. Because of the ladder's self-similar geometry, the Thevenin equivalent resistance at every node is exactly $R$. When the MSB switch is at $V_{REF}$, the current entering the ladder splits equally at each successive node, halving at every step. The current contributed to the summing junction by bit $b_i$ is:

$$I_i = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{REF}}{2R} \cdot \frac{1}{2^i}$$

The op-amp converts this summed current to an output voltage through $R_f$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Total current at the inverting input:

$$I_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=0}^{3} \frac{b_i V_{REF}}{2R \cdot 2^i} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2R} \sum_{i=0}^{3} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

Output voltage:

$$V_o = -R_f \cdot I_{\text{total}} = -\frac{R_f V_{REF}}{2R} \sum_{i=0}^{3} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

With $R_f = R$:

$$V_o = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2} \sum_{i=0}^{3} \frac{b_i}{2^i}$$

**Two Advantages (1 mark):**

1. Only two resistor values ($R$ and $2R$) are needed, making it ideal for IC fabrication.
2. The ladder structure is modular — adding more bits requires only appending more $R/2R$ sections, with no change in resistor spread.

---

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Definitions and Formulas (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

**(i) Resolution (Step Size):** The smallest analog input change distinguishable by the ADC.

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} \text{ (volts)}$$

**(ii) Quantization Error (peak):** The maximum deviation between the actual analog input and its quantized digital representation.

$$e_{q,\text{peak}} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**(iii) Dynamic Range:** Ratio of full-scale input to the smallest resolvable signal, in dB.

$$\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}(2^N) = 6.02\,N \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Theoretical SNR:** For a full-scale sine wave input, the ratio of signal power to quantization-noise power.

$$\text{SNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

**Numerical Calculations for 14-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 10\,\text{V}$ (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

For a bipolar $\pm 5\,\text{V}$ input, the full-scale range is $V_{FS} = 10\,\text{V}$.

**(i) Step size:**

$$\Delta = \frac{10}{2^{14}} = \frac{10}{16384} \approx 6.104 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 0.6104 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Percentage resolution:**

$$\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^{14}} \times 100 = \frac{1}{16384} \times 100 \approx 0.0061\,\%$$

**(iii) Dynamic Range:**

$$\text{DR} = 6.02 \times 14 = 84.28 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Theoretical SNR:**

$$\text{SNR} = 6.02 \times 14 + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04 \text{ dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — KTU Examiner's Warning:**
> 1. **Bipolar range handling:** If the input is $\pm 5\,\text{V}$, the *full-scale range* is $V_{FS} = 10\,\text{V}$, not $5\,\text{V}$. A common error is to use $5\,\text{V}$ in the step-size formula. This leads to a halved step size and an incorrect answer — full loss of marks for that sub-part.
> 2. **SNR vs Dynamic Range confusion:** SNR is $6.02N + 1.76$ dB; DR is $6.02N$ dB. The 1.76 dB offset is non-negotiable.
> 3. **MSB/LSB indexing:** The MSB always has weight $2^0$ in the *binary-weighted sum formula* but $2^{N-1}$ in the *step-size formula*. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read These Bullets 5 Times Before the Exam:**

### DAC Architectures
- [ ] **Weighted Resistor DAC**: Resistors are $R, 2R, 4R, ..., 2^{N-1}R$. Output (with $R_f = R$): $V_o = -V_{REF} \sum b_i/2^i$. Drawback: huge resistor spread for large $N$.
- [ ] **R-2R Ladder DAC**: Only $R$ and $2R$ are used. Output: $V_o = -(V_{REF}/2) \sum b_i/2^i$. Advantage: easy IC fabrication, modular extension.
- [ ] **Sign Convention:** Both architectures produce a *negative* output for positive $V_{REF}$ (because of the inverting op-amp). Do not forget the negative sign in your answer.
- [ ] **$R_f = R$ assumption** is the KTU default. If the problem specifies a different $R_f$, scale the output by $R_f/R$.

### ADC Specifications
- [ ] **Resolution $N$ bits** → $2^N$ discrete codes.
- [ ] **Step size** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$ (volts).
- [ ] **% Resolution** = $(1/2^N) \times 100$ (%).
- [ ] **Quantization error** (peak) = $\pm \Delta/2$.
- [ ] **Quantization error** (RMS) = $\Delta/\sqrt{12}$.
- [ ] **Dynamic Range** = $6.02\,N$ dB.
- [ ] **Theoretical SNR** = $6.02\,N + 1.76$ dB.
- [ ] **ENOB** (from measured SNR) = $(\text{SNR} - 1.76) / 6.02$.
- [ ] **Conversion time** for SAR and counter-type ADCs scales as $2^N \cdot T_{\text{clock}}$.
- [ ] **Aperture jitter error** $\propto f_{in} \cdot t_j$ — increases with input frequency.
- [ ] **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.

### High-Yield Numerical Problem Patterns
- [ ] **"Find $V_o$ for input word X"** → Use the appropriate formula (weighted or R-2R) with the given $R$, $V_{REF}$, $R_f$.
- [ ] **"Find step size / resolution in mV"** → $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$.
- [ ] **"Find SNR in dB for N-bit ADC"** → $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$.
- [ ] **"Find ENOB from measured SNR"** → $(\text{SNR} - 1.76)/6.02$.
- [ ] **"Compare weighted vs R-2R"** → 3-advantages / 3-disadvantages table.
- [ ] **"Define aperture time and aperture jitter"** → Time uncertainty at sampling instant; jitter is the standard deviation of that uncertainty.

### Quick-Reference Memory Aid: "**6.02 and 1.76**"
- $6.02$ = $20 \log_{10}(2)$ — appears in both DR and SNR formulas.
- $1.76$ = $10 \log_{10}(1.5)$ — the SNR-only correction for sine-wave RMS.
- **Mnemonic:** "**DR is *6.02N*; SNR is *6.02N + 1.76*; the extra 1.76 is the sine-wave tax.**"

### Mnemonic for DAC Output Formulas
- **Weighted:** "*Vref times the binary sum, with a negative sign, no half.*"
- **R-2R:** "*Same, but divided by 2.*"

### Common Mistakes to AVOID
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting the negative sign in $V_o$.
- [ ] ❌ Confusing $2^N$ with $2^N - 1$ in the step-size formula (use $2^N$ for $\Delta$ in volts).
- [ ] ❌ Using the bipolar range value (e.g., $\pm 5$ V) instead of the full-scale range (10 V) in step-size calculations.
- [ ] ❌ Confusing MSB/LSB indexing when using $\sum b_i / 2^i$.
- [ ] ❌ Adding the 1.76 dB offset to DR (DR has NO 1.76 offset).
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting to mention *virtual ground* when deriving the DAC output.
- [ ] ❌ Confusing the **inverting** summing amplifier with a non-inverting configuration (the latter would give positive output but is rarely used in monolithic DACs).

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Warning:**
> Module 4 (DACs and ADC specifications) is heavily numerical in KTU ESE. Always:
> 1. **Show the formula** before substituting numbers.
> 2. **State the units** (V, mV, dB, %) in the final answer.
> 3. **Draw the circuit diagram** for any "explain the operation" question — *at minimum* a labelled block diagram earns 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Carry at least 4 significant digits** in intermediate calculations to avoid rounding errors in the final answer.
> 5. **Mention at least one advantage and one disadvantage** when comparing architectures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash Type and Successive Approximation Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT403 - LINEAR INTEGRATED CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flash Type and Successive Approximation Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Flash Type ADC (Parallel ADC)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Flash ADC** (also called the **Parallel ADC**) is the fastest type of analog-to-digital converter. It performs conversion in a single step (one comparator propagation delay) by using a bank of **2^n − 1** high-speed comparators that simultaneously compare the input analog voltage against **2^n − 1** equally spaced reference voltages generated by a precision resistor ladder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Flash ADC is the textbook example of a *high-speed, low-resolution* converter. KTU frequently tests its component count formula and its limitation for high bit-counts.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a weighing scale that does not weigh your luggage progressively. Instead, it has **many arms pre-fixed at different reference weights** (say 1 kg, 2 kg, 3 kg … 15 kg). When you place your bag, every arm that is *lighter than your bag* lifts up at the exact same instant. A small "decoder" then reads the highest arm that lifted, and instantly tells you the digital weight. That is exactly how a Flash ADC works — every comparator decides *simultaneously*, and the winner is encoded into binary.

---

### 1.2 Successive Approximation Type ADC (SAR ADC)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** is a *medium-speed, medium-to-high resolution* converter that uses a **binary search algorithm** to find the digital equivalent of an analog input. It requires exactly **n clock cycles** to produce an n-bit output, and it is built around four key blocks: a **DAC**, a **single comparator**, a **SAR register**, and a **control/sequencing logic**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The SAR ADC is the most popular general-purpose ADC architecture in the world (used in almost every microcontroller, sensor, and instrumentation system). KTU expects a clear understanding of its *n-cycle binary search process* and the role of each sub-block.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of the classic game *"Higher or Lower"*. You secretly pick a number between 0 and 15. I start by guessing **8** (half of 15). If you say *"higher"*, I know the answer is 9–15, so I next guess **12**. If you say *"lower"*, I know it is 9–11, so I next guess **10**. After exactly **4 such guesses** I always reach the exact number. The SAR ADC plays this exact game, with the comparator playing the role of your *"higher/lower"* response, and the DAC playing the role of my *"guess"*.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Resolution (Q):** $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale voltage.
- **Flash ADC comparator count:** $\mathbf{N_c = 2^n - 1}$.
- **Flash ADC resistor count:** $\mathbf{N_R = 2^n}$.
- **SAR ADC conversion cycles:** $\mathbf{N_{clk} = n}$ clock pulses per conversion.
- Standard supply rails for monolithic ICs: **±15 V (analog)**, **+5 V (digital)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Flash Type ADC — Operational Walk-Through

The Flash ADC works on the principle of **simultaneous parallel comparison**.

**Step 1 — Reference Ladder Generation:**
A string of **2^n** equal-value resistors is connected between the negative reference (−Vref) and the positive reference (+Vref). This creates a voltage divider, producing **2^n − 1** discrete tap voltages.

**Step 2 — Parallel Comparison:**
Each of the **2^n − 1** comparators receives the analog input $V_{in}$ on its non-inverting terminal and one tap voltage on its inverting terminal. All comparators operate *concurrently*.

**Step 3 — Thermometer Code Output:**
A comparator whose tap voltage is *lower* than $V_{in}$ produces a logic **1**. The combined output is a string of consecutive 1s followed by consecutive 0s — this is called the **"thermometer code"** because it resembles a mercury thermometer.

**Step 4 — Priority Encoding:**
A priority encoder converts the thermometer code into an n-bit binary output. The conversion is complete in essentially *one propagation delay*.

**Why so fast?** There is no iterative feedback — everything happens in parallel.

**Why so expensive?** Doubling the resolution (n → n+1) doubles the number of comparators. A 10-bit Flash ADC needs 1023 comparators; a 16-bit one would need 65,535 — practically impossible.

---

### 2.2 SAR Type ADC — Operational Walk-Through

The SAR ADC works on the principle of **iterative binary search**.

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
At the start of a conversion, the SAR register is cleared to `0...0`. The MSB is then forced to `1`, producing a trial code `100...0`.

**Step 2 — DAC Conversion:**
The trial code is fed to an internal DAC, which generates an analog voltage $V_{DAC}$.

**Step 3 — Single Comparison:**
The single comparator tests whether $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$:
- If **YES** → the MSB remains `1` (the guess was too low).
- If **NO** → the MSB is reset to `0` (the guess was too high).

**Step 4 — Bit Progression:**
The next bit is then forced to `1`, the process repeats, and so on down to the LSB. After exactly **n clock cycles**, all n bits are decided and a *conversion-complete* flag (EOC) is asserted.

**Why is it popular?** It achieves excellent resolution with only **one comparator and one DAC**, making it cheap, low-power, and very accurate.

**Trade-off:** Conversion time grows linearly with bit-count, so it is slower than Flash but vastly more practical for n ≥ 8.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resolution (LSB size) | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Volts per step |
| 2 | Number of comparators in Flash | $N_c = 2^n - 1$ | Pure count |
| 3 | Number of ladder resistors in Flash | $N_R = 2^n$ | Pure count |
| 4 | Flash k-th tap voltage | $V_k = -V_{ref} + \left(k + \tfrac{1}{2}\right) \dfrac{2V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Volts, k = 0, 1, …, 2^n − 2 |
| 5 | SAR conversion time | $T_c = n \cdot T_{clk}$ | Seconds |
| 6 | SAR maximum input frequency (Nyquist) | $f_{in,max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \, n \, T_{clk}}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Percentage resolution | $\%\text{Res} = \dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100$ | Percent |
| 8 | Flash throughput advantage | 1 conversion per comparator delay | Constant w.r.t. n |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always state units explicitly** (e.g., *"The resolution is 0.0049 V or 4.9 mV"*). The examiner deducts marks if units are missing for a numerical answer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flash ADCs** dominate in **digital oscilloscopes, radar receivers, high-speed data acquisition cards, and optical communication links** where GHz sampling is required.
- **SAR ADCs** dominate in **microcontroller ADCs, industrial sensors, battery monitors, digital multimeters, and medical instrumentation** (ECG, blood-pressure monitors) where resolution, power, and cost matter more than raw speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Tap Voltages of a Flash ADC Resistor Ladder

Consider an n-bit Flash ADC with a resistor ladder of **2^n** equal resistors **R** connected between $-V_{ref}$ and $+V_{ref}$. The total ladder voltage is $2V_{ref}$, so the voltage drop per resistor is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{2V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

The first comparator's tap (k = 0) sits one resistor above $-V_{ref}$:

$$V_0 = -V_{ref} + \Delta V = -V_{ref} + \frac{2V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

The k-th tap voltage (k = 0, 1, …, 2^n − 2) is:

$$V_k = -V_{ref} + \left(k + \frac{1}{2}\right) \Delta V$$

Substituting $\Delta V = \dfrac{2V_{ref}}{2^n}$:

$$V_k = -V_{ref} + \left(k + \frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{2V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

This is the *mid-tap* voltage of each resistor segment, chosen so that the comparator threshold lies in the *center* of the quantization band. This placement minimizes the *maximum comparator offset error*.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Flash ADC Component Count

**Question:** An 8-bit Flash ADC uses a resistor ladder and a priority encoder. Find the number of comparators, the number of ladder resistors, and the resolution for a full-scale range of **10 V**.

**Step 1 — Number of comparators:**
$$N_c = 2^n - 1 = 2^8 - 1 = 256 - 1 = 255$$

**Step 2 — Number of resistors:**
$$N_R = 2^n = 2^8 = 256$$

**Step 3 — Resolution (LSB size):**
$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^8} = \frac{10}{256} = 0.0390625 \text{ V} \approx 39.06 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage drop per resistor:**
$$\Delta V = \frac{2V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{2 \times 5}{256} = \frac{10}{256} = 0.0390625 \text{ V}$$

> **Conclusion:** An 8-bit Flash ADC over a 10 V FS range needs 255 comparators, 256 resistors, and 1 priority encoder, with a step size of 39.06 mV.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — SAR ADC Binary Search

**Question:** A 4-bit SAR ADC has $V_{ref} = 16$ V. Convert an analog input $V_{in} = 10$ V into its digital equivalent using the binary search algorithm.

**Step 0 — Define variables:**
- Bit positions: b3 (MSB), b2, b1, b0 (LSB)
- Weight per bit: 8 V, 4 V, 2 V, 1 V
- Initial trial code: `1000` (binary), $V_{DAC} = 8$ V

**Step 1 — Test b3 (MSB):**
Trial code = `1000`, $V_{DAC} = 8$ V.

Since $V_{in} = 10$ V $> V_{DAC} = 8$ V, the bit b3 is **kept as 1** (the guess was too low).

**Step 2 — Test b2:**
Trial code = `1100`, $V_{DAC} = 8 + 4 = 12$ V.

Since $V_{in} = 10$ V $< V_{DAC} = 12$ V, the bit b2 is **reset to 0** (the guess was too high).

**Step 3 — Test b1:**
Trial code = `1010`, $V_{DAC} = 8 + 0 + 2 = 10$ V.

Since $V_{in} = 10$ V $= V_{DAC} = 10$ V (treated as $\geq$), the bit b1 is **kept as 1**.

**Step 4 — Test b0 (LSB):**
Trial code = `1011`, $V_{DAC} = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11$ V.

Since $V_{in} = 10$ V $< V_{DAC} = 11$ V, the bit b0 is **reset to 0**.

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$\text{Output} = b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1010_{\text{bin}} = 10_{\text{dec}}$$

This perfectly matches $V_{in} = 10$ V, since $V_{ref} = 16$ V gives a step size of $Q = 16/2^4 = 1$ V.

> **Conclusion:** A 4-bit SAR ADC required **exactly 4 clock cycles** to perform the complete conversion. Notice the binary-search nature: each cycle halves the remaining uncertainty.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Flash vs SAR)

| Parameter | Flash ADC | SAR ADC |
|---|---|---|
| Conversion time | 1 clock ≈ few ns | n clocks |
| Number of comparators | $2^n - 1$ | $1$ |
| Number of DACs | $0$ | $1$ |
| Resolution achievable | 6–8 bits (practical) | 8–20 bits (commercial) |
| Power consumption | Very high | Low |
| Cost per channel | High | Low |
| Typical use | Oscilloscopes, radar | Sensors, MCUs, DMMs |
| Susceptibility to comparator offset | High | Low (single comp.) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flash ADC — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcREF["Reference Vref plus"] --- ladderR["Resistor Ladder 2 to n resistors"]
    ladderR --- srcREFneg["Reference Vref minus"]
    ladderR -- tap voltages --> compBank["Comparator Bank 2 to n minus 1 comparators"]
    vin["Analog Input Vin"] --> compBank
    compBank -- thermometer code --> priEnc["Priority Encoder"]
    priEnc -- n bit binary --> out["Digital Output D0 to D n minus 1"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Every rectangle is a functional block. Arrows represent the *direction of signal flow*. Notice that **Vin branches into all comparators simultaneously**, which is the *defining parallel feature* of the Flash architecture.

---

### 4.2 SAR ADC — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Conversion Start pulse"] --> sar["SAR Register n bits"]
    sar -- trial code --> dac["Internal DAC"]
    dac -- VDAC analog --> comp["Comparator"]
    vin["Analog Input Vin"] --> comp
    comp -- HIGH or LOW --> sar
    clk["Clock Generator"] -- clock pulses --> sar
    sar -- n bit binary --> outReg["Output Latch"]
    outReg -- EOC flag --> ready["End of Conversion flag"]
    sar -- final code --> outReg
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is a **closed feedback loop** — the SAR register drives the DAC, the DAC drives the comparator, and the comparator's output drives the SAR. The loop runs **n times**, then a flag signals completion.

---

### 4.3 SAR State Machine (Per-Cycle Behaviour)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset
    Reset --> TestMSB: Cycle 1
    TestMSB --> TestBit2: Cycle 2
    TestBit2 --> TestBit1: Cycle 3
    TestBit1 --> TestLSB: Cycle n
    TestLSB --> AssertEOC: All bits decided
    AssertEOC --> [*]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Each `TestBitX` block internally does the *compare-and-decide* step. After exactly n transitions, the **End of Conversion (EOC)** flag goes HIGH and the digital code is latched to the output pins.

---

### 4.4 Flash ADC Resistor Ladder — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vrefP["+Vref"] --- r1["R"]
    r1 --- t1["Tap 1"]
    t1 --- r2["R"]
    r2 --- t2["Tap 2"]
    t2 --- r3["R"]
    r3 --- t3["Tap 3"]
    t3 --- rd["..."]
    rd --- rn["R"]
    rn --- vrefN["-Vref"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Each "Tap" feeds the inverting input of one comparator. The taps are spaced at exactly $\Delta V = 2V_{ref}/2^n$ apart. Only the ladder structure is shown for clarity — comparators are omitted.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the major building blocks of a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC. Briefly state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **SAR Register (1 mark):** Holds the current trial code; performs bit-by-bit binary search starting from MSB.
2. **DAC (1 mark):** Converts the trial digital code into an equivalent analog voltage $V_{DAC}$ for comparison with $V_{in}$.
3. **Comparator (1/2 mark) + Control Logic (1/2 mark):** A single high-gain comparator evaluates $V_{in}$ vs $V_{DAC}$ and tells the SAR whether to keep or reset the current bit; the controller sequences bits MSB → LSB.

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Why is a Flash ADC called a "parallel" ADC, and what is its biggest practical limitation for high resolution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Flash ADC is called *parallel* because **all 2^n − 1 comparators evaluate the input simultaneously** in a single parallel step, rather than serially (1 mark). The conversion happens in essentially *one clock period* (1 mark).

**Biggest limitation:** The number of comparators grows as $2^n - 1$. For an 8-bit ADC, 255 comparators are required; for 16 bits, 65,535 comparators would be needed, which is **prohibitively expensive, power-hungry, and physically impossible to fabricate on a single chip** (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** In Part B questions, students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Both are provided below for full coverage.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a 3-bit Flash ADC and explain its working. Compute the number of comparators and resistors required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
A 3-bit Flash ADC consists of a resistor ladder (8 resistors) generating 7 reference voltages, 7 comparators that compare $V_{in}$ with each reference, and a 7-to-3 priority encoder that converts the thermometer code into binary.

```
Vref(+)---R--R--R--R--R--R--R--R---Vref(-)
          |  |  |  |  |  |  |
         C1 C2 C3 C4 C5 C6 C7       (Comparators)
          |  |  |  |  |  |  |
          +--+--+--+--+--+--+
                  |
            Priority Encoder
                  |
              D2 D1 D0  (3-bit output)
```

**Working (3 marks):**
Each comparator has $V_{in}$ on its non-inverting input and one tap voltage on its inverting input. The comparator outputs `1` if $V_{in}$ exceeds its tap voltage, else `0`. The combined outputs form a thermometer code (e.g., `0000111` for $V_{in}$ just above the 4th tap). The priority encoder reads the position of the *highest* `1` and converts it into the corresponding 3-bit binary number.

**Component Count (1 mark):**
- Comparators: $N_c = 2^n - 1 = 2^3 - 1 = 7$
- Resistors: $N_R = 2^n = 2^3 = 8$

**(b)** An 8-bit Flash ADC is designed for a full-scale input of 0 to 5 V. Calculate (i) the resolution, (ii) the percentage resolution, and (iii) the number of comparators. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Resolution (3 marks):**
$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^8} = \frac{5}{256} = 0.01953125 \text{ V} \approx 19.53 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Percentage Resolution (2 marks):**
$$\%\text{Res} = \frac{Q}{V_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{1}{2^n} \times 100 = \frac{1}{256} \times 100 = 0.390625\,\%$$

**(iii) Number of Comparators (2 marks):**
$$N_c = 2^n - 1 = 2^8 - 1 = 255 \text{ comparators}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 4-bit Successive Approximation ADC. How many clock cycles are required for one complete conversion? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
A 4-bit SAR ADC contains:
- A **4-bit SAR register** that holds the trial code
- A **4-bit DAC** (e.g., R-2R ladder) that converts the trial code to $V_{DAC}$
- A **single comparator** that decides `HIGH` if $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, else `LOW`
- A **control/sequencing logic** block driven by a clock

```
SAR Register ---> DAC ---> (+) Comparator (-) <--- Vin
       ^              |
       |              v
       +-- (compare decision) <-- Clock & Control Logic
```

**Working (3 marks):**
At the start of conversion, the SAR is reset and the MSB (b3) is set to 1, giving the trial code `1000`. The DAC outputs $V_{DAC} = V_{ref}/2$. The comparator checks:
- If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$ → b3 is **retained** as 1.
- If $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$ → b3 is **reset** to 0.

The process repeats for b2, b1, b0 in sequence. Each bit decision takes exactly **one clock cycle**. After 4 cycles, the EOC flag is asserted and the digital code is available on the output lines.

**Number of Clock Cycles (1 mark):**
$$N_{clk} = n = 4 \text{ clock pulses per conversion}$$

**(b)** A 6-bit SAR ADC with $V_{ref} = 10$ V receives an analog input $V_{in} = 6.8$ V. Perform the binary search step-by-step and find the final digital output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup (1 mark):**
- $n = 6$ bits, weights = 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1 V (LSB = 10/64 = 0.15625 V)
- $V_{in} = 6.8$ V

**Cycle 1 — Test b5 (32 V):**
Trial = `100000`, $V_{DAC} = 32$ V.
$6.8 < 32$ → b5 = **0**.

**Cycle 2 — Test b4 (16 V):**
Trial = `010000`, $V_{DAC} = 16$ V.
$6.8 < 16$ → b4 = **0**.

**Cycle 3 — Test b3 (8 V):**
Trial = `001000`, $V_{DAC} = 8$ V.
$6.8 < 8$ → b3 = **0**.

**Cycle 4 — Test b2 (4 V):**
Trial = `000100`, $V_{DAC} = 4$ V.
$6.8 > 4$ → b2 = **1**.

**Cycle 5 — Test b1 (2 V):**
Trial = `000110`, $V_{DAC} = 4 + 2 = 6$ V.
$6.8 > 6$ → b1 = **1**.

**Cycle 6 — Test b0 (1 V):**
Trial = `000111`, $V_{DAC} = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7$ V.
$6.8 < 7$ → b0 = **0**.

**Final Output (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{000110_{\text{bin}} = 6_{\text{dec}} \quad \text{(representing 6 V; quantization error} = 0.8 \text{ V)}}$$

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the −1 in comparator count** — Students often write 256 comparators for an 8-bit Flash ADC instead of 255. The ladder has 2^n resistors, but only 2^n − 1 *taps between them*.
> 2. **Confusing Q and Vref** — Q is the *step size* (Vref/2^n), not the reference itself.
> 3. **Wrong SAR cycle direction** — Always start from the **MSB** and go down to the LSB, *not* the reverse.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always write V, mV, µV, %, or cycles explicitly.
> 5. **Omitting the role of the priority encoder** — Many students draw the Flash ADC but forget to explain how the thermometer code becomes binary; this costs 1–2 marks easily.
> 6. **Forgetting the EOC flag** — The End-of-Conversion flag is a *vital* part of every SAR ADC; omitting it costs marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flash ADC = parallel, ultra-fast, low resolution, expensive.**
- **SAR ADC = iterative, medium speed, high resolution, cheap and popular.**
- Flash component count: **2^n − 1 comparators + 2^n resistors + 1 priority encoder.**
- SAR component count: **1 SAR + 1 DAC + 1 comparator + control logic.**
- Flash conversion time ≈ **one comparator delay** (independent of n).
- SAR conversion time = **n clock cycles** (linear in n).
- Resolution formula: $Q = V_{FS}/2^n$ — applies to *both* architectures.
- Tap voltages of the Flash ladder are placed at the **midpoint of each segment** to minimize offset error.
- SAR algorithm: start with MSB = 1, compare, decide, move to next bit, repeat.
- Quantization error: bounded by $\pm \tfrac{1}{2} \text{LSB} = \pm \tfrac{Q}{2}$.
- For n ≥ 8, **SAR is almost always preferred** over Flash due to cost, power, and die-area advantages.
- Always include **units, formulas, and intermediate steps** in KTU numerical answers.
